<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define IoT Infrastructure. List any FOUR of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

> **IoT Infrastructure** is the integrated set of hardware, software, networks, and services that enable sensing, communication, computation, and actuation in an IoT ecosystem.

**Four key characteristics:**
1. **Scalability** — supports millions of heterogeneous devices.
2. **Interoperability** — works across diverse protocols and vendors.
3. **Real-time Responsiveness** — low latency for time-critical applications.
4. **Security & Privacy** — end-to-end encryption and access control.

*(For full marks, students should mention at least one standard metric such as latency in **ms** or scalability in **node count**.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Edge Computing and Cloud Computing in the context of IoT infrastructure.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Edge Computing | Cloud Computing |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Location** | Near the data source (gateway) | Remote data centers |
| **Latency** | Very low (< 10 ms) | Higher (50–500 ms) |
| **Compute Power** | Limited, constrained | Virtually unlimited |
| **Use Case** | Real-time control, ML inference | Batch analytics, big-data storage |
| **Connectivity Need** | Can work intermittently | Requires constant connectivity |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Only **ONE choice** (either Q A or Q B) is to be attempted.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer architecture of IoT Infrastructure**. Describe the function of each layer with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A LoRaWAN node transmits at $f = 915$ MHz over a distance of $d = 12$ km. Compute the **Free-Space Path Loss** in dB. If the transmit power is **+20 dBm** and the receiver sensitivity is **−130 dBm**, determine the **available link margin**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### (a) Five-Layer Architecture (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
The five-layer architecture provides a more granular, deployment-ready view of IoT infrastructure than the simple three-layer model.

**Step 2 — Layer-by-Layer Explanation (5 marks, 1 per layer):**

1. **Perception Layer** — Acquires raw data from the physical world using **sensors** (e.g., DHT22 for temperature) and **actuators** (e.g., servo motors). Uses RFID/QR codes for identification.

2. **Transport / Network Layer** — Transfers data from perception layer to processing centers. Uses **Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT, 6LoWPAN, RPL**.

3. **Processing Layer** — Stores, analyses, and acts on data. Includes **Edge** (local), **Fog** (LAN-level), and **Cloud** (global) tiers. Technologies: AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, FIWARE.

4. **Application Layer** — Delivers user-facing services such as **smart home control, predictive maintenance, remote patient monitoring**.

5. **Business Layer** — Manages overall system goals, ROI, SLA compliance, and governance. Uses **BI tools, dashboards**.

**Step 3 — Diagram (1 mark):**
[Draw a clean labeled block diagram of the 5 layers stacked vertically with arrows showing data flow — similar to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above.]

##### (b) Path Loss and Link Margin (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the FSPL formula.**

$$
FSPL \;(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $d = 12$ km and $f = 915$ MHz.**

$$
FSPL = 20\log_{10}(12) + 20\log_{10}(915) + 32.44
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term.**

$\log_{10}(12) = 1.0792$, so $20\log_{10}(12) = 21.58$ dB.

$\log_{10}(915) = \log_{10}(9.15 \times 10^{2}) = 2 + \log_{10}(9.15) = 2 + 0.9614 = 2.9614$.

So $20\log_{10}(915) = 20 \times 2.9614 = 59.23$ dB.

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$
FSPL = 21.58 + 59.23 + 32.44 = 113.25 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute link margin.**

$$
\text{Margin} = P_{tx} - \text{Sensitivity} - \text{FSPL}
$$

$$
\text{Margin} = 20 - (-130) - 113.25 = 150 - 113.25 = 36.75 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
A positive margin of **+36.75 dB** confirms the link is viable, with **comfortable headroom** for foliage, rain, and interference.

$$
\boxed{FSPL = 113.25 \text{ dB} \quad ; \quad \text{Link Margin} = 36.75 \text{ dB}}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **three-layer** and **five-layer** IoT architectures. Justify why the five-layer model is preferred for industrial deployments. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IoT smart-metering network has **$N = 1000$ nodes**, each with a raw bit rate of **$R = 50$ kbps**, operating at a duty cycle of **$DC = 1\%$**. Calculate the **aggregate network capacity** and comment on whether a **1 Mbps backhaul** is sufficient. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### (a) Comparison of Architectures (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Three-Layer | Five-Layer |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Layers | Perception, Network, Application | Perception, Transport, Processing, Application, Business |
| Granularity | Low | High |
| Suitable for | Academic, simple prototypes | Industrial, production-grade |
| Edge processing | Not explicit | Explicit layer |
| Business logic | Not modeled | Dedicated layer |

**Advantages of Five-Layer in Industry (3 marks):**
1. **Separation of concerns** — network, compute, and business are independently scalable.
2. **Explicit edge/fog support** — critical for **< 10 ms** control loops in IIoT.
3. **Security and management can be embedded** as a cross-cutting layer.
4. **Better alignment** with reference architectures such as **IIC (Industrial Internet Consortium)** and **RAMI 4.0**.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Include a labeled block diagram.

##### (b) Aggregate Network Capacity (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the duty-cycled capacity formula.**

$$
C_{net} = N \cdot R \cdot DC
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
C_{net} = 1000 \times (50 \times 10^{3}) \times 0.01
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply.**

$$
C_{net} = 1000 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.01 = 500{,}000 \text{ bps} = 500 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare against the 1 Mbps backhaul.**

$$
C_{net} = 500 \text{ kbps} \quad \text{vs.} \quad C_{backhaul} = 1000 \text{ kbps}
$$

Utilization = $\dfrac{500}{1000} \times 100\% = 50\%$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
A **1 Mbps backhaul is sufficient**, operating at only **50% utilization**, leaving **500 kbps of headroom** for control traffic, retries, and future scalability to ~2000 nodes.

$$
\boxed{C_{net} = 500 \text{ kbps} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Backhaul is sufficient with 50\% headroom.}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "Infrastructure" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Layers:** Students often call the *Transport Layer* the *Network Layer*. KTU expects **Network Layer = Layer 3 (IP/6LoWPAN)** and **Transport Layer = TCP/UDP**.
> 2. **Missing Units in FSPL:** Always state "**d in km, f in MHz**" before substituting — losing 1 mark otherwise.
> 3. **Wrong Direction of Margin:** Link margin is computed as **$P_{tx}$ − Sensitivity − FSPL**, not the other way around.
> 4. **Ignoring Security Layer:** In any architecture diagram, omitting the **cross-cutting security plane** is an automatic **−1 mark** deduction.
> 5. **Cloud ≠ Edge:** Writing "Edge is a small cloud" loses 2 marks. State that **edge is constrained, decentralized, and latency-optimized**, while **cloud is centralized, elastic, and throughput-optimized**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "IoT Infrastructure" (Module 1):**
> 
> - **Definition:** Integrated HW + SW + Network + Cloud + Security that underpins every IoT deployment.
> - **Three-Layer Architecture:** Perception → Network → Application *(quick-draw answer)*.
> - **Five-Layer Architecture:** Perception → Transport → Processing → Application → Business *(preferred for full marks)*.
> - **Components:** Sensors, actuators, gateways, edge/fog/cloud, middleware, security.
> - **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — appear in **every numerical paper**.
> - **FSPL:** $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ dB — units are **km, MHz**.
> - **Link Budget:** $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - L_{path} - L_{margin}$.
> - **Duty-Cycled Capacity:** $C_{net} = N \cdot R \cdot DC$.
> - **Protocols to remember:** **MQTT** (pub/sub), **CoAP** (REST over UDP), **HTTP**, **6LoWPAN**, **RPL**.
> - **Key Communication Technologies:** Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G mMTC.
> - **Edge vs Cloud:** Always mention **latency (ms vs seconds)**, **location**, and **use case**.
> - **Security is cross-cutting**, not a separate layer.
> - **Golden diagram:** Five-layer architecture block with security overlay = **guaranteed marks**.

---

*End of Notes — Infrastructure (Module 1, OEITT723)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Networks and Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Networks and Communication

## 1.1 Introduction to Computer Networks

### Core Technical Definition
A **computer network** is a set of interconnected computing devices (nodes) that exchange data and share resources using a standardized set of rules known as communication protocols. In the context of the Internet of Things (IoT), networks form the digital backbone that allows physical "things" (sensors, actuators, embedded devices) to communicate with each other, with edge gateways, with the cloud, and with end-user applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Networks and Communication is a foundational module in OEITT723. Students must clearly differentiate between OSI/TCP-IP layered architecture, the various PAN/LAN/MAN/WAN classifications, and the unique communication constraints imposed by resource-constrained IoT devices.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **postal system** in a country:
- The **letters** are your **data packets**.
- The **addresses written on the envelope** are the **IP/MAC addresses**.
- The **postal rules** (how to sort, how to route, what to do if the address is wrong) are the **protocols**.
- The **post offices and delivery trucks** are the **routers and physical media**.
- The **houses and offices** are the **end devices (nodes)**.

In an IoT network, your sensor (a temperature sensor in a warehouse) acts like a house sending letters. It packages the temperature value, addresses it to a cloud server, and hands it over to a gateway (local post office) which routes it through the internet (long-haul trucks) to the destination (cloud server). The protocol dictates how the envelope is formatted, how errors are handled, and how delivery is confirmed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** Modern IoT networks must balance **four constraints simultaneously**: bandwidth, latency, energy consumption, and security. This trade-off is the central design challenge of IoT communication.

### Why Networks Matter in IoT
IoT fundamentally extends the internet beyond traditional computing devices to **physical objects**. Without robust communication networks, sensors would collect data in isolation, and actuators would have no way to receive commands. The network layer is what transforms a collection of isolated embedded devices into a true **"Internet of Things."**

---

## 1.2 Network Classification by Geographic Scale

Networks are classified by their physical span, which directly impacts the choice of technology, protocols, and topology used.

| Classification | Geographic Span | Typical IoT Use Case | Example Technologies |
|----------------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **PAN** (Personal Area Network) | Few meters (around a person) | Wearables, smartphone peripherals | Bluetooth, ZigBee, NFC, BLE |
| **LAN** (Local Area Network) | Building or campus | Smart home, smart office | Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Thread |
| **MAN** (Metropolitan Area Network) | City-wide | Smart traffic, smart grid | WiMAX, Metro Ethernet |
| **WAN** (Wide Area Network) | Country/Global | Global IoT telemetry | Cellular (4G/5G), Satellite, LoRaWAN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for Exams:** PAN < LAN < MAN < WAN. Each scale-up generally introduces higher latency, lower bandwidth density, and higher infrastructure cost per node.

---

## 1.3 Network Topologies

A **topology** defines the geometric arrangement of nodes and the logical paths data takes.

### 1.3.1 Physical vs Logical Topology
- **Physical topology**: The actual physical layout of cables and devices.
- **Logical topology**: The actual data flow path, which may differ from the physical layout.

### 1.3.2 Common Topologies
1. **Bus Topology** – All nodes share a single backbone cable. Simple but a single break disables the network.
2. **Star Topology** – All nodes connect to a central hub/switch. Most common in LANs; failure of the hub is catastrophic, but individual node failures are isolated.
3. **Ring Topology** – Each node connects to exactly two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Used in older token-ring networks.
4. **Mesh Topology** – Each node connects to multiple other nodes. Highly fault-tolerant; the foundation of robust IoT deployments.
5. **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology** – A hybrid of star and bus, used in large corporate networks.
6. **Hybrid Topology** – Combines two or more topologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Insight:** **Mesh topology** is heavily favored in IoT protocols like **ZigBee, Thread, and BLE Mesh** because if one node fails, packets can be re-routed through alternative paths — critical for industrial and home automation deployments.

---

## 1.4 The OSI Reference Model

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model** is a 7-layer conceptual framework that standardizes network communication. Understanding it is essential for diagnosing IoT communication issues.

### The 7 Layers (Bottom-Up)

| Layer | Name | Function | IoT Example |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| 7 | Application | User interface, services | MQTT, HTTP, CoAP, SNMP |
| 6 | Presentation | Encryption, compression, encoding | TLS, SSL, JSON, XML |
| 5 | Session | Establishes, maintains, terminates sessions | SIP, RPC |
| 4 | Transport | Reliable end-to-end delivery, flow control | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | Network | Logical addressing, routing | IPv4, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL |
| 2 | Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | IEEE 802.15.4, Ethernet, Wi-Fi MAC |
| 1 | Physical | Bits over physical medium | Radio waves, copper, fiber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"** (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application — bottom to top).

### 1.4.1 PDUs at Each Layer
Each layer encapsulates data from the layer above. The unit of data has a specific name:
- **Layer 7-5:** Data
- **Layer 4:** Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP)
- **Layer 3:** Packet
- **Layer 2:** Frame
- **Layer 1:** Bits

---

## 1.5 The TCP/IP Protocol Suite

The **TCP/IP model** is the practical, 4-layer (or 5-layer) model that powers the modern internet. IoT protocols are predominantly built on top of this stack.

### 1.5.1 The 4 Layers
1. **Link / Network Access Layer** – Combines OSI Layers 1 & 2
2. **Internet Layer** – Corresponds to OSI Layer 3
3. **Transport Layer** – Corresponds to OSI Layer 4
4. **Application Layer** – Combines OSI Layers 5, 6, 7

### 1.5.2 TCP vs UDP – The Core Trade-Off

| Feature | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) |
|---------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery with ACK, retransmit | Best-effort, no guarantee |
| Ordering | Packets arrive in order | No ordering guarantee |
| Flow Control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (minimum) | **8 bytes** |
| Speed | Slower due to overhead | Faster, lower latency |
| IoT Suitability | Less suited for tiny, battery-powered nodes | **Preferred for low-power IoT** (MQTT, CoAP both run over UDP variants) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** For constrained IoT devices, UDP-based or even custom protocols are preferred because TCP's handshake and ACK overhead consumes precious battery and bandwidth. However, TCP is used when data integrity is non-negotiable (e.g., firmware updates).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bandwidth, Throughput, and Latency

These three metrics are the "trinity" of network performance and appear frequently in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.1.1 Bandwidth ($BW$)
The maximum theoretical data transfer rate of a link, measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- Common units: kbps, Mbps, Gbps.

### 2.1.2 Throughput ($T$)
The **actual** successful data transfer rate, which is always less than bandwidth due to protocol overhead, collisions, and errors.

$$T \leq BW$$

### 2.1.2 Latency ($L$)
The total time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination. Composed of four components:

$$L_{total} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$$

Where:
- $L_{prop}$ = Propagation delay
- $L_{trans}$ = Transmission delay
- $L_{queue}$ = Queuing delay (at routers/switches)
- $L_{proc}$ = Processing delay (at nodes)

### 2.1.3 Propagation Delay
The time for a signal to physically travel across the medium.

$$L_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$$

Where:
- $d$ = distance of the link (meters)
- $v$ = propagation speed (≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** in copper, **3 × 10⁸ m/s** in vacuum, ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** in fiber)

### 2.1.4 Transmission Delay
The time to push all the packet's bits onto the link.

$$L_{trans} = \frac{L_{packet}}{R}$$

Where:
- $L_{packet}$ = packet length in **bits**
- $R$ = link bandwidth in **bits/second**

### 2.1.5 Round-Trip Time (RTT)
The time for a packet to go from source to destination **and** an acknowledgement to return. Critical for TCP performance.

$$RTT = 2 \cdot L_{prop} + 2 \cdot L_{trans} + L_{proc,total}$$

### 2.1.6 Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)
The amount of data that can be "in flight" on a link at any moment. Important for sizing TCP windows and buffers.

$$BDP = BW \cdot L_{prop}$$

Units: **bits**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In 5G IoT networks, BDP is critical because high bandwidth combined with low latency allows massive sensor data streams. In satellite IoT, BDP is very large due to high $L_{prop}$, requiring large buffers.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| $L_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay | seconds |
| $L_{trans} = \dfrac{L_{packet}}{R}$ | Transmission delay | seconds |
| $L_{total} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$ | End-to-end latency | seconds |
| $RTT = 2 \cdot L_{prop} + 2 \cdot L_{trans} + L_{proc}$ | Round-trip time | seconds |
| $BDP = BW \cdot L_{prop}$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product | bits |
| $\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{useful} + T_{overhead}}$ | Protocol efficiency | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $T_{utilization} = \dfrac{L_{prop}}{L_{prop} + L_{trans}}$ (for stop-and-wait) | Link utilization | dimensionless |
| $P_{error-free} = (1 - BER)^N$ | Probability packet of N bits is error-free | dimensionless |

Where **BER** = Bit Error Rate, and **N** = number of bits in a packet.

---

## 2.3 Wireless Communication Fundamentals for IoT

IoT is dominated by wireless communication. The critical concept is the trade-off between **range, data rate, and power consumption**.

### 2.3.1 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)
The loss of signal strength as it propagates through free space.

$$FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4 \pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$

In decibels (dB):

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

Where:
- $d$ = distance (meters)
- $f$ = frequency (Hertz)
- $c$ = speed of light ≈ **3 × 10⁸ m/s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Doubling the distance causes a **6 dB** loss (signal becomes **4× weaker**). Doubling the frequency also causes a **6 dB** loss. This is why higher-frequency signals (5G, mmWave) have shorter range.

### 2.3.2 The IoT Wireless Trade-Off Triangle
1. **Range** ↔ **Power**: Longer range requires more transmission power.
2. **Data Rate** ↔ **Range**: Higher data rates typically require more bandwidth → higher frequency → shorter range.
3. **Power** ↔ **Battery Life**: Lower duty cycling extends battery life.

Different IoT protocols optimize for different corners of this triangle:
- **BLE/ZigBee**: Short range, low power, moderate data rate
- **Wi-Fi**: Short range, high power, high data rate
- **LoRaWAN**: Long range, very low power, very low data rate
- **Cellular (NB-IoT, LTE-M)**: Long range, moderate power, moderate data rate

---

## 2.4 IoT Communication Architecture

The IoT communication stack is layered, but with critical adaptations for constrained devices.

### 2.4.1 The IoT Protocol Stack vs OSI

| OSI Layer | Standard Internet | IoT Variant |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Application | HTTP, FTP, SMTP | **MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, DDS, XMPP** |
| Transport | TCP, UDP | UDP (CoAP), TCP (MQTT) |
| Network | IPv4 | **IPv6 / 6LoWPAN** |
| Data Link | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | **IEEE 802.15.4, BLE MAC, LoRa MAC** |
| Physical | Copper/Fiber/RF | RF bands (sub-GHz, 2.4 GHz) |

### 2.4.2 Why IoT Cannot Just Use "Normal Internet"
- Standard IPv4 addresses (32-bit) cannot address trillions of IoT devices.
- Full TCP/IP stack is too heavy for 8-bit microcontrollers with kilobytes of RAM.
- HTTP headers are verbose; a 1-byte sensor reading can be wrapped in a 200-byte HTTP packet.
- 6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN) was designed to solve this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** A typical IEEE 802.15.4 frame carries a maximum of **127 bytes**, of which only **~80 bytes** are usable payload after MAC/security headers. This is why protocols like 6LoWPAN aggressively compress IPv6 headers.

---

## 2.5 Key IoT Communication Protocols

### 2.5.1 MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)
- **Architecture:** Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub) with a central **broker**.
- **Transport:** TCP (port 1883, TLS on 8883).
- **Topics:** Hierarchical strings (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`).
- **QoS Levels:**
  - **QoS 0** – At most once (fire and forget)
  - **QoS 1** – At least once (with ACK)
  - **QoS 2** – Exactly once (4-step handshake)
- **Use Case:** Telemetry, low-bandwidth, unreliable networks.

### 2.5.2 CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)
- **Architecture:** Request/Response (REST-like), but with **observe** pattern for Pub/Sub.
- **Transport:** UDP (port 5683, DTLS on 5684).
- **Header:** Only **4 bytes** (vs HTTP's hundreds).
- **Use Case:** Truly constrained devices, machine-to-machine (M2M).

### 2.5.3 HTTP/HTTPS
- Standard web protocol, but **too heavy** for many IoT scenarios.
- Used where existing web infrastructure is reused (e.g., REST APIs from cloud platforms).

### 2.5.4 AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)
- Enterprise-grade message broker protocol.
- More feature-rich than MQTT, but heavier.

### 2.5.5 DDS (Data Distribution Service)
- Real-time, peer-to-peer, no broker.
- Used in autonomous vehicles, military, industrial IoT.

### 2.5.6 Comparison Table

| Protocol | Transport | Pattern | Header Size | Best For |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------------|----------|
| **MQTT** | TCP | Pub/Sub | 2-byte fixed | Telemetry, mobile |
| **CoAP** | UDP | Req/Resp, Observe | 4 bytes | Constrained sensors |
| **HTTP** | TCP | Req/Resp | Hundreds of bytes | Web integration |
| **AMQP** | TCP | Pub/Sub + Queues | Variable | Enterprise |
| **DDS** | TCP/UDP | Pub/Sub (P2P) | Variable | Real-time control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Worthy Distinction:** MQTT uses a **broker-based Pub/Sub** model (central server mediates). DDS uses a **broker-less Pub/Sub** model (peers discover each other dynamically). CoAP is **REST-based**, not Pub/Sub, but supports a server-push via the "Observe" extension.

---

## 2.6 Addressing in IoT: Why IPv6?

The explosion of IoT devices made **IPv4 (4.3 billion addresses)** insufficient. **IPv6 (3.4 × 10³⁸ addresses)** provides:
- **128-bit addresses** → ~670 quintillion addresses per square meter of Earth.
- **Stateless auto-configuration (SLAAC)** → devices can configure their own addresses without a DHCP server (critical for unattended IoT).
- **Better multicast support** → efficient one-to-many updates.
- **IPsec built-in** → security at network layer.

However, IPv6 headers are **40 bytes**, which is too large for IEEE 802.15.4's 127-byte frame. **6LoWPAN** adapts IPv6 by:
- Header compression (compresses 40-byte header down to a few bytes).
- Fragmentation at the adaptation layer.
- Mesh-under/route-over forwarding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** 6LoWPAN is the linchpin that makes IPv6 truly viable for IoT — it bridges the gap between the full Internet protocol suite and tiny, low-power radios.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Delay in a Packet-Switched Network

This is a classic KTU-style derivation. Let us derive the total time taken for a packet of size $L$ bits to traverse a network with $N$ links, each of bandwidth $R_i$ and physical length $d_i$.

### Step 1: Define Transmission Delay per Link
For the $i$-th link with bandwidth $R_i$ bits/second, the time to push all $L$ bits onto the link is:

$$L_{trans,i} = \frac{L}{R_i}$$

This is because $L$ bits are pushed at a rate of $R_i$ bits/second.

### Step 2: Define Propagation Delay per Link
For the $i$-th link with physical length $d_i$ meters and propagation speed $v$ m/s:

$$L_{prop,i} = \frac{d_i}{v}$$

### Step 3: Sum All Delays
Assuming negligible queuing and processing delays for the ideal case, the **store-and-forward** delay of the packet across all $N$ links is:

$$L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(L_{trans,i} + L_{prop,i}\right)$$

$$L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L}{R_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{d_i}{v}$$

### Step 4: Simplify for Uniform Links
If all $N$ links have the same bandwidth $R$ and the total path length is $D = \sum d_i$:

$$L_{total} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + \frac{D}{v}$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example
A packet of size **L = 1500 bytes** traverses **3 links**, each of bandwidth **R = 1 Mbps**, and each of length **d = 2000 km**. Propagation speed in fiber is **v = 2 × 10⁸ m/s**.

**Convert units:**
- $L = 1500 \times 8 = 12000$ bits
- $R = 10^6$ bps
- $d = 2000 \text{ km} = 2 \times 10^6$ m per link

**Compute transmission delay per link:**

$$L_{trans} = \frac{12000}{10^6} = 0.012 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}$$

**Compute propagation delay per link:**

$$L_{prop} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Total delay for 3 links (store-and-forward):**

$$L_{total} = 3 \cdot (12 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms}) = 66 \text{ ms}$$

**Note:** A "cut-through" switch could reduce this, but classical store-and-forward requires each link to fully receive the packet before forwarding.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stop-and-Wait Protocol Utilization

The Stop-and-Wait protocol sends one frame, waits for an ACK, then sends the next. This is a common KTU question.

### Step 1: Time to Send One Frame
The transmission time for one frame of $L$ bits over bandwidth $R$:

$$t_{frame} = \frac{L}{R}$$

### Step 2: Time to Receive ACK
The ACK is tiny, but it still experiences propagation. The total round-trip time is $RTT = 2 L_{prop} + 2 L_{trans} + L_{proc}$. For simplicity, often:

$$t_{total} = t_{frame} + RTT$$

### Step 3: Utilization
The fraction of time the link is actually carrying useful data:

$$U = \frac{t_{frame}}{t_{frame} + RTT}$$

$$U = \frac{\frac{L}{R}}{\frac{L}{R} + RTT}$$

$$U = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{R \cdot RTT}{L}}$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
- If $R \cdot RTT \gg L$ (high-bandwidth, long-latency link, e.g., satellite), $U$ is very small — Stop-and-Wait is **highly inefficient**.
- If $R \cdot RTT \ll L$, $U$ approaches 1 — Stop-and-Wait is efficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the precise reason TCP uses a sliding window** with a window size proportional to the BDP. For a BDP of 100,000 bits, a window size of ~100,000 bits allows ~100 frames in flight, achieving near-100% utilization.

### Step 5: Numerical Example
Channel bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps, frame size $L = 1000$ bits, RTT = 100 ms.

$$U = \frac{1000 / 10^6}{1000 / 10^6 + 0.1} = \frac{0.001}{0.001 + 0.1} = \frac{0.001}{0.101} \approx 0.0099$$

Utilization is only **0.99%** — a clear demonstration of why window-based protocols are essential.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product and TCP Window Sizing

### Step 1: Start with BDP
A link of bandwidth $BW$ and one-way propagation delay $L_{prop}$ has:

$$BDP = BW \cdot L_{prop} \text{ bits}$$

This is the maximum number of bits that can be in transit on the link at any instant.

### Step 2: Why This Matters for TCP
TCP must keep the pipe full. If the TCP window is smaller than BDP, the sender must wait for ACKs, leaving the link idle.

### Step 3: Optimal Window Size
For maximum throughput, the TCP window $W$ should be at least:

$$W_{optimal} = BDP = BW \cdot L_{prop}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example
A transcontinental link: $BW = 100$ Mbps, $L_{prop} = 30$ ms (one-way).

$$BDP = 100 \times 10^6 \times 0.03 = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 375 \text{ KB}$$

So a TCP window of at least **375 KB** is needed to saturate this link. This is why early TCP's 64 KB maximum window size was a bottleneck on high-bandwidth, long-latency paths — solved by **TCP Window Scaling (RFC 7323)**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: IoT Protocol Selection

Use the following decision matrix to select an appropriate IoT protocol based on system requirements.

| Requirement | Recommended Protocol(s) | Justification |
|-------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Battery-powered sensor, sub-1 Hz updates | **MQTT (QoS 0) over TCP** or **CoAP** | Low overhead, small payloads |
| Real-time control loop (e.g., robotics) | **DDS** | Low latency, deterministic |
| Need guaranteed exactly-once delivery | **MQTT QoS 2** or **AMQP** | Multi-step handshake ensures uniqueness |
| Direct device-to-device (no internet) | **BLE Mesh, ZigBee, Thread** | Self-contained mesh networks |
| Long-range, low-power (km-scale) | **LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Sigfox** | Sub-GHz, chirp spread spectrum |
| Existing REST/web integration | **HTTP/REST, CoAP** | Easy integration with cloud APIs |
| Multicast firmware update to many devices | **CoAP (with multicast), MQTT (with broker)** | Efficient one-to-many delivery |
| High-security (medical, financial) | **MQTT over TLS, CoAP over DTLS, AMQP** | Encryption and authentication |
| Peer-to-peer, no central server | **DDS** | Broker-less Pub/Sub |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Takeaway:** There is no single "best" IoT protocol. The choice is a function of bandwidth, power, latency, security, and existing infrastructure. The KTU exam often tests this trade-off understanding.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) in dB

Starting from the Friis transmission equation in free space:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $P_r$ = received power
- $P_t$ = transmitted power
- $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains (assumed 1 for isotropic)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength
- $d$ = distance

### Step 1: Path Loss Definition
Path loss is the ratio of transmitted to received power (assuming unity gains):

$$PL = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

### Step 2: Convert to dB
$$PL_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

### Step 3: Use Frequency Instead of Wavelength
Since $\lambda = c / f$:

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

Expanding:

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}(4\pi) - 20 \log_{10}(c)$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example
A Wi-Fi AP at 2.4 GHz transmits to a client 50 m away. Compute FSPL.

- $f = 2.4 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $d = 50$ m
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(50) + 20 \log_{10}(2.4 \times 10^9) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right)$$

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \cdot 1.699 + 20 \cdot 9.380 + 20 \log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8})$$

$$PL_{dB} = 33.98 + 187.6 + (-147.56)$$

$$PL_{dB} \approx 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

So about **74 dB of path loss** — a typical value for indoor Wi-Fi at 50 m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Doubling the distance or frequency adds 6 dB of loss. A 20 dB loss means a 100× reduction in received power. This is why RF designers obsess over path loss budgets.

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram: TCP 3-Way Handshake (For Context)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Server
    Client->>Server: SYN (seq=x)
    Server->>Client: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
    Client->>Server: ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)
    Note over Client,Server: Connection Established
```

**Note:** This is why TCP is "heavy" for IoT — even before sending a single byte of sensor data, **3 packets** must be exchanged. UDP skips this entirely, which is why CoAP uses UDP.
