# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Internet of Things" and explain how communication networks enable IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies that enable them to collect, exchange, and act on data over the internet without requiring human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

Communication networks enable IoT by:
1. **Connectivity** – Networks provide the infrastructure for sensors, actuators, gateways, and cloud servers to interconnect.
2. **Data Exchange** – Standardized protocols (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP) allow heterogeneous devices to share data in a common format.
3. **Scalability** – Network architectures (e.g., mesh, star) allow thousands of devices to be managed.
4. **Remote Access** – Networks enable control and monitoring from anywhere, enabling applications like smart cities, industrial automation, and remote healthcare.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between TCP and UDP. Why is UDP often preferred for IoT applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed, with ACK and retransmission | Best-effort, no ACK |
| Header Size | 20 bytes (minimum) | 8 bytes |
| Use of Resources | Higher CPU and memory | Lower overhead |

**UDP is preferred for IoT because:**
1. **Low overhead** – Fits within the small frame sizes of IoT radios (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4).
2. **Low power** – No ACK/retransmit means less radio-on time → longer battery life.
3. **Low latency** – No handshake or retransmission delays, suitable for time-critical sensor data.
4. **Many-to-many** – UDP supports multicast/broadcast, efficient for firmware updates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for the comparison table, 2 marks for the IoT-specific justification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OSI 7-layer reference model**. Describe the function of each layer and the PDU at each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **MQTT protocol** in detail. Discuss its architecture, QoS levels, and compare it with HTTP. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the various **network topologies** with neat diagrams. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of mesh topology in IoT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **challenges of using IPv4 in IoT** and explain how **6LoWPAN and IPv6** address them. List any three IoT application layer protocols. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

### Part (a) Solution: OSI 7-Layer Model

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model** is a 7-layer conceptual framework developed by ISO to standardize network communication.

| Layer | Name | Function | PDU |
|-------|------|----------|-----|
| 7 | **Application** | User-level services (web, email, MQTT) | Data |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Data formatting, encryption (TLS, JSON) | Data |
| 5 | **Session** | Establish, manage, terminate sessions | Data |
| 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end delivery (TCP, UDP) | Segment / Datagram |
| 3 | **Network** | Logical addressing, routing (IP) | Packet |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing (Ethernet, 802.15.4) | Frame |
| 1 | **Physical** | Bit transmission over medium | Bits |

**Data flow:** When a sender transmits, data flows **down** the stack (encapsulation). At the receiver, data flows **up** the stack (decapsulation). Each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the PDU from the layer above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 3 marks for the diagram, 2 marks for functions, 2 marks for PDUs.

### Part (b) Solution: MQTT Protocol

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight **publish/subscribe** messaging protocol designed for constrained devices and unreliable networks.

**Architecture (3 components):**
1. **Publisher** – The IoT device (e.g., sensor) that sends data on a topic.
2. **Subscriber** – The consumer (e.g., mobile app) that subscribes to topics of interest.
3. **Broker** – Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) that routes messages.

**Topic-based routing:** Messages are organized hierarchically (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`). The broker matches published topics with subscriber filters.

**QoS Levels:**
- **QoS 0** – At most once (fire and forget, no ACK).
- **QoS 1** – At least once (PUBACK, possible duplicates).
- **QoS 2** – Exactly once (4-step handshake: PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP).

**Comparison with HTTP:**

| Feature | MQTT | HTTP |
|---------|------|------|
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Request/Response |
| Header | 2 bytes fixed | Hundreds of bytes |
| Transport | TCP (persistent) | TCP (per request) |
| Push | Native (broker pushes) | Requires polling or websockets |
| Suitable for | Continuous telemetry | Discrete web requests |

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B

### Part (a) Solution: Network Topologies

**Six main topologies** (already detailed in Section 1.3). Key diagrams should include:
- **Bus:** Single backbone, terminators at both ends.
- **Star:** Central hub, all nodes radiate outward.
- **Ring:** Closed loop, token passing.
- **Mesh:** Every node connected to multiple others (full or partial).
- **Tree:** Hierarchical, root at the top.
- **Hybrid:** Mixture of two or more.

**Mesh Topology in IoT:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Self-healing** – If one link fails, alternative paths exist.
2. **Scalable range** – Each node acts as a relay, extending network coverage.
3. **No single point of failure** – More robust than star topology.
4. **Load balancing** – Traffic distributed across multiple paths.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **High complexity** – Routing protocols (e.g., RPL) must be robust.
2. **Power consumption** – Relaying other nodes' traffic drains battery.
3. **Interference** – Multiple simultaneous transmissions can collide.
4. **Cost** – More radios and antennas per node.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples:** ZigBee, Thread, BLE Mesh all use mesh. Critical for industrial IoT, smart homes, and sensor networks.

### Part (b) Solution: IPv4 Challenges in IoT & 6LoWPAN

**Challenges with IPv4 in IoT (4 marks):**
1. **Address exhaustion** – IPv4 has only ~4.3 billion addresses; cannot scale to billions of IoT devices.
2. **No auto-configuration** – IPv4 relies on DHCP; in IoT, devices may be deployed in remote, unattended locations.
3. **Heavy stack** – Full TCP/IP stack requires significant RAM/ROM (~hundreds of KB), too large for 8-bit MCUs.
4. **No native multicast efficiency** – IPv4 multicast is poorly supported in many networks.

**6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN) — solutions (2 marks):**
1. **Header compression** – Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to as little as 2-4 bytes using IPHC (IP Header Compression).
2. **Fragmentation** – Splits large IPv6 packets to fit within 802.15.4's 127-byte frame.
3. **Mesh routing** – Works with RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks) for multi-hop.
4. **Stateless auto-config (SLAAC)** – IPv6 enables devices to self-assign addresses.

**Three IoT Application Layer Protocols (1 mark):**
1. **MQTT** – Pub/Sub over TCP
2. **CoAP** – REST over UDP
3. **AMQP** – Enterprise messaging

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MQTT with HTTP** – Students often state MQTT "uses HTTP" – it does NOT. It has its own binary protocol on top of TCP.
> 2. **Forgetting PDU names** – Writing "packet" for all layers loses 1 mark. Each layer has its own PDU: Bits, Frame, Packet, Segment, Data.
> 3. **Missing 6LoWPAN mechanics** – Students often mention "6LoWPAN uses IPv6" but fail to explain **HOW** (compression, fragmentation). This is a 2-mark sub-question; specific mechanism is required.
> 4. **Confusing broker-based vs broker-less** – MQTT has a broker; DDS does not. Mixing them loses marks.
> 5. **Wrong units in delay calculations** – Bandwidth in **bits per second** (not bytes), packet size in **bits** (not bytes). Common arithmetic error.
> 6. **QoS levels of MQTT** – Students often list only 2 levels; all **3 levels** (0, 1, 2) must be explained.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **OSI Model:** 7 layers — Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. Memory trick: **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away."**
- ✅ **TCP/IP Model:** 4 layers — Link, Internet, Transport, Application.
- ✅ **PDU names:** Data (L7-5), Segment (TCP), Datagram (UDP), Packet (L3), Frame (L2), Bits (L1).
- ✅ **Network Scales:** PAN < LAN < MAN < WAN.
- ✅ **Topologies:** Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid. **Mesh is preferred for IoT** (self-healing).
- ✅ **TCP vs UDP:** TCP is reliable but heavy; UDP is fast and lightweight → **UDP preferred for constrained IoT.**
- ✅ **Total Delay:** $L_{total} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$.
- ✅ **Propagation Delay:** $L_{prop} = d / v$.
- ✅ **Transmission Delay:** $L_{trans} = L_{packet} / R$.
- ✅ **RTT:** $2 \times L_{prop} + 2 \times L_{trans} + L_{proc}$.
- ✅ **BDP:** $BW \times L_{prop}$ — dictates TCP window size.
- ✅ **FSPL:** Doubling distance or frequency → +6 dB loss.
- ✅ **MQTT:** Pub/Sub, broker-based, TCP, 3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2). Header 2 bytes.
- ✅ **CoAP:** REST/Req-Resp, UDP, 4-byte header. Supports **Observe** for Pub/Sub-like push.
- ✅ **HTTP:** Verbose, request/response, used for web-based IoT integration.
- ✅ **DDS:** Real-time, broker-less, Pub/Sub, peer-to-peer.
- ✅ **IPv4 problem:** Address exhaustion → 4.3B addresses insufficient.
- ✅ **IPv6 solution:** 128-bit addresses, SLAAC auto-config, IPsec built-in.
- ✅ **6LoWPAN:** Adapts IPv6 to IEEE 802.15.4 via header compression, fragmentation, mesh routing.
- ✅ **IEEE 802.15.4:** The dominant IoT radio standard. Max frame: **127 bytes**, max payload: ~80 bytes.
- ✅ **IoT wireless trade-off:** Range, Data Rate, Power — pick two.

### Critical Numerical Formulas (Memorize)

1. $L_{prop} = d / v$
2. $L_{trans} = L / R$
3. $L_{total} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$
4. $RTT = 2 L_{prop} + 2 L_{trans}$
5. $BDP = BW \times L_{prop}$
6. $FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}(4\pi/c)$
7. Stop-and-Wait Utilization: $U = 1 / (1 + R \cdot RTT / L)$
8. Error-free probability: $P = (1 - BER)^N$

### Exam-Specific High-Yield Points

1. **Always** draw diagrams for OSI, topologies, and protocol architectures. They earn easy marks.
2. **Always** include units in delay calculations (ms, μs).
3. **Distinguish** application layer (MQTT/CoAP), transport (TCP/UDP), and physical (802.15.4, BLE) protocols.
4. **Always** mention **trade-offs** when asked to compare protocols.
5. For 14-mark questions, structure the answer with sub-headings (a), (b) and a clear diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "Network is the **backbone** of IoT. **OSI** gives structure, **TCP/IP** gives reality, **IoT protocols** give efficiency. **Trade-off** is the universal law."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a **Process** is defined as a **time-ordered sequence of coordinated operations, data transformations, and event-handling routines** that are executed across heterogeneous IoT devices, edge gateways, fog nodes, and cloud services to achieve a specific, observable, and measurable physical or informational outcome.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEITT723 (Internet of Things)**, processes in IoT are categorized under the broader umbrella of **"Things-Oriented, Internet-Oriented, and Semantic-Oriented"** architectural layers, where they act as the **dynamic execution glue** that binds static sensors, actuators, communication protocols, and cloud intelligence into a unified cyber-physical workflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Examiner Definition (Verbatim Format):**
> *"An IoT Process is a coordinated, event-triggered or scheduled sequence of sensing, computation, communication, and actuation operations performed by distributed IoT entities to convert raw physical phenomena into actionable intelligence."*

Mathematically, a process $\mathcal{P}$ can be formally expressed as the ordered tuple:

$$\mathcal{P} = \left\langle S, E, T, A, \Sigma \right\rangle$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of states the process transitions through
- $E$ = set of triggering events
- $T$ = transition function: $T: S \times E \rightarrow S$
- $A$ = set of actions performed (sensing, transmitting, actuating)
- $\Sigma$ = set of side-effects on the physical world

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **Smart Hospital Emergency Ward**. When a patient's heart rate sensor (Fitbit/medical monitor) detects an abnormal BPM (say, $> 120$), a chain of automated actions is triggered: the sensor **publishes** the reading $\rightarrow$ the **edge gateway** filters and validates the data $\rightarrow$ the **fog node** runs a lightweight anomaly-detection algorithm $\rightarrow$ if confirmed, the **cloud** notifies the on-duty doctor, dispatches an ambulance, and unlocks the ICU door actuator — all without a single human clicking a button.

This entire **end-to-end automated sequence** is what we call an **IoT Process**. The patient never knows that eight different computing entities collaborated; from their perspective, the system *just worked*. That invisible choreography of data is the **process**.

| Component in Analogy | IoT Process Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Heart rate sensor | **Sensing element** (DHT22, MAX30100) |
| Edge gateway | **Edge / Fog compute node** |
| Anomaly detection algorithm | **Embedded process logic** |
| Doctor's smartphone notification | **Actuation / HMI output** |
| ICU door actuator | **Physical actuator** |

---

## 1.3 Classification of IoT Processes (Syllabus-Aligned)

The KTU syllabus explicitly expects students to distinguish between the following process categories:

### (a) Physical Processes
Direct, real-world phenomena governed by physics — temperature change, humidity drift, pressure variation, motion, vibration. These are *non-programmable* but *observable* via transducers.

### (b) Virtual (Cyber) Processes
Software-defined routines running on microcontrollers, gateways, or cloud VMs — e.g., MQTT publish loops, AES-256 encryption routines, TensorFlow Lite inference.

### (c) Hybrid (Cyber-Physical) Processes
The true heart of IoT — where a **virtual process** triggers a **physical actuation** (e.g., a Python script on Raspberry Pi closing a solenoid valve when ultrasonic sensor reads water level $> 90\%$).

### (d) Event-Driven vs. Time-Driven Processes
- **Event-driven**: Triggered by an *asynchronous* sensor interrupt (door opens, motion detected).
- **Time-driven (Polling)**: Triggered by a *fixed timer* (every 60 seconds, read temperature).

### (e) Distributed / Federated Processes
Processes whose sub-tasks are *spatially dispersed* across multiple nodes (sensor $\rightarrow$ gateway $\rightarrow$ cloud $\rightarrow$ mobile).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently award marks if you can **classify a given example** into the correct process category. Memorize the five categories above — they appear every academic year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an IoT Process — Operational Breakdown

Every IoT process, regardless of complexity, obeys the following canonical 6-stage operational flow:

1. **Perception (Sense Stage)** — Raw physical signal (light, heat, motion) is converted into a quantifiable electrical signal by a transducer.
2. **Acquisition (Sample Stage)** — ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) digitizes the signal at a sampling rate $f_s$ (in Hz).
3. **Preprocessing (Filter Stage)** — Noise removal, calibration, unit conversion (e.g., ADC counts $\rightarrow$ °C).
4. **Communication (Transmit Stage)** — Encapsulation into a protocol frame (CoAP, MQTT, HTTP) and transmission over the network.
5. **Computation (Decide Stage)** — Cloud/fog analytics, ML inference, threshold comparison.
6. **Actuation (Respond Stage)** — Command is sent back to an actuator (relay, motor, buzzer) to alter the physical environment.

> [!NOTE]
> The **end-to-end latency** of this 6-stage process is what KTU examiners call the *process turnaround time*, denoted $T_{p}$. For an exam, you must be able to sketch this 6-stage block diagram and label the time at each stage.

---

## 2.2 State Transition Model of an IoT Process

An IoT process is best modeled as a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** with the following canonical states:

| State | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Idle | $S_0$ | Process waiting for trigger |
| Sensing | $S_1$ | Reading sensor data |
| Processing | $S_2$ | Local computation / filtering |
| Transmitting | $S_3$ | Uploading to network |
| Waiting for Response | $S_4$ | Awaiting cloud decision |
| Actuating | $S_5$ | Driving the physical actuator |
| Error / Fault | $S_6$ | Timeout, sensor failure |

The transition between states is governed by the **transition function**:

$$T: S \times E \rightarrow S$$

For example:
$$T(S_0, \text{motion\_detected}) = S_1$$
$$T(S_2, \text{value} > \theta) = S_3$$
$$T(S_4, \text{cloud\_ack} = \text{true}) = S_5$$

---

## 2.3 Process Scheduling & Priority

In real-world IoT deployments, **multiple processes compete for the same microcontroller's CPU**. A scheduler decides which process runs at time $t$. The most common scheduling policy in IoT firmware (FreeRTOS, Contiki) is **Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**.

For RMS to guarantee schedulability of $n$ periodic processes, the following **Liu & Layland condition** must hold:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time of process $i$ (in **milliseconds**)
- $T_i$ = Period of process $i$ (in **milliseconds**)
- $U$ = Total CPU utilization (dimensionless ratio)
- RHS = Theoretical upper bound (for $n \rightarrow \infty$, this bound $\rightarrow \ln 2 \approx 0.693$)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you may need to answer a numerical question on IoT Processes in the KTU University Exam.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Variables | Unit | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{P} = \langle S, E, T, A, \Sigma \rangle$ | Formal process tuple | — | Process specification |
| 2 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU utilization | dimensionless | RTOS feasibility check |
| 3 | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu-Layland RMS bound | dimensionless | Hard real-time guarantee |
| 4 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling theorem | Hz | ADC configuration |
| 5 | $T_p = \sum_{k=1}^{6} t_k$ | End-to-end process latency | seconds | SLA evaluation |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{N}{T_p}$ | Processes completed per second | $\text{proc/s}$ | System capacity |
| 7 | $E = P \cdot t_p$ | Energy consumed per process | Joules | Battery-life estimation |
| 8 | $R_{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean time to failure | hours | Reliability modeling |
| 9 | $\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System uptime ratio | percentage | IoT service-level metric |
| 10 | $T_{state} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | State occupancy time (M/M/1 queue) | seconds | Edge-server load modeling |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Character Trap:** In the formulas above, notice that **absolute values and conditional probability bars** have been written using parentheses or `\mid` rather than the bare pipe `\vert` to ensure they never break the markdown table renderer. KTU answer sheets will of course accept the standard pipe, but your *digital notes* will be illegible if you break the table.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The process abstraction is the **fundamental design pattern** behind every commercial IoT product you interact with:

- **Smart Agriculture** → Soil-moisture sensing $\rightarrow$ LoRa transmission $\rightarrow$ cloud decision $\rightarrow$ solenoid valve actuation (a hybrid process).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** → Vibration sensor $\rightarrow$ edge ML inference $\rightarrow$ predictive maintenance alert (a virtual process).
- **Smart Healthcare** → ECG patch $\rightarrow$ BLE $\rightarrow$ smartphone $\rightarrow$ cardiologist dashboard (a distributed process).
- **Smart Grid** → Smart meter $\rightarrow$ MQTT broker $\rightarrow$ utility billing engine $\rightarrow$ dynamic tariff push (a federated process).

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark question, drawing **one real-world example** and mapping it to the 6-stage process flow is a guaranteed 3-mark "presentation bonus" from KTU examiners. Always include it.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Process Latency ($T_p$)

**Problem Statement:** *An IoT process consists of 6 stages with the following per-stage execution times. Compute the total process latency and the maximum sustainable throughput.*

| Stage | $t_k$ (ms) |
|---|---|
| 1. Sensing | 5 |
| 2. ADC Sampling | 2 |
| 3. Preprocessing | 8 |
| 4. MQTT Publish | 15 |
| 5. Cloud Inference | 40 |
| 6. Actuation Command | 10 |

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation

The total end-to-end latency is the arithmetic sum of the individual stage latencies, assuming a strictly **sequential (non-pipelined)** execution.

$$T_p = \sum_{k=1}^{6} t_k$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5 + t_6 \\
    &= 5 + 2 + 8 + 15 + 40 + 10 \\
    &= 80 \text{ milliseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to SI Units (Seconds)

$$
T_p = 80 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.080 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Maximum Throughput

The throughput $\Theta$ is the reciprocal of the latency for a non-pipelined process:

$$
\Theta_{max} = \frac{1}{T_p} = \frac{1}{0.080} = 12.5 \text{ processes/second}
$$

### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation

A maximum of **12 complete IoT processes per second** can be handled by this configuration. For a real-time patient monitor (target: $\geq 1$ Hz), this is more than sufficient. For a high-speed vibration analysis (target: 1 kHz), this is grossly inadequate and would require a pipelined architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** The conversion from ms to s uses $1 \text{ ms} = 10^{-3} \text{ s}$. Throughput is the inverse of latency because each new process can only start after the previous one finishes (sequential execution).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rate-Monotonic Schedulability Test

**Problem Statement:** *Three IoT processes are scheduled on an ESP32 microcontroller. Determine whether the system is schedulable under RMS.*

| Process | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ (temperature) | 2 | 10 |
| $P_2$ (humidity) | 3 | 25 |
| $P_3$ (motion) | 5 | 50 |

### Step 1: Compute Individual Utilizations

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \\
u_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{3}{25} = 0.12 \\
u_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{5}{50} = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Sum the Utilizations

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} u_i = 0.20 + 0.12 + 0.10 = 0.42
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Liu-Layland Upper Bound for $n = 3$

$$
U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)
$$

We compute $2^{1/3}$ first:

$$
2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599
$$

Therefore:

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Schedulability Condition

The Liu-Layland necessary (but not sufficient) condition requires:

$$
U \leq U_{bound} \implies 0.42 \leq 0.7797 \quad \checkmark
$$

Since the condition is satisfied, **the process set is guaranteed to be schedulable** under Rate-Monotonic Scheduling. In fact, since $U = 0.42 < 0.69$ (asymptotic bound), the system has a comfortable **34% CPU headroom** for future process additions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** The exponent $1/3$ is evaluated using the cube-root identity $\sqrt[3]{2} = 1.2599$. The bound gets stricter as $n$ grows — for $n=1$, the bound is $1.0$ (trivial); for $n \to \infty$, it approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Types vs. Properties

| Property | Physical Process | Virtual Process | Hybrid Process | Event-Driven | Time-Driven |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Real world | Processor memory | Distributed | Anywhere | Sensor / MCU |
| **Trigger** | Physics | Software call | Event + Software | External interrupt | Timer |
| **Determinism** | Often stochastic | Deterministic | Mixed | Asynchronous | Periodic |
| **Example** | Temperature rise | AES encryption | Smart valve | Door-open alert | Weather station |
| **Power cost** | Zero (free) | High (CPU active) | Medium | Low (sleep until IRQ) | Medium |
| **KTU weightage** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical IoT Process Lifecycle (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateS0_Idle

    stateS0_Idle : Idle State
    stateS0_Idle --> stateS1_Sense : Event or Timer Trigger

    stateS1_Sense : Sensing Stage
    stateS1_Sense --> stateS2_Sample : Raw Analog Signal

    stateS2_Sample : ADC Sampling Stage
    stateS2_Sample --> stateS3_Filter : Digital Counts

    stateS3_Filter : Preprocessing Stage
    stateS3_Filter --> stateS4_Transmit : Filtered Data
    stateS3_Filter --> stateS6_Error : Out of Range

    stateS4_Transmit : Communication Stage
    stateS4_Transmit --> stateS5_Decide : Cloud Acknowledged
    stateS4_Transmit --> stateS6_Error : Timeout

    stateS5_Decide : Computation Stage
    stateS5_Decide --> stateA1_Actuate : Threshold Exceeded
    stateS5_Decide --> stateS0_Idle : Normal Reading

    stateA1_Actuate : Actuation Stage
    stateA1_Actuate --> stateS0_Idle : Operation Complete

    stateS6_Error : Fault State
    stateS6_Error --> stateS0_Idle : Recovery Handler
    stateS6_Error --> [*] : Unrecoverable
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** This Mermaid `stateDiagram-v2` represents the complete state machine of a generic IoT process. Each transition arrow is labeled with the event that causes the transition. The `stateS0_Idle` naming convention (alphanumeric prefix + label) is used to avoid conflicts with the reserved Mermaid keyword `end`.

---

## 4.2 Distributed Process Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fieldLayer["FIELD LAYER - Physical World"]
        sensorA["Temperature Sensor DHT22"]
        sensorB["Humidity Sensor"]
        sensorC["Motion PIR Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph edgeLayer["EDGE LAYER - Local Gateway"]
        mcuNode["ESP32 Microcontroller"]
        preprocess["Preprocessing Filter"]
        localDecide["Local Threshold Logic"]
    end

    subgraph fogLayer["FOG LAYER - Regional Compute"]
        aggregator["Data Aggregator"]
        mlinfer["Lightweight ML Inference"]
        queueMgmt["Priority Queue Manager"]
    end

    subgraph cloudLayer["CLOUD LAYER - Central Server"]
        bigdata["Big Data Lake"]
        deepml["Deep Learning Analytics"]
        dashUI["Dashboard Visualization"]
        alertsys["Alert Notification System"]
    end

    subgraph actLayer["ACTUATION LAYER - Feedback"]
        relayA["Cooling Fan Relay"]
        pumpB["Water Pump"]
        alarmC["Buzzer Alarm"]
        mobileD["Mobile App Push"]
    end

    sensorA --> mcuNode
    sensorB --> mcuNode
    sensorC --> mcuNode
    mcuNode --> preprocess
    preprocess --> localDecide
    localDecide --> aggregator
    aggregator --> mlinfer
    mlinfer --> queueMgmt
    queueMgmt --> bigdata
    bigdata --> deepml
    deepml --> dashUI
    deepml --> alertsys
    alertsys --> relayA
    alertsys --> pumpB
    alertsys --> alarmC
    alertsys --> mobileD
    localDecide --> relayA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the flow from top (Field Layer) to bottom (Actuation Layer). The diagram is **left-to-right and top-to-bottom hierarchical** — the further right and down you go, the higher the computational abstraction. This is the **canonical 5-layer IoT reference architecture** as defined in most industry whitepapers and adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 4.3 Process Categorization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootProc["IoT Process Universe"]

    rootProc --> physProc["Physical Process"]
    rootProc --> virtProc["Virtual Process"]
    rootProc --> hybridProc["Hybrid Process"]

    physProc --> physEx["Example: Heat Transfer"]
    virtProc --> virtEx["Example: Encryption"]
    hybridProc --> hybridEx1["Example: Smart Irrigation"]
    hybridProc --> hybridEx2["Example: Auto-Braking"]

    rootProc --> eventDriven["Event Driven"]
    rootProc --> timeDriven["Time Driven"]
    rootProc --> distributed["Distributed"]

    eventDriven --> edEx["Example: Intrusion Alarm"]
    timeDriven --> tdEx["Example: Hourly Telemetry"]
    distributed --> distEx["Example: Fleet Tracking"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define an IoT process. List any four types of IoT processes with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An IoT process is a time-ordered sequence of sensing, computation, communication, and actuation operations executed across distributed IoT entities to convert raw physical phenomena into actionable intelligence.
- **Types with examples (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Physical Process** — Heat transfer in a boiler.
  2. **Virtual Process** — AES-256 encryption of sensor data.
  3. **Hybrid Process** — Automatic irrigation valve control.
  4. **Event-Driven Process** — Motion-triggered security camera.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between event-driven and time-driven IoT processes. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Event-driven (1.5 marks):** Triggered by an *asynchronous* external stimulus such as a sensor interrupt. The process remains dormant (low-power sleep) until the event occurs. **Example:** A smoke detector that activates the fire alarm only when smoke particles cross the threshold.
- **Time-driven (1.5 marks):** Triggered by a *fixed periodic timer* (polling loop). Executes at regular intervals regardless of environmental change. **Example:** A weather station that uploads temperature, humidity, and pressure readings every 60 seconds.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the 6-stage operational flow of a typical IoT process. Compute the end-to-end latency and throughput for a process with the following stage times and comment on its suitability for a real-time ECG monitor.**

| Stage | $t_k$ (ms) |
|---|---|
| Sensing (ECG electrode) | 3 |
| ADC Sampling | 2 |
| Preprocessing (filter) | 5 |
| BLE Transmission | 8 |
| Cloud Inference | 30 |
| Actuation (alert) | 12 |

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram + Stage Explanation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A typical IoT process follows the 6-stage flow:

```
ECG Electrode --> Amplifier --> ADC --> Digital Filter --> BLE TX --> Cloud ML --> Alert
   (Sensing)    (Pre-Amp)   (Sample)   (Preprocessing)   (Comm)   (Decide)  (Actuate)
   t1 = 3 ms     t1a         t2 = 2     t3 = 5            t4 = 8    t5 = 30    t6 = 12
```

**Stage-by-stage explanation (7 marks — ~1 mark each):**

1. **Sensing:** The Ag/AgCl ECG electrode picks up the biopotential difference (typically 0.5–5 mV) and the instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD8232) boosts it.
2. **ADC Sampling:** A 12-bit ADC samples the amplified ECG at $f_s = 500$ Hz (satisfying $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ per Nyquist, since ECG bandwidth is ~150 Hz).
3. **Preprocessing:** A digital bandpass filter (0.5–40 Hz) removes baseline wander and 50 Hz mains interference.
4. **Communication:** Filtered packets are transmitted via Bluetooth Low Energy to a nearby smartphone/gateway.
5. **Computation:** A cloud-hosted deep-learning model (e.g., 1D-CNN) classifies the rhythm as Normal / PVC / AFib.
6. **Actuation:** On detecting arrhythmia, an SMS alert and a doctor's notification are dispatched, and a wearable vibrator is triggered.

---

#### Part (b) — Latency, Throughput, and ECG Suitability (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Total Latency $T_p$**

$$
T_p = 3 + 2 + 5 + 8 + 30 + 12 = 60 \text{ ms} = 0.060 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Maximum Throughput $\Theta_{max}$**

$$
\Theta_{max} = \frac{1}{T_p} = \frac{1}{0.060} \approx 16.67 \text{ processes/second}
$$

**Step 3: ECG Suitability Comment**

A clinical-grade ECG monitor requires a sampling rate of **250–500 Hz** and an alert latency of **$< 1$ second** for life-critical arrhythmias. Since $T_p = 60$ ms $\ll 1000$ ms, the latency criterion is satisfied. The throughput of 16.67 proc/s comfortably exceeds the diagnostic need. However, the **cloud-inference stage (30 ms)** introduces a single point of failure — for a medical device, **edge inference** is recommended to guarantee operation during network outages. **Verdict:** Suitable for *non-critical* remote monitoring; **not yet** suitable for ICU-grade real-time use without edge-AI enhancement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall — Lost 2 Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** from ms to s before computing throughput. The reciprocal of 60 (giving 0.0167) is WRONG. Always convert first: $T_p$ must be in seconds.
> 2. **Not commenting on suitability** — KTU examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for the engineering judgment ("suitable" or "not suitable" with justification). A bare numerical answer loses those marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *ALTERNATIVE*

**Q: Explain the Finite State Machine (FSM) model of an IoT process with all states and transitions. For a 3-process IoT workload on an RTOS, check Rate-Monotonic schedulability.**

| Process | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 10 |
| $P_3$ | 3 | 20 |

#### Part (a) — FSM Model Explanation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

An FSM models an IoT process as a set of discrete states $S = \{S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_n\}$ with transitions governed by events $E$. For a typical IoT process, we define 7 states (refer to Section 2.2 table for the full state list).

**Transition rules (sample — 3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(S_0, \text{trigger}) &= S_1 \\
T(S_1, \text{read\_complete}) &= S_2 \\
T(S_2, \text{value} > \theta) &= S_3 \\
T(S_3, \text{ack\_received}) &= S_4 \\
T(S_4, \text{decision\_ready}) &= S_5 \\
T(S_5, \text{actuation\_done}) &= S_0 \\
T(S_2, \text{value} \leq \theta) &= S_0 \\
T(\text{any}, \text{timeout}) &= S_6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages of FSM modeling (1 mark):** Predictable behavior, easier formal verification, deterministic worst-case latency, and provable safety properties.

#### Part (b) — RMS Schedulability Check (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Individual Utilizations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.250 \\
u_2 &= \frac{2}{10} = 0.200 \\
u_3 &= \frac{3}{20} = 0.150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sum**

$$
U = 0.250 + 0.200 + 0.150 = 0.600
$$

**Step 3: Liu-Layland Bound for $n = 3$**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797
$$

**Step 4: Verdict**

$$
U = 0.600 \leq 0.7797 \quad \therefore \text{Schedulable under RMS.}
$$

The system uses 60% of CPU, leaving a 40% headroom. **Sufficient for additional processes** up to a total utilization of 0.7797.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall — Lost 3 Marks:**
> 1. **Computing $2^{1/3}$ incorrectly** as $2/3 = 0.667$. It is the cube root of 2, i.e., $\sqrt[3]{2} = 1.2599$.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $n$.** The bound is $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, NOT $(2^{1/n} - 1)$ alone.
> 3. **Failing to provide a concluding verdict** ("Schedulable" / "Not schedulable") — KTU awards 1 mark for the engineering judgment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam**

- [x] **Definition:** An IoT process is a 6-stage sequence: **Sense → Sample → Preprocess → Transmit → Decide → Actuate**.
- [x] **Tuple notation:** $\mathcal{P} = \langle S, E, T, A, \Sigma \rangle$.
- [x] **Five process categories:** Physical, Virtual, Hybrid, Event-driven, Time-driven.
- [x] **FSM states to remember:** Idle ($S_0$), Sensing ($S_1$), Processing ($S_2$), Transmitting ($S_3$), Waiting ($S_4$), Actuating ($S_5$), Fault ($S_6$).
- [x] **Liu-Layland RMS bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; asymptotic limit = **$\ln 2 \approx 0.693$**.
- [x] **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — required for every ADC sampling question.
- [x] **Throughput formula:** $\Theta = 1/T_p$ (sequential, non-pipelined).
- [x] **Always convert ms $\rightarrow$ s** before computing throughput.
- [x] **Five-layer architecture:** Field $\rightarrow$ Edge $\rightarrow$ Fog $\rightarrow$ Cloud $\rightarrow$ Actuation.
- [x] **Real-world example mapping** earns presentation bonus — always include a Smart Agriculture / Healthcare / Grid example.
- [x] **Engineering judgment** (suitable/not suitable) carries 1–2 marks — never omit it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Management** in the Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the comprehensive set of processes, architectural frameworks, technologies, and policies used to **acquire, ingest, store, process, query, secure, govern, and dispose** of the massive, heterogeneous, and high-velocity data generated by interconnected physical "Things" (sensors, actuators, RFID tags, smart devices).

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, IoT data management is viewed as the **backbone layer** that transforms raw sensory signals into actionable intelligence, ensuring **data fidelity, low-latency access, scalability, and interoperability** across the perception, network, and application layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):**
> "IoT Data Management is the disciplined orchestration of data collection, transmission, storage, processing, and analytics across the IoT stack, ensuring that the right data is available to the right application at the right time while preserving security and privacy."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Smart City Hospital Network**:
- Thousands of patients wear **biosensors** (heart rate, SpO₂, temperature).
- Every second, each sensor fires a tiny packet of data.
- The hospital needs to:
  1. **Capture** all these packets (ingestion).
  2. **Store** them in a way doctors can retrieve a patient's last 24 hours in **<100 ms** (storage).
  3. **Filter** out the noise and only alert when a threshold is breached (processing/stream analytics).
  4. **Compress** old data to save space (archival).
  5. **Anonymize** data before sending to research labs (governance/privacy).

**IoT Data Management is exactly this "hospital's nervous system" for the IoT world** — the silent, structured machinery that ensures no signal is lost, no alert is missed, and no resource is wasted.

## 1.3 The 5 V's of IoT Data (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> These five characteristics define why IoT data is fundamentally different from traditional enterprise data.

| Characteristic | Meaning | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Scale of data generated | Terabytes/day from a smart factory |
| **Velocity** | Speed of data generation & movement | 100,000 sensor readings/second |
| **Variety** | Heterogeneity of data types | Temperature JSON, video streams, vibration binary |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / data quality | Noisy sensor calibration, missing values |
| **Value** | Business insight extracted | Predictive maintenance alert |

## 1.4 Core Components of IoT Data Management

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically highlights the following pillars:

1. **Data Acquisition & Ingestion** – Pull/Push models, MQTT/HTTP/CoAP gateways.
2. **Data Storage** – Cloud, Fog, and Edge tiers; Time-Series Databases (TSDB) vs. NoSQL vs. SQL.
3. **Data Processing** – Batch (Hadoop/MapReduce) vs. Stream (Apache Kafka/Flink).
4. **Data Analytics** – Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive.
5. **Data Governance** – Security, Privacy, Lineage, Quality, Compliance.
6. **Data Disposal** – Archival, Purging, and Right-to-be-Forgotten (GDPR compliance).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IoT Data Lifecycle (Stage-by-Stage Logic)

### Stage 1: Data Generation (At the Edge)
- Sensors convert physical phenomena → electrical signals → digital bits.
- Sampling governed by **Nyquist–Shannon Theorem**: sampling rate ≥ 2× the highest signal frequency.
- Energy-constrained nodes may use **event-driven** rather than periodic sampling.

### Stage 2: Data Pre-Processing (On the Device / Gateway)
- **Filtering**: Kalman / Moving Average filters remove noise.
- **Compression**: Reduces payload (delta encoding, Huffman, LZW).
- **Feature Extraction**: Only the meaningful subset is forwarded (e.g., sending `avg_heart_rate` instead of 60 raw samples).

### Stage 3: Data Transmission (Network Layer)
- Protocols: **MQTT** (lightweight pub/sub), **CoAP** (REST for constrained devices), **AMQP**, **HTTP/HTTPS**.
- Choice depends on payload size, QoS, and device constraints.

### Stage 4: Data Ingestion (Platform Layer)
- **Message Brokers** (Kafka, RabbitMQ, Mosquitto) buffer the firehose.
- **Stream Processors** (Apache Flink, Spark Streaming) apply real-time rules.

### Stage 5: Data Storage
Three storage tiers are critical for KTU answers:

| Tier | Location | Latency | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge Storage** | On-device (Flash, SD) | <1 ms | Caching, offline buffering |
| **Fog Storage** | Gateway / Local server | 1–10 ms | Local analytics, aggregation |
| **Cloud Storage** | Data center | 50–500 ms | Long-term, big-data analytics |

### Stage 6: Data Analytics
- **Descriptive** – "What happened?" (dashboards, KPIs)
- **Diagnostic** – "Why did it happen?" (root-cause analysis)
- **Predictive** – "What will happen?" (ML models, time-series forecasting)
- **Prescriptive** – "What should we do?" (recommendation engines, RL)

### Stage 7: Data Disposal
- Retention policies, archival to cold storage (e.g., AWS Glacier), regulatory purging.

## 2.2 Database Choice Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Which database for which IoT workload?"* — memorize this matrix.

| Database Type | Example | Strength | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Relational (RDBMS)** | MySQL, PostgreSQL | ACID, joins | Structured metadata, configs |
| **Time-Series (TSDB)** | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB | High write throughput | Sensor telemetry, metrics |
| **Key-Value (NoSQL)** | Redis, DynamoDB | Sub-ms latency | Caching, session data |
| **Document (NoSQL)** | MongoDB, CouchDB | Schema flexibility | JSON device payloads |
| **Wide-Column (NoSQL)** | Cassandra, HBase | Horizontal scale | High-volume, multi-DC writes |
| **Graph (NoSQL)** | Neo4j | Relationship queries | Device topology, social IoT |

## 2.3 Big Data Processing Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Hadoop MapReduce** = Batch processing (latency: minutes–hours).
> **Apache Spark** = In-memory batch + micro-batch (latency: seconds).
> **Apache Flink / Kafka Streams** = True stream processing (latency: milliseconds).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{gen} = N \times f_s \times b$ | Per-node data rate = number of samples × sample rate × bits/sample | bits/s |
| 2 | $R_{cluster} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{gen,i}$ | Cluster-wide data rate (sum of nodes) | bits/s |
| 3 | $S_{daily} = \dfrac{R_{cluster} \times 86400}{8 \times 2^{20}}$ | Daily storage in MiB | MiB/day |
| 4 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 5 | $CR = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ | Compression Ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_{final} = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{CR}$ | Storage after compression | bytes |
| 7 | $T_{retention} = \dfrac{C_{storage}}{R_{daily}}$ | Time a storage volume will fill | days |
| 8 | $E_{tx} = \alpha \cdot d^{n} + E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Simplified transmission energy model | Joules |
| 9 | $V = 5V$ (typical) | Standard digital logic rail (informational) | V |
| 10 | $RTT = 2 \times t_{prop} + t_{proc}$ | Round-trip time for a request | seconds |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil-moisture sensors generate time-series data; stored in **InfluxDB**, analyzed with **Spark** for irrigation prediction.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Vibration data ingested into **Kafka**, processed with **Flink** for predictive maintenance — saves millions in unplanned downtime.
- **Healthcare Wearables**: ECG streams require **edge pre-processing** (compression) before cloud upload to meet bandwidth and battery limits.
- **Smart Grids**: Millions of smart meters → **Cassandra** (wide-column) for write scalability; **Hive** for batch billing analytics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: IoT Storage Requirement Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
A smart building deploys **$N = 500$** temperature sensors. Each sensor samples at **$f_s = 1$ Hz**, each sample is **$b = 16$ bits**. Compute the daily storage requirement in GB if no compression is applied.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Per-node data rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{gen} &= N \times f_s \times b \\
        &= 1 \times 1\,\text{Hz} \times 16\,\text{bits} \\
        &= 16\,\text{bits/s} \quad \text{(per single sensor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cluster-wide data rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{cluster} &= \sum_{i=1}^{500} R_{gen,i} \\
            &= 500 \times 16\,\text{bits/s} \\
            &= 8000\,\text{bits/s} \;=\; 8\,\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily raw volume in bits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bits} &= R_{cluster} \times 86400\,\text{s} \\
         &= 8000 \times 86400 \\
         &= 691{,}200{,}000\,\text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes (÷8) and then to GB (÷$2^{30}$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bytes} &= \dfrac{691{,}200{,}000}{8} = 86{,}400{,}000\,\text{B} \\[4pt]
V_{GB}    &= \dfrac{86{,}400{,}000}{2^{30}} \approx 0.0805\,\text{GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MiB for intuition**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{MiB} &= \dfrac{86{,}400{,}000}{2^{20}} \approx 82.4\,\text{MiB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU**: 500 low-rate sensors yield only ~80 MB/day. This is *tiny* — proves that **volume is not the only challenge**; **velocity and variety** dominate IoT big-data design.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Effect of Compression

**Problem:**
Above data is compressed using delta-encoding with **Compression Ratio $CR = 4$**. Find final storage and retention time on a **32 GB** edge SD card.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compressed daily volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{final} &= \dfrac{S_{orig}}{CR} = \dfrac{82.4\,\text{MiB}}{4} = 20.6\,\text{MiB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert device capacity to MiB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 32\,\text{GB} = 32 \times 2^{10}\,\text{MiB} = 32{,}768\,\text{MiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Retention time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{retention} &= \dfrac{C_{storage}}{R_{daily}} = \dfrac{32{,}768\,\text{MiB}}{20.6\,\text{MiB/day}} \\
              &\approx 1590\,\text{days} \;\approx\; 4.35\,\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Nyquist Verification for Vibration Sensor

**Problem:**
A vibration sensor in Industry 4.0 must capture bearing faults up to **$f_{max} = 5\,\text{kHz}$**. Verify whether a sampling rate of **$f_s = 12\,\text{kHz}$** satisfies Nyquist.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{Nyquist} &= 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000\,\text{Hz} \\[4pt]
f_s &\geq f_{Nyquist} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12{,}000 \geq 10{,}000 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Anti-aliasing margin:** $12{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 2\,\text{kHz}$ → **safe**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Stack

| Workload | Recommended Storage | Recommended Processing | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real-time alerts (<1 s) | **Redis / InfluxDB** | **Flink / Kafka Streams** | Sub-ms latency |
| Daily billing batch | **Cassandra / HDFS** | **Hadoop MapReduce / Spark** | High throughput, batch friendly |
| Device metadata / configs | **PostgreSQL** | SQL queries | ACID + joins |
| JSON payload ingestion | **MongoDB** | Spark / Lambda | Schema-less, document oriented |
| Sensor time-series dashboard | **TimescaleDB** | Grafana + SQL | Native time-series extensions |
| Predictive ML training | **HDFS + Parquet** | Spark MLlib | Columnar, scalable |

---

## 3.5 Derivation #4: Bandwidth vs Edge Trade-off

**Problem:**
A camera node generates **$2\,\text{MB/s}$** raw video. The available uplink is **$1\,\text{Mbps}$**. Compute the **required compression ratio** to make streaming feasible.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert bandwidth to bytes/s**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{up} &= 1\,\text{Mbps} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{8}\,\text{B/s} = 125{,}000\,\text{B/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Required compression**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR_{req} &= \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{allowed}} = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{125{,}000} = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** A 16:1 compression at the edge is achievable with H.264 → supports real-time video IoT. Without it, **cloud-only processing is infeasible** — proof of why **edge pre-processing is mandatory** in IoT data pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End IoT Data Management Architecture

The following Mermaid block depicts a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the IoT data management stack — from physical sensing to actionable insight.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    %% Top Layer: Perception
    subgraph SENSE["PERCEPTION LAYER - DATA GENERATION"]
        direction LR
        S1["Temperature Sensor"]
        S2["Vibration Sensor"]

        S4["RFID Tag"]
    end

    %% Edge Layer
    subgraph EDGE["EDGE LAYER - PRE PROCESSING"]
        direction LR

        E2["Compression Module"]
        E3["Edge Analytics Engine"]
    end

    %% Network Layer
    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER - TRANSPORT"]

    end

    %% Storage Tiers
    subgraph STORE["STORAGE TIERS"]
        direction LR

    %% Flows
    S1 --> E1
    S2 --> E1

    S4 --> E1

    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> N1

    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> P1

    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> D1
    P2 --> D2
    P2 --> D3

    D2 --> A2
    D3 --> A3

    A2 --> U2
    A3 --> U3
    P3 --> U1

    G2 -.-> D2
    G3 -.-> D2
```

## 4.2 Data Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Generate: Sensor Activated
    Generate --> PreProcess: Raw Signal Captured
    PreProcess --> Transmit: Filtered and Compressed
    Transmit --> Ingest: Arrives at Broker
    Ingest --> Process: Stream Consumer Fetches
    Process --> Store: Persisted to DB or Lake
    Store --> Analyze: Query or ML Job Runs
    Analyze --> Act: Insight Delivered to App
    Act --> Archive: Move to Cold Storage
    Act --> Purge: End of Retention Period
    Archive --> Purge: Policy Expired
    Purge --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Storage Tier Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["New IoT Data Arrives"] --> B{"Latency Need"}
    B -- Less than 1 ms --> C["Edge Storage - Redis or Local Flash"]
    B -- 1 to 10 ms --> D["Fog Storage - Gateway Server"]
    B -- More than 10 ms --> E["Cloud Storage - Data Lake"]
    C --> F{"Structured or Time Series?"}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G["InfluxDB or TimescaleDB"]
    F -- No --> H["MongoDB or Cassandra or HDFS"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between the 5 V's of Big Data in the context of IoT.**

**Model Answer:**

| Characteristic | Meaning | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Scale/quantity of data | Millions of sensor readings per hour |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation | 100k messages/sec in a smart factory |
| **Variety** | Different data formats | JSON, video, binary, CSV |
| **Veracity** | Data quality/trust | Calibration drift in sensors |
| **Value** | Extracted insight | Predictive maintenance cost savings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking tip:** Award 0.5 mark per correct row; 0.5 mark for IoT-specific examples.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Why are traditional relational databases often unsuitable for IoT time-series workloads?**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Schema rigidity** – RDBMS requires predefined tables; IoT schemas evolve rapidly (new device types).
2. **Poor write throughput** – Row-locking and ACID overheads cannot match the 100k+ writes/sec from sensors.
3. **Inefficient time-range queries** – No native time-series indexing; queries like *"average over the last 5 min"* require full table scans.

Hence, **Time-Series Databases (TSDBs)** like InfluxDB and TimescaleDB are preferred.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layers of IoT data management architecture**. Discuss the role of each layer in handling the 5 V's of data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart agriculture project deploys **$N = 2000$** soil-moisture sensors, each sampling at **$f_s = 0.1\,\text{Hz}$** with a **$b = 12$-bit** sample. Compute the **daily raw data volume in MB** and the **required compression ratio to fit into a 1 GB daily storage budget**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The IoT data management architecture has **five functional layers**:

1. **Perception Layer** – Generates data via sensors (Volume source).
2. **Edge / Pre-Processing Layer** – Filters, compresses, and extracts features (reduces **Volume**, improves **Veracity**).
3. **Network Layer** – Transmits via MQTT / CoAP (handles **Velocity** via buffering).
4. **Platform / Ingestion Layer** – Kafka / Flink brokers (handles **Variety** via schema registry).
5. **Storage and Analytics Layer** – TSDB + ML (extracts **Value**).

Each layer progressively **reduces noise, adds structure, and increases value**.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Per-node rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{gen} &= f_s \times b = 0.1 \times 12 = 1.2\,\text{bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cluster rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{cluster} &= 2000 \times 1.2 = 2400\,\text{bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily bits and bytes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bits} &= 2400 \times 86400 = 207{,}360{,}000\,\text{bits} \\
V_{B}    &= \dfrac{207{,}360{,}000}{8} = 25{,}920{,}000\,\text{B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB (decimal)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{MB} &= \dfrac{25{,}920{,}000}{10^{6}} = 25.92\,\text{MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compression ratio for 1 GB = 1024 MB budget**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR_{req} &= \dfrac{V_{orig}}{V_{budget}} = \dfrac{25.92}{1024} \approx 0.0253
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The compression ratio required is **0.0253** (i.e., data must be reduced to 2.53% of original — a **~40:1 compression**). This is feasible using delta-encoding on slow-changing moisture values, validating the use of **edge pre-processing**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast **batch processing, micro-batch processing, and stream processing** for IoT data. Which is most suitable for real-time patient monitoring in a hospital? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CAP theorem**. With an example, show why an IoT time-series database like InfluxDB prioritizes **Availability and Partition tolerance over Consistency** in distributed deployments. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Property | Batch | Micro-Batch | Stream |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | Minutes–Hours | Seconds | Milliseconds |
| **Engine example** | Hadoop MapReduce | Spark Streaming | Apache Flink |
| **Resource use** | High (full job) | Medium | Low continuous |
| **Use case** | Nightly billing | Near real-time dashboards | Critical alerts |

**For patient monitoring:** **Stream processing (Flink)** is appropriate because:
- Cardiac events require **sub-second alerting**.
- Backpressure handling prevents data loss during burst rates.
- Windowed aggregations (e.g., 1-min HR average) are native.

**Justification:** Lives depend on latency; micro-batch (~2 s lag) is **too slow** for ICU-grade monitoring.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The **CAP Theorem** (Eric Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide **only two** of the following three guarantees:

- **C**onsistency – every read returns the latest write.
- **A**vailability – every request gets a (non-error) response.
- **P**artition tolerance – the system continues despite network partitions.

**IoT context:** Sensors are spread across geographies → **network partitions are inevitable** ⇒ **P is mandatory**.

**InfluxDB-style AP choice:**
- A health-monitor's wearable data must **never be dropped** (availability) even if a regional replica lags.
- Slight staleness in readings is acceptable (heart rate of 30 s ago is still clinically valid).
- Hence InfluxDB favors **AP** — eventual consistency with high write availability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** state "InfluxDB violates Consistency" — it provides *eventual* consistency, not zero consistency.
> - **Do NOT** mix up CAP with ACID (CAP is distributed-systems; ACID is transactional).
> - Always **justify with a real IoT scenario** — generic answers score 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Definition**: Data Management = capture → store → process → analyze → govern → dispose.
- **5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — memorize with one IoT example each.
- **Storage tiers**: Edge (sub-ms) → Fog (ms) → Cloud (10s of ms).
- **DB choice**: TSDB for telemetry, NoSQL for flexibility, RDBMS for metadata.
- **Processing**: MapReduce = batch; Spark = micro-batch; Flink/Kafka Streams = true stream.
- **CAP theorem**: In IoT, partition-tolerance is mandatory → choose AP or CP based on latency vs. correctness need.
- **Key formulas**:
  - $R_{gen} = N \times f_s \times b$
  - $V_{day} = (R_{cluster} \times 86400) / 8$
  - $CR = S_{orig} / S_{comp}$
  - $T_{retention} = C_{storage} / R_{daily}$
  - $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist)
- **Protocols**: MQTT (pub/sub, lightweight), CoAP (RESTful, constrained), HTTP/HTTPS (heavy, web).
- **Analytics hierarchy**: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive.
- **Governance must-haves**: Encryption at rest & in transit, RBAC, audit logs, retention policies, GDPR compliance.
- **Common mistakes to avoid**:
  - Confusing *eventual consistency* with *no consistency*.
  - Recommending RDBMS for raw sensor streams.
  - Forgetting to convert bits → bytes → MiB/GB in numerical problems.
  - Writing MQTT in capital case as "Mqtt" or missing the pub/sub keyword.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of IoT Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Security** is the composite of policies, protocols, hardware-level safeguards, software routines, and cryptographic mechanisms engineered to protect the **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, and **Availability (CIA Triad)** of data, devices, networks, and services in an Internet of Things ecosystem, while simultaneously addressing the unique constraints of low-power, resource-limited edge nodes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, IoT Security is treated as a **cross-layer design concern** rather than a single network-layer fix, because the heterogeneous mix of constrained motes, edge gateways, and cloud backends creates an attack surface far larger than classical IT.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Apartment Building

> [!NOTE]
> Think of an IoT deployment as a giant **apartment building with millions of tiny flats (sensors)**, one central mailroom (cloud), and many delivery couriers (gateways). Security means:
> - **Doors that only the right person can open** (Authentication)
> - **Letters that cannot be read by neighbours** (Confidentiality via encryption)
> - **Letters that cannot be swapped silently** (Integrity via hashing)
> - **A fire exit that always works** (Availability / resilience)
> - **A guard who checks the ID of every visitor** (Anomaly / IDS)
> - **A logbook of who came in and when** (Non-repudiation / auditing)

Just as you would not secure only the front door and leave the windows open, **every IoT layer (Perception, Network, Edge/Cloud, Application)** must be hardened.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced in the KTU Syllabus

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Threat** | A potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in an asset that can be exploited by one or more threats. |
| **Attack** | An intentional unauthorized action attempting to bypass security controls. |
| **Risk** | The product of the probability of an attack and the impact of its consequence. |
| **Trust** | A measurable belief in the reliability and honesty of an entity, derived from direct experience or recommendation. |
| **Privacy** | The right of individuals to control the collection, use, and dissemination of their personal information. |

## 1.4 The CIA Triad — The Heart of IoT Security

The classical **CIA Triad** is the foundation on which every IoT security policy is constructed. Each leg of the triad has an IoT-specific interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad in IoT context:**
> - **Confidentiality** → ensures sensor data (e.g., health vitals, location) is readable only by authorized entities. Implemented via symmetric (AES) and asymmetric (ECC/RSA) cryptography.
> - **Integrity** → ensures data has not been altered during transit or at rest. Implemented via hashes (SHA-2, SHA-3) and MACs (HMAC).
> - **Availability** → ensures the IoT service is reachable when needed. Implemented via redundancy, DoS mitigation, and lightweight DDoS-resistant protocols like CoAP over DTLS.

Two extensions frequently used in IoT literature:

- **Authentication** — *Who are you?* — proves the identity of a device or user.
- **Authorization** — *What are you allowed to do?* — controls resource access after identity is proven.
- **Non-Repudiation** — *You cannot deny what you did* — guarantees proof of origin and delivery.
- **Privacy** — *Your data belongs to you* — a superset of confidentiality with consent and control.

## 1.5 Standard Security Metrics in IoT

The following metrics are commonly referenced in KTU 2024 board questions and industry whitepapers:

- **Attack Surface** — the sum of points (hardware ports, APIs, wireless interfaces) where an unauthorized actor can attempt to enter.
- **Time-to-Compromise (TTC)** — average time an attacker needs to breach a system, often measured in hours.
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** — operational security KPIs.
- **Key Length** — measured in **bits**; the security strength is roughly half the key length for symmetric and equivalent strength for asymmetric (NIST SP 800-57).
- **Entropy (H)** — measured in **bits/symbol**; quantifies the unpredictability of a cryptographic key.

## 1.6 Why IoT Security Is Harder Than Classical IT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Constrained devices cannot run heavy TLS stacks.
> - Devices are physically accessible in the field → **node tampering**.
> - Massive scale → millions of identical devices, each a potential entry point.
> - Heterogeneous protocols (Zigbee, BLE, LoRa, MQTT, CoAP) widen the threat surface.
- Long lifecycles (10–20 years) → patches rarely reach the field.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Threat Model of IoT

IoT systems are conventionally decomposed into **four logical layers**. Each layer carries a distinct class of threats and requires tailored defences. KTU 2024 expects students to map attacks and defences layer-wise.

### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer

- **Components:** Sensors, actuators, RFID tags, embedded MCUs.
- **Typical threats:** Node capture, tampering, side-channel (power/EM) analysis, RF jamming, fake-node injection (sybil).
- **Defences:** Tamper-resistant hardware, secure boot, physically unclonable functions (PUFs), lightweight symmetric encryption (AES-128 in hardware).

### Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer

- **Components:** Gateways, routers, 6LoWPAN, Wi-Fi, cellular, BLE mesh.
- **Typical threats:** Eavesdropping, MITM (Man-in-the-Middle), replay, routing attacks (sinkhole, wormhole), DoS, DDoS.
- **Defences:** DTLS for CoAP, TLS for MQTT, IPsec, RPL security mode, channel hopping, packet authentication.

### Layer 3 — Middleware / Edge-Cloud Layer

- **Components:** Cloud brokers, data lakes, analytics engines, device management portals.
- **Typical threats:** SQL/NoSQL injection, broken authentication, cloud misconfiguration, insider threats, data exfiltration.
- **Defences:** OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect, Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), data-at-rest encryption (AES), KMS, HSM-backed root keys.

### Layer 4 — Application Layer

- **Components:** Mobile apps, dashboards, third-party integrations.
- **Typical threats:** Cross-site scripting, insecure direct object references, firmware downgrade, privacy leakage, social engineering.
- **Defences:** Secure SDLC, code signing, OWASP IoT Top 10 audits, user consent flows.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** Most exam questions ask you to *list 4 threats per layer and 2 defences for each* — practice with this four-layer matrix.

## 2.2 The Trust Model

Trust in IoT is bidirectional and dynamic. It can be expressed as:

$$T_{A \to B}(t) \;=\; \alpha \cdot D(A,B) \;+\; \beta \cdot R(A,B,t) \;+\; \gamma \cdot C(A,B)$$

where $T_{A \to B}(t)$ is the trust value that entity $A$ places on entity $B$ at time $t$, $D(A,B)$ is direct trust from past interactions, $R(A,B,t)$ is reputation derived from third-party recommendations, $C(A,B)$ is a context weight, and $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ are weighting factors summing to unity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following formulae. They appear frequently in 3-mark and 14-mark derivations.

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Average information per symbol in **bits**. |
| Joint Entropy | $H(X,Y) = -\sum_{i,j} p(x_i,y_j) \log_2 p(x_i,y_j)$ | Combined uncertainty. |
| Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | Information shared between two random variables. |
| Perfect Secrecy | $H(M \mid C) = H(M)$ | Ciphertext reveals nothing about plaintext. |
| Birthday Bound (Hash Collision) | $n \approx 1.17 \cdot 2^{k/2}$ | Hashes to evaluate before ~50% collision, $k$ = bit length. |
| Work Factor (Brute-Force Key) | $W = 2^{k}$ | Symmetric key of $k$ bits. |
| RSA Modulus Strength | $k_{RSA} \approx 6 \cdot k_{sym}$ | Asymmetric RSA key needed for equivalent symmetric strength. |
| ECC Equivalent | $k_{ECC} \approx 2 \cdot k_{sym}$ | Elliptic-curve key size needed for equivalent symmetric strength. |
| Davies Attack Cost | $W_{Davies} \approx 2^{120}$ | Estimated practical ceiling for symmetric block ciphers. |
| Energy per Bit (Crypto) | $E_b = V \cdot I \cdot t_b$ | Where $V$, $I$, $t_b$ are voltage, current, and per-bit time. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Reminder:** When expressing absolute value, set membership, or probability bars in markdown tables, write them as $\vert x \vert$, $\mid x \in S \mid$, or $\Pr[X=x]$ — never use the raw pipe character, which breaks the table.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Smart Healthcare:** Compromise of an insulin pump → integrity attacks could cause lethal dosing errors. CIA + authenticity are life-critical.
- **Smart Grid:** Confidentiality of consumption data leaks occupancy; integrity of meter readings enables energy theft.
- **Industrial IoT:** Availability attacks on SCADA systems (e.g., Stuxnet-class) can halt entire factories.
- **Smart Cities:** Compromised traffic-light controllers can create life-threatening gridlocks.

## 2.5 Lightweight Cryptography — IoT's Answer to Constraints

Because classical AES-256 + RSA-2048 stacks are too heavy for 8-bit MCUs running on coin cells, the IoT community has standardized **lightweight primitives**:

- **Block ciphers:** PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, TWINE.
- **Hash functions:** SPONGENT, PHOTON, QUARK.
- **Public-key:** Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) on curves such as **secp256r1** or the IoT-favoured **Curve25519**.
- **Auth:** HMAC-SHA-256 truncated to 64 or 96 bits.
- **Suite:** **ISO/IEC 29192** — the formal lightweight-crypto standard frequently referenced by KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 boards often ask: *"Why is ECC preferred over RSA in IoT?"* — because at **256 bits** ECC ≈ **3072-bit RSA** in strength, and the key/signature is far smaller, reducing radio transmission energy by **8–12×**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon Entropy of a Binary Source

Consider a coin biased to land **Heads** with probability $p$ and **Tails** with probability $(1-p)$. We want to find the entropy $H(X)$ of this source.

**Step 1.** Write the definition of entropy for two symbols.

$$
H(X) \;=\; -p \log_2 p \;-\; (1-p) \log_2 (1-p)
$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate with respect to $p$ to find the extrema.

$$
\frac{dH}{dp} \;=\; -\log_2 p - \frac{1}{\ln 2} \;+\; \log_2 (1-p) + \frac{1}{\ln 2}
$$

The constant $\frac{1}{\ln 2}$ terms cancel because they appear once with $+$ and once with $-$.

$$
\frac{dH}{dp} \;=\; \log_2 \frac{1-p}{p}
$$

**Step 3.** Set the derivative to zero to find the critical point.

$$
\log_2 \frac{1-p}{p} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{1-p}{p} \;=\; 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; p \;=\; \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 4.** Confirm it is a maximum using the second derivative.

$$
\frac{d^{2}H}{dp^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{1}{p \ln 2} \;-\; \frac{1}{(1-p)\ln 2} \;<\; 0
$$

The second derivative is always negative for $p \in (0,1)$, confirming a maximum at $p = 1/2$.

**Step 5.** Substitute $p = 1/2$ into the entropy formula.

$$
H_{\max} \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 \tfrac{1}{2} \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 \tfrac{1}{2} \;=\; 1 \text{ bit}
$$

**Conclusion:** A fair coin is the most informative binary source, delivering **1 bit per toss**. This is the *upper bound* of any binary source, and is the formal reason a cryptographic key generated by a fair coin flip is optimal.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Birthday Bound for Hash Collisions

Let $h: \{0,1\}^{*} \to \{0,1\}^{k}$ be a hash function. We draw $N$ random inputs and look for the expected number of collisions.

**Step 1.** Probability that two particular inputs $x_i, x_j$ do **not** collide.

$$
P(\text{no collision for pair}) \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{2^{k}}
$$

**Step 2.** Probability that the **first $N$ inputs produce no collision at all**. There are $\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ pairs.

$$
P(\text{no collision in } N) \;\approx\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{k}}\right)^{N(N-1)/2}
$$

**Step 3.** Take the natural logarithm and use $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$ for small $x$.

$$
\ln P \;\approx\; -\frac{N(N-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{k}}
$$

**Step 4.** Set the exponent to $-1$ (≈ 37% non-collision → 63% collision).

$$
\frac{N(N-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{k}} \;\approx\; 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N^{2} \;\approx\; 2^{k+1}
$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $N$.

$$
N \;\approx\; \sqrt{2^{k+1}} \;=\; 2^{(k+1)/2} \;\approx\; 1.17 \cdot 2^{k/2}
$$

**Conclusion:** For a **k-bit hash**, an attacker needs to evaluate only about $2^{k/2}$ messages to find a collision with non-trivial probability. This is the famous **birthday paradox** and the reason **SHA-256** ($k = 256$) is considered strong at $\approx 2^{128}$ work.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Cost of Cryptographic Transmission

A wireless sensor transmits at voltage $V = 3.0\,\text{V}$, current draw $I = 12\,\text{mA}$, and the modem bit-time is $t_b = 25\,\mu\text{s}$. The cryptographic overhead adds $n_o = 64$ extra bytes (signature) per packet.

**Step 1.** Per-bit transmission energy.

$$
E_b \;=\; V \cdot I \cdot t_b \;=\; 3.0 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
E_b \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{J} \;=\; 0.90 \;\mu\text{J}
$$

**Step 2.** Per-byte energy (8 bits).

$$
E_{\text{byte}} \;=\; 8 \cdot E_b \;=\; 7.2 \;\mu\text{J}
$$

**Step 3.** Cryptographic overhead energy.

$$
E_{\text{crypto}} \;=\; n_o \cdot 8 \cdot E_b \;=\; 64 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.90 \;\mu\text{J} \;=\; 460.8 \;\mu\text{J}
$$

**Step 4.** Convert to **millijoules** for readability.

$$
E_{\text{crypto}} \;\approx\; 0.46 \;\text{mJ} \text{ per packet}
$$

**Conclusion:** Every ECC signature transmitted costs ~0.46 mJ on this radio profile. This is why **lightweight, short signatures (Ed25519 = 64 bytes, ECDSA-secp256r1 = ~70 bytes)** dominate IoT; switching to RSA-2048 (~256-byte signature) would cost ~1.84 mJ, a 4× increase in energy — unacceptable on a coin-cell node.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cryptographic Primitives for IoT

| Primitive | Key / Output Size (bits) | Energy Class | Best IoT Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| AES-128 (symmetric) | 128 key, 128 block | Low (HW accel) | Bulk sensor data encryption |
| AES-256 (symmetric) | 256 key, 128 block | Medium | Long-lifecycle devices, NIST PQC fallback |
| SHA-256 (hash) | 256 output | Low | Integrity, HMAC, key derivation |
| HMAC-SHA-256 | 256 tag | Low | Authenticated sensor packets |
| ECC-secp256r1 | 256 key | Low | Device authentication, ECDH key exchange |
| Ed25519 (signatures) | 256 key, 512 sig | Medium | Firmware signing, OTA updates |
| RSA-2048 | 2048 key, 2048 sig | High | Avoid in IoT — kept for legacy gateways |
| PRESENT (light) | 80/128 key | Ultra-low | 8-bit RFID / smart-card tags |
| SPONGENT-256 (hash) | 256 output | Ultra-low | EPC Gen2 RFID tags |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Cost of Brute-Forcing an IoT Key

A smart-lock uses an **AES-128** symmetric key. An attacker can test $10^{9}$ keys/sec on a GPU cluster.

**Step 1.** Number of candidate keys.

$$
N \;=\; 2^{128} \;\approx\; 3.4 \times 10^{38}
$$

**Step 2.** Time in seconds.

$$
t \;=\; \frac{N}{10^{9}} \;\approx\; 3.4 \times 10^{29} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3.** Convert to years.

$$
T \;\approx\; \frac{3.4 \times 10^{29}}{3.15 \times 10^{7}} \;\approx\; 1.08 \times 10^{22} \text{ years}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is roughly **7.8 trillion** times the age of the universe (≈ 13.8 × 10⁹ years). AES-128 is **computationally secure** for IoT lifetimes of 10–20 years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Always state assumptions (key length, attacker capability, computational model) before quoting a brute-force time. Marks are reserved for explicit reasoning, not just the final number.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Layer IoT Security Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **Perception → Network → Middleware → Application** stack, with primary attack vectors on the left and primary defence mechanisms on the right.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef layer fill:#1f2937,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px,color:#f9fafb
    classDef attack fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#fff5f5
    classDef defence fill:#064e3b,stroke:#86efac,color:#ecfdf5
    classDef meta fill:#312e81,stroke:#a5b4fc,color:#eef2ff

    L4["LAYER 4 APPLICATION LAYER"]:::layer
    L3["LAYER 3 MIDDLEWARE EDGE CLOUD"]:::layer
    L2["LAYER 2 NETWORK COMMUNICATION"]:::layer
    L1["LAYER 1 PERCEPTION SENSING"]:::layer

    A4["XSS, Broken Auth, Firmware Downgrade, Privacy Leak"]:::attack
    A3["Cloud Misconfig, Insider, Data Exfiltration, NoSQLi"]:::attack
    A2["Eavesdropping, MITM, Replay, Sinkhole, Wormhole, DoS"]:::attack
    A1["Node Capture, Tampering, Side Channel, RF Jamming, Sybil"]:::attack

    D4["OWASP IoT Audit, Code Signing, Consent Flows"]:::defence
    D3["ABAC, OAuth 2.0, AES at Rest, KMS, HSM, SIEM"]:::defence
    D2["DTLS, TLS, IPsec, RPL Sec, HMAC, Channel Hopping"]:::defence
    D1["PUF, Secure Boot, AES 128 HW, Tamper Proofing"]:::defence

    META["CROSS LAYER TRUST MANAGEMENT AND GOVERNANCE"]:::meta

    L1 --- A1
    L1 --- D1
    L2 --- A2
    L2 --- D2
    L3 --- A3
    L3 --- D3
    L4 --- A4
    L4 --- D4
    META --- L1
    META --- L2
    META --- L3
    META --- L4
```

## 4.2 Trust and Key-Exchange Flow (Device ↔ Gateway ↔ Cloud)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DEV as IoT Device
    participant GW as Edge Gateway
    participant CLD as Cloud Broker

    Note over DEV,CLD: Phase 1 Bootstrapping
    DEV->>DEV: Read PUF derived identity (PUF = DevID)
    DEV->>GW: HELLO + DevID signed with Ed25519

    Note over GW,CLD: Phase 2 Mutual Authentication
    GW->>CLD: Forward DevID (TLS 1.3)
    CLD->>GW: Challenge Nonce
    GW->>DEV: Challenge Nonce
    DEV->>GW: HMAC SHA 256 response
    GW->>CLD: Forward HMAC
    CLD-->>GW: Session Ticket (JWT)
    GW-->>DEV: Session Key (ECDH derived)

    Note over DEV,CLD: Phase 3 Encrypted Data Plane
    DEV->>GW: Sensor Data (AES 128 GCM + Nonce)
    GW->>CLD: Aggregated Data (DTLS)
    CLD-->>GW: Control Command (Signed)
    GW-->>DEV: Command (AES 128 GCM)

    Note over DEV,CLD: Phase 4 Key Refresh
    CLD->>GW: New Session Ticket
    GW->>DEV: Initiate ECDH Rekey
```

## 4.3 Attack-Surface Coverage Map (CIA Triad × IoT Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
