```mermaid
    classDef c fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#7dd3fc,color:#f0f9ff
    classDef i fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fdba74,color:#fff7ed
    classDef a fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,color:#f0fdf4

    C["Confidentiality Threats"]:::c
    I["Integrity Threats"]:::i
    A["Availability Threats"]:::a

    C --> C1["Eavesdropping on Wireless Channel"]
    C --> C2["Side Channel Power Analysis"]
    C --> C3["Cloud Bucket Misconfiguration"]
    C --> C4["Plaintext MQTT Payloads"]

    I --> I1["Forged Firmware Updates"]
    I --> I2["Replay of Sensor Readings"]
    I --> I3["Routing Sinkhole Modification"]
    I --> I4["MITM TLS Strip Downgrade"]

    A --> A1["RF Jamming at 2.4 GHz"]
    A --> A2["MQTT Broker DDoS"]
    A --> A3["Battery Drain Sleep Deprivation"]
    A --> A4["Botnet Mirai Class Compromise"]
```

## 4.4 Lightweight Crypto Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef dec fill:#1e1b4b,stroke:#c4b5fd,color:#eef2ff
    classDef leaf fill:#064e3b,stroke:#6ee7b7,color:#ecfdf5

    Q1{"MCU Class?"}:::dec
    Q2{"Need Asymmetric?"}:::dec
    Q3{"Long Lifecycle > 10 years?"}:::dec

    L1["AES 128 Hardware Accelerate"]:::leaf
    L2["PRESENT 80 bit or SIMON 64 bit"]:::leaf
    L3["ECC secp256r1 via TinyECC"]:::leaf
    L4["Ed25519 for Signatures"]:::leaf
    L5["AES 256 GCM HMAC SHA 384"]:::leaf

    Q1 -->|8 bit RFID tag| Q2
    Q1 -->|32 bit Cortex M| Q2
    Q1 -->|64 bit Gateway| Q3

    Q2 -->|No, symmetric only| L1
    Q2 -->|Yes, mutual auth| L3
    Q2 -->|Yes, firmware sign| L4

    Q3 -->|No| L1
    Q3 -->|Yes, PQC ready| L5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **KTU University Exam Style:** Two or three crisp points and one line-diagram sketch will earn full marks.

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between a Threat, a Vulnerability, and an Attack in the context of an IoT network. Give one example of each from the perception layer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Concept | Definition (1 mark) | IoT Example (1 mark) | Distinguishing Note (1 mark) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Threat** | A potential cause of an unwanted incident. | An unpatched Zigbee firmware on a smart bulb. | Threats are *latent*; they have not happened yet. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness that can be exploited. | Hard-coded default password `admin:1234` on a camera. | Vulnerabilities are *systemic*; they live inside the system. |
| **Attack** | An intentional action to exploit a vulnerability. | A Mirai-style botnet brute-forcing that default password. | Attacks are *active*; they consume attacker resources. |

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**State Shannon's theorem of perfect secrecy. Why is one-time pad (OTP) considered to provide perfect secrecy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Shannon's Theorem (1.5 marks):** A cipher provides *perfect secrecy* iff for every plaintext $M$ and ciphertext $C$,

$$
\Pr[M = m \mid C = c] \;=\; \Pr[M = m]
$$

Equivalently, $H(M \mid C) = H(M)$, meaning the ciphertext reveals no information about the plaintext.

2. **One-Time Pad Proof (1.5 marks):** In OTP, the key $K$ is a uniformly random bit-string as long as the plaintext, used only once. For any fixed ciphertext $c = m \oplus k$, every plaintext $m'$ maps to exactly one key $k' = m' \oplus c$, and since $k'$ is equiprobable, $\Pr[M = m' \mid C = c] = \Pr[K = k'] = 2^{-n}$. The conditional probability equals the prior, satisfying perfect secrecy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern:** Module 1 carries an internal choice between two 14-mark questions, each split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. We provide two fully independent alternatives.

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 1]
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the four-layer IoT architecture. For each layer, list **three** security threats and **two** corresponding defence mechanisms.

**Model Solution:**

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer (2 marks)**
- *Threats:* Node capture, side-channel (power/EM) analysis, RF jamming, Sybil attack (any three).
- *Defences:* Tamper-resistant enclosures, PUF-based device identity, secure boot ROM, hardware AES-128 engine (any two).

**Layer 2 — Network Layer (2 marks)**
- *Threats:* Eavesdropping on IEEE 802.15.4, MITM on gateway handshakes, sinkhole/wormhole in RPL, replay attacks.
- *Defences:* DTLS for CoAP, IPsec, RPL security mode with message authentication codes, channel hopping, AES-CCM link-layer encryption.

**Layer 3 — Middleware / Edge-Cloud Layer (1.5 marks)**
- *Threats:* Cloud storage misconfiguration (S3 leaks), broken authentication, insider threats, NoSQL injection.
- *Defences:* OAuth 2.0 + OpenID Connect, ABAC policies, AES-256 at rest, HSM-backed key management, audit logging via SIEM.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (1.5 marks)**
- *Threats:* Cross-site scripting in dashboards, insecure direct object references, firmware downgrade attacks, social engineering.
- *Defences:* OWASP IoT Top-10 audit, signed firmware with rollback prevention, secure SDLC, privacy-by-design consent flows.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a diagram, explain **mutual authentication** between an IoT device and a cloud broker using **DTLS handshake over CoAP**. Why is mutual authentication critical in IoT?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Why Mutual Authentication is Critical (2 marks):**
- In a one-way (server-only) TLS, a rogue gateway could impersonate the cloud and harvest sensor data.
- Mutual authentication ensures **both** the device *and* the server are genuine, preventing rogue-device injection and rogue-gateway MITM.
- Critical for medical, industrial, and grid IoT, where a single fake node can corrupt thousands of downstream decisions.

**2. DTLS Handshake over CoAP (4 marks — describe the flow with reference to the diagram below):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CoAP Client (Device)
    participant S as CoAP Server (Cloud)

    C->>S: ClientHello (random nonce, cipher suites, ECC)
    S->>C: HelloVerifyRequest (cookie)
    C->>S: ClientHello (with cookie)
    S->>C: ServerHello + ServerKeyExchange + ServerHelloDone
    C->>S: ClientKeyExchange + ChangeCipherSpec + Finished
    S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec + Finished
    Note over C,S: Application data encrypted with AES-CCM
```

- Steps 1–2: Device sends `ClientHello` containing supported ECC curves (e.g., secp256r1), cipher suites (TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_CCM_8).
- Server replies with `ServerHello`, its **ECDSA-signed certificate** (mutual auth = client must also present a cert), and ephemeral ECDH parameters.
- Both sides derive a **pre-master secret** from ECDH, then HKDF gives session keys.
- Finished messages are MACed with the new keys; on verification, the secure channel is up.
- CoAP messages (CON, NON, ACK) are then encrypted with AES-128-CCM-8, which is 8-byte authenticated, optimized for 6LoWPAN's 127-byte MTU.

**3. Comparison vs. TLS over TCP (1 mark):**
- DTLS adds explicit sequence numbers and a stateless cookie exchange to defeat UDP-spoofing and DoS amplification — both critical over lossy 6LoWPAN links.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 1 — Alternative]
**(a) [7 marks]** Define the **CIA Triad**. For each leg of the triad, give a real-world IoT attack scenario and propose a cryptographic countermeasure.

**Model Solution:**

**Confidentiality (2 marks)**
- *Attack scenario:* An attacker parks a vehicle 50 m from a smart home and uses an SDR to sniff Zigbee traffic carrying door-lock codes.
- *Countermeasure:* AES-128-CCM link-layer encryption, with a per-device key provisioned at manufacture; rotate every 90 days.

**Integrity (2 marks)**
- *Attack scenario:* An adversary replays a recorded "open garage door" packet from yesterday, gaining physical access.
- *Countermeasure:* Replay protection via monotonically increasing nonces and HMAC-SHA-256 truncation, plus tight receive-window filtering at the gateway.

**Availability (2 marks)**
- *Attack scenario:* A Mirai-variant botnet launches a UDP-amplification attack on a CoAP broker, blacking out an entire factory.
- *Countermeasure:* Rate-limiting, stateful packet inspection, anycast with multiple broker endpoints, and CoAP-Observe with exponential back-off.

**Synthesis (1 mark):** Modern IoT frameworks (e.g., Matter, LwM2M) integrate all three by enforcing *authenticated encryption with associated data* (AEAD) — combining confidentiality, integrity, and replay protection in a single primitive such as AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **birthday bound** for hash collisions. If a sensor node uses **SHA-256**, what is the expected number of messages an attacker must try before a collision is found with 50% probability?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up probability of no collision (2 marks):**
For $N$ random messages into a $k$-bit hash space, the probability that all $N$ outputs are distinct is

$$
P_{\text{no-coll}} \;=\; \prod_{i=0}^{N-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{2^{k}}\right) \;\approx\; e^{-N(N-1)/2^{k+1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the collision probability to 0.5 (2 marks):**
We want $P_{\text{no-coll}} = 0.5$, i.e., $e^{-N^{2}/2^{k+1}} = 0.5$. Taking natural log:

$$
-\frac{N^{2}}{2^{k+1}} \;=\; \ln 0.5 \;=\; -0.693
$$

$$
N \;=\; \sqrt{0.693 \cdot 2^{k+1}} \;\approx\; 1.177 \cdot 2^{k/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $k = 256$ for SHA-256 (2 marks):**

$$
N \;\approx\; 1.177 \cdot 2^{128}
$$

Numerically:

$$
N \;\approx\; 4.0 \times 10^{38} \text{ messages}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
At a network rate of $10^{9}$ hashes/sec on a GPU farm, the wall-clock time is

$$
T \;\approx\; \frac{4 \times 10^{38}}{10^{9} \cdot 3.15 \times 10^{7}} \;\approx\; 1.27 \times 10^{22} \text{ years}
$$

This is **~10¹² times the age of the universe**, confirming that SHA-256 provides collision resistance far beyond any practical IoT device lifetime. Hence, SHA-256 is suitable for firmware integrity, sensor-data MAC, and key derivation in resource-constrained IoT nodes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 1 Security Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Threat with Vulnerability (–1 mark):** Students often write *"SQL injection is a threat"* — it is actually an *attack* exploiting a *vulnerability* (unsanitized input).
> 2. **Quoting bit-lengths without units (–0.5 mark):** Always say *bits* explicitly. Saying *"key length 128"* is ambiguous; the unit is implied only in cryptography contexts and examiners look for it.
> 3. **Forgetting Non-Repudiation:** Many students stop at CIA. KTU 2024 often awards a bonus mark for listing **non-repudiation and privacy** as extended CIA legs.
> 4. **Mixing layers:** A perception-layer attack (e.g., node capture) and a network-layer attack (e.g., MITM) are *not* the same — examiners will not give layer-credit if you misclassify.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark sub-question that demands a diagram *without one* typically loses 2 marks, even if the prose is correct.
> 6. **Hand-waving ECC vs. RSA:** If asked to compare, *quantify* — "ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072 in strength" is required; vague "ECC is better" earns partial credit only.
> 7. **Math errors in log base:** Entropy uses $\log_2$; information-theoretic results using natural log must explicitly state the base or convert via $1\,\text{nat} = 1/\ln 2 \approx 1.443\,\text{bits}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IoT Security (Module 1)**

- **CIA Triad** is the foundation: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability. Extend with **A**uthentication, **A**uthorization, **N**on-repudiation, **P**rivacy.
- **Four-layer model:** Perception → Network → Middleware → Application. Each layer has unique threats and defences — memorize **3 threats + 2 defences per layer**.
- **Trust equation:** $T_{A \to B}(t) = \alpha D + \beta R + \gamma C$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.
- **Shannon entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$; maximum for a binary source is **1 bit per symbol** at $p = 1/2$.
- **Perfect secrecy condition:** $H(M \mid C) = H(M)$, achieved by **One-Time Pad**.
- **Birthday bound for hash collisions:** $N \approx 1.17 \cdot 2^{k/2}$. For SHA-256, $N \approx 2^{128}$.
- **Key-length equivalence:** AES-128 ≈ ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072 in security strength.
- **Lightweight crypto standards:** ISO/IEC 29192; primitives: PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, SPONGENT, PHOTON, ECC.
- **DTLS** is to CoAP what TLS is to HTTP — it is the workhorse of IoT secure transport.
- **Mutual authentication** is mandatory in IoT — one-way auth allows rogue gateways.
- **PUF** (Physically Unclonable Function) is a hardware fingerprint used for device identity.
- **AEAD ciphers** (AES-CCM, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) provide confidentiality + integrity in one primitive — preferred for constrained nodes.
- **Common IoT attacks to memorize:** Mirai botnet, Stuxnet, KRACK (Wi-Fi), BlueBorne (BLE), Sybil, sinkhole, wormhole, replay, side-channel, node capture, RFID skimming.
- **Quantitative numbers to remember verbatim:** 1 bit (max entropy of fair coin), $2^{128}$ (work for AES-128), $2^{80}$ (minimum recommended for IoT keys in 2025 per NIST), $2^{128}$ (collision work for SHA-256).
- **Standard citations for exam answers:** NIST SP 800-57 (key management), ISO/IEC 29192 (lightweight crypto), RFC 7252 (CoAP), RFC 6347 (DTLS), OWASP IoT Top 10.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy & Trust
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Privacy & Trust

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy in IoT (KTU 2024 Definition):** The right of individuals, devices, and entities to control the collection, storage, processing, sharing, and dissemination of their personal, sensitive, or contextual information generated, transmitted, or inferred by interconnected IoT devices, sensors, and actuators across heterogeneous networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trust in IoT (KTU 2024 Definition):** A measurable, dynamic, and context-dependent confidence level established between two or more IoT entities (device-to-device, device-to-user, or device-to-cloud) regarding the reliability, integrity, authenticity, and expected behavior of exchanged data, services, or actions.

**Standard KTU Metrics Used:**

- **Trust Value (T):** A normalized scalar in the interval **[0, 1]**, where **0** represents complete distrust and **1** represents absolute trust.
- **Reputation Score (R):** An aggregated, time-decayed historical trust value computed across multiple observers.
- **Privacy Risk Index (PRI):** A weighted score quantifying the exposure level of sensitive data attributes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Smart Home Scenario:**

Imagine your house is fitted with smart speakers, security cameras, smart locks, and fitness wearables. Every day, these devices silently observe:
- *Who is at the door?* (Camera → Cloud)
- *What did you eat?* (Fridge → Cloud)
- *How healthy is your heart?* (Watch → Cloud)
- *When do you sleep?* (Bed sensor → Cloud)

**Privacy** asks: *"Who is allowed to see this data? Can the fridge company sell your eating habits to advertisers? Can the camera record your guests without consent?"*

**Trust** asks: *"Can I rely on this smart lock to actually lock the door? Will the fitness watch send my heart data to a legitimate hospital, or has it been spoofed by a hacker?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Privacy =** "I control **what** is shared, **with whom**, and **how much**."
> - **Trust =** "I believe **who** I am communicating with is genuine, reliable, and behaves as expected."

### 1.3 Why Privacy & Trust Are Critical in IoT

| IoT Characteristic | Privacy Impact | Trust Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Massive device count (billions) | Larger data harvesting surface | Difficult identity verification |
| Always-on sensors | Continuous surveillance risk | Sustained authentication required |
| Heterogeneous devices | Inconsistent privacy policies | Variable security postures |
| Resource-constrained nodes | Lightweight crypto = weaker privacy | Hard to embed trust anchors |
| Cloud + Edge data flow | Data crosses multiple jurisdictions | Multi-hop trust chains needed |
| Context-aware automation | Behavior inference & profiling | Decisions based on unverified sources |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of IoT Privacy

1. **Data Minimization** — Collect only the data strictly necessary for the intended function.
2. **Purpose Limitation** — Data collected for one purpose (e.g., fitness) cannot be reused for another (e.g., insurance profiling) without consent.
3. **Consent & Control** — The user must explicitly authorize data collection and retain revocation rights.
4. **Anonymization & Pseudonymization** — Stripping or masking personally identifiable information (PII) before transmission.
5. **Transparency** — Users must be informed about what is collected, how long it is retained, and with whom it is shared.

### 2.2 The Three Dimensions of IoT Trust

1. **Identity Trust** — *Is this entity genuinely who it claims to be?* (Authentication)
2. **Behavioral Trust** — *Does it perform its declared function honestly?* (Integrity, non-tampering)
3. **Reputation Trust** — *Have other entities in the network had good prior experiences with it?* (Aggregate historical scoring)

### 2.3 Trust Computation Models — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Direct Trust (Experience-based):**
A node $N_i$ evaluates its direct interaction with $N_j$ using:

$$
T_{direct}(N_i, N_j) = \frac{\text{Successful Interactions}}{\text{Total Interactions}}
$$

**Step 2 — Indirect Trust (Recommendation-based):**
Other nodes $N_k$ that have interacted with $N_j$ share their opinions:

$$
T_{indirect}(N_i, N_j) = \frac{1}{\vert K \vert} \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot T(N_k, N_j)
$$

where $w_k$ is the credibility weight of recommender $N_k$.

**Step 3 — Composite Trust Value:**
$$
T_{composite}(N_i, N_j) = \alpha \cdot T_{direct} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot T_{indirect}
$$

where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is the **self-confidence factor**.

**Step 4 — Time Decay (Freshness):**
Older trust recommendations are weighted less using exponential decay:

$$
T_{fresh}(t) = T_{composite} \cdot e^{-\lambda (t_{now} - t_{last})}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** and $t_{last}$ is the last update timestamp.

### 2.4 Privacy Preservation Techniques

- **Differential Privacy:** Add calibrated noise to datasets so that inclusion/exclusion of any single record does not significantly affect the output.
- **Homomorphic Encryption:** Perform computations on encrypted data without decrypting it.
- **Federated Learning:** Train ML models locally on the device; share only model parameters, not raw data.
- **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP):** Prove a statement is true without revealing the underlying data.
- **k-Anonymity:** Ensure each record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ other records.
- **Data Obfuscation:** Replace real values with realistic but synthetic substitutes.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{direct} = S / I$ | Direct trust from successful $S$ out of total $I$ interactions | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | $T_{indirect} = \frac{1}{\vert K \vert} \sum w_k T_k$ | Indirect trust via $K$ recommenders | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | $T_{composite} = \alpha T_d + (1-\alpha) T_{ind}$ | Weighted composite trust | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | $T_{fresh}(t) = T \cdot e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$ | Time-decayed trust freshness | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |
| 5 | $R_j(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \beta_i T(i, j)$ | Reputation of node $j$ from $N$ peers | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | $\varepsilon$-Differential Privacy | Privacy budget; lower $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy | Real, $\varepsilon > 0$ |
| 7 | $P(M(D) = r) \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot P(M(D') = r)$ | Adjacent dataset probability bound (Differential Privacy) | Probability ratio |
| 8 | k-Anonymity Parameter $k$ | Each tuple indistinguishable from $k-1$ others | Integer, $k \ge 2$ |
| 9 | Entropy $H(X) = -\sum p(x) \log_2 p(x)$ | Information leakage metric (bits) | Bits |
| 10 | Mutual Information $I(X;Y)$ | Information shared between data $X$ and observable $Y$ | Bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **smart healthcare**, trust scores prevent injection of forged ECG data into hospital systems.
> - In **smart grids**, privacy-preserving aggregation prevents leakage of individual household consumption.
> - In **autonomous vehicles**, V2X (vehicle-to-everything) trust models reject malicious road-side unit messages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Trust Update Using Bayesian Inference

**Problem Setup:** Node $N_i$ wants to update its belief about node $N_j$ after observing $s$ successful and $f$ failed interactions out of a Beta prior.

**Step 1 — Define the prior distribution (Beta distribution):**
The prior belief about the trustworthiness of $N_j$ is modeled as:

$$
P(\theta) = \frac{\theta^{\alpha_0 - 1} (1 - \theta)^{\beta_0 - 1}}{B(\alpha_0, \beta_0)}
$$

where $\theta \in [0, 1]$ is the unknown trust probability, $\alpha_0, \beta_0$ are prior hyperparameters, and $B(\cdot)$ is the Beta function.

**Step 2 — Observe $s$ successes and $f$ failures:**
Likelihood of the observed data is Binomial:

$$
P(D \vert \theta) = \binom{s + f}{s} \theta^s (1 - \theta)^f
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to obtain the posterior:**
$$
P(\theta \vert D) = \frac{P(D \vert \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(D)}
$$

**Step 4 — Conjugate update (Beta is conjugate to Binomial):**
Multiplying the prior Beta and likelihood yields another Beta distribution:

$$
P(\theta \vert D) = \text{Beta}(\alpha_0 + s, \; \beta_0 + f)
$$

**Step 5 — Extract the expected trust value (posterior mean):**
$$
T_{expected} = E[\theta \vert D] = \frac{\alpha_0 + s}{\alpha_0 + \beta_0 + s + f}
$$

This is the **Bayesian trust update rule** widely used in IoT trust management literature.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Prior: $\alpha_0 = 2$, $\beta_0 = 2$ (neutral prior)
- Observed: $s = 8$ successes, $f = 2$ failures

**Compute Expected Trust:**

$$
T = \frac{\alpha_0 + s}{\alpha_0 + \beta_0 + s + f} = \frac{2 + 8}{2 + 2 + 8 + 2} = \frac{10}{14} = 0.714
$$

**Interpretation:** The posterior expected trust is **0.714**, indicating moderately high trust with a 71.4% confidence that the node will behave reliably.

**Apply Time Decay (assume $\lambda = 0.05$, $\Delta t = 10$ hours):**

$$
T_{fresh} = 0.714 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 10} = 0.714 \cdot e^{-0.5} = 0.714 \cdot 0.6065 \approx 0.433
$$

**Interpretation:** Without fresh interaction, trust decays to **0.433** — the node would need to re-validate.

### 3.3 Derivation: Differential Privacy Noise Calibration

**Goal:** Add Laplace noise to query output $f(D)$ so that individual record privacy is preserved.

**Step 1 — Compute Global Sensitivity $\Delta f$:**
$$
\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert
$$

where $D$ and $D'$ are adjacent datasets differing by one record.

**Step 2 — Laplace Mechanism:**
Add noise $\eta$ drawn from a Laplace distribution:

$$
\eta \sim \text{Lap}\left(0, \; \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Output the noisy result:**
$$
\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \eta
$$

**Step 4 — Privacy Guarantee (Adjacent Probability Bound):**
For any outcome $r$:

$$
P(\tilde{f}(D) = r) \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot P(\tilde{f}(D') = r)
$$

**Interpretation:** Lower $\varepsilon$ ⇒ stronger privacy, but greater noise ⇒ lower data utility. This is the fundamental **privacy-utility trade-off** in IoT analytics.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Privacy Strength | Computational Cost | Suitable for Constrained IoT? | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Privacy | High | Low–Medium | Yes (with edge servers) | High |
| Homomorphic Encryption | Very High | Very High | No (heavy) | Medium |
| Federated Learning | High | Medium | Yes (local training) | High |
| k-Anonymity | Medium | Low | Yes | Medium |
| ZKP | Very High | High | Limited (lightweight ZKP exists) | Low–Medium |
| Data Obfuscation | Low–Medium | Very Low | Yes (best for sensors) | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Trust & Privacy Threat Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph src["DATA SOURCES"]
        A1[Smart Wearable]
        A2[Home Sensor]
        A3[Industrial Sensor]
        A4[Vehicle ECU]
    end

    subgraph nwk["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        B1[WiFi]
        B2[Bluetooth]
        B3[Zigbee]
        B4[5G NB-IoT]
    end

    subgraph proc["EDGE / FOG / CLOUD"]
        C1[Edge Gateway]
        C2[Fog Aggregator]
        C3[Cloud Analytics]
    end

    subgraph threats["THREAT ACTORS"]
        T1[Eavesdropper]
        T2[Impersonator]
        T3[Data Aggregator]
        T4[Malicious Insider]
    end

    subgraph priv["PRIVACY CONTROLS"]
        P1[Encryption]
        P2[Anonymization]
        P3[Differential Privacy]
        P4[Access Control]
    end

    subgraph trust["TRUST MECHANISMS"]
        R1[Authentication]
        R2[Reputation Scoring]
        R3[Bayesian Trust]
        R4[Blockchain Ledger]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B4

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C3

    T1 -.->|intercepts| B1
    T2 -.->|spoofs| B3
    T3 -.->|mines| C3
    T4 -.->|leaks| C2

    C1 --> P1
    C2 --> P2
    C3 --> P3
    C1 --> P4

    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R2
    C3 --> R3
    C2 --> R4
```

### 4.2 Trust Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Direct Interaction] --> S2[Step 2: Compute T_direct]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Query Neighbors]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Collect Recommendations]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute T_indirect]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Apply Weighting Alpha]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: T_composite]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Apply Time Decay]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Final Trust Score T_final]
    S9 --> S10{Decision}
    S10 -->|T >= Threshold| S11[Allow Interaction]
    S10 -->|T less than Threshold| S12[Isolate or Warn]
```

### 4.3 IoT Privacy-by-Design Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Device Level] --> L2[Layer 2: Network Level]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Edge Level]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Cloud Level]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Application Level]

    L1 --> P1[Lightweight Encryption + Secure Boot]
    L2 --> P2[TLS / DTLS + VPN Tunneling]
    L3 --> P3[Local Anonymization + Federated Inference]
    L4 --> P4[Differential Privacy + Homomorphic Compute]
    L5 --> P5[User Consent Dashboard + Policy Engine]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Privacy in the context of IoT. List any two challenges of maintaining privacy in IoT systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Privacy in IoT refers to the right of users and devices to control the collection, storage, processing, and sharing of personal or sensitive data generated by interconnected IoT devices, ensuring that data is used only for authorized purposes.
**Two Challenges (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Heterogeneity:** Diverse devices use different communication protocols and lack a unified privacy framework, making consistent enforcement difficult.
2. **Resource Constraints:** Many IoT nodes are low-power and cannot run heavy cryptographic privacy mechanisms (e.g., homomorphic encryption).
3. *(Any two valid points acceptable)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*What is Trust in IoT? Differentiate between Direct Trust and Indirect Trust.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Trust Definition (1 Mark):** Trust in IoT is a measurable confidence level that an entity places in another entity's reliability, integrity, and authenticity during data exchange or service interaction.
**Direct Trust (1 Mark):** Trust derived from the entity's **own past direct interactions** with the target node. Computed as the ratio of successful interactions to total interactions.
**Indirect Trust (1 Mark):** Trust derived from **recommendations or reputation** provided by neighboring nodes that have previously interacted with the target.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Model]**

**(a)** Explain the major privacy concerns and threats in IoT environments. Discuss the privacy preservation techniques used to mitigate them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe a Trust Management framework for IoT. With a neat diagram, explain the components involved in computing composite trust. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Major Privacy Concerns in IoT (3.5 Marks):**

- **Data Collection Surveillance:** Always-on sensors (cameras, mics) collect user behavior 24/7.
- **Inferring Sensitive Information:** Combining innocuous data (e.g., fridge + watch) can reveal health conditions.
- **Location Tracking:** GPS and mobile sensors track user movement patterns.
- **Identity Exposure:** PII linkage across services enables user re-identification.
- **Secondary Use Without Consent:** Data collected for one purpose is repurposed for advertising or profiling.
- **Insecure Data Transmission:** Unencrypted IoT links expose data to eavesdropping.
- **Long-term Data Retention:** Cloud-stored IoT data creates a permanent surveillance record.

**2. Privacy Preservation Techniques (3.5 Marks):**

- **Data Anonymization / Pseudonymization:** Replace direct identifiers with pseudonyms.
- **Differential Privacy:** Inject calibrated Laplace/Gaussian noise to mask individual contribution.
- **Encryption (AES, RSA, ECC):** Protects data in transit and at rest.
- **Homomorphic Encryption:** Enables computation on encrypted data.
- **Federated Learning:** Models trained locally; only gradients shared, not raw data.
- **Access Control (RBAC / ABAC):** Restrict who can read which data.
- **Edge / Fog Computing:** Process data locally to avoid cloud exposure.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Trust Management Framework (3 Marks):**

A Trust Management framework in IoT evaluates the reliability of entities through a structured pipeline:

- **Trustor:** The entity seeking trust evaluation.
- **Trustee:** The entity being evaluated.
- **Trust Evidence Collector:** Gathers direct observations and indirect recommendations.
- **Trust Evaluator:** Applies mathematical models (Bayesian, fuzzy, D-S evidence theory) to compute trust score.
- **Trust Decision Engine:** Compares trust value with threshold and allows/denies interaction.
- **Trust Storage / Ledger:** Blockchain or distributed ledger for tamper-proof reputation records.

**2. Composite Trust Computation Diagram & Explanation (4 Marks):**

Composite Trust is computed by combining Direct Trust and Indirect Trust with a self-confidence factor $\alpha$:

$$
T_{composite} = \alpha \cdot T_{direct} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot T_{indirect}
$$

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Trustor Node N_i] --> B[Direct Interaction History]
    A --> C[Neighbour Recommendations]
    B --> D[Compute T_direct]
    C --> E[Compute T_indirect]
    D --> F[Apply Weight alpha]
    E --> G[Apply Weight 1-alpha]
    F --> H[Composite Trust T_c]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply Time Decay]
    I --> J[Final Trust Score T_final]
    J --> K{Threshold Check}
    K -->|Pass| L[Trust Established]
    K -->|Fail| M[Interaction Blocked]
```

**Explanation:** The trustor $N_i$ gathers both firsthand data and recommendations, weights them using $\alpha$, decays older data, and decides based on a configured threshold. Higher $\alpha$ implies the trustor relies more on its own experience; lower $\alpha$ means more reliance on community reputation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style Model]**

**(a)** What is the role of Differential Privacy in IoT? Derive the Laplace mechanism and explain the privacy-utility trade-off. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Bayesian Trust Update model with a numerical example. Show how time decay affects long-term trust values. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Role of Differential Privacy in IoT (2 Marks):**
Differential Privacy (DP) protects individual user/device records in statistical IoT datasets by adding mathematically calibrated noise. It ensures that the inclusion or removal of a single record does not significantly change query outputs, preventing re-identification of devices in smart meter or wearable analytics.

**2. Derivation of Laplace Mechanism (3 Marks):**

- **Step 1:** Define adjacent datasets $D$ and $D'$ differing in one record.
- **Step 2:** Compute global sensitivity $\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$.
- **Step 3:** Sample noise $\eta$ from Laplace distribution:
$$
\eta \sim \text{Lap}\left(0, \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$
- **Step 4:** Output $\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \eta$.
- **Step 5:** Privacy guarantee:
$$
P(\tilde{f}(D) = r) \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot P(\tilde{f}(D') = r)
$$

**3. Privacy-Utility Trade-off (2 Marks):**
Smaller $\varepsilon$ → stronger privacy but more noise → lower accuracy. Larger $\varepsilon$ → better accuracy but weaker privacy. IoT applications must choose $\varepsilon$ based on data sensitivity (e.g., $\varepsilon = 0.1$ for medical IoT, $\varepsilon = 1$ for general analytics).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Bayesian Trust Update Model (3.5 Marks):**

- Prior belief about node trustworthiness modeled as $\text{Beta}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)$.
- After observing $s$ successes and $f$ failures, posterior becomes:
$$
\text{Beta}(\alpha_0 + s, \; \beta_0 + f)
$$
- Expected trust value:
$$
T = \frac{\alpha_0 + s}{\alpha_0 + \beta_0 + s + f}
$$

**2. Numerical Example (2 Marks):**
- Prior: $\alpha_0 = 1, \beta_0 = 1$ (uniform).
- Observe: $s = 9, f = 1$.
- Trust:
$$
T = \frac{1 + 9}{1 + 1 + 9 + 1} = \frac{10}{12} = 0.833
$$

**3. Time Decay Effect (1.5 Marks):**
With $\lambda = 0.1$ and $\Delta t = 15$:
$$
T_{fresh} = 0.833 \cdot e^{-0.1 \times 15} = 0.833 \cdot e^{-1.5} = 0.833 \cdot 0.2231 \approx 0.186
$$

**Interpretation:** Trust has decayed to **0.186**, requiring fresh re-validation through new interactions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Privacy with Security:** Privacy is about *data control and consent*; security is about *protection from threats*. Examiners often deduct 1–2 marks if these are not distinguished.
> 2. **Skipping the formula derivation:** In trust computation questions, simply stating $T = \alpha T_d + (1-\alpha) T_{ind}$ without explaining the meaning of each term and the self-confidence factor loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring time decay:** Most students forget to apply the exponential decay $e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$, which is a frequent KTU follow-up.
> 4. **Wrong interpretation of Differential Privacy:** Writing "DP encrypts data" is incorrect. DP adds *statistical noise*, not encryption.
> 5. **Diagram missing arrows/labels:** Block diagrams in trust frameworks must label the trustor, trustee, evaluator, and decision engine.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Privacy in IoT** = control over data collection, storage, sharing, and purpose.
- **Trust in IoT** = confidence in identity, behavior, and reputation of an entity.
- **Five Pillars of IoT Privacy:** Minimization, Purpose Limitation, Consent, Anonymization, Transparency.
- **Three Dimensions of Trust:** Identity Trust, Behavioral Trust, Reputation Trust.
- **Direct Trust Formula:** $T_{direct} = S / I$ (successful/total interactions).
- **Indirect Trust Formula:** $T_{ind} = \frac{1}{\vert K \vert} \sum w_k T_k$ (recommender-weighted).
- **Composite Trust:** $T_c = \alpha T_d + (1 - \alpha) T_{ind}$ where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.
- **Time Decay:** $T_{fresh} = T \cdot e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$.
- **Bayesian Trust Update:** Posterior is $\text{Beta}(\alpha_0 + s, \beta_0 + f)$; expected trust is $\frac{\alpha_0 + s}{\alpha_0 + \beta_0 + s + f}$.
- **Differential Privacy:** Adds Laplace noise scaled to $\Delta f / \varepsilon$.
- **Privacy-Utility Trade-off:** Lower $\varepsilon$ → stronger privacy → lower accuracy.
- **k-Anonymity:** Each record indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others.
- **Federated Learning:** Preserves privacy by training locally, sharing only model parameters.
- **Homomorphic Encryption:** Computation on ciphertexts without decryption.
- **IoT Privacy Challenges:** Surveillance, inference attacks, location tracking, identity linking, insecure transmission.
- **IoT Trust Challenges:** Identity spoofing, Sybil attacks, on-off attacks, bad-mouthing attacks.
- **Common KTU Keywords:** Authentication, Authorization, PII, Pseudonymization, Reputation System, Blockchain-based Trust, Edge Privacy.
- **Expected 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Either (Privacy threats + techniques) OR (Trust framework + computation with formula).
- **Expected 3-Mark Question Pattern:** Definition + 2 distinguishing points OR a simple trust formula explanation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Device Level Energy Issues and IoT Reference Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Device Level Energy Issues and IoT Reference Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Device Level Energy Issues in IoT

### Formal Definition
> **Device Level Energy Issues** refer to the challenges, constraints, and optimization strategies associated with **power consumption, energy harvesting, and energy management at the individual IoT node/edge device level**. Since most IoT endpoints (sensors, actuators, wearables, smart nodes) are deployed in remote, inaccessible, or large-scale environments, energy efficiency is a first-class design constraint alongside functionality and connectivity.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the problem is often framed as: *"How do we keep a billion battery-powered IoT devices operational for years without human intervention?"*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Light-Bulb in a Sealed Box" Intuition

Imagine installing a tiny **smart light sensor** deep inside a forest to monitor wildlife. The box is sealed, the battery is non-replaceable, and the sensor must run for **5 years** on a single coin cell. The sensor is "alive" only for 3 milliseconds every 10 minutes to take a reading and transmit it. This is exactly how IoT devices behave — they spend ~99.99% of their lifetime in **deep sleep** and wake up briefly to sense, compute, and communicate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In IoT, energy is not just a hardware concern — it is a **system-wide design philosophy** that influences the choice of communication protocol, OS, data rate, duty cycle, and even the cryptographic algorithm used.

### Why Energy is the #1 Bottleneck in IoT

| IoT Characteristic | Energy Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Massive scale** (billions of nodes) | Manual battery replacement is impossible |
| **Harsh/remote deployment** | No grid power available |
| **Small form factor** | Tiny battery capacity (often < 500 mAh) |
| **Always-on sensing** | Continuous power drain |
| **Wireless transmission** | Radio is the most power-hungry subsystem |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):** Students must clearly distinguish between *energy harvesting*, *energy storage*, and *energy management*, and map them to the three layers of an IoT node (sensing, processing, communication).

### Energy Sources for IoT Devices (Classification)

IoT nodes draw energy from one or a hybrid of the following sources:

1. **Battery (Primary/Secondary Cells)** — e.g., Li-ion, Li-SOCl₂, alkaline AA
2. **Energy Harvesting (Ambient Sources)** — Solar, Thermal (TEG), Vibration/Piezo, RF, Wind
3. **Grid / Mains Power** — only for fixed indoor gateways
4. **Capacitor / Supercapacitor** — for ultra-low duty-cycle nodes
5. **Wireless Power Transfer (WPT)** — inductive/RF coupling

### Formal Definition — IoT Reference Model

> **IoT Reference Model** is a **standardized, layered architectural blueprint** that abstracts the end-to-end functionality of an IoT system into discrete, interoperable layers. The most widely accepted KTU-referenced model is the **IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-Layer Reference Model**, standardized by Cisco and adopted in the KTU 2024 OEITT723 syllabus.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Service" Intuition

Think of sending a letter from a village to a friend in another country:
- **Handwritten note** (Physical Device) → put in an **envelope** (Connectivity) → goes to a **local post office** (Edge Computing) → forwarded to a **sorting hub** (Data Accumulation) → translated/standardized (Data Abstraction) → delivered to the **recipient's app** (Application Layer) → triggers a **"Happy Birthday" reply** (Business/Process).

Each "layer" adds value but consumes some resource (time, energy, money). The **IoT Reference Model** does the same for data generated by sensors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an IoT Node — Where Energy is Spent

An IoT device (e.g., a temperature sensor node) consists of **three energy-consuming subsystems**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to compare their power profiles.

### Subsystem 1: Sensing Subsystem
- Converts physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, motion) into electrical signals.
- Includes: Sensor element, **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)**, signal conditioning.
- Typical power: **µW to mW range**.
- Optimization: Use **digital sensors** (e.g., BME280) that integrate ADC internally.

### Subsystem 2: Processing / Computation Subsystem
- The "brain" — usually a **microcontroller (MCU)**, sometimes an SoC.
- Examples: ARM Cortex-M0/M4 (e.g., STM32, CC2538), ESP32, MSP430.
- Power states: **Active, Idle, Sleep, Deep Sleep, Hibernate**.
- Typical power (active): **mW range**; (deep sleep): **µW range**.

### Subsystem 3: Communication / Radio Subsystem
- The **most power-hungry** subsystem by 1–2 orders of magnitude.
- Examples: Wi-Fi (ESP8266), BLE (nRF52), LoRa (SX1276), Zigbee.
- Typical power (TX): **50–500 mW**; (RX): **20–50 mW**; (sleep): **< 1 µW**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Trick:** When asked "Which subsystem consumes the most power?", the correct answer is **Radio (Communication)**, NOT the sensor or the processor. Many students lose marks by picking the wrong subsystem.

## 2.2 Battery Life Estimation — The Core KTU Formula

For a KTU numerical, you must master the **battery lifetime equation**:

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}$$

Where:
- $T_{life}$ = battery lifetime in **hours**
- $C_{battery}$ = battery capacity in **mAh**
- $I_{avg}$ = average current draw in **mA**

### The Duty-Cycle Refined Formula

Because IoT nodes spend most of their time in sleep, the **average current** is:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{(I_{active} \cdot t_{active}) + (I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep})}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}$$

Where $t_{active}$ is the active duration per cycle and $t_{sleep}$ is the sleep duration per cycle. The **duty cycle (D)** is:

$$D = \frac{t_{active}}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}} \times 100\%$$

So we can rewrite:

$$I_{avg} \approx (I_{active} \cdot D) + (I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D))$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery Lifetime | $T_{life} = C_{battery} / I_{avg}$ | Hours of operation |
| 2 | Duty Cycle | $D = t_{on} / (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Fraction of time node is active |
| 3 | Average Current | $I_{avg} \approx I_{active} \cdot D + I_{sleep} \cdot (1-D)$ | Time-averaged current draw |
| 4 | Energy of a Pulse | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Joules consumed per activity |
| 5 | Solar Harvested Power | $P_{harv} = \eta \cdot A \cdot G$ | Watts from a solar cell |
| 6 | Link Budget (dB) | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{path}$ | RF received power |
| 7 | Path Loss (FSPL) | $L_{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space loss in dB (d in km, f in MHz) |
| 8 | Energy per Bit | $E_b = P_{TX} \cdot t_{bit}$ | Energy efficiency metric |
| 9 | Energy-Neutral Operation | $E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed}$ | Self-sustaining condition |
| 10 | Layer Count (IoTWF Model) | **7 layers** | KTU-defined reference model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder for KTU Valuation:** Always state units (mW, mAh, µJ, dBm) in your final answer. Examiners explicitly allocate 0.5 marks for correct unit notation in numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The energy-aware design principles derived above directly enable:
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors running 5+ years on a single Li-SOCl₂ cell.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Vibration sensors on bridges powered by piezo harvesters.
- **Wearables & Implantables:** Pacemakers, fitness bands with sub-µW sleep currents.
- **Smart Logistics:** Asset trackers using BLE beacons with 1-year coin-cell life.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Wireless vibration sensors on rotating machinery harvesting energy from the rotation itself.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Battery Lifetime Problem

> **[KTU-Style Numerical — 7 Marks Model]** A wireless temperature sensor node uses a **2400 mAh** battery. During each 10-minute cycle, the node is **active for 3 seconds** consuming **45 mA**, and in deep sleep for the remaining time consuming **8 µA**.
> **(a)** Calculate the duty cycle.
> **(b)** Calculate the average current drawn.
> **(c)** Estimate the battery lifetime in years.

### Part (a): Duty Cycle Calculation

$$D = \frac{t_{active}}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}$$

Substituting the given values. The total cycle time is **10 minutes = 600 seconds**, and the active time is **3 seconds**:

$$D = \frac{3}{3 + 597} = \frac{3}{600} = 0.005 = 0.5\%$$

So the node is active only **0.5%** of the time — a hallmark of energy-efficient IoT design.

### Part (b): Average Current Calculation

We apply the duty-cycle weighted average current formula. The active current is 45 mA and the sleep current is 8 µA = 0.008 mA:

$$I_{avg} = (I_{active} \cdot D) + (I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D))$$

Plugging in the numerical values:

$$I_{avg} = (45 \cdot 0.005) + (0.008 \cdot 0.995)$$

$$I_{avg} = 0.225 + 0.00796 = 0.23296 \text{ mA}$$

### Part (c): Battery Lifetime in Years

We apply the fundamental battery life equation. The battery capacity is 2400 mAh and the average current is 0.23296 mA:

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}$$

$$T_{life} = \frac{2400}{0.23296} = 10302 \text{ hours}$$

Converting hours into years by dividing by the number of hours in one year (24 × 365 = 8760):

$$T_{life} = \frac{10302}{8760} = 1.176 \text{ years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** The moment you convert µA to mA (or vice versa), write down the conversion explicitly: *"1 µA = 10⁻³ mA"*. Examiners reward unit discipline. Also, **always show the final unit** (years/hours/days) in the answer box.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Power Profile of Common IoT Radios

| Radio Technology | TX Current | RX Current | Sleep Current | Range | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (ESP8266)** | 170 mA | 56 mA | 10 µA | ~50 m | High-bandwidth, indoor |
| **BLE (nRF52832)** | 7.5 mA | 7.5 mA | 0.5 µA | ~30 m | Wearables, beacons |
| **LoRa (SX1276)** | 100 mA | 12 mA | 0.2 µA | ~10 km | Long-range outdoor |
| **Zigbee (CC2538)** | 34 mA | 20 mA | 1 µA | ~100 m | Smart home mesh |
| **NB-IoT (BC95)** | 220 mA (peak) | 40 mA | 5 µA | ~15 km | Cellular IoT |
| **Sigfox** | 45 mA | 12 mA | 0.5 µA | ~50 km | Ultra-narrowband |

**Key Observation:** Notice the **1000× difference** between TX and sleep currents. This is why **duty cycling** is the single most effective energy-saving technique in IoT.

## 3.3 The IoT Reference Model — Layer-by-Layer Analytical Breakdown

The **IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-Layer Reference Model** is the canonical architecture tested in KTU 2024. Each layer has well-defined responsibilities:

### Layer 1: Physical Devices & Controllers
- The "Things" themselves — sensors, actuators, RFID tags, smart meters.
- Function: Sense the physical world or actuate on it.
- Energy Concern: Sensor selection, sampling rate, energy harvesting.

### Layer 2: Connectivity
- Communication between devices and the network.
- Function: Reliable, timely, secure data transport.
- Protocols: Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, NB-IoT.
- Energy Concern: Radio duty cycling, MAC protocols, transmit power.

### Layer 3: Edge / Fog Computing
- Local processing at the gateway or edge node.
- Function: Filter, aggregate, preprocess data to reduce cloud load.
- Examples: Raspberry Pi gateway, edge AI inferencing.
- Energy Concern: Trade-off between local compute vs. raw data transmission.

### Layer 4: Data Accumulation
- The transition from network domain to IT/compute domain.
- Function: High-rate data ingestion, buffering, and storage.
- Technologies: MQTT brokers, Kafka, time-series databases.
- Energy Concern: Storage media selection, data compression.

### Layer 5: Data Abstraction
- Normalization, structuring, and semantic annotation of data.
- Function: Convert raw streams into queryable, interoperable formats.
- Technologies: JSON, Avro, Parquet, OPC-UA, CoAP.
- Energy Concern: In-network data fusion to reduce payload size.

### Layer 6: Application
- The software layer delivering value to the end user.
- Function: Dashboards, control logic, analytics, alerting.
- Examples: Smart-home apps, industrial SCADA, fleet management.
- Energy Concern: Indirect — query frequency, push vs. pull design.

### Layer 7: Collaboration & Processes
- The "business" layer — making IoT data actionable.
- Function: Cross-organizational workflows, monetization, KPIs.
- Example: A smart grid tying consumption data to dynamic pricing.
- Energy Concern: Process orchestration efficiency, SLA-driven scaling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"Please Carry Eggs Don't Add Apples Carefully"* → **P**hysical, **C**onnectivity, **E**dge, **D**ata Accumulation, **A**bstraction, **A**pplication, **C**ollaboration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoTWF 7-Layer Reference Model (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["LAYER 7 COLLABORATION AND PROCESSES"]:::top
    L6["LAYER 6 APPLICATION LAYER DASHBOARDS ANALYTICS"]:::mid
    L5["LAYER 5 DATA ABSTRACTION NORMALIZATION SEMANTICS"]:::mid
    L4["LAYER 4 DATA ACCUMULATION INGESTION STORAGE"]:::mid
    L3["LAYER 3 EDGE FOG COMPUTING LOCAL PROCESSING"]:::mid
    L2["LAYER 2 CONNECTIVITY WIFI BLE LORA ZIGBEE"]:::mid
    L1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL DEVICES SENSORS ACTUATORS RFID"]:::bot

    L1 ==> L2
    L2 ==> L3
    L3 ==> L4
    L4 ==> L5
    L5 ==> L6
    L6 ==> L7

    classDef top fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#055,color:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef bot fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#a80,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 IoT Node — Energy Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H["ENERGY SOURCE LAYER"]
        HB["Battery Coin Cell Li-ion"]:::src
        HV["Energy Harvester Solar Piezo RF"]:::src
        HC["Capacitor Supercapacitor"]:::src
    end

    subgraph P["POWER MANAGEMENT UNIT PMU"]
        REG["Voltage Regulator LDO Buck"]:::pmu
        MGR["Energy Management Controller"]:::pmu
    end

    subgraph D["DEVICE SUBSYSTEMS"]
        SE["Sensing Unit Sensor + ADC"]:::dev
        CP["Processing Unit MCU SoC"]:::dev
        RA["Radio Unit Wi-Fi BLE LoRa"]:::dev
    end

    HB --> REG
    HV --> REG
    HC --> REG
    REG --> MGR
    MGR --> SE
    MGR --> CP
    MGR --> RA

    classDef src fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    classDef pmu fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Duty-Cycle Energy State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DeepSleep
    DeepSleep --> WakeUp : Timer interrupt or external event
    WakeUp --> Sensing : MCU active, ADC sampling
    Sensing --> Processing : Data digitized, local compute
    Processing --> Transmit : Radio TX, packet sent
    Transmit --> ReceiveAck : Wait for ACK window
    ReceiveAck --> DeepSleep : ACK received or timeout
    DeepSleep --> [*] : Battery depleted
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** List **any three** energy sources used for powering IoT devices. Give one example device for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Primary Battery** — Example: Li-SOCl₂ coin cell used in smart water meters. (1 mark)
2. **Solar Energy Harvesting** — Example: Outdoor air quality sensor node with a small photovoltaic panel. (1 mark)
3. **Piezoelectric / Vibration Harvesting** — Example: Self-powered bridge vibration sensor using a PZT harvester. (1 mark)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Define the term **"Duty Cycle"** in the context of IoT energy management. Why is it a critical parameter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Duty cycle (D) is the fraction of total time an IoT node is in the *active state* (sensing, processing, transmitting) versus the *sleep state*. Mathematically, $D = t_{active} / (t_{active} + t_{sleep})$.
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** Because the active current is typically **1000× higher** than the sleep current, even a small reduction in D dramatically reduces the average current draw and extends battery life. For example, reducing D from 5% to 0.5% can increase lifetime by ~10×.

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**
> **Q3. (a)** Explain the major **device-level energy issues** in IoT systems. Discuss the role of **duty cycling** and **energy harvesting** as mitigation strategies with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a)

1. **Introduction to Device-Level Energy Issues (2 marks):**
   IoT devices are typically deployed in remote, inaccessible, or large-scale scenarios where battery replacement is impractical or impossible. The major energy issues are:
   - **Limited battery capacity** due to small form factor.
   - **High communication overhead** — radio transmission dominates the energy budget.
   - **Always-on sensing requirements** that prevent aggressive sleep.
   - **Harsh environmental conditions** affecting battery performance (temperature, humidity).
   - **Lack of grid power** in field deployments.

2. **Duty Cycling as a Mitigation Strategy (2.5 marks):**
   Duty cycling keeps the node in low-power sleep mode for the majority of its lifetime. For example, a soil moisture sensor that wakes up every 15 minutes for 2 seconds has a duty cycle of 0.22%. The energy savings are:
   - Active: 50 mA × 2 s = 100 mA·s
   - Sleep: 0.01 mA × 898 s = 8.98 mA·s
   - This 11× reduction in consumed energy per cycle translates directly to multi-year battery life.

3. **Energy Harvesting as a Mitigation Strategy (2.5 marks):**
   Energy harvesting converts ambient energy into electrical energy. Examples:
   - **Solar (Photovoltaic):** A 5 cm² solar cell at 1000 lux provides ~0.5 mW — sufficient for BLE beacon nodes.
   - **Vibration (Piezoelectric):** Industrial motors vibrating at 50 Hz yield 10–100 µW, enough for wireless vibration sensors.
   - **RF Harvesting:** Ambient Wi-Fi/RF signals harvested to trickle-charge a supercapacitor.
   - The goal is to achieve **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO)**, where harvested energy ≥ consumed energy.

### **(b)** Describe the **IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-Layer Reference Model** with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of **Layers 1, 2, and 3** in detail. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (b)

1. **Introduction to IoTWF Reference Model (1 mark):**
   The IoTWF 7-Layer Reference Model, introduced by Cisco, is a vendor-neutral architectural framework that decomposes an IoT system into seven discrete, interoperable layers, from physical sensing to business processes.

2. **Block Diagram (1 mark):**
   *(Draw the 7-layer stack with arrows showing data flow from Layer 1 to Layer 7, exactly as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

3. **Layer 1 — Physical Devices & Controllers (1.5 marks):**
   This is the "Things" layer containing physical sensors (temperature, pressure, motion), actuators (valves, motors), and RFID/NFC tags. Their function is to interface with the physical world and convert phenomena into electrical signals.

4. **Layer 2 — Connectivity (1.5 marks):**
   This layer handles reliable, secure communication between devices and the network. It encompasses radio technologies (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, NB-IoT) and protocols (MQTT, CoAP, 6LoWPAN, RPL). Key concerns are range, bandwidth, and energy efficiency.

5. **Layer 3 — Edge / Fog Computing (2 marks):**
   This layer performs local data processing at the edge of the network, near the data source. Functions include data filtering, aggregation, local alerting, and AI inferencing. Examples include a Raspberry Pi gateway running TensorFlow Lite for object detection, or an industrial PLC preprocessing vibration data before cloud upload. The benefits are reduced latency, lower bandwidth consumption, and improved energy efficiency (less raw data to transmit).

---

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Alternative Question for the Same 14 Marks**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**
> **Q4. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal architecture of an IoT node** and identify the **most power-hungry subsystem**. Justify your answer with quantitative reasoning. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a)

1. **Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**
   *(Draw the block diagram showing Sensing, Processing, Communication, and Power Management subsystems, as in Section 4.2.)*

2. **Subsystem Description (3 marks):**
   - **Sensing Subsystem:** Sensors + ADC, typically consuming µW–mW.
   - **Processing Subsystem:** MCU/SoC with active and sleep modes, consuming mW (active) to µW (sleep).
   - **Communication Subsystem:** Radio transceiver, consuming 50–500 mW during TX.
   - **Power Management Unit:** Voltage regulators, energy harvesters, charge controllers.

3. **Most Power-Hungry Subsystem (2 marks):**
   The **Communication (Radio) Subsystem** is the most power-hungry. For example, a LoRa SX1276 module consumes ~100 mA in TX (3.3 V) = 330 mW, while a Cortex-M0 MCU consumes only ~10 mW active. Therefore, **minimizing radio on-time** is the single most effective energy optimization in IoT.

### **(b)** Compare the **3-Layer IoT Model** with the **5-Layer IoT Model**. State the additional capabilities introduced by the extra layers. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (b)

1. **3-Layer Model (2 marks):**
   The simplest model proposed in early IoT literature has three layers:
   - **Perception Layer** (sensors, actuators)
   - **Network Layer** (communication, routing)
   - **Application Layer** (user-facing services)
   This model is intuitive but **lacks support for data processing, storage, and business logic**.

2. **5-Layer Model (2.5 marks):**
   An enhanced model splits the middle and top layers:
   - **Perception Layer** — physical sensors
   - **Network/Transport Layer** — communication
   - **Middleware/Processing Layer** — data storage, analytics, decision-making *(new capability)*
   - **Application Layer** — service delivery
   - **Business Layer** — process orchestration, monetization *(new capability)*

3. **Comparison & Additional Capabilities (2.5 marks):**
   | Feature | 3-Layer | 5-Layer |
   |---|---|---|
   | Sensing & Actuation | ✔ | ✔ |
   | Communication | ✔ | ✔ |
   | In-network processing | ✘ | ✔ (Middleware) |
   | Business process modeling | ✘ | ✔ (Business) |
   | Suitable for | Academic/introductory use | Industrial/commercial IoT |
   
   The 5-Layer model adds **computational intelligence** (Middleware) and **business context** (Business Layer), making it suitable for production-grade IoT systems requiring analytics, SLAs, and ROI tracking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "5-Layer" with "IoTWF 7-Layer" model.** They are DIFFERENT. The 5-layer model is the academic decomposition (Perception/Network/Middleware/Application/Business). The 7-layer model is the IoTWF industrial model. Do not mix them in answers.
> 2. **Writing "sensor" as the most power-hungry component.** This is incorrect. The **radio** is. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this common error.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert µA ↔ mA in numericals.** Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting the duty cycle formula** when asked about energy-saving techniques. Just saying "use sleep mode" earns only partial credit; showing $D = t_{on}/(t_{on}+t_{off})$ earns full marks.
> 5. **Listing layers without explaining functions.** KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates 1 mark per layer description — don't just list names.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Energy is the #1 design constraint** in IoT, more critical than bandwidth or compute.
- ✅ The **Radio/Communication subsystem** is the most power-hungry part of an IoT node.
- ✅ **Duty cycling** (D) is the most effective energy-saving technique — typical IoT D = 0.1% to 1%.
- ✅ **Battery lifetime formula:** $T_{life} = C_{battery} / I_{avg}$, with $I_{avg} \approx I_{active} \cdot D + I_{sleep} \cdot (1-D)$.
- ✅ **Energy harvesting** sources: Solar, Piezo (vibration), Thermal (TEG), RF, Wind.
- ✅ **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO):** Harvested energy ≥ Consumed energy.
- ✅ **IoTWF 7-Layer Model:** Physical → Connectivity → Edge → Data Accumulation → Data Abstraction → Application → Collaboration.
- ✅ **5-Layer Academic Model:** Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business.
- ✅ **Layer 1 (Physical)** = "Things" (sensors, actuators, RFID). **Layer 2 (Connectivity)** = radio protocols. **Layer 3 (Edge)** = local processing at gateway.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"Please Carry Eggs Don't Add Apples Carefully"* for the 7 layers.
- ✅ Always state **units** (mW, mAh, µA, dBm) explicitly in numericals to earn full marks.
- ✅ Key exam keywords: **duty cycle, energy harvesting, edge computing, middleware, ENO, PMU, FSPL**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Architecture and Core IoT Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Architecture and Core IoT Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is IoT Architecture?

**Internet of Things (IoT) Architecture** is a structured, layered reference framework that defines how heterogeneous physical devices (sensors, actuators, gateways, edge nodes, and cloud servers) interact, communicate, and cooperate to deliver intelligent services across a unified network infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *IoT Architecture is a system of interrelated building blocks — sensing, communication, computation, actuation, and management — orchestrated through layered protocols to enable seamless data flow from the physical world to the cloud and back.*

The architecture is **not a single protocol**; it is a **blueprint of functions** that allows any IoT deployment (smart home, industrial IoT, smart city) to scale and interoperate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Nervous System"

Think of an IoT system like the **human nervous system**:

| Human Body Part | IoT Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Skin / Eyes / Ears (Senses) | **Sensors** | Capture environmental data |
| Spinal Cord / Nerves | **Network / Gateways** | Transmit signals to the brain |
| Brain | **Cloud / Edge Server** | Process & decide |
| Hands / Muscles | **Actuators** | Perform physical actions |
| Memory | **Database / Storage** | Retain historical data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the **"4-Layer IoT Reference Model"** and the **"IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-Layer Model"** as the primary architectural frameworks. Students must know **layer names + their core functions**.

## 1.3 What are "Core IoT Functions"?

**Core IoT Functions** are the **fundamental building-block capabilities** that every IoT system — regardless of scale — must possess to be considered a valid IoT deployment. These were standardized by the **IoT World Forum Reference Model (IoTWF)** and adopted across academic and industry curricula.

The **five core IoT functions** are:

1. **Sensing & Identification** — gathering raw data
2. **Communication** — transporting data
3. **Computation & Analytics** — processing and reasoning
4. **Actuation** — influencing the physical world
5. **Service & Application** — delivering end-user value

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Layer Functions** (running across all layers) include **Security, Privacy, Trust, Identity, and Management**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Values

| Parameter | Standard Reference Value |
|---|---|
| Number of layers in IoTWF Model | **7** |
| Number of layers in simplified KTU Model | **4** |
| Core IoT Functions (Standard) | **5** |
| Cross-cutting concerns | **3** (Security, Management, Privacy) |
| Typical data latency (edge processing) | **< 10 ms** |
| Typical data latency (cloud processing) | **100 ms – 2 s** |
| Common sensing ranges | **0–200 m** (ultrasonic), **0.1–40 m** (LiDAR) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4-Layer IoT Reference Architecture (KTU Standard)

The simplified reference model used in KTU 2024 evaluation groups the IoT ecosystem into **four logical layers**, each with a clearly defined role.

### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer (Device Layer)

- **What it does:** This is the **physical interface** between the real world and the digital world.
- **Components:** Sensors (temperature, humidity, pressure, accelerometer, PIR, gas), RFID tags, GPS modules, cameras, smart meters, and wearable biosensors.
- **Core job:** Convert physical phenomena → **electrical signals** → **digital data**.
- **Output:** Raw, unprocessed data in engineering units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Term:** *"Thing"* in IoT refers to the **physical device + sensor + actuator + communication module** as a single identifiable entity.

### Layer 2 — Network / Connectivity Layer (Transport Layer)

- **What it does:** Reliably transport data from the perception layer to the processing layer and vice-versa.
- **Technologies:** Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Bluetooth / BLE, ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4), LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 5G, MQTT brokers, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS.
- **Core job:** Address assignment, routing, data encapsulation, QoS enforcement, and protocol translation.

### Layer 3 — Processing / Edge-Cloud Layer (Middleware Layer)

- **What it does:** The "**brain**" of the IoT system. Performs storage, analytics, decision-making, and event correlation.
- **Sub-divisions:**
  - **Edge Computing** — processing done at or near the device (low latency).
  - **Fog Computing** — intermediate processing between edge and cloud.
  - **Cloud Computing** — large-scale, centralized analytics (high storage, ML training).
- **Core job:** Convert raw data → **information** → **actionable knowledge**.

### Layer 4 — Application Layer (Business Layer)

- **What it does:** Deliver **user-facing services**.
- **Examples:** Smart home dashboards, industrial SCADA, fleet management portals, remote health monitoring apps.
- **Core job:** Present insights to humans or trigger automated actuation.

## 2.2 The IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-Layer Reference Model

This is a **deeper, more granular** decomposition expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

| Layer # | Layer Name | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical Devices & Controllers | Raw sensing/actuation | Sensors, motors |
| 2 | Connectivity | Wired/wireless transport | Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa |
| 3 | Edge / Fog Computing | Local processing | Gateway, Raspberry Pi |
| 4 | Data Accumulation | Storage, normalization | InfluxDB, MongoDB |
| 5 | Data Abstraction | Aggregation, virtualization | REST APIs |
| 6 | Application | Business logic, analytics | ML, dashboards |
| 7 | Collaboration & Processes | End-user workflow | ERP integration |

> [!NOTE]
> The **first 3 layers** are often grouped as **"Network Edge"**, the **next 3 as "Data Center / Cloud"**, and the **last as "User / Business"**.

## 2.3 The Five Core IoT Functions (Deep Dive)

### Function 1: Sensing & Identification
- Converts physical phenomena to electrical signals.
- Uses **transducers**: devices that convert one form of energy → another.
- Identification via **RFID, NFC, QR codes, IP/MAC addresses, IPv6 unique IDs**.

### Function 2: Communication
- **Short-range:** BLE, ZigBee, NFC.
- **Long-range:** LoRaWAN, SigFox, NB-IoT, 5G.
- **IP-based:** TCP/IP, UDP, MQTT, CoAP, AMQP.

### Function 3: Computation & Analytics
- **Edge analytics:** Local threshold checking, filtering.
- **Cloud analytics:** Big Data, Hadoop, Spark, TensorFlow.
- Real-time decision making via **stream processing**.

### Function 4: Actuation
- Reverses the sensing process: **digital signal → physical action**.
- Examples: Smart thermostats turning on AC, automatic door openers, irrigation valves.

### Function 5: Service Management & Application
- Provides **APIs, dashboards, notifications, automation rules**.
- Includes SLA management, fault tolerance, and business KPIs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (IoT Computation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For IoT module 1, "formulas" center on **data rates, transmission delays, and energy consumption** of edge/transport layers.

| Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Data Rate} = \dfrac{N_b}{T_s}$ | $N_b$ = bits per sample, $T_s$ = sampling period (s) | Sensor bandwidth sizing |
| $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{P_{\text{payload}}}{T_{\text{tx}}}$ | $P_{\text{payload}}$ = payload size (bits), $T_{\text{tx}}$ = transmission time (s) | Network capacity planning |
| $\text{Total Delay} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | $T_{\text{prop}}$ = propagation, $T_{\text{tx}}$ = transmit, $T_{\text{proc}}$ = processing, $T_{\text{queue}}$ = queuing | End-to-end latency estimation |
| $T_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = bit rate (bps) | Transmission time |
| $E_{\text{total}} = N \cdot (E_{\text{tx}} + E_{\text{rx}} + E_{\text{proc}} + E_{\text{sense}})$ | $E_{\text{tx}}$ = transmit energy, etc. | Battery life estimation |
| $\text{Power} = V \cdot I$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current | Energy budgeting |
| $\text{IPv6 Address Space} = 2^{128}$ | Total unique IoT devices | Scalability metric |
| $\text{QoS}_{\text{delay}} = \dfrac{\text{Required Latency}}{\text{Achieved Latency}}$ | Ratio-based metric | SLA verification |

> **Note:** All formulas use \vert or \mid to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in the rendered notes.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (IIoT):** Predicts equipment failure via vibration + temperature sensors feeding edge analytics.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil moisture + weather sensors → LoRaWAN → cloud ML → automated irrigation.
- **Healthcare (IoMT):** Wearable ECG → BLE → smartphone → cloud cardiologist dashboard.
- **Smart Cities:** Air quality sensors + traffic cameras → 5G → municipal command center.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Layer vs Core Function Mapping

This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions. Students are often asked to *"map core functions onto architectural layers."*

| Core Function ↓ \ Layer → | Perception | Network | Processing | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensing & Identification** | ★★★ (Primary) | ★ | ★ | ★ |
| **Communication** | ★ | ★★★ (Primary) | ★★ | ★ |
| **Computation & Analytics** | ★ | ★ | ★★★ (Primary) | ★★ |
| **Actuation** | ★★★ (Primary) | ★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Service Management** | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★ (Primary) |

> ★ = minor role | ★★ = supporting role | ★★★ = primary role

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: End-to-End IoT Data Latency

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
> *A temperature sensor samples data at 1 sample/second. Each sample is 16 bits. The data is transmitted over a LoRaWAN link at 50 kbps to a gateway, which forwards it via Wi-Fi (10 Mbps) to a cloud server. The edge gateway adds a 32-byte header. Cloud processing takes 200 ms. Find the end-to-end delay.*

### Step 1: Sample Size and Rate
$$N_b = 16 \text{ bits/sample}$$
$$f_s = 1 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2: Effective Payload
$$L = N_b + H_{\text{header}} = 16 \text{ bits} + (32 \times 8) \text{ bits}$$

Compute the header size first:
$$32 \text{ bytes} = 32 \times 8 = 256 \text{ bits}$$

Now add:
$$L = 16 + 256 = 272 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3: Transmission Delay on LoRaWAN
$$T_{\text{tx,LoRa}} = \dfrac{L}{R_{\text{LoRa}}} = \dfrac{272 \text{ bits}}{50{,}000 \text{ bps}}$$

$$T_{\text{tx,LoRa}} = 0.00544 \text{ s} = 5.44 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4: Transmission Delay on Wi-Fi
$$T_{\text{tx,WiFi}} = \dfrac{L}{R_{\text{WiFi}}} = \dfrac{272 \text{ bits}}{10{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps}}$$

$$T_{\text{tx,WiFi}} = 2.72 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.0272 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5: Propagation Delay (assume distance 5 km, speed of signal $\approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s)
$$T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{c} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 1.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.0167 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 6: Cloud Processing Delay
$$T_{\text{proc}} = 200 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 7: Total End-to-End Delay
$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{tx,LoRa}} + T_{\text{tx,WiFi}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc}}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 5.44 + 0.0272 + 0.0167 + 200$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 205.48 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 8: Interpretation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **dominant delay component is cloud processing (200 ms)**, accounting for **~97.3%** of total delay. This proves that **edge computing is essential** for time-critical IoT (e.g., autonomous vehicles, robotic surgery).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Energy Estimation for a Battery-Powered Sensor Node

**Given:** Sensor node transmits 1000 packets/day. Each packet = 256 bits. Transmit current = 50 mA, Receive current = 20 mA, Sleep current = 0.01 mA, Voltage = 3.3 V, Bit rate = 50 kbps.

### Step 1: Transmit Time per Packet
$$T_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{256}{50{,}000} = 0.00512 \text{ s}$$

### Step 2: Energy per Transmission
$$E_{\text{tx}} = V \times I_{\text{tx}} \times T_{\text{tx}} = 3.3 \times 0.050 \times 0.00512$$

$$E_{\text{tx}} = 8.448 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.8448 \text{ mJ}$$

### Step 3: Daily Transmission Energy
$$E_{\text{tx,day}} = 1000 \times 0.8448 = 844.8 \text{ mJ}$$

### Step 4: Daily Sleep Energy (assuming 24 hours sleep minus 5.12 s active)
$$T_{\text{sleep}} = 86{,}400 - 5.12 = 86{,}394.88 \text{ s}$$

$$E_{\text{sleep,day}} = 3.3 \times 0.00001 \times 86{,}394.88$$

$$E_{\text{sleep,day}} = 2.85 \text{ J}$$

### Step 5: Total Daily Energy
$$E_{\text{total,day}} = 0.8448 \text{ mJ} + 2.85 \text{ J} \approx 2.851 \text{ J}$$

### Step 6: Battery Life Estimation (assume 2000 mAh, 3.3 V battery)
$$E_{\text{battery}} = 3.3 \times 2000 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 23{,}760 \text{ J}$$

$$\text{Days} = \dfrac{23{,}760}{2.851} \approx 8{,}334 \text{ days} \approx 22.8 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Sleep current dominates, demonstrating why IoT protocols aggressively use **duty cycling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Simplified 4-Layer IoT Architecture (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["LAYER 4 APPLICATION"]
        appA["Smart Home Dashboard"]
        appB["Industrial SCADA"]
        appC["Health Monitoring Portal"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["LAYER 3 PROCESSING EDGE CLOUD"]
        edge1["Edge Gateway"]
        fog1["Fog Node"]
        cloud1["Cloud Server ML Engine"]
    end

    subgraph NET["LAYER 2 NETWORK CONNECTIVITY"]
        proto1["Wi Fi"]
        proto2["BLE ZigBee"]
        proto3["LoRaWAN NB IoT 5G"]
    end

    subgraph PER["LAYER 1 PERCEPTION SENSING"]
        sens1["Temperature Sensor"]
        sens2["Humidity Sensor"]

    sens1 --> proto1
    sens2 --> proto2
    sens3 --> proto3

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — IoT World Forum 7-Layer Model


## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Core IoT Functions Flow

```mermaid

    ENV["Physical Environment"]
    SENS["SENSING IDENTIFICATION"]
    COMM["COMMUNICATION"]

    ENV --> SENS
    SENS --> COMM

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Data Path: Sensor to Cloud

```mermaid

    A["Sensor Node 16 bit sample"] --> B["Edge Gateway Header addition 256 bits"]
    B --> C["LoRaWAN Transmit 50 kbps"]
    C --> D["Internet Backbone"]
    D --> E["Cloud Ingestion API"]
    E --> F["Database Storage"]
    F --> G["Analytics ML Engine"]
    G --> H["Application Dashboard"]
    H --> I{"Action Required"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Command Sent to Actuator"]
    I -->|No| K["Notification to User"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define the term "Internet of Things" and list any FOUR core IoT functions.**

**Model Answer:**
> The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity that enables them to collect, exchange, and act upon data with minimal human intervention.
>
> **Four Core IoT Functions:**
> 1. **Sensing & Identification** — capturing data from the physical world.
> 2. **Communication** — transmitting data over networks.
> 3. **Computation & Analytics** — processing data into information.
> 4. **Actuation** — performing physical actions based on decisions.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between the Perception Layer and the Application Layer of IoT architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Perception Layer | Application Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Position** | Layer 1 (bottom) | Layer 4 (top) |
