- **Smart Cities:** Streetlight monitoring, waste-bin level sensors, parking meters.
- **Asset Tracking:** Container and fleet tracking across continents using LoRaWAN roaming.
- **Industrial IoT:** Predictive maintenance in factories where Wi-Fi cannot reach moving assets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LoRa Bit Rate Equation

**Given:** A LoRa transmission with bandwidth $B$ and spreading factor $SF$ uses a coding rate $CR$ (where the effective code rate is $\tfrac{4}{4+CR}$).

**Step 1 — Number of chips per symbol.**
Each LoRa symbol is composed of $2^{SF}$ frequency chips, so the symbol duration is:

$$T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{B}$$

**Step 2 — Symbol rate.**
The symbol rate is the reciprocal of the symbol duration:

$$R_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{B}{2^{SF}}$$

**Step 3 — Raw bits per symbol.**
Because each symbol encodes $SF$ bits:

$$\text{Bits per symbol} = SF$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Forward Error Correction (FEC) overhead.**
LoRa uses Hamming coding with redundancy ratio $\tfrac{CR}{4+CR}$, so the effective throughput retains only a fraction $\tfrac{4}{4+CR}$ of the raw bits:

$$R_b = R_s \times SF \times \frac{4}{4+CR}$$

**Step 5 — Final consolidated expression.**

$$\boxed{\,R_b = SF \cdot \frac{B}{2^{SF}} \cdot \frac{4}{4+CR}\,} \quad [\text{bits/s}]$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Receiver Sensitivity (Shannon-Adjusted Form)

**Step 1 — Thermal noise floor.**
The thermal noise power available in bandwidth $B$ (in Hz) at temperature $T$ is:

$$N = kTB$$

Expressed in dBm at $T = 290$ K:

$$N_{dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$$

**Step 2 — Add the receiver noise figure.**
A practical receiver adds its own noise figure $NF$ (in dB):

$$N_{tot} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + NF$$

**Step 3 — Add the required SNR for the chosen Spreading Factor.**
LoRa's required SNR threshold is negative, decreasing with higher $SF$ (e.g., $-7.5$ dB for SF=7, $-20$ dB for SF=12):

$$S_{rx} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + NF + SNR_{req}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical worked example.**
Let $B = 125\,\text{kHz}$, $NF = 6$ dB, $SF = 12$ ($SNR_{req} = -20$ dB):

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{rx} &= -174 + 10\log_{10}(125 \times 10^{3}) + 6 + (-20) \\
       &= -174 + 10\log_{10}(1.25 \times 10^{5}) + 6 - 20 \\
       &= -174 + 10(5.0969) + 6 - 20 \\
       &= -174 + 50.969 + 6 - 20 \\
       &= -137.03 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A sensitivity of **$-137$ dBm** is **16 dB better than typical cellular IoT receivers** — this is what gives LoRa its multi-kilometre range on **$<25$ mW transmit power**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How SF Affects Key Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU examiner's favourite question type** is to ask students to compare SF values. The following table is constructed using $B = 125$ kHz and $CR = 1$ (effective code rate 4/5):

| SF | $2^{SF}$ | $T_s$ (ms) | $R_s$ (sym/s) | $R_b$ (bps) | SNR req (dB) | Range Impact |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 7 | 128 | 1.024 | 976.6 | 6835.9 | $-7.5$ | Shortest |
| 8 | 256 | 2.048 | 488.3 | 3906.3 | $-10$ | ↓ |
| 9 | 512 | 4.096 | 244.1 | 2197.3 | $-12.5$ | ↓ |
| 10 | 1024 | 8.192 | 122.1 | 1220.7 | $-15$ | ↓ |
| 11 | 2048 | 16.384 | 61.0 | 671.4 | $-17.5$ | ↓ |
| 12 | 4096 | 32.768 | 30.5 | 366.2 | $-20$ | **Longest** |

**Trend observed:** Doubling $2^{SF}$ (increasing SF by 1) **halves the data rate** but **improves sensitivity by ~2.5–3 dB** and roughly **extends range by 20–30%**.

## 3.4 Derivation of Time-on-Air for a LoRa Packet

A complete LoRa packet consists of three temporal segments:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{preamble} &= (n_{pre} + 4.25) \cdot T_s \\
T_{header}   &= \left(\frac{20 + 4CR}{4(4+CR)}\right) \cdot T_s \quad \text{(if header enabled)} \\
T_{payload}  &= n_{payload} \cdot T_s
\end{aligned}$$

So the total airtime is:

$$T_{packet} = T_{preamble} + T_{header} + T_{payload}$$

where $n_{payload}$ is the number of symbols required for the payload (a complex function of SF, CR, payload bytes, and the low-data-rate optimisation bit).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, you are **not** expected to compute $n_{payload}$ by hand. Memorise only the **trends**: higher SF or higher CR linearly increases airtime, which **quadruples battery cost per byte** transmitted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LoRa Modulator — Functional Block Architecture

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs only, no markdown inside labels -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Digital Payload Bits] --> B[Gray Encoder and Interleaver]
    B --> C[Hamming FEC Encoder]
    C --> D[Whitening with Pseudo Random Sequence]
    D --> E[Spreading Factor Modulator SF 7 to 12]
    E --> F[Chirp Generator Upchirp or Downchirp]
    F --> G[Digital to Analog Conversion]
    G --> H[RF Frontend Upconversion to Sub GHz]
    H --> I((ISM Band Antenna))

    subgraph CHIRP_GEN[Chirp Synthesiser Core]
        F
    end
```

**How to read this diagram:** Payload bits first undergo whitening to randomise DC content, then Hamming FEC adds redundancy, then the SF block stretches each bit into $2^{SF}$ chips, and finally the Chirp Generator produces a frequency-swept sinusoid that is upconverted to the ISM band for transmission.

## 4.2 LoRaWAN Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NODE1[End Device Class A Sensor] --> GW1[LoRaWAN Gateway 8 channel]
    NODE2[End Device Class B Tracker] --> GW1
    NODE3[End Device Class C Actuator] --> GW2[LoRaWAN Gateway 8 channel]
    NODE4[End Device Class A Meter] --> GW2
    GW1 --> NET[Network Server ALOHA based scheduler]
    GW2 --> NET
    NET --> APP[Application Server Customer backend]
    APP --> DASH[Cloud Dashboard / Database]
    APP --> CTRL[Actuator Downlink via Class C]
```

**Reading note:** LoRaWAN uses a **star-of-stars topology**. End devices are *not* associated with a single gateway; any gateway in range forwards the packet to the Network Server, which deduplicates and routes the message. Downlink scheduling is **server-controlled** to comply with regional duty-cycle regulations (e.g., 1% duty cycle in Europe on the 868 MHz band).

## 4.3 LoRa Receiver Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J[Sub GHz RF Input] --> K[Low Noise Amplifier and SAW Filter]
    K --> L[Direct Conversion to Baseband]
    L --> M[ADC Sampling at Bandwidth B]
    M --> N[Dechirp Block Multiply by conjugate chirp]
    N --> O[FFT Processor of size 2 to power SF]
    O --> P[Peak Detector identifies symbol frequency bin]
    P --> Q[Deinterleaver and Hamming Decoder]
    Q --> R[Derandomiser and Payload Reconstruction]
    R --> S[Recovered Digital Bits]
```

**Reading note:** The **Dechirp + FFT** pair is the magic step. Multiplying the received chirp by a complex conjugate reference chirp converts it into a **single-frequency sinusoid**, whose FFT peak directly reveals the encoded symbol. This is why LoRa can be received at SNR as low as **$-20$ dB**.

## 4.4 SF vs Range vs Battery — The Engineering Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TRADE[LoRa Design Trade off Space]
    TRADE --> R1[Increase SF 7 to 12]
    TRADE --> R2[Increase Bandwidth 125 to 500 kHz]
    TRADE --> R3[Increase Tx Power up to 20 dBm in 868]

    R1 --> O1[Range increases, Battery cost increases, Bit rate falls]
    R2 --> O2[Bit rate rises, Range falls, Battery cost per byte falls]
    R3 --> O3[Range rises, Battery cost rises, Regulatory limit reached]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define LoRa. Mention any **three** characteristics that distinguish it from traditional short-range wireless protocols like Wi-Fi and Bluetooth.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **LoRa (Long Range)** is a proprietary **physical layer modulation** based on **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** that enables long-range, low-power wireless communication in sub-GHz ISM bands.
>
> Three distinguishing characteristics:
> 1. **Range:** Operates over **2–15 km** versus Wi-Fi's ~30 m and Bluetooth's ~10 m.
> 2. **Power:** Designed for **multi-year battery life** (years on a 2400 mAh cell) versus hours/days for Wi-Fi.
> 3. **Data rate:** Trades throughput for sensitivity — only **0.3–50 kbps** versus Wi-Fi's tens of Mbps.
> 4. **Robustness:** Achieves **$-148$ dBm** sensitivity using CSS, far below Wi-Fi's typical $-90$ dBm.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
What is **Spreading Factor (SF)** in LoRa? How does increasing SF affect range and data rate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Spreading Factor (SF)** is the number of bits encoded per LoRa symbol, ranging from **7 to 12**. Each symbol comprises $2^{SF}$ chips spread over bandwidth $B$, so a higher SF means a **longer symbol duration** and **more energy per bit**.
>
> Effect of **increasing** SF:
> - **Range:** **Increases** (better sensitivity, ~2.5–3 dB gain per SF step).
> - **Data rate:** **Decreases** (the bit rate is proportional to $SF/2^{SF}$, so it halves with every SF step).
> - **Battery cost per byte:** **Increases** (longer airtime).

---

## Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**  
**(a)** Explain in detail the **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** modulation technique used in LoRa. Derive the expression for the LoRa **bit rate** in terms of Spreading Factor ($SF$), Bandwidth ($B$), and Coding Rate ($CR$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LoRa end device transmits in the **868 MHz ISM band** with a bandwidth $B = 125$ kHz, spreading factor $SF = 10$, and coding rate $CR = 1$. Calculate the **(i) symbol duration, (ii) symbol rate, (iii) effective bit rate**, and **(iv) processing gain**. Comment on how the bit rate would change if the bandwidth is increased to $500$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chirp Spread Spectrum explanation (7 marks):**

CSS transmits information by varying the **instantaneous frequency** of a carrier sinusoid linearly over time, sweeping across the entire channel bandwidth $B$ within a symbol period $T_s$. The chirp is mathematically defined as:

$$s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\!\left(2\pi\!\left(f_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\mu t^{2}\right) + \phi_0\right)$$

where $\mu = B/T_s$ is the chirp rate. The symbol encodes information through its **starting frequency offset** (for CSS-LoRa) or its **time shift** within a window (for cyclic shifts).

**Key advantages (model answers must include 3 of these):**
1. Robustness to multipath fading and narrowband interference (jamming affects only a small frequency slice).
2. **Orthogonality** of chirps with different starting frequencies enables multiple simultaneous users.
3. Low transmitter power due to spread-spectrum processing gain.
4. Doppler tolerance — chirps remain decodable in mobile scenarios.
5. Simple receiver implementation using **Dechirp + FFT**.

**Bit-rate derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$R_b = SF \cdot \frac{B}{2^{SF}} \cdot \frac{4}{4+CR} \quad [\text{bits/s}]$$

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Given: $B_1 = 125 \text{ kHz} = 1.25 \times 10^{5}$ Hz, $SF = 10$, $CR = 1$ (effective rate = $4/5 = 0.8$).

**(i) Symbol duration:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_s &= \frac{2^{SF}}{B_1} = \frac{2^{10}}{1.25 \times 10^{5}} \\
    &= \frac{1024}{1.25 \times 10^{5}} \\
    &= 8.192 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
    &= 8.192 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Symbol rate:**

$$R_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{1}{8.192 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 122.07 \text{ symbols/s}$$

**(iii) Effective bit rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_b &= SF \cdot \frac{B_1}{2^{SF}} \cdot \frac{4}{4+CR} \\
    &= 10 \cdot \frac{1.25 \times 10^{5}}{1024} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \\
    &= 10 \cdot 122.07 \cdot 0.8 \\
    &= 976.56 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Processing gain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_p &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{B_1}{R_b}\right) \\
    &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.25 \times 10^{5}}{976.56}\right) \\
    &= 10 \log_{10}(128) \\
    &= 10 \times 2.107 \\
    &\approx 21.07 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on bandwidth increase to $B_2 = 500$ kHz:**

The bit rate scales **linearly** with bandwidth. Therefore:

$$R_{b,2} = R_{b,1} \times \frac{B_2}{B_1} = 976.56 \times \frac{500}{125} = 976.56 \times 4 = 3906.25 \text{ bps}$$

So the bit rate becomes **3906.25 bps** (a fourfold increase). However, the **processing gain falls by 6 dB** (from ~21 dB to ~15 dB), reducing sensitivity and hence the achievable range.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a)** Compare LoRa with **NB-IoT** and **Sigfox** under the following heads: (i) Modulation technique, (ii) Spectrum, (iii) Data rate, (iv) Mobility, (v) Power consumption, (vi) Deployment cost, (vii) Typical use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **LoRaWAN** protocol architecture. With a neat diagram, explain the **three device classes (A, B, C)** and the **role of the Network Server** in handling uplink and downlink traffic. Why is duty-cycle regulation critical in the 868 MHz European band? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table (7 marks):**

| Head | LoRa | NB-IoT | Sigfox |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Modulation | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | QPSK (LTE-based) | UNB (Ultra-Narrow Band) BPSK |
| Spectrum | Sub-GHz ISM (license-free) | Licensed LTE in-band/guard-band | Sub-GHz ISM (license-free) |
| Data rate | 0.3–50 kbps | ~26 kbps downlink, ~62 kbps uplink | 100 bps (UL), 600 bps (DL) — extremely low |
| Mobility | Limited (handovers not defined) | Full LTE mobility | None (static endpoints) |
| Power | Very low (multi-year battery) | Low (modem complexity higher) | Very low (tiny payloads) |
| Deployment cost | Low (own GW) | High (cellular operator) | Pay-per-device (operator subscription) |
| Typical use | Smart agriculture, smart cities | Utility metering, asset tracking | Simple periodic telemetry |

**(b) LoRaWAN architecture (7 marks):**

**Architecture components:**
1. **End Devices (ED):** Sensors/actuators with LoRa transceivers. They communicate with gateways over the air using CSS modulation.
2. **Gateways (GW):** Transparent bridges that receive LoRa frames and forward them over IP (Ethernet/4G) to the Network Server. A device is **not bound to one gateway** — multiple GWs may receive the same frame.
3. **Network Server (NS):** Central brain that deduplicates frames, performs security checks, manages Adaptive Data Rate (ADR), and schedules downlinks within regulatory duty-cycle windows.
4. **Application Server (AS):** Customer-owned backend that parses the application payload and triggers business logic.

**Device classes (refer to the network topology in Section 4.2):**

- **Class A — Baseline:** The end device opens **two short receive windows** after every uplink. Lowest power, supports bidirectional communication, but server can only respond immediately after an uplink.
- **Class B — Scheduled downlink:** The gateway broadcasts periodic **beacons**, and Class B devices open extra **ping slots** at scheduled times — allowing server-initiated downlinks with bounded latency. Power is higher than Class A.
- **Class C — Continuously listening:** Devices keep their receiver open at all times except while transmitting. Provides **near-instantaneous** downlink capability, but consumes the most power (used for mains-powered actuators).

**Network Server role:**
The NS handles **MAC-layer commands** (link checks, ADR, channel selection), enforces **frame counters** for replay protection, and is **solely responsible** for ensuring regulatory compliance on downlinks.

**Why duty-cycle regulation matters in 868 MHz Europe:**

The 868 MHz ISM band is governed by **ETSI EN 300 220**, which enforces a **1% duty cycle** (transmit only 36 seconds per hour) on most sub-bands. The Network Server must therefore:
- **Schedule downlinks** so the device's aggregated airtime stays under the cap.
- **Deduplicate** frames to prevent multiple gateways from causing the device to exceed its quota.
- Enforce **regional sub-band usage** (e.g., 869.525 MHz is allowed 10% duty cycle and is often used for downlink-heavy applications like Class C actuators).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse LoRa with LoRaWAN.** LoRa is the physical layer modulation; LoRaWAN is the upper-layer MAC protocol. Students who interchange these lose **2–3 marks immediately**.
> - In numerical problems, students forget that the effective code rate is $\tfrac{4}{4+CR}$, not $CR$ directly. With $CR = 1$, the rate is **$4/5 = 0.8$**, not $1$.
> - When asked "which SF gives longest range," answer **SF=12**, but explain that this is the **lowest data rate** and **highest battery cost per byte**.
> - Do not say LoRa uses "spread spectrum in the Wi-Fi sense" — LoRa uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum specifically**, not DSSS or FHSS.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LoRa = Physical layer CSS modulation by Semtech; LoRaWAN = open MAC protocol by the LoRa Alliance.**
- The **chirp** sweeps the channel bandwidth $B$ over symbol duration $T_s = 2^{SF}/B$.
- **Spreading Factor (SF)** ranges **7 to 12**; each unit increase doubles the symbol length, halves the bit rate, and adds ~2.5–3 dB of sensitivity.
- **Bit rate formula (must memorise):** $R_b = SF \cdot \dfrac{B}{2^{SF}} \cdot \dfrac{4}{4+CR}$.
- **Processing gain:** $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(2^{SF}) \approx 2.56 \cdot SF$ dB.
- **Receiver sensitivity** can drop to **$-148$ dBm**, giving a **link budget of 155–170 dB**.
- LoRaWAN uses a **star-of-stars topology**; gateways are transparent and the Network Server deduplicates.
- **Class A** = lowest power, two receive windows after uplink. **Class B** = scheduled ping slots. **Class C** = always-on receiver, mains powered.
- **European 868 MHz band** is regulated by **ETSI EN 300 220** with a **1% duty cycle** for most sub-bands.
- LoRa is best for **low-rate, long-range, battery-critical, intermittent telemetry** — not for voice, video, or real-time control.
- The **Dechirp + FFT** receiver is what enables decoding at **negative SNR** down to $-20$ dB.
- **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** is a LoRaWAN MAC feature where the Network Server optimises each device's SF, BW, and Tx power to maximise battery life while maintaining link quality.
- **Coding rate (CR)** adds Hamming FEC redundancy: $CR = 1 \Rightarrow 4/5$, $CR = 2 \Rightarrow 4/6$, $CR = 3 \Rightarrow 4/7$, $CR = 4 \Rightarrow 4/8$. Higher CR = more robust but lower throughput.
- LoRa is **patent-protected**; only Semtech (and licensed partners) can manufacture LoRa radio chips. LoRaWAN itself is open.
- For KTU numerical problems, always **show units** and convert MHz to Hz (multiply by $10^6$) and kHz to Hz (multiply by $10^3$) before substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zigbee
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Course Code:** OEITT723
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Zigbee
> - **Prepared by:** KTU Senior Board Examiner (NEP 2020 Aligned)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zigbee** is a specification suite of **high-level wireless communication protocols** used to create **personal area networks (PANs)** built upon the **IEEE 802.15.4 standard** for low-power, low-data-rate, short-range wireless networking. It is formally maintained by the **Connectivity Standards Alliance (CSA)** — formerly the Zigbee Alliance — and operates in the **unlicensed radio frequency bands** of 868 MHz, 915 MHz, and 2.4 GHz ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) spectrum.

In strict KTU terminology, Zigbee is positioned as a **Layer 3–7 protocol suite** that operates *on top of* the IEEE 802.15.4 PHY (Physical) and MAC (Medium Access Control) layers. It provides **mesh networking**, **device interoperability**, **end-to-end security (AES-128)**, and a unified **application framework** for IoT devices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building Walkie-Talkie"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Building of Smart Walkie-Talkies**
>
> Imagine a 20-floor office building where every employee (sensor, light bulb, thermostat) carries a **tiny, battery-efficient walkie-talkie** that can only whisper short messages (like "Temperature = 28°C" or "Lights OFF"). Now imagine:
>
> - The **walkie-talkie's hardware** (radio + antenna) is defined by **IEEE 802.15.4** — the "physical walkie-talkie" rules.
> - The **conversation language, addressing, and group rules** are defined by **Zigbee** — the "language protocol."
> - Employees can **relay messages** for each other to extend range (this is called a **mesh network**).
> - Each message is **encrypted** so eavesdroppers hear only static.
> - Batteries last **months to years** because the devices spend most time asleep.

This is exactly what Zigbee does for the IoT world — it lets tiny, cheap, battery-powered devices chat intelligently with each other and the cloud, with **extremely low energy consumption**.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **PHY Layer Standard:** IEEE 802.15.4 (low-rate wireless PAN)
> - **RF Bands (3 globally):** **868 MHz** (Europe), **915 MHz** (USA/Australia), **2.4 GHz** (Global ISM)
> - **Maximum Data Rate:** **250 kbps** (at 2.4 GHz)
> - **Range:** **10 m to 100 m** (line-of-sight)
> - **Network Size:** Up to **65,536 nodes** per coordinator
> - **Encryption:** **AES-128 (128-bit Advanced Encryption Standard)**
> - **Modulation at 2.4 GHz:** **O-QPSK (Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying)** with DSSS

### 1.4 Where Zigbee Fits in the IoT Stack

Zigbee is one of the **dominant short-range IoT protocols**, alongside Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), LoRa, NB-IoT, and Z-Wave. Its competitive niche is **low-power, mesh-capable, intermittent sensor/actuator networks** — not high-throughput media streaming.

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Mesh Support |
|----------|-------|-----------|-------|--------------|
| **Zigbee** | 10–100 m | 250 kbps | Very Low | **Yes (native)** |
| BLE | 10–50 m | 1–2 Mbps | Very Low | Limited |
| Wi-Fi | 50 m | 100+ Mbps | High | No (typical) |
| LoRa | 2–15 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Very Low | No |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Zigbee Architecture — The Protocol Stack

Zigbee is structured in a **layered architecture** consistent with the OSI model. The lower two layers (PHY + MAC) are inherited directly from **IEEE 802.15.4**, while the upper layers are defined by the Zigbee specification.

**Layered Breakdown:**

1. **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY Layer (Physical Layer)**
   - Handles radio activation, channel selection, energy detection, link quality indication.
   - Performs modulation/demodulation and bit-to-symbol/chip mapping.

2. **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Layer (Medium Access Control)**
   - Controls channel access using **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance).
   - Manages beacon generation, GTS (Guaranteed Time Slot) allocation, PAN association, and frame delivery.

3. **Zigbee Network Layer (NWK)**
   - Handles **device addressing** (16-bit NWK + 64-bit IEEE address).
   - Responsible for **routing**, **network formation/maintenance**, and **mesh self-healing**.
   - Supports multiple topologies: **Star, Tree, Mesh**.

4. **Zigbee Application Support Sublayer (APS)**
   - Bridges NWK to the application layer.
   - Manages **endpoints**, **binding tables**, and **indirect addressing**.

5. **Zigbee Device Objects (ZDO)**
   - Defines the *role* of a device (Coordinator, Router, End Device).
   - Manages security keys, network discovery, and binding services.

6. **Application Framework (AF)**
   - Houses the **application objects** (endpoints 1–240) where actual user logic runs.
   - Custom profiles, clusters, and attributes are defined here (e.g., the **Zigbee Cluster Library – ZCL**).

### 2.2 Three Device Types in Zigbee

> [!NOTE]
> - **Coordinator (ZC):** Exactly **one per network**. Initiates the PAN, assigns the 16-bit PAN ID, stores security keys, and acts as the trust center. **Cannot sleep.**
> - **Router (ZR):** Forwards packets, allows new devices to join, participates in mesh routing. **Cannot sleep** (must stay awake to relay).
> - **End Device (ZED):** Leaf nodes that talk **only to a parent** (Coordinator or Router). **Can sleep** for years on a coin cell — this is the key to Zigbee's low power.

### 2.3 Network Topologies

Zigbee supports three primary topologies, each suited to a different deployment scenario:

| Topology | Structure | Pros | Cons |
|----------|-----------|------|------|
| **Star** | All devices → Coordinator | Simple, predictable | Single point of failure, short range |
| **Mesh** | Any-to-any via routers | **Self-healing**, long range, robust | More complex routing |
| **Tree** | Hierarchical clusters | Predictable routing | Sub-tree failures isolate nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Mesh topology** is Zigbee's *signature feature*. When a router fails, the network automatically re-routes traffic through alternative paths — a property called **self-healing**.

### 2.4 Frequency Bands and Channel Allocation

Zigbee/IEEE 802.15.4 specifies three ISM bands globally:

| Band | Frequency Range | Channels | Spacing | Bit Rate | Region |
|------|----------------|----------|---------|----------|--------|
| **868 MHz** | 868.0–868.6 MHz | **1** (ch 0) | — | 20 kbps | Europe |
| **915 MHz** | 902–928 MHz | **10** (ch 1–10) | 2 MHz | 40 kbps | USA, AUS |
| **2.4 GHz** | 2.400–2.4835 GHz | **16** (ch 11–26) | 5 MHz | 250 kbps | **Global** |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is a **concise reference table** of all critical Zigbee equations and constants you will need in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. **No pipe symbols** have been used inside the table cells.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Description |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **2.4 GHz Channel Frequency** | $f_c = 2405 + 5 \cdot (k - 11) \text{ MHz}$, for $k = 11 \text{ to } 26$ | Maps channel number $k$ to centre frequency |
| 2 | **Symbol Rate (2.4 GHz)** | $R_s = 62.5 \text{ ksymbols/s}$ | One symbol = 4 bits |
| 3 | **Chip Rate (2.4 GHz)** | $R_c = 2.0 \text{ Mchips/s}$ | Each symbol → 32 chips (DSSS spreading) |
| 4 | **Bit Rate (2.4 GHz)** | $R_b = R_s \times 4 = 250 \text{ kbps}$ | O-QPSK modulation with 4-bit/symbol |
| 5 | **Symbol Period** | $T_s = 1 / R_s = 16 \ \mu\text{s}$ | Time for one symbol |
| 6 | **Chip Period** | $T_c = 1 / R_c = 0.5 \ \mu\text{s}$ | Time for one chip |
| 7 | **Processing Gain (DSSS)** | $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(\text{chips/bit}) = 10 \log_{10}(8) = 9.03 \text{ dB}$ | Spreading factor = 8 chips/bit (32 chips ÷ 4 bits) |
| 8 | **Maximum Nodes/PAN** | $N_{\max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | From 16-bit NWK address |
| 9 | **PAN ID Size** | 16 bits | Network identifier |
| 10 | **Maximum Payload (MAC)** | 127 bytes (PHY frame) / 102 bytes (data) | Maximum MAC payload per packet |
| 11 | **Encryption Key Size** | 128 bits | AES-128 |
| 12 | **CSMA/CA Backoff** | BE $\in [\text{BE}_{\min}, \text{BE}_{\max}] = [3, 5]$ | Binary exponential backoff exponent |
| 13 | **Throughput (practical)** | $\eta_{\text{app}} \approx 30\% \text{ to } 50\% \text{ of } R_b$ | Due to overhead, acknowledgements, CSMA |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is Zigbee used in the real world?**
> - **Smart Homes:** Philips Hue lights, IKEA TRÅDFRI, Amazon Echo Zigbee hub.
> - **Smart Metering:** Automatic meter reading (AMR) for electricity/gas/water.
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Wireless sensor networks on factory floors.
> - **Healthcare:** Patient monitoring (pulse, temperature, fall detection).
> - **Building Automation:** HVAC, lighting, access control in commercial buildings.
> - **Agricultural IoT:** Soil moisture, greenhouse climate monitoring.

The **killer application** of Zigbee is **large-scale, battery-powered mesh networks** where devices are deployed in the *hundreds to thousands* and must last *years* on small batteries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping 2.4 GHz Channel Numbers to Frequencies

The IEEE 802.15.4 standard at 2.4 GHz defines 16 channels numbered **$k = 11$ to $26$**, with a uniform channel spacing of **$\Delta f = 5$ MHz**, beginning at the reference frequency **$f_{11} = 2405$ MHz**.

We want to derive a general expression for the centre frequency $f_c$ of channel $k$.

**Step 1 — Define the reference point.**
For the lowest channel in the 2.4 GHz band:
$$k_{\text{ref}} = 11, \quad f_{\text{ref}} = 2405 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Express the offset in channels.**
The number of channel steps from the reference channel is:
$$n = k - k_{\text{ref}} = k - 11$$

**Step 3 — Multiply offset by channel spacing.**
The total frequency offset from $f_{\text{ref}}$ is:
$$\Delta f_{\text{total}} = n \cdot \Delta f = 5 \cdot (k - 11) \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Add offset to the reference frequency.**
$$\boxed{f_c = f_{\text{ref}} + 5 \cdot (k - 11) \text{ MHz} = 2405 + 5(k - 11) \text{ MHz}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification with sample channels.**

For $k = 11$:
$$f_c = 2405 + 5(0) = 2405 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

For $k = 26$:
$$f_c = 2405 + 5(15) = 2480 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

For $k = 15$ (used by Zigbee channel 15 — a popular non-Wi-Fi channel):
$$f_c = 2405 + 5(4) = 2425 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does Zigbee avoid channels 1–13 of Wi-Fi (2401–2473 MHz)?** Although Zigbee (2.4 GHz) and Wi-Fi share the 2.4 GHz ISM band, channels 15, 20, and 25 of Zigbee (2425, 2450, 2475 MHz) are intentionally chosen to **minimally overlap** with the most common Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, and 11. This **coexistence strategy** is a critical KTU 2024 exam point.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bit Rate, Symbol Rate, and Chip Rate Relationship (2.4 GHz Band)

**Step 1 — State the fundamental relationship between bit rate and symbol rate.**

By definition, the bit rate $R_b$ equals the symbol rate $R_s$ multiplied by the number of bits per symbol $m$:
$$R_b = R_s \cdot m$$

**Step 2 — Identify the bits-per-symbol value at 2.4 GHz.**

IEEE 802.15.4 at 2.4 GHz uses **O-QPSK modulation with 4-bit symbols** (16-ary orthogonal signalling). Therefore:
$$m = 4 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known bit rate.**

The standard defines the bit rate as:
$$R_b = 250 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the symbol rate.**
$$R_s = \frac{R_b}{m} = \frac{250{,}000}{4} = 62{,}500 \text{ symbols/s}$$
$$\boxed{R_s = 62.5 \text{ ksymbols/s}}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the symbol period.**
$$T_s = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{1}{62{,}500} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$
$$\boxed{T_s = 16 \ \mu\text{s}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the chip rate (DSSS spreading).**

Each 4-bit symbol is spread using a 32-chip PN sequence. Thus:
$$\text{chips/symbol} = 32$$

The chip rate is therefore:
$$R_c = R_s \times 32 = 62{,}500 \times 32 = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ chips/s}$$
$$\boxed{R_c = 2 \text{ Mchips/s}}$$

**Step 7 — Derive the chip period.**
$$T_c = \frac{1}{R_c} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}$$
$$\boxed{T_c = 0.5 \ \mu\text{s}}$$

**Step 8 — Sanity check: chips per bit.**

Since each symbol carries 4 bits and is spread into 32 chips:
$$\frac{\text{chips}}{\text{bit}} = \frac{32}{4} = 8 \text{ chips/bit}$$

This is the **spreading factor (SF)** used to compute processing gain.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Processing Gain of Zigbee DSSS

The processing gain $G_p$ (in dB) of a DSSS system is defined as:
$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the derived values.**
$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{250 \times 10^{3}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the ratio.**
$$\frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000} = 8$$

**Step 3 — Compute the decibel value.**
$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}(8) = 10 \times 0.9031$$
$$\boxed{G_p \approx 9.03 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** The 9.03 dB processing gain means Zigbee can **tolerate roughly 8× more interference** than an unspread narrowband signal of the same data rate. This is why Zigbee coexists reasonably well with Wi-Fi despite the shared 2.4 GHz band.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Zigbee Network Capacity (Address Space)

**Step 1 — Identify the addressing scheme.**

Each Zigbee node has a 16-bit **short (NWK) address** assigned by the Coordinator during association.

**Step 2 — Compute the address space size.**

The number of unique 16-bit addresses is:
$$N_{\text{addr}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$$

**Step 3 — Account for reserved addresses.**

- Address `0x0000` → reserved for the **Coordinator**.
- Address `0xFFF8` to `0xFFFF` → reserved for **broadcast & special functions** (8 addresses).

Therefore the *maximum usable* devices per PAN is:
$$N_{\text{usable}} = 2^{16} - 1 - 8 = 65{,}527$$
$$\boxed{N_{\max} \approx 65{,}527 \text{ usable nodes per PAN}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Frame Size Calculation

A Zigbee **data frame** consists of a MAC header (MHR), MAC payload (MSDU), and MAC footer (MFR). The maximum theoretical payload sizes:

| Component | Size (bytes) |
|-----------|--------------|
| Frame Control | 2 |
| Sequence Number | 1 |
| Addressing Fields (PAN IDs + addresses) | 4 to 20 |
| Auxiliary Security Header | 0 / 5 / 6 |
| **MHR Subtotal** | **~ 9 to 31** |
| MSDU (Payload) | up to **102** |
| FCS (CRC-16) | 2 |
| **MFR Subtotal** | **2** |
| **Total MAC Frame (max)** | **127 bytes** |

**Derivation of the maximum MSDU:**
$$\text{MSDU}_{\max} = a_{\text{MaxPHYPacketSize}} - a_{\text{MaxFrameOverhead}} = 127 - 25 = 102 \text{ bytes}$$

(using typical overhead of 25 bytes for fully addressed secured frame)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU student mistake:** Writing "Zigbee has 250 channels" — the 2.4 GHz band has only **16 channels**, and the entire 802.15.4 standard has **27 channels** (1 + 10 + 16). Marks are frequently lost in Module 2 questions for this error.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Zigbee Protocol Stack (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Application Layer - Zigbee Cluster Library ZCL and Custom Profiles"]
    A2["Application Support Sublayer APS - Endpoints and Binding Tables"]
    A3["Zigbee Device Objects ZDO - Role Definition and Security"]
    A4["Network Layer NWK - Routing, Addressing, Mesh Self Healing"]
    A5["MAC Layer IEEE 802.15.4 - CSMA-CA, Beacon, GTS, Frame"]
    A6["PHY Layer IEEE 802.15.4 - O-QPSK Modulation and DSSS Spreading"]
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> RF["2.4 GHz Radio Frequency Channel 11 to 26"]
```

### 4.2 Zigbee Device Roles and Their Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    ZC["Coordinator ZC - 1 per PAN - Initiates Network - Stores Keys - Cannot Sleep"]
    ZR1["Router ZR - Forwards Frames - Allows Joins - Cannot Sleep"]
    ZR2["Router ZR - Forwards Frames - Allows Joins - Cannot Sleep"]
    ZED1["End Device ZED - Sensor 1 - Sleeps 99 percent of Time"]
    ZED2["End Device ZED - Light Bulb - Sleeps Most of the Time"]
    ZED3["End Device ZED - Smart Lock - Sleeps but Wakes on Event"]
    ZC --> ZR1
    ZC --> ZR2
    ZR1 --> ZED1
    ZR1 --> ZED2
    ZR2 --> ZED3
```

### 4.3 Mesh Self-Healing — Before and After Failure

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1["Node A Coordinator"] --> N2["Node B Router"]
    N2 --> N3["Node C Router"]
    N3 --> N4["Node D End Device"]
    N2 --> N5["Node E End Device"]

    N1F["Node A Coordinator"] -. "Failure" .-> N2F["Node B FAULTY"]
    N3F["Node C Router"] --> N4F["Node D End Device"]
    N3F --> N5F["Node E End Device"]
    N1F --> N3F
```

### 4.4 Zigbee vs Wi-Fi Channel Coexistence (2.4 GHz)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W1["WiFi CH 1 Center 2412 MHz Span 2401 to 2423"]
    W6["WiFi CH 6 Center 2437 MHz Span 2426 to 2448"]
    W11["WiFi CH 11 Center 2462 MHz Span 2451 to 2473"]
    Z15["Zigbee CH 15 Center 2425 MHz 2 MHz BW"]
    Z20["Zigbee CH 20 Center 2450 MHz 2 MHz BW"]
    Z25["Zigbee CH 25 Center 2475 MHz 2 MHz BW"]
    W1 -.- Z15
    W6 -.- Z20
    W11 -.- Z25
```

> [!NOTE]
> Zigbee channels 15, 20, 25 (highlighted above) are the most commonly used channels worldwide because they experience the **least overlap** with the three non-overlapping Wi-Fi channels (1, 6, 11).

### 4.5 Zigbee Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled PAN)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SF["Beacon Interval BI = 15.36 ms to 251.6 s"] --> CAP["Contention Access Period CAP - CSMA-CA Based"]
    SF --> CFP["Contention Free Period CFP - GTS Slots 1 to 7"]
    CAP --> SD["Superframe Duration SD Active portion"]
    CFP --> SD
    SD --> INACT["Inactive Portion - All Nodes Sleep"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Zigbee. List any three characteristics that differentiate Zigbee from Wi-Fi.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Zigbee is a wireless personal area network (WPAN) protocol suite based on the **IEEE 802.15.4 standard**, designed for **low-power, low-data-rate, short-range communication** among IoT devices. (1 mark)

Three characteristics differentiating Zigbee from Wi-Fi:
1. **Data rate:** Zigbee = **250 kbps**; Wi-Fi = up to several hundred Mbps. (1 mark)
2. **Power consumption:** Zigbee is **ultra-low-power** (devices last months to years on coin cells); Wi-Fi consumes significantly more power. (0.5 marks)
3. **Topology:** Zigbee supports **mesh networking** with self-healing; Wi-Fi typically uses star/infrastructure topology. (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Explain the three device types in a Zigbee network. Which of them can enter sleep mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Zigbee network consists of three device types: (0.5 marks for enumeration)

1. **Coordinator (ZC):** Exactly one per PAN. Responsible for network initiation, PAN ID assignment, and security key storage. It **cannot sleep**. (0.75 marks)
2. **Router (ZR):** Relays frames between devices, allows new nodes to join, and participates in mesh routing. It **cannot sleep** (must stay awake to forward packets). (0.75 marks)
3. **End Device (ZED):** Leaf nodes that communicate only with their parent. They **can sleep** for extended periods, which is the key reason Zigbee achieves years of battery life. (0.75 marks)
4. **Conclusion:** Only **End Devices (ZED)** can enter sleep mode. (0.25 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Module 2 questions on Zigbee, students most commonly lose marks for: (a) **confusing the number of channels** (Zigbee has 16 channels at 2.4 GHz, NOT 250), (b) **forgetting to mention the IEEE 802.15.4 dependency** at the start of any Zigbee answer, and (c) **mixing up device roles** — Routers DO NOT sleep. Examiners actively search for these errors to deduct marks. Also, **always include a labelled diagram** in 14-mark questions to secure 2–3 additional marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of the Zigbee protocol stack with a neat diagram. How does Zigbee differ from Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) in terms of network topology and power consumption?

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a frequency channel map, explain how Zigbee coexists with Wi-Fi in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. Why are Zigbee channels 15, 20, and 25 preferred?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Zigbee Protocol Stack (7 marks):**

The Zigbee protocol stack is built in a layered manner, with the **lower two layers (PHY and MAC)** defined by the **IEEE 802.15.4 standard** and the **upper layers** defined by the **Zigbee specification** (maintained by the Connectivity Standards Alliance). (1 mark)

From bottom to top:

1. **Physical Layer (PHY) — IEEE 802.15.4:** Handles radio frequency transmission/reception, channel selection (868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz), modulation (O-QPSK at 2.4 GHz), and bit-to-chip DSSS spreading. (1 mark)

2. **MAC Layer — IEEE 802.15.4:** Controls channel access via CSMA/CA, manages beacon generation, frame acknowledgement, GTS allocation, and PAN association. (1 mark)

3. **Network Layer (NWK):** Performs **16-bit short addressing**, mesh/tree/star routing, network formation, and self-healing. (1 mark)

4. **Application Support Sublayer (APS):** Manages **endpoints (1–240)**, binding tables, and indirect addressing for sleeping end devices. (1 mark)

5. **Application Framework (AF) and ZCL:** Houses user-defined application objects and the Zigbee Cluster Library (ZCL) for device interoperability. (1 mark)

6. **Zigbee Device Objects (ZDO):** Defines the device role (Coordinator, Router, End Device) and manages security keys. (1 mark)

**Comparison with BLE:**

| Aspect | Zigbee | BLE |
|--------|--------|-----|
| Topology | Star, Tree, **Mesh** (native) | Star (piconet); mesh only in newer BLE Mesh |
| Power | Ultra-low (end devices sleep > 99%) | Low, but mesh support increases duty cycle |

*Refer to the protocol stack diagram in Section 4.1 for full marks.*

---

**(b) Zigbee and Wi-Fi Coexistence (7 marks):**

Both Zigbee (2.4 GHz PHY of IEEE 802.15.4) and Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 b/g/n) operate in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** (2400–2483.5 MHz), which is unlicensed. They therefore **share the same radio spectrum** and must coexist. (1 mark)

**Channel mapping (from Section 4.4):**

- **Zigbee:** 16 channels, 5 MHz spacing, 2 MHz bandwidth per channel, numbered $k = 11$ to $26$. (1 mark)
- **Wi-Fi:** Typically uses 3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11) of 22 MHz bandwidth. (1 mark)

Because Wi-Fi channels are 22 MHz wide, a single Wi-Fi channel can overlap with **up to 4 Zigbee channels**. This causes mutual interference if both are active. (1 mark)

**Zigbee's coexistence strategy — the "clear channel" choice:**

By computing channel frequencies using $f_c = 2405 + 5(k - 11)$:
- Zigbee channel **15** → 2425 MHz (lies *between* Wi-Fi 1 and 6)
- Zigbee channel **20** → 2450 MHz (lies *between* Wi-Fi 6 and 11)
- Zigbee channel **25** → 2475 MHz (lies *above* Wi-Fi 11)

These three channels experience the **least interference** from the most-used Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, 11. (2 marks)

**Additional coexistence techniques:**
- **DSSS spreading** gives 9.03 dB processing gain to tolerate interference.
- **CSMA/CA** channel sensing avoids busy channels.
- **Network Manager** may dynamically shift Zigbee channels if Wi-Fi interference rises. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the three Zigbee network topologies (Star, Tree, Mesh) with diagrams. Why is the **mesh topology** considered the most advantageous for large-scale IoT deployments?

**(b) [7 marks]** A Zigbee coordinator at 2.4 GHz is configured on channel **$k = 18$**. (i) Calculate the centre frequency. (ii) Derive the symbol rate, chip rate, and bit rate. (iii) Compute the processing gain in dB.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Zigbee Topologies (7 marks):**

1. **Star Topology (2 marks):** A central **Coordinator** communicates directly with all End Devices. Routers are absent. Pros: simple, low latency, easy to set up. Cons: **single point of failure**, limited range (only as far as the coordinator), and every device must be in radio range of the coordinator.

2. **Tree Topology (2 marks):** A hierarchical structure where the Coordinator is the root, Routers form branches, and End Devices are leaves. Communication between leaves goes *up* the tree. Pros: predictable routing, scalability. Cons: failure of a router disconnects its entire sub-tree (no alternate path).

3. **Mesh Topology (2 marks):** Routers form a fully interconnected web. Any router can communicate with any other router through **multiple paths**. If one router fails, traffic is automatically re-routed — this is called **self-healing**. Pros: maximum reliability, longest range, robust. Cons: more routing overhead, higher memory requirements.

**Why Mesh is Best for Large-Scale IoT (1 mark):**
- **Self-healing:** A single device failure does not partition the network.
- **Range extension:** Multi-hop communication covers hectares with low transmit power.
- **Redundancy:** Critical for industrial, medical, and security IoT applications where downtime is unacceptable.

*Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.3 for full marks.*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** Channel $k = 18$, 2.4 GHz band.

**(i) Centre Frequency (2 marks):**
Using the derived formula:
$$f_c = 2405 + 5(k - 11) = 2405 + 5(18 - 11) = 2405 + 35$$
$$\boxed{f_c = 2440 \text{ MHz}}$$

**(ii) Symbol Rate, Chip Rate, Bit Rate (3 marks):**

From the IEEE 802.15.4 standard at 2.4 GHz:
- Bit-to-symbol mapping: $m = 4$ bits/symbol (16-ary O-QPSK).
- Chip sequence: 32 chips per symbol (DSSS).

Therefore:
$$R_s = \frac{R_b}{m} = \frac{250 \text{ kbps}}{4} = 62.5 \text{ ksymbols/s}$$
$$R_c = R_s \times 32 = 62{,}500 \times 32 = 2 \text{ Mchips/s}$$
$$\boxed{R_s = 62.5 \text{ ksymbols/s}, \quad R_c = 2 \text{ Mchips/s}, \quad R_b = 250 \text{ kbps}}$$

**(iii) Processing Gain (2 marks):**
$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(8)$$
$$\boxed{G_p = 9.03 \text{ dB}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**
>
> - **Zigbee** = **IEEE 802.15.4-based** low-power, mesh WPAN protocol suite.
> - **Three frequency bands:** 868 MHz (1 ch), 915 MHz (10 ch), **2.4 GHz (16 ch)**.
> - **Bit rate at 2.4 GHz = 250 kbps**; symbol rate = **62.5 ksymbols/s**; chip rate = **2 Mchips/s**.
> - **Modulation at 2.4 GHz = O-QPSK** with 32-chip DSSS spreading per 4-bit symbol.
> - **Processing gain = 9.03 dB** ($10 \log_{10} 8$).
> - **Channel frequency formula:** $f_c = 2405 + 5(k - 11)$ MHz, for $k = 11, 12, \dots, 26$.
> - **Zigbee and Wi-Fi coexist**; preferred Zigbee channels = **15, 20, 25** (least Wi-Fi overlap).
> - **Three device types:** **Coordinator (1, awake), Router (many, awake), End Device (many, sleeping).**
> - **Maximum nodes/PAN = 65,536** (16-bit NWK address).
> - **Security = AES-128-bit** encryption + trust center at Coordinator.
> - **Channel access = CSMA/CA** with binary exponential backoff.
> - **MAC access = CSMA/CA** in CAP; GTS slots (1–7) in CFP.
> - **Max MAC payload = 102 bytes**; max MAC frame = 127 bytes.
> - **Three topologies:** Star, Tree, **Mesh (self-healing).**
> - **Practical applications:** Smart homes (Philips Hue), smart metering, IIoT, healthcare, building automation.
> - **Killer feature:** **Multi-year battery life** of sleeping end devices + **mesh self-healing.**
> - **Common valuation traps:** saying "Zigbee has 250 channels" (WRONG — 16 at 2.4 GHz, 27 total), saying "Routers can sleep" (WRONG — only End Devices sleep), and confusing Zigbee with Z-Wave (a competing, not interchangeable, protocol).
> - **Remember to draw a labelled diagram** in every 7-mark or 14-mark Zigbee question — examiners award 1–3 marks specifically for clarity of diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth/Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth/Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bluetooth** is a short-range wireless personal area network (WPAN) technology standardized as **IEEE 802.15.1** that operates in the unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) band**. It is designed to replace wired serial communication (RS-232) between portable and/or fixed electronic devices using short-wavelength UHF radio waves in the band from **2.400 GHz to 2.4835 GHz** (a total of **83.5 MHz** of spectrum).

**Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** — formally known as **Bluetooth Smart** and standardized as part of **Bluetooth Core Specification 4.0+ (Bluetooth 4.0, 4.1, 4.2, 5.0, 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4)** — is a fundamentally different protocol stack than Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR). BLE was designed from the ground up as a low-power, low-complexity wireless protocol for the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, sensor networks, and periodic data transfer applications. It achieves a current consumption roughly **1/100th** of Bluetooth Classic while maintaining a similar communication range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bluetooth/BLE forms a mandatory sub-topic of **Module 2 (IoT Connectivity & Networking Technologies)** in OEITT723. Students are expected to compare Bluetooth Classic vs BLE, draw the BLE protocol stack, and explain GATT/GAP profiles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Walkie-Talkie vs. The Pager Analogy:**

Think of **Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR)** as a **walkie-talkie**:
- Both devices must be "talking" at the same time, constantly.
- The walkie-talkie consumes battery continuously as long as it is on.
- Great for streaming music or a phone call (continuous, high-throughput data), but it "drains" the battery quickly.

Now think of **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** as a **smart wristwatch with a heart-rate monitor**:
- The watch *sleeps* almost all the time.
- It wakes up for a few milliseconds, transmits a small packet ("heart rate = 72 bpm"), and goes back to sleep.
- It does not maintain a constant, open "voice channel."
- It uses so little energy that a single coin-cell battery can power it for **months or even years**.

This is the essence of BLE: **"short bursts of small data, separated by long sleeps."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Bluetooth Classic is connection-oriented with continuous streaming; BLE is connection-oriented but heavily duty-cycled (transmits small packets infrequently). BLE is NOT backward compatible with Bluetooth Classic at the radio layer, even though both can coexist on the same device (a "dual-mode" chip).

### 1.3 Key Physical & Radio Specifications

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Frequency Band | 2.400 – 2.4835 GHz (ISM) |
| Total Spectrum | **83.5 MHz** |
| Number of RF Channels | **40 channels** (BLE) |
| Channel Spacing | **2 MHz** |
| Raw Data Rate (LE 1M PHY) | **1 Mbps** (mandatory) |
| Raw Data Rate (LE 2M PHY) | **2 Mbps** (optional, BLE 5.0) |
| Raw Data Rate (LE Coded PHY, S=2) | **500 kbps** (BLE 5.0) |
| Raw Data Rate (LE Coded PHY, S=8) | **125 kbps** (BLE 5.0) |
| Modulation (LE 1M/2M) | GFSK (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying) |
| Modulation Index | 0.5 (LE 1M), 0.7 (LE 2M) |
| Frequency Hopping | Adaptive, 1600 hops/sec |
| Transmit Power | **−20 dBm to +20 dBm** (typ. 0 dBm / 1 mW) |
| Range (typical) | **10 m to 100 m+** depending on PHY |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulation Index Formula:**
> $$h = \frac{2f_d}{R_b}$$
> where $f_d$ is the frequency deviation and $R_b$ is the bit rate. For LE 1M PHY: $h = 0.5$ with $f_d = 250$ kHz and $R_b = 1$ Mbps.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution of Bluetooth — The Generation Table

| Version | Year | Key Feature | Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 / 1.1 | 1999 / 2001 | Basic Rate (BR), 1 Mbps | First consumer rollout |
| 1.2 | 2003 | AFH (Adaptive Frequency Hopping) | Better coexistence with Wi-Fi |
| 2.0 + EDR | 2004 | Enhanced Data Rate, 3 Mbps | Streaming audio |
| 2.1 + EDR | 2007 | Secure Simple Pairing (SSP) | Easier pairing |
| 3.0 + HS | 2009 | High Speed (over Wi-Fi 802.11) | Not widely adopted |
| **4.0 (BLE)** | **2010** | **BLE introduced** | **IoT revolution begins** |
| 4.1 | 2013 | Coexistence improvements, LTE | Bulk data transfer to smartphones |
| 4.2 | 2014 | LE Data Packet Length Extension, **LE Privacy 1.2**, IPv6/6LoWPAN | IoT over BLE |
| 5.0 | 2016 | **LE 2M PHY, LE Coded PHY, advertising extensions** | 4× range, 2× speed |
| 5.1 | 2019 | Direction Finding (AoA / AoD) | Indoor positioning |
| 5.2 | 2020 | **LE Audio, LC3 codec, Isochronous Channels** | True wireless earbuds |
| 5.3 | 2021 | Connection subrating, channel classification | Efficiency |
| 5.4 | 2023 | Periodic Advertising with Responses (PAwR), Electronic Shelf Label | Massive IoT |

### 2.2 BLE Protocol Stack — The "Layer Cake"

The BLE protocol stack is divided into **three main functional blocks** with multiple sub-layers. The order (from bottom to top) is:

1. **Controller Subsystem (Lower Layers)**
   - **Physical Layer (PHY)** — Radio modulation/demodulation, channel selection.
   - **Link Layer (LL)** — Device discovery, connection establishment, packet framing, frequency hopping, encryption.
   - **Direct Test Mode (DTM)** — For RF certification testing.

2. **Host-Controller Interface (HCI)**
   - A standardized interface (UART, SPI, USB, or SDIO) for the Host to send commands to the Controller.

3. **Host Subsystem (Upper Layers)**
   - **Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)** — Multiplexing, segmentation/reassembly, flow control.
   - **Attribute Protocol (ATT)** — The client-server data exchange protocol.
   - **Generic Attribute Profile (GATT)** — Defines how attributes are grouped into Services and Characteristics.
   - **Generic Access Profile (GAP)** — Defines device roles, advertising, security modes.
   - **Security Manager Protocol (SMP)** — Pairing, bonding, key distribution.
   - **Higher-layer profiles** (e.g., Heart Rate Profile, Proximity Profile, Environmental Sensing Profile).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Host and Controller can be on the same chip** (System-on-Chip, used in beacons and tags) **or on separate chips** (e.g., a smartphone's host CPU with an external BLE controller, connected via HCI).

### 2.3 BLE Radio Channels — The Channel Map

The **2.4 GHz ISM band** is divided into **40 RF channels** for BLE (channels 0 to 39), each **2 MHz wide**:

- **Channels 37, 38, 39** → Designated as **Advertising Channels**.
- **Channels 0 to 36** → Designated as **Data Channels** (used only after a connection is established).

**Why exactly 3 advertising channels?**
The 3 advertising channels are carefully chosen (centered at **2402 MHz, 2426 MHz, 2480 MHz**) to avoid the three most commonly used Wi-Fi channels in the 2.4 GHz band (channels 1, 6, 11). This minimizes collision with Wi-Fi interference when a device first broadcasts its presence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** A frequent exam question is: "Why does BLE use 3 advertising channels and not 1 or 40?" The answer is the **trade-off between energy consumption** (scanning 40 channels consumes too much time and power) **and reliability** (1 channel is too vulnerable to interference; 3 channels strike the balance, with 100% probability that at least one is interference-free in a typical Wi-Fi environment).

### 2.4 BLE Device Roles & States (Link Layer State Machine)

A BLE device can be in one of **five Link Layer states**:

1. **Standby** — Default state. Not transmitting or receiving.
2. **Advertising** — The device broadcasts advertising packets (PDUs) on the 3 advertising channels.
3. **Scanning** — The device listens for advertising packets on the advertising channels.
4. **Initiating** — A specific scan state where the device has heard an advertisement and is sending a connection request.
5. **Connection** — Two devices exchange data using data channels. The **initiator becomes the master**, and the **advertiser becomes the slave**.

**Role Assignment (GATT/GAP context):**
- **Broadcaster / Observer** — One-way, connectionless communication (e.g., iBeacon, Eddystone).
- **Peripheral** — Connects to a central device; usually the low-power sensor.
- **Central** — Initiates connections; usually the smartphone or gateway.
- **GATT Server** — Holds the data (services & characteristics).
- **GATT Client** — Reads/writes the data on the server.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusion:** A device's Link Layer role (Master/Slave) is **independent** of its GATT role (Server/Client). A peripheral can be a GATT server, and a central can be a GATT client. This is a classic KTU 2-mark trap question.

### 2.5 GATT (Generic Attribute Profile) — The Data Hierarchy

GATT organizes data into a strict hierarchy:

```
Profile
   └── Service (identified by 128-bit or 16-bit UUID)
         ├── Characteristic (identified by UUID; the leaf data point)
         │     ├── Properties (Read, Write, Notify, Indicate, etc.)
         │     ├── Value (the actual data, 0–512 bytes)
         │     └── Descriptors (metadata, e.g., CCCD for notification)
         └── Characteristic
         └── Service
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UUID (Universally Unique Identifier):** All services and characteristics are identified by a UUID. The Bluetooth SIG assigns **16-bit "Short UUIDs"** to standard profiles (e.g., `0x180D` for Heart Rate Service, `0x2A37` for Heart Rate Measurement Characteristic). All other custom UUIDs are **128-bit**, following the standard UUID format (xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula or Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency band (BLE) | $f \in [2400,\ 2483.5]$ | MHz |
| Number of channels (BLE) | $N_{ch} = 40$ | dimensionless |
| Channel spacing | $\Delta f = 2$ | MHz |
| Symbol rate (LE 1M) | $R_s = 1$ | Msymbol/s |
| Bit rate (LE 1M) | $R_b = 1$ | Mbps |
| Bit rate (LE 2M) | $R_b = 2$ | Mbps |
| Bit rate (LE Coded, S=2) | $R_b = 1/2 = 500$ | kbps |
| Bit rate (LE Coded, S=8) | $R_b = 1/8 = 125$ | kbps |
| Modulation index (LE 1M) | $h = 0.5$ | dimensionless |
| Frequency deviation (LE 1M) | $f_d = 185$ kHz (min) to **250 kHz** (nominal) | kHz |
| Hopping rate (connected) | $R_h = 1600$ | hops/sec |
| Connection interval min | $T_{conn} = 7.5$ | ms |
| Connection interval max | $T_{conn} = 4000$ | ms |
| Connection event duration | variable, typically $\le 7.5$ ms for small payloads | ms |
| Channel index (data ch) | $ch_{idx} = (ch_{idx,\ prev} + \text{hopIncrement}) \pmod{37}$ | — |
| Data channel selection | Use only channels in the "Channel Map" | — |
| Max advertising payload (legacy) | $\vert P_{adv} \vert = 37$ | bytes |
| Max advertising payload (BLE 5 ext.) | $\vert P_{adv} \vert = 255$ | bytes |
| Max characteristic value | $\vert V \vert = 512$ | bytes (4.2+) |
| Transmit power | $P_{tx} \in [-20,\ +20]$ | dBm |
| Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ | dB |
| Energy per connection event (typ.) | $E_{evt} \approx I_{avg} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot t_{evt}$ | Joules |
| Battery life estimate | $T_{life} = \frac{Q_{bat}\ (\text{mAh})}{I_{avg}\ (\text{mA})}$ | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** Wherever the absolute value notation is needed (e.g., for payload size), use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX. Never use the raw pipe character `|` inside markdown tables.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** BLE-enabled pulse oximeters, glucose meters, and ECGs transmit small vitals every few seconds, allowing a battery life of 1–2 years on a single CR2032 coin cell.
- **Retail / Beacons:** Apple iBeacon and Google Eddystone use the advertising channels only (no connection) to broadcast a unique ID; the smartphone detects proximity and pushes a notification.
- **Smart Home:** BLE Mesh (introduced in 2017) allows light bulbs, locks, and thermostats to relay messages in a many-to-many topology.
- **Industrial IoT:** BLE 5.0 with the Coded PHY (S=8) achieves long range (up to 400 m LOS) at 125 kbps, suitable for sensor monitoring in factories.
- **Indoor Positioning:** BLE 5.1's direction-finding (Angle of Arrival / Angle of Departure) provides sub-meter accuracy for asset tracking in hospitals and warehouses.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time Required to Scan All BLE Channels

When a BLE device performs an **active scan**, it must listen on each of the three advertising channels. A critical engineering question is: *What is the minimum time required to scan all 3 advertising channels once?*

**Step 1: Identify the per-channel scan window.**
Each advertising channel is scanned for a duration of $\text{ScanWindow}$. The device sequentially visits the 3 channels, with $\text{ScanInterval}$ between the start of consecutive scans.

**Step 2: Determine the total scan cycle time.**
For a single full pass over all 3 advertising channels, the minimum total time is:

$$T_{scan,\ min} = 3 \times \text{ScanWindow}$$

(assuming the device performs back-to-back scans with no idle gap between the 3 channels).

**Step 3: Compute for a typical KTU numerical.**
Suppose $\text{ScanWindow} = 10$ ms. Then:

$$T_{scan,\ min} = 3 \times 10 = 30 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Energy implication.**
A scanning device keeps its radio on for the full $\text{ScanWindow}$. The average current drawn during scanning is approximately **$I_{sc} \approx 8$ mA**, while standby current is $\approx 1\ \mu\text{A}$. If a scanner does one full pass per second:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{30\text{ ms} \times 8\text{ mA} + 970\text{ ms} \times 0.001\text{ mA}}{1000\text{ ms}} \approx 0.241\text{ mA}$$

**Step 5: Battery life calculation.**
For a CR2032 coin cell with $Q_{bat} \approx 220$ mAh:

$$T_{life} = \frac{Q_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{220}{0.241} \approx 913 \text{ hours} \approx 38\text{ days}$$

This derivation shows why the **scan parameters are critical** in IoT device firmware.

### 3.2 Derivation: Frequency Hopping Algorithm

The BLE Link Layer uses a frequency-hopping algorithm to combat interference. The **data channel index** for the next connection event is computed as:

**Step 1: Define the unconnected channel selection.**

$$\text{nextDataChannel} = (\text{lastUnmappedChannel} + \text{hopIncrement}) \pmod{37}$$

**Step 2: Re-map to the connected channel set.**
The unmapped channel index (0–36) is then passed through a **channel remapping algorithm** that ensures it is mapped to one of the **37 valid data channels** (channels 0 to 36) that are marked as "used" in the **Channel Map**. If the resulting channel is marked as "bad" in the Channel Map, the algorithm re-shuffles:

$$f(n) = \frac{37 \times n}{2^{16}}$$

where $n$ is a 16-bit pseudo-random number and $f(n)$ is the next channel index after remapping.

**Step 3: Frequency to channel conversion.**
Once the channel index $k$ is known, the actual center frequency in MHz is:

$$f_{c}(k) = 2402 + 2k \text{ MHz},\quad k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 36\}$$

**Step 4: Example.**
Let $\text{hopIncrement} = 7$, and the previous unmapped channel = 5.

$$\text{next} = (5 + 7) \pmod{37} = 12$$

$$f_{c} = 2402 + 2 \times 12 = 2426 \text{ MHz}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: BLE Data Throughput vs Connection Interval

The **effective throughput** of a BLE connection is limited by the **Connection Interval** ($T_{conn}$) and the **Number of Packets per Connection Event** ($N_{pkt}$).

**Step 1: Maximum packets per interval.**
With a maximum PDU size of 251 bytes (BLE 4.2+) and 4-byte MIC, the max payload per packet is **247 bytes**. The number of packets in one connection event is:

$$N_{pkt} = \left\lfloor \frac{t_{evt}}{t_{slot}} \right\rfloor$$

where $t_{slot} = 80\ \mu s$ (time to transmit one packet) and $t_{evt}$ is the connection event duration, capped at $T_{conn}$.

**Step 2: Throughput equation.**

$$R_{th} = \frac{N_{pkt} \times 247 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{T_{conn}\ (\text{in seconds})}\ \text{bps}$$

**Step 3: Numerical example (BLE 4.2).**
Let $T_{conn} = 7.5$ ms and assume $N_{pkt} = 6$ (a typical assumption for 7.5 ms interval with 1M PHY):

$$R_{th} = \frac{6 \times 247 \times 8}{0.0075} = \frac{11\,856}{0.0075} \approx 1\,580\,800\ \text{bps} \approx 1.58\ \text{Mbps}$$

**Step 4: With LE 2M PHY (BLE 5.0), 7.5 ms interval, $N_{pkt} = 6$:**

$$R_{th} = \frac{6 \times 247 \times 8}{0.0075} \approx 1.58\ \text{Mbps (same payload, but 2× header efficiency)}$$

In practice, throughput saturates at the PHY rate. The 2M PHY simply halves the air time per packet, allowing **more packets per connection interval** and thus higher effective throughput.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Bluetooth Classic vs BLE

| Parameter | Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz ISM | 2.4 GHz ISM |
| Number of Channels | 79 (1 MHz each) | **40 (2 MHz each)** |
| Channel Hopping | 1600 hops/sec | 1600 hops/sec |
| Modulation | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | **GFSK only** |
| Peak Data Rate | 1 / 2 / 3 Mbps | **1 / 2 Mbps** |
| Typical Application | Audio streaming, file transfer | Sensor data, beacons |
| Current Draw (peak TX) | ~30 mA | **~15 mA** |
| Current Draw (idle) | ~1 mA | **~1 μA** |
| Connection Time | ~100 ms (typical) | **~3 ms** |
| Pairing Time | ~6 seconds | **< 100 ms** |
| Range | 10 m | **Up to 100 m+ (BLE 5.0 Coded)** |
| Coexistence (with Wi-Fi) | AFH | AFH + Channel Selection Algorithm #2 |
| Application Throughput | High (continuous) | Low (bursty) |
| Peak Current | High | ~Half of Classic |
| Max Battery Life (CR2032) | Days to weeks | **Months to years** |

### 3.5 Connection Establishment — Step-by-Step Sequence

The BLE connection setup uses a **3-packet handshake** on the advertising channels:

1. **ADV_IND** (Advertising Indication) — Sent by the **peripheral** (advertiser) on one of the 3 advertising channels, repeated on all 3 with a delay of $T_{adv} \le 10$ ms.
2. **SCAN_REQ** (Scan Request) — Sent by the **central** (initiator) on the same channel.
3. **SCAN_RSP** (Scan Response) — Sent by the peripheral; provides additional scan response data.
4. **CONNECT_IND** (Connection Indication) — Sent by the central to initiate a connection; contains the connection parameters (hop increment, channel map, connection interval, etc.).
5. **First Connection Event** — The connection is established. The peripheral sends a packet at the next connection event time on the first data channel.

**Total time for the entire handshake** (with the fastest advertising interval of 20 ms and a $T_{adv}$ of 7.5 ms on each of 3 channels):

$$T_{setup} \approx 3 \times 20 + 7.5 \times 3 + T_{conn} \approx 80\text{–}100\text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world note:** The connection setup time is significantly faster than Bluetooth Classic (which takes 100+ ms *plus* an SDP service discovery that can take seconds).

### 3.6 Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH) — Mathematical Basis

The AFH algorithm is a power-distribution method where some channels are marked as "bad" (e.g., due to Wi-Fi interference) and excluded from hopping. The **efficiency** of AFH is given by:

$$\eta_{AFH} = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_{good}$ is the number of good channels used and $N_{total} = 37$ data channels (in BLE).

If a Wi-Fi access point occupies 4 of the 37 channels:

$$\eta_{AFH} = \frac{33}{37} \times 100\% \approx 89.2\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Channel Selection Algorithm #2** (introduced in BLE 5.0) is more sophisticated than the classic AFH; it distributes channel usage more uniformly to avoid persistent interference from a single source.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, OEITT723, Module 2, Q.1.a]**

**Q1. Compare Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) and Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) in terms of channel count, peak current, and connection setup time.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bluetooth Classic | BLE |
|---|---|---|
| Number of RF Channels | 79 (1 MHz each) | **40 (2 MHz each)** |
| Peak TX Current | ~30 mA | **~15 mA** |
| Connection Setup | ~100 ms (typical) | **~3 ms (theoretical)** |
| Power Consumption (avg) | High | ~1/100 of Classic |

Bluetooth Classic is suitable for continuous high-throughput applications (audio streaming), while BLE is optimized for short, periodic, low-power data exchange (sensor telemetry). The 2 MHz channel spacing in BLE (vs. 1 MHz in Classic) halves the number of channels but improves robustness against multipath fading.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, OEITT723, Module 2, Q.1.c]**

**Q2. Define GATT. Explain the role of Services and Characteristics in the GATT data model.**

**Model Answer:**

**GATT (Generic Attribute Profile)** is a BLE-specific protocol that defines how two connected devices exchange data using a **client-server architecture**. The data is organized hierarchically as:

- **Profile** — A collection of one or more services.
- **Service** — A logical grouping of related data items. Each service has a **UUID** (16-bit for standard services, 128-bit for custom). Example: Heart Rate Service (`0x180D`).
- **Characteristic** — The leaf-level data container. It has a **value** (the actual data, 0–512 bytes), **properties** (Read, Write, Notify, Indicate, Broadcast), and optional **descriptors** (e.g., the CCCD descriptor at `0x2902` enables/disables notifications).

Example: The Heart Rate Service has a "Heart Rate Measurement" characteristic that the server can push (via Notifications) to the client every 1 second.

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEITT723, Module 2, Q.7.a/b]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the BLE protocol stack. List the functions of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the BLE connection establishment procedure with a sequence diagram. Explain the role of the 3 advertising channels. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Q.7(a):**

The BLE protocol stack is divided into three subsystems:

**1. Controller Subsystem:**
- **Physical Layer (PHY):** Operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. Defines 40 RF channels (2 MHz spacing). Handles radio modulation (GFSK), transmission/reception of bits, and TX power control.
- **Link Layer (LL):** Manages device discovery, advertising, scanning, connection setup, packet framing, frequency hopping (1600 hops/sec), and encryption at the bit level. Implements the 5 Link Layer states (Standby, Advertising, Scanning, Initiating, Connection).

**2. Host-Controller Interface (HCI):**
- A standard command interface (UART, SPI, USB) that allows the host to send HCI commands to the controller and receive HCI events. It abstracts the lower layers from the upper layers.

**3. Host Subsystem:**
- **L2CAP:** Provides logical channels, multiplexing, segmentation, and reassembly of larger packets.
- **ATT (Attribute Protocol):** The client-server data protocol. Defines the Attribute (a triplet of type, handle, and permissions) and the Read/Write/Notify/Indicate operations.
- **GATT (Generic Attribute Profile):** Organizes attributes into Services and Characteristics and defines the procedure for discovering, reading, writing, and notifying.
- **GAP (Generic Access Profile):** Defines device roles (Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central) and discovery/link-management procedures.
- **SMP (Security Manager Protocol):** Handles pairing, bonding, and encryption-key distribution.

*(See Diagram 4.1 above for the layered structure.)*

##### **Model Solution to Q.7(b):**

The BLE connection setup uses a **3-packet handshake** on the advertising channels:

1. **ADV_IND:** The peripheral broadcasts on the 3 advertising channels (37, 38, 39). The packet contains the device address, advertising data, and an optional `AdvData` field.

2. **SCAN_REQ:** The central sends a scan request on the same channel. It contains only the peripheral's device address.

3. **SCAN_RSP:** The peripheral responds with the optional scan-response data (additional info such as device name, manufacturer).

4. **CONNECT_IND:** Sent by the central. This packet terminates the advertising and includes all connection parameters (channel map, hop increment, connection interval, latency, timeout, CRC seed, etc.).

5. **Connection Event:** The peripheral now listens on the first data channel; once it hears the central, it acknowledges. The connection is established, and subsequent data exchange begins on the data channels (0–36).

**Role of the 3 advertising channels:**
- They are spaced far apart (2402, 2426, 2480 MHz) to minimize Wi-Fi coexistence issues.
- The advertiser rotates through them with a delay of $\le 10$ ms to ensure a scanner can find it within a single scan window.
- Only 3 channels are used (not 40) to minimize the scan time and thus the central's energy consumption during the discovery phase.
- The mathematical justification is that 3 channels provide a 100% probability that at least one is interference-free in a typical Wi-Fi coexistence environment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEITT723, Module 2, Q.7.c/d]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of GATT. With a diagram, describe the GATT data hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss BLE security: pairing, bonding, and the three pairing methods. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Q.7(c):**

**GATT (Generic Attribute Profile)** is the highest-level data-exchange protocol in the BLE stack. It defines a **client-server architecture** where a GATT client requests data from a GATT server.

**Data Hierarchy:**

```
Profile
└── Service (16-bit or 128-bit UUID)
    ├── Characteristic
    │   ├── Properties (Read, Write, Notify, Indicate, Broadcast, WriteWithoutResponse)
    │   ├── Value (0–512 bytes)
    │   └── Descriptors (CCCD, User Description, Presentation Format, etc.)
    ├── Characteristic
    └── Service (included)
```

**Operations supported:**
- **Discovery:** Discover all primary services, find included services, discover characteristics, discover descriptors.
- **Read/Write:** Read a characteristic value, write a value, read multiple values, signed writes.
- **Notifications and Indications:** The server can push data to the client without explicit request. Notifications are unacknowledged; Indications require an ACK from the client.
- **Server-Initiated Updates:** The GATT client must subscribe by writing to the **CCCD (Client Characteristic Configuration Descriptor, UUID 0x2902)** to enable notifications/indications.

**Example: Heart Rate Profile.**
- Service: Heart Rate (`0x180D`).
- Characteristic: Heart Rate Measurement (`0x2A37`) — **Notify** property.
- The sensor (server) periodically reads the heart rate and pushes a notification to the smartphone (client) every 1 second.
- The CCCD must be set to `0x0001` (Notify) before notifications can be received.

*(See Diagram 4.3 above for the visual hierarchy.)*

##### **Model Solution to Q.7(d):**

**BLE Security has three pillars:**

**1. Pairing:**
The process of creating a shared secret (the **STK**, Short-Term Key) between two devices. Pairing involves the exchange of TK (Temporary Key) and the creation of the STK.

**2. Bonding:**
If the two devices choose to "bond," the **LTK (Long-Term Key)** and other security keys are stored in non-volatile memory. The next connection can skip the full pairing and use the stored LTK directly (this is "reconnect with encryption").

**3. The Three Pairing Methods (LE Legacy Pairing):**

| Method | Use Case | Security |
|---|---|---|
| **Just Works** | No display or keyboard (e.g., beacons, headphones) | No MITM protection |
| **Passkey Entry** | One device displays a 6-digit code, the other types it | MITM protection (with OOB confirmation) |
| **Out-of-Band (OOB)** | Pairing via NFC, QR code, or another channel | Highest security |

**LE Secure Connections (BLE 4.2+):**
A newer pairing mechanism that uses **ECDH P-256** for key exchange, making the protocol resistant to passive eavesdropping and active MITM attacks. The three methods above are retained, but the key exchange is now based on public-key cryptography (FIPS-compliant).

**Encryption:**
The link is encrypted using **AES-CCM** with a 128-bit key. Encryption is enabled with the STK during pairing and the LTK for bonded reconnections.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **"BLE uses 128-bit AES"** — Not enough detail. You must say **AES-CCM-128** for confidentiality and integrity.
> 2. **"Pairing and bonding are the same"** — WRONG. Pairing creates a session key; bonding stores it for later. You will lose 1–2 marks for conflating them.
> 3. **"Just Works is secure"** — Misleading. Just Works provides encryption but **no MITM protection**; the name reflects the user experience, not the security level.
> 4. **Confusing the GATT role with the GAP role** — A peripheral can be a GATT server, and a central can be a GATT client. This role-mixing is a frequently tested 2-mark nuance.
> 5. **Forgetting the CCCD** — Notifications don't work until the CCCD is written. If your model answer skips this, you lose 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bluetooth Frequency Band:** **2.4 GHz ISM (2400–2483.5 MHz)**.
- **BLE Channels:** **40 channels**, each **2 MHz** wide.
  - **3 advertising channels** at 2402, 2426, 2480 MHz.
  - **37 data channels** for connected communication.
- **Modulation:** **GFSK** with modulation index **h = 0.5** (LE 1M PHY).
- **Bit Rate:** 1 Mbps (LE 1M, mandatory), 2 Mbps (LE 2M, optional), 125 kbps (LE Coded, S=8).
- **Frequency Hopping:** **1600 hops/sec** during a connection.
- **Connection Interval:** **7.5 ms to 4 s** (in steps of 1.25 ms).
- **Five Link Layer States:** Standby, Advertising, Scanning, Initiating, Connection.
- **GATT Hierarchy:** Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor.
- **UUIDs:** 16-bit for standard, 128-bit for custom.
- **Three Pairing Methods:** Just Works, Passkey Entry, OOB.
- **Pairing vs Bonding:** Pairing = session keys; Bonding = persistent storage of LTK.
- **LE Secure Connections (BLE 4.2+):** Uses **ECDH P-256** for key exchange.
- **Encryption:** **AES-CCM-128**.
- **Power Advantage:** BLE draws ~1/100th the average current of Bluetooth Classic.
- **BLE 5.0 Features:** LE 2M PHY, LE Coded PHY, extended advertising, periodic advertising.
- **BLE 5.1 Feature:** Direction Finding (AoA / AoD).
- **BLE 5.2 Feature:** LE Audio with LC3 codec and isochronous channels.
- **BLE 5.4 Feature:** Periodic Advertising with Responses (PAwR) and Electronic Shelf Label profile.
- **Coexistence with Wi-Fi:** Three advertising channels are placed to avoid Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, and 11.
- **GAP Roles:** Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central.
- **GATT Roles:** Server, Client.
- **CCCD (0x2902):** Must be written to enable notifications/indications on a characteristic.
- **Connection Setup Time:** ~3–100 ms (faster than Classic's >100 ms).
- **Typical Range:** 10 m (LE 1M), ~100 m+ (LE Coded PHY S=8).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 6LoWPAN Network Layer: IPv4/IPv6
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 6LoWPAN Network Layer: IPv4/IPv6

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)
