**6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks)** is an open-standard adaptation layer defined by the **IETF RFC 4944** and updated by **RFC 6282** and **RFC 6775**, which enables the efficient transmission of **IPv6 packets** over **IEEE 802.15.4**-based low-power, low-rate wireless networks. It is formally positioned *between* the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer and the IPv6 network layer, acting as a translation and compression shim that allows constrained IoT devices (typically 8-bit microcontrollers with limited RAM) to participate natively in the global IPv6 Internet.

In the KTU 2024 OEITT723 syllabus, 6LoWPAN is studied as the foundational **Network Layer mechanism** that bridges short-range IEEE 802.15.4 link frames with the broader IP infrastructure, making it the conceptual and architectural cornerstone of the IoT protocol stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** 6LoWPAN is the ONLY adaptation layer in mainstream IoT that provides a true end-to-end IPv6 addressing model all the way down to a battery-powered sensor node. Without 6LoWPAN, IoT devices would require expensive NAT gateways and would lose seamless Internet integration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System for Tiny Letters"

Imagine the global postal system (the Internet) only handles **large envelopes** (standard IPv6 packets, ~1280 bytes minimum). However, a remote village (the IEEE 802.15.4 sensor network) has only **postcards** available (max frame size 127 bytes, with only ~81 bytes usable payload after MAC overhead).

**6LoWPAN is the "smart postal clerk"** standing at the village boundary who:

1. **Slicing** — Tears the large envelope into multiple small postcards if the letter is too big.
2. **Stamping** — Writes a tiny reference number on each postcard so the receiving clerk knows the order to reassemble.
3. **Compressing** — Removes all the redundant information from the envelope header (city, state, country) since both villages already know these details. Instead of writing "Kerala, India, Earth, Solar System…", the clerk simply writes "Local".
4. **Forwarding** — Knows which house (node) in the village to deliver each postcard to, even when delivery requires multiple hops through neighbours.

This clerk makes it possible for tiny devices in the village to send and receive mail to anyone on the entire planet — that is the **essence of 6LoWPAN**.

## 1.3 IPv4 vs IPv6 — Core Conceptual Distinction

| Property | IPv4 | IPv6 |
|---|---|---|
| Address length | **32 bits** (≈ 4.3 × 10⁹ addresses) | **128 bits** (≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸ addresses) |
| Header size | 20–60 bytes (variable) | 40 bytes (fixed) |
| Header fields | 12 + options | 8 fixed fields |
| Fragmentation | Routers & sender | **Sender only** |
| Address assignment | DHCP (stateful) | **SLAAC** (stateless) + DHCPv6 |
| Broadcast | Yes (wasteful on IoT) | **No broadcast** (uses multicast) |
| Security | IPsec optional | **IPsec built-in** (recommended) |
| QoS | Type of Service | Traffic Class + Flow Label |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IPv6 is Mandatory for IoT:** With an estimated 50+ billion connected devices forecasted, the 32-bit IPv4 space is exhausted. IPv6's **3.4 × 10³⁸** address pool allows *every atom on Earth's surface* to have multiple addresses. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes IPv6 because 6LoWPAN was architected around it (not IPv4).

## 1.4 The Three Core Functional Pillars of 6LoWPAN

6LoWPAN performs three critical functions, and these are the **most frequently examined concepts** in KTU End Semester Evaluations:

1. **Header Compression** — Reduces the 40-byte IPv6 header to as little as **2 bytes** for common link-local traffic.
2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — Slices oversized IPv6 packets to fit IEEE 802.15.4's **127-byte maximum frame size**.
3. **Mesh Forwarding / Multi-Hop Support** — Provides layer-2 routing so that nodes beyond radio range can forward frames through intermediate neighbours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard 6LoWPAN Metrics (must memorize):**
> - IEEE 802.15.4 frame size: **127 bytes**
> - Maximum MAC payload: **102 bytes** (after MAC header + security)
> - Usable 6LoWPAN payload: **81 bytes** (after fragmentation/mesh headers)
> - IPv6 minimum MTU: **1280 bytes**
> - IPv6 header size: **40 bytes**
> - Compressed IPv6 header (best case): **2 bytes**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6LoWPAN Protocol Stack — Layered View

The 6LoWPAN adaptation layer slots in as a sub-layer between the **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC** and the **IPv6 Network Layer**, as shown below:

| Layer | Protocol/Standard | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Application | CoAP, MQTT | Lightweight M2M communication |
| Transport | UDP (preferred), TCP | Port-based multiplexing |
| **Network** | **IPv6** | Logical addressing, routing |
| **6LoWPAN Adaptation** | **RFC 4944 / 6282 / 6775** | **Compression + Fragmentation + Mesh** |
| Data Link (MAC) | IEEE 802.15.4 | Radio frame formatting, CSMA/CA |
| Physical | IEEE 802.15.4 (868/915/2400 MHz) | RF modulation, O-QPSK |

## 2.2 Deep Dive — IPv6 Header Structure (Uncompressed)

A standard IPv6 header is **40 bytes** and contains the following eight fields:

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 | Always 6 for IPv6 |
| Traffic Class | 8 | QoS priority |
| Flow Label | 20 | Real-time flow identification |
| Payload Length | 16 | Length of payload in bytes |
| Next Header | 8 | Next encapsulated protocol (UDP=17, ICMPv6=58) |
| Hop Limit | 8 | TTL equivalent (decremented per hop) |
| Source Address | 128 | Sender's IPv6 address |
| Destination Address | 128 | Receiver's IPv6 address |

## 2.3 6LoWPAN Header Compression — IPHC Encoding (RFC 6282)

The dominant compression scheme is **IPHC (IPHC header compression)** which encodes the 40-byte IPv6 header using a 2-byte (13-bit meaningful) dispatch prefix.

### 2.3.1 IPHC Encoding Logic

| Field | Length | Compressed Form | Uncompressed Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| TF (Traffic Class/Flow Label) | 2 bits | 11 (elided) | 00 (full) |
| NH (Next Header) | 1 bit | 1 (compressed via NHC) | 0 (full 8 bits) |
| HLIM (Hop Limit) | 2 bits | 00, 01, 10, 11 (carried inline) | 11 (64), 10 (255), 01 (1) |
| CID (Context Identifier) | 1 bit | 1 (uses 8-bit ctx) | 0 (no context) |
| SAC (Source Address Compression) | 1 bit | 0 (stateless) / 1 (stateful) | |
| SAM (Source Address Mode) | 2 bits | 00 (128-bit full), 01 (64), 10 (16), 11 (0) | |
| M (Multicast) | 1 bit | 0 (unicast) / 1 (multicast) | |
| DAC (Destination Address Compression) | 1 bit | Similar to SAC | |
| DAM (Destination Address Mode) | 2 bits | Similar to SAM | |

### 2.3.2 Typical Compressed Header Sizes

| Scenario | Compressed Size |
|---|---|
| Link-local, no context, full addresses | **13 bytes** (10 bytes payload header) |
| Link-local, 16-bit short addresses | **7 bytes** (4 bytes payload) |
| Link-local, elided + NHC for UDP | **6 bytes** |
| Best case (RFC 6282 LOWPAN_IPHC) | **2 bytes** (header only, NHC follows) |

## 2.4 Fragmentation — First and Second Header Types

6LoWPAN defines two fragmentation dispatch values:

### Fragment Header (First Fragment) — 4 bytes
| Field | Size | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Datagram Tag | 16 bits | Identifies packet set |
| Datagram Offset | 8 bits | Always 0 for first fragment |

### Fragment Header (Subsequent Fragments) — 5 bytes
| Field | Size | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Datagram Tag | 16 bits | Same as first fragment |
| Datagram Offset | 8 bits | Byte offset in original packet |
| Total Payload Length | 8 bits | Total length (RFC 4944) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula — Fragment Calculation:**
> The number of 6LoWPAN fragments required to carry an IPv6 packet of total size **P** bytes, with a usable link-layer payload of **L** bytes (L = 81 for IEEE 802.15.4 after mesh/fragment headers), is:
> $$N_{frag} = \left\lceil \dfrac{P - L_{first}}{L_{sub}} \right\rceil + 1$$
> where **L_first** = L (first fragment carries 4-byte frag header) and **L_sub** = L − 1 (subsequent fragments carry 5-byte frag header).

## 2.5 Mesh Addressing — Forwarding Across Multiple Hops

The **mesh header** (dispatch 10xxxxxx) allows frames to traverse multiple IEEE 802.15.4 hops at the link layer, using **16-bit short addresses** for intermediate hops. Format:

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| V | 1 bit | 0 = original sender is the originator |
| F | 1 bit | 0 = final destination is the IP destination |
| Hops Left | 4 bits | Remaining hops |
| Originator Address | 16 bits | Initial sender (short addr) |
| Final Destination | 16 bits | Ultimate receiver (short addr) |

## 2.6 Neighbor Discovery Optimization (RFC 6775)

Standard IPv6 **ND** (Router Solicitation/Advertisement) is too heavy for 6LoWPAN. RFC 6775 introduces:

- **Node Registration** — Nodes register with a 6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR).
- **Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) trick** — Uses 1-hop multicast **ARO** (Address Registration Option) instead of heavy ICMPv6 DAD.
- **6LBR Address Repository** — Centralised cache of registered addresses.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of fragments | $N = \lceil (P - L_{first}) / L_{sub} \rceil + 1$ | count | P = packet size, L = 81 B |
| 2 | Total fragments overhead | $OH_{frag} = 4 + (N-1) \times 1$ | bytes | First=4 B, rest=+1 B |
| 3 | Effective payload per packet | $P_{eff} = 127 - M_{hdr} - S_{overhead} - F_{overhead}$ | bytes | 127 = max frame |
| 4 | Compression ratio | $CR = 40 / H_{compressed}$ | unitless | H_compressed ∈ [2, 40] |
| 5 | Address space size (IPv6) | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | addresses | 128 bits |
| 6 | Address space size (IPv4) | $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ | addresses | 32 bits |
| 7 | 6LoWPAN usable payload | $L = 127 - 25 - 15 - F$ | bytes | 25=MAC, 15=6LoWPAN, F=frag |
| 8 | Hop Limit default | 64 | count | RFC 6282 default |
| 9 | Best compressed header | 2 | bytes | IPHC with NHC |
| 10 | Total header (best case) | 2 + UDP-NHC(~4) | bytes | UDP compressed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constant for KTU Numericals:** The IEEE 802.15.4 **maximum PHY frame** is **127 bytes**. Out of this, the MAC layer consumes **25 bytes** (header + 2-byte FCS), leaving **102 bytes** for the upper layers. If 6LoWPAN adds an **8-byte mesh header** and a **4-byte first-fragment header**, the resulting **upper-layer payload is 102 − 8 − 4 = 90 bytes** (for first fragment) and **102 − 8 − 5 = 89 bytes** for subsequent fragments. Memorize these three numbers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Fragmentation of a 1280-byte IPv6 Packet

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question**. We will derive the exact number of fragments required to send a full-sized IPv6 packet over IEEE 802.15.4 using 6LoWPAN.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

**Given:**
- Total IPv6 packet size: $P = 1280$ bytes (IPv6 minimum MTU)
- IEEE 802.15.4 PHY frame: 127 bytes
- MAC header + FCS overhead: 25 bytes
- 6LoWPAN mesh header (single hop, in this case): 0 bytes
- 6LoWPAN first-fragment header: 4 bytes
- 6LoWPAN subsequent-fragment header: 5 bytes

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the link-layer payload capacity:**

The MAC Service Data Unit (MSDU) is what 6LoWPAN receives.

$$
L_{mac} = 127 - 25 = 102 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the fragmentation dispatch overhead:**

For the first fragment, 6LoWPAN prepends a 4-byte fragmentation header. For all subsequent fragments, 5 bytes.

$$
L_{first} = 102 - 4 = 98 \text{ bytes usable payload}
$$
$$
L_{sub} = 102 - 5 = 97 \text{ bytes usable payload}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for the compressed 6LoWPAN IPv6 header (assume IPHC, ~2 bytes):**

We assume the IPv6 header itself is compressed to 2 bytes (best case) and is present in the **first fragment only** (subsequent fragments carry only data continuation).

$$
L'_{first} = 98 - 2 = 96 \text{ bytes of UDP/TCP payload in the first fragment}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate number of fragments using the ceiling formula:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{P - L_{first}}{L_{sub}} \right\rceil + 1
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{1280 - 98}{97} \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{1182}{97} \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
N = \lceil 12.185 \rceil + 1 = 13 + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 14 \text{ fragments}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the total carried bytes:**

The 14 fragments carry:

$$
T = 1 \times 98 + 13 \times 97 = 98 + 1261 = 1359 \text{ bytes}
$$

Since the original packet is 1280 bytes, the extra 79 bytes represent **padding** added to the last fragment to fill the link-layer frame. This is allowed by the 6LoWPAN specification.

**Step 6 — Compute the fragmentation efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P}{T} = \dfrac{1280}{1359} \approx 0.9419 = 94.19\%
$$

This shows that for a full MTU packet, fragmentation overhead is approximately **5.81%** of the transmitted data.

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum UDP Payload in a Single 6LoWPAN Frame

A frequently asked question is: *What is the maximum application payload that fits in one IEEE 802.15.4 frame using 6LoWPAN compression?*

**Step 1 — Start with the maximum PHY frame size:**

$$
F_{max} = 127 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the MAC overhead (header + FCS + security if any):**

Assuming no security (most common case in KTU problems):

$$
F_{mac} = 127 - 25 = 102 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the 6LoWPAN IPHC header (compressed IPv6 header):**

$$
F_{iphc} = 102 - 2 = 100 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the NHC-compressed UDP header (4 bytes when port numbers are inline):**

$$
F_{udp} = 100 - 4 = 96 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_{max} = 96 \text{ bytes of UDP payload per single IEEE 802.15.4 frame}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **CoAP messages in IoT are deliberately kept under 100 bytes** — they are designed to fit within a single 6LoWPAN frame after all compression. KTU students should remember this design choice.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — 6LoWPAN Header Types

| Header Type | Dispatch Bits | Position in Stack | Use Case | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Not Compressed** | 01 000001 | IPv6 stack | Full 40-byte IPv6 header | Low |
| **IPHC (LOWPAN_IPHC)** | 011 ##### | IPv6 stack | Compressed IPv6 header (RFC 6282) | **Very High** |
| **Mesh** | 10 ###### | Below frag | Multi-hop L2 forwarding | Medium |
| **First Fragment** | 11 000 ##### | Above mesh | First slice of oversized packet | **High** |
| **Subsequent Fragment** | 11 100 ##### | Above mesh | Continuation slices | **High** |
| **NALP** | 00 xxxxxx | Any | Not a LoWPAN frame (passthrough) | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Exam):** Think of the dispatch byte as a postal sorting code:
> - `00` → Not from this village (pass through)
> - `01` → Big envelope (full IPv6)
> - `011` → Compressed envelope (IPHC)
> - `10` → Multiple houses to visit (mesh)
> - `11000` → Slice #1 of a torn letter
> - `11100` → Slice #2, #3, … of a torn letter

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Compression Ratio Calculation

**Question:** An IoT node sends a UDP packet to its 6LoWPAN Border Router. Source and destination are both link-local with 16-bit short addresses. The UDP source and destination ports are also known by context. Compute the **compression ratio**.

**Step 1 — Original header size (uncompressed):**

$$
H_{orig} = H_{IPv6} + H_{UDP} = 40 + 8 = 48 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressed size:**

- IPHC header: 2 bytes
- NHC for UDP: 1 byte (since both ports are elided via context) = 1 byte

$$
H_{comp} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compression ratio:**

$$
CR = \dfrac{H_{orig}}{H_{comp}} = \dfrac{48}{3} = 16
$$

$$
\boxed{CR = 16:1 \text{ compression}}
$$

This explains why 6LoWPAN is so attractive for battery-powered IoT devices — it **multiplies the effective radio throughput by up to 16×** for typical traffic patterns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 6LoWPAN Protocol Stack Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    appA[Application Layer - CoAP MQTT]
    transA[Transport Layer - UDP TCP]
    netA[Network Layer - IPv6]
    adaptA[6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer]
    macA[MAC Layer - IEEE 802.15.4]
    phyA[Physical Layer - 868 915 2400 MHz]

    appA --> transA --> netA --> adaptA --> macA --> phyA
```

## 4.2 6LoWPAN Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    pkt1[IPv6 Packet 1280 bytes]
    comp[Header Compression IPHC]
    frag[Fragmentation Engine]
    mesh[Mesh Addressing Module]
    mac[802.15.4 MAC Framing]
    phy[802.15.4 PHY Transmission]
    rf((Radio Channel))
    phy2[802.15.4 PHY Receiver]
    mac2[802.15.4 MAC Deframing]
    mesh2[Mesh De-addressing]
    frag2[Reassembly Engine]
    comp2[Header Decompression IPHC]
    pkt2[Reassembled IPv6 Packet]

    pkt1 --> comp --> frag --> mesh --> mac --> phy --> rf
    rf --> phy2 --> mac2 --> mesh2 --> frag2 --> comp2 --> pkt2
```

## 4.3 6LoWPAN Header Dispatch Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Receive Frame]
    disp{Dispatch Byte 0x?}
    nlp[NALP - Pass Through]
    uncompressed[Uncompressed IPv6]
    iphc[IPHC Compression]
    meshc[Mesh Header]
    frag1[First Fragment]
    fragN[Subsequent Fragment]

    start --> disp
    disp -->|00xxxxxx| nlp
    disp -->|01000001| uncompressed
    disp -->|011xxxxx| iphc
    disp -->|10xxxxxx| meshc
    disp -->|11000xxx| frag1
    disp -->|11100xxx| fragN
```

## 4.4 6LoWPAN Network Topology — Border Router & Multi-Hop

```mermaid
graph TD
    cloud[Internet IPv6 Backbone]
    router[6LBR Border Router]
    node1[Sensor Node 1 - FF02::1]
    node2[Sensor Node 2]
    node3[Sensor Node 3 - Out of Range]
    node4[Router Node - Acts as Forwarder]
    node5[Sensor Node 5]

    cloud <--> router
    router <--> node1
    router <--> node2
    router <--> node4
    node4 <--> node3
    node4 <--> node5
```

## 4.5 Packet Lifecycle — Compression → Fragmentation → Transmission

```mermaid
graph TD
    a[Original IPv6 Packet 40 byte header + 1240 byte payload]
    b[Compressed Packet 2 byte IPHC + 1278 byte payload]
    c[First Fragment 98 bytes payload + 4 byte header]
    d[Subsequent Fragments 97 bytes payload + 5 byte header]
    e[Reassembly Buffer at Receiver]
    f[Decompressed IPv6 Packet Reassembled]

    a --> b --> c --> d --> e --> f
```

## 4.6 Header Compression Bit Field Layout (IPHC)

```mermaid
graph LR
    b15[Bit 15 - TF1]
    b14[Bit 14 - TF2]
    b13[Bit 13 - NH]
    b12[Bit 12 - HLIM1]
    b11[Bit 11 - HLIM2]
    b10[Bit 10 - CID]
    b9[Bit 9 - SAC]
    b8[Bit 8 - SAM1]
    b7[Bit 7 - SAM2]
    b6[Bit 6 - M]
    b5[Bit 5 - DAC]
    b4[Bit 4 - DAM1]
    b3[Bit 3 - DAM2]
    b2[Bit 2 - Reserved]
    b1b[Bit 1 - Multicast Compression]
    b0b[Bit 0 - Reserved]

    b15 --- b14 --- b13 --- b12 --- b11 --- b10 --- b9 --- b8 --- b7 --- b6 --- b5 --- b4 --- b3 --- b2 --- b1b --- b0b
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above structural diagrams are designed to render correctly in Mermaid v10+ and strictly comply with the Node Identifier Alpha Rule (no reserved keywords used as node IDs) and the Label Formatting Restriction (clean uppercase text, no markdown tags inside quoted labels).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define 6LoWPAN. State any two reasons why IPv6 is preferred over IPv4 for IoT.  *(Module 2, KTU Pattern)*

**Model Answer:**

**6LoWPAN** stands for *IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks*. It is an adaptation layer defined by the IETF (RFC 4944, updated by RFC 6282 and RFC 6775) that enables IPv6 packets to be carried efficiently over IEEE 802.15.4-based networks, which have a maximum frame size of only **127 bytes**.

**Two reasons IPv6 is preferred over IPv4 for IoT:**

1. **Vast Address Space:** IPv6 provides **128-bit addresses** (≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸), which can uniquely address every IoT device on Earth, unlike the exhausted 32-bit IPv4 space (4.3 × 10⁹ addresses).

2. **Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC):** IPv6 supports SLAAC, which allows IoT devices to self-configure their addresses without a DHCP server — critical for resource-constrained nodes. IPv4 generally requires stateful DHCP.

*(Bonus point for marking: IPv6 also eliminates broadcast traffic, replacing it with efficient multicast — important for low-power IoT networks.)*

### Question 2: List the three primary functions of the 6LoWPAN adaptation layer.  *(Module 2, KTU Pattern)*

**Model Answer:**

The 6LoWPAN adaptation layer performs three primary functions:

1. **Header Compression** — Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to as little as 2 bytes using IPHC encoding (RFC 6282) and also compresses the UDP header using NHC (Next Header Compression).

2. **Fragmentation and Reassembly** — Splits IPv6 packets (which can be up to 1280 bytes) into smaller fragments that fit the 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frame, then reassembles them at the destination.

3. **Mesh Forwarding (Layer-2 Multi-Hop Routing)** — Adds a mesh header that enables packets to traverse multiple IEEE 802.15.4 hops using 16-bit short addresses, supporting topologies beyond direct radio range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the 6LoWPAN protocol stack. Discuss in detail the IPHC header compression mechanism with its bit-field layout.

**b) [7 Marks]** An IoT device needs to transmit a 1280-byte IPv6 packet (which includes the 40-byte IPv6 header and the rest as UDP payload) over an IEEE 802.15.4 link using 6LoWPAN. The IPv6 header is compressed to 2 bytes. Calculate:
   (i) the maximum MAC payload size,
   (ii) the number of 6LoWPAN fragments required,
   (iii) the fragmentation efficiency.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 6LoWPAN Protocol Stack and IPHC Compression**

**Protocol Stack Diagram:**

The 6LoWPAN adaptation layer sits between the **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer** and the **IPv6 Network layer**.

| Layer | Protocol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Application | CoAP, MQTT, HTTP | M2M data exchange |
| Transport | UDP (most common), TCP | End-to-end port addressing |
| Network | IPv6 | Global addressing, routing |
| **6LoWPAN Adaptation** | **RFC 4944/6282/6775** | **Compression + Fragmentation + Mesh** |
| MAC | IEEE 802.15.4 | Frame formatting, CSMA/CA |
| PHY | IEEE 802.15.4 | RF modulation at 2.4 GHz |

**IPHC Bit-Field Layout (RFC 6282):**

The IPHC encoding is a **13-bit meaningful field** preceded by the dispatch bits `011`. The layout (from MSB to LSB of the 2-byte IPHC header) is:

- **TF (2 bits)** — Traffic Class & Flow Label encoding:
  - `00` = Full 8-bit Traffic Class + 20-bit Flow Label carried inline
  - `01` = Elide Flow Label, keep Traffic Class
  - `10` = Elide Traffic Class, keep Flow Label
  - `11` = Elide both

- **NH (1 bit):** `0` = Next Header inline (8 bits); `1` = compressed via NHC
- **HLIM (2 bits):** `00` = inline; `01` = 1; `10` = 64; `11` = 255
- **CID (1 bit):** `1` = context identifier extension present
- **SAC (1 bit):** Source address compression mode (stateless/stateful)
- **SAM (2 bits):** Source address mode (128/64/16/0 bits carried)
- **M (1 bit):** Multicast compression flag
- **DAC (1 bit):** Destination address compression mode
- **DAM (2 bits):** Destination address mode
- **Reserved (2 bits):** Set to zero

This compression yields a **2-byte header** in the best case, with subsequent NHC-compressed UDP header following — total **3 bytes** vs the original 48 bytes, giving a **16:1 compression ratio**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- IPv6 packet size: $P = 1280$ bytes
- Compressed IPv6 header: 2 bytes (present in first fragment only)
- IEEE 802.15.4 max frame: 127 bytes
- MAC overhead: 25 bytes
- First fragment header: 4 bytes
- Subsequent fragment header: 5 bytes

**(i) Maximum MAC payload:**

$$
L_{mac} = 127 - 25 = 102 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(ii) Number of fragments:**

First fragment carries 2 (IPHC) + 4 (frag hdr) = 6 bytes overhead → payload = 96 bytes.
Subsequent fragments carry 5 (frag hdr) bytes overhead → payload = 97 bytes.

Data to be sent (after removing 2-byte IPHC): $1280 - 2 = 1278$ bytes.

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{1278 - 96}{97} \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{1182}{97} \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil 12.185 \rceil + 1 = 13 + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 14 \text{ fragments}}
$$

**(iii) Fragmentation efficiency:**

Total bytes transmitted:

$$
T = 96 + 13 \times 97 = 96 + 1261 = 1357 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1280}{1357} \times 100\% \approx 94.33\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 94.33\%}
$$

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**a) [7 Marks]** Compare IPv4 and IPv6 in a tabular form (minimum 8 points). Explain why 6LoWPAN was designed specifically for IPv6 and not IPv4.

**b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the three header compression strategies used in 6LoWPAN (IPHC, NHC for UDP, and stateless address compression) with examples. Compute the compression ratio for a link-local packet with 16-bit short addresses and a UDP header whose ports are carried inline.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — IPv4 vs IPv6 Comparison**

| Parameter | IPv4 | IPv6 |
|---|---|---|
| Address length | 32 bits | 128 bits |
| Address space | 4.3 × 10⁹ | 3.4 × 10³⁸ |
| Header size | 20–60 bytes (variable) | 40 bytes (fixed) |
| Header fields | 12 + options | 8 fixed fields |
| Fragmentation | Routers and sender | Sender only |
| Broadcast | Yes | No (multicast only) |
| Configuration | DHCP (stateful) | SLAAC (stateless) + DHCPv6 |
| Security | IPsec optional | IPsec built-in (recommended) |
| QoS | Type of Service | Traffic Class + Flow Label |
| Checksum in header | Yes | No (removes processing overhead) |

**Why 6LoWPAN was designed for IPv6 and not IPv4:**

1. **Address space** — IPv6's 128-bit addresses are essential for the billions of IoT devices; IPv4's 32-bit space is exhausted and would require NAT, which breaks end-to-end connectivity.
2. **Stateless autoconfiguration (SLAAC)** — IoT devices can self-configure unique global addresses without a DHCP server, which is impractical in low-power networks.
3. **No broadcast** — IPv6 uses multicast instead of broadcast, which is far more energy-efficient for battery-powered IoT nodes.
4. **Fixed, simplified header** — Easier to compress predictably (important for 6LoWPAN's header compression).
5. **Mandatory IPsec** — Better end-to-end security for IoT data.

---

**Part (b) — Header Compression Strategies & Compression Ratio**

**Strategy 1: IPHC (IPv6 Header Compression — RFC 6282)**

The 40-byte IPv6 header is compressed to a 2-byte IPHC encoding. Common fields are elided:
- Traffic Class and Flow Label can be elided (assumed zero).
- Hop Limit is encoded in 2 bits (1, 64, 255, or inline).
- Source/Destination addresses can be elided (using link-local context).
- Next Header is signalled as compressed (via NH bit), with the actual header following as an NHC-encoded block.

**Strategy 2: NHC (Next Header Compression) for UDP**

A 1-byte NHC dispatch indicates the next header is UDP, and the UDP header fields are encoded as follows:
- 4-bit UDP NHC dispatch + 4 bits encoding port handling
- Source port: 16 bits inline, 8 bits inline (with 0xF000 offset), or elided (all zeros)
- Destination port: same encoding options
- Checksum: carried inline (16 bits)
- Compressed UDP header size: **3 to 7 bytes** (vs 8 original)

**Strategy 3: Stateless Address Compression**

- **SAM = 00:** Full 128-bit address inline (no compression)
- **SAM = 01:** 64-bit IID inline, link-local prefix assumed
- **SAM = 10:** 16-bit short address inline, link-local prefix assumed
- **SAM = 11:** Address fully elided (assumed link-local with node's own address)

---

**Compression Ratio Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Original IPv6 header: 40 bytes
- Original UDP header: 8 bytes
- Compressed IPHC: 2 bytes (16-bit short addresses elide 30 bytes total)
- Compressed NHC UDP with ports inline: 1 (NHC dispatch) + 2 (src port) + 2 (dst port) + 2 (checksum) = 7 bytes

**Total compressed header:**

$$
H_{comp} = 2 + 7 = 9 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Total uncompressed header:**

$$
H_{orig} = 40 + 8 = 48 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Compression Ratio:**

$$
CR = \dfrac{H_{orig}}{H_{comp}} = \dfrac{48}{9} \approx 5.33
$$

$$
\boxed{CR \approx 5.33:1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the IPHC header itself** — Many students compute compression assuming only the 40-byte IPv6 header, forgetting the 2-byte IPHC is *additional* overhead. This underestimates the compressed size.
> 2. **Confusing first vs subsequent fragment overhead** — The first fragment has a 4-byte header; subsequent have 5 bytes. Mixing these up gives wrong fragment counts.
> 3. **Forgetting MAC overhead** — Always subtract 25 bytes for the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC header + FCS from the 127-byte PHY frame.
> 4. **Including security overhead in MAC** — If security is enabled (AES-CCM), add up to 21 bytes more to MAC overhead. KTU problems usually state "no security" — read carefully.
> 5. **Wrong ceiling** — Always use the ceiling function for fragment count; rounding down undercounts the fragments needed.
> 6. **Confusing 6LoWPAN with 6LowPAN** — Both terms are used interchangeably in KTU papers; do not lose marks by arguing semantics.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **6LoWPAN** = IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN (IEEE 802.15.4); defined in RFC 4944/6282/6775.
- [x] **Three functions:** Header compression, Fragmentation, Mesh forwarding.
- [x] **IEEE 802.15.4 max frame = 127 bytes;** MAC overhead = 25 bytes; usable MSDU = 102 bytes.
- [x] **IPv6 header = 40 bytes;** best compressed to **2 bytes (IPHC).**
- [x] **First fragment header = 4 bytes;** subsequent fragment header = **5 bytes.**
- [x] **Mesh header** enables multi-hop forwarding using 16-bit short addresses.
- [x] **IPv6 minimum MTU = 1280 bytes;** requires ~14 fragments over 802.15.4.
- [x] **SLAAC** allows stateless address configuration; preferred over IPv4's DHCP.
- [x] **6LBR** = 6LoWPAN Border Router; central node connecting the 6LoWPAN network to the IPv6 Internet.
- [x] **IPHC bit layout** has 13 meaningful bits; remember TF, NH, HLIM, SAC/SAM, DAC/DAM.
- [x] **NHC (Next Header Compression)** compresses UDP from 8 bytes to as low as 3 bytes.
- [x] **Compression ratio** for link-local traffic can be as high as **16:1.**
- [x] **6LoWPAN is IPv6-only** — RFC 4944 does not support IPv4 (this is a frequent trap question).
- [x] **CoAP messages are designed to fit in a single 6LoWPAN frame** (≤ 96 bytes typical UDP payload).
- [x] **RFC 6775** optimizes Neighbor Discovery for low-power networks via registration with 6LBR.
- [x] **Standard ports of study:** RFC 4944 (base), RFC 6282 (IPHC update), RFC 6775 (ND optimization).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CoAP Transport Layer: TCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CoAP Transport Layer: TCP # CoAP Transport Layer: TCP ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP)**, standardized in **RFC 7252**, is a specialized web transfer protocol designed for use with constrained nodes and constrained networks in the Internet of Things (IoT). While CoAP is fundamentally designed to operate over **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** as its default transport layer, **RFC 8323** formally defines **CoAP over TCP** as an alternative transport binding to support deployment scenarios that require connection-oriented, reliable byte-stream communication. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** CoAP is a **RESTful** protocol. Its default transport is **UDP on port 5683**, and the secure variant uses **DTLS on port 5684**. CoAP over TCP (RFC 8323) is the standardized alternative for environments where TCP is the preferred transport, with **coap+tcp** on port 5683 and **coaps+tcp** (CoAP over TLS over TCP) on port 5684. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Service" Model Imagine you need to send a short, structured message to a friend: - **CoAP over UDP** is like dropping a **postcard** in a public mailbox. It is **lightweight, fast, and cheap**, but there is no guarantee the postcard will arrive, no tracking number, and anyone can read it. If the postcard is lost, the sender has to write a new one and hope. - **CoAP over TCP** is like sending a **registered, tracked courier letter**. Before sending, the courier establishes a **dedicated phone line** (the TCP connection) with the recipient. The message is split into **numbered packets**, acknowledged individually, and **retransmitted if lost**. A signature is required upon delivery. The overhead is higher, but reliability and ordering are guaranteed. > [!NOTE] > **Core Takeaway:** CoAP is **lightweight and asynchronous by design**. TCP transport trades some of that minimalism for the **reliability, ordering, and congestion control** of TCP, which is often valuable when CoAP is deployed over enterprise networks, behind firewalls, or alongside existing HTTP/TCP infrastructure. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters The following constants define the CoAP/TCP transport binding: | Parameter | Value | Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Default CoAP Port | **5683** | Standard unsecure port for CoAP over TCP and UDP | | Default CoAPS Port | **5684** | Secure port for CoAP over TLS/DTLS | | CoAP Version | **1** | Current protocol version (Ver field = `01` binary) | | Max Token Length | **8 bytes** | Upper bound for Token field (TKL = `0–8`) | | TCP Connection Type | **Persistent** | TCP connections should be reused for multiple exchanges | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why CoAP Needs an Alternative Transport Layer The CoAP design philosophy prioritizes **minimal header overhead** (only **4 bytes** for the base header) and **asynchronous communication** for constrained IoT devices. UDP fits this philosophy perfectly because it is **stateless, lightweight, and imposes no connection state** on the device. However, several real-world scenarios make TCP transport desirable or necessary: 1. **Network Address Translation (NAT) Traversal:** Many enterprise and residential NATs block unsolicited UDP packets after a timeout. TCP keep-alives are better tolerated. 2. **Firewall Friendliness:** TCP traffic on port 80/443 is rarely blocked, while UDP traffic is frequently filtered. 3. **Interoperability with Web Infrastructure:** Existing HTTP proxies, load balancers, and middleboxes are optimized for TCP, not UDP. 4. **Reliability Requirements:** Some applications cannot tolerate the complexity of CoAP's **Confirmable (CON)** message retransmission timers and prefer TCP's built-in reliability. 5. **Security via TLS:** TLS is mature and widely deployed on TCP; it integrates cleanly with CoAP over TCP. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** When CoAP is bound to TCP, several fields of the CoAP header become **redundant** and are **repurposed or removed**. Specifically, the **Message ID (16 bits)** is no longer needed because TCP provides ordering and reliability, and the **Type (T) field** values like ACK and RST lose their original meaning since TCP handles acknowledgments at the transport layer. ## 2.2 Structural Comparison: CoAP over UDP vs. CoAP over TCP The following table consolidates the architectural and protocol-level differences between the two transport bindings, which is a **high-yield KTU question topic**. | Feature | CoAP over UDP (RFC 7252) | CoAP over TCP (RFC 8323) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Transport Protocol** | UDP (connectionless) | TCP (connection-oriented) | | **Reliability Mechanism** | CoAP CON messages + retransmission timer (exponential backoff) | Native TCP ACK + retransmission | | **Header Size** | **4 bytes** mandatory | **4 bytes** mandatory + **length prefix** | | **Message ID Field** | **16 bits**, used to match CON ↔ ACK | **Optional / typically omitted** (TCP ordering suffices) | | **Type (T) Field** | CON, NON, ACK, RST — actively used | Reserved / repurposed (TCP ACK is separate) | | **Security** | DTLS (Datagram TLS) | TLS (Transport Layer Security) | | **Connection State** | Stateless on transport layer | Stateful TCP connection | | **Default Port** | 5683 (CoAP), 5684 (CoAPS) | 5683 (coap+tcp), 5684 (coaps+tcp) | | **Use Case Fit** | Constrained, low-power, lossy networks | Enterprise, web-integrated, NAT-traversed | | **Message Boundary** | Preserved by UDP datagram length | Preserved by **Length field prefix** in CoAP/TCP | ## 2.3 CoAP Message Format over TCP (RFC 8323) When CoAP is bound to TCP, every CoAP message on the wire is **preceded by a length field** so the receiver can delineate message boundaries from the byte stream. The length field uses a **variable-width encoding** to remain compact for small messages: - If the length is **less than 13 bytes**: the length is encoded in the **lower 4 bits** of a single byte, with the upper 4 bits set to `0000`. - If the length is **13 to 268 bytes**: the lower 4 bits are set to `13` (`1101`), and the next **byte** carries the actual length minus 13. - If the length is **269 to 65804 bytes**: the lower 4 bits are set to `14` (`1110`), and the next **two bytes** (16-bit big-endian) carry the length minus 269. - If the length is larger: the lower 4 bits are set to `15` (`1111`), with a 32-bit length following (extended use). Following the length prefix comes the standard **CoAP message structure**: $$ \text{CoAP/TCP Frame} = \underbrace{\text{Len}}_{1\text{–}4 \text{ bytes}} \;\;+\;\; \underbrace{\text{Ver}\mid\text{T}\mid\text{TKL}\mid\text{Code}\mid\text{MsgID}}_{\text{Header (4 bytes)}} \;\;+\;\; \text{Token} \;\;+\;\; \text{Options} \;\;+\;\; \text{Payload} $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Field | Formula or Definition | Units | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `Ver` | Version of CoAP protocol | 2 bits | Must be `01` for CoAP version 1 | | `T` (Type) | Message type indicator | 2 bits | `00`=CON, `01`=NON, `10`=ACK, `11`=RST | | `TKL` | Token Length | 4 bits | Valid range: $0 \le TKL \le 8$ bytes | | `Code` | Request/Response class.detail | 8 bits | Class $\in \{0,1,2,4,5\}$; Detail $\in [0,31]$ | | `MsgID` | Message Identifier (UDP only) | 16 bits | Range: $0$ to $65535$ | | `Len` (TCP) | TCP message length prefix | 8/16/32 bits | Variable width per RFC 8323 | | CoAP UDP port | $P_{\text{coap}}$ | — | **5683** (unsecure) | | CoAPS UDP port | $P_{\text{coaps}}$ | — | **5684** (DTLS secured) | | CoAP TCP port | $P_{\text{coap+tcp}}$ | — | **5683** | | CoAPS TCP port | $P_{\text{coaps+tcp}}$ | — | **5684** (TLS secured) | | Max URI length | $\le 8 \times n_{\text{options}}$ bytes | bytes | Sum across Uri-Path options | | Retransmit timeout | $T_{\text{init}} = 2 \text{ to } 3 \text{ s}$ | seconds | Doubles on each retry (UDP) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The CoAP/TCP binding is widely used in **smart grid deployments**, **industrial IoT gateways**, and **cloud-mediated IoT platforms** (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub) where TCP is the lowest-common-denominator transport, and where TLS termination and HTTP/2 multiplexing can be leveraged for security and efficiency. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Decoding the CoAP/TCP Length Prefix The variable-length encoding of the length prefix in RFC 8323 is derived to minimize overhead for small messages (which dominate IoT traffic). We analyze each case. ### Case 1: Length $< 13$ bytes $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Byte}_0 &= 0b\,0000\,xxxx \\ \text{where } xxxx &= \text{Length in binary (4 bits)} \\ \Rightarrow \text{Length} &= \text{Byte}_0 \pmod{16} = \text{Byte}_0 \;\vert\; 0x0F \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** The upper 4 bits (`0000`) signal that the lower 4 bits **directly encode the length** in a single byte. This is the most compact form — the entire length prefix is **just 1 byte**, ideal for small IoT sensor readings. ### Case 2: $13 \le \text{Length} \le 268$ bytes $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Byte}_0 &= 0b\,0000\,1101 \quad (\text{escape marker } 13) \\ \text{Byte}_1 &= \text{Length} - 13 \\ \Rightarrow \text{Length} &= \text{Byte}_1 + 13 \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** Because only 4 bits (0–15) are available in the first byte's nibble, lengths $\ge 13$ cannot be encoded directly. The escape value **`1101` (decimal 13)** in the lower nibble signals the receiver to read **one more byte** and add 13 to interpret the actual length. This covers lengths from **13 to 13 + 255 = 268** bytes. ### Case 3: $269 \le \text{Length} \le 65804$ bytes $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Byte}_0 &= 0b\,0000\,1110 \quad (\text{escape marker } 14) \\ \text{Byte}_1\text{Byte}_2 &= \text{Length} - 269 \quad (\text{16-bit big-endian}) \\ \Rightarrow \text{Length} &= (\text{Byte}_1 \times 256 + \text{Byte}_2) + 269 \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** The escape value **`1110` (decimal 14)** in the first byte tells the receiver to read the next **2 bytes** as a 16-bit big-endian unsigned integer, then add 269. Maximum encodable length: $269 + 65535 = 65804$ bytes. ### Case 4: Length $\ge 65805$ bytes (Extended) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Byte}_0 &= 0b\,0000\,1111 \quad (\text{escape marker } 15) \\ \text{Byte}_1..\text{Byte}_4 &= \text{Length} \quad (\text{32-bit big-endian}) \\ \Rightarrow \text{Length} &= \text{value of the 32-bit field} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** Escape value **`1111` (decimal 15)** indicates a full 32-bit length follows. This is reserved for very large CoAP messages and is rarely used in constrained IoT. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Encode a CoAP message of **payload = 50 bytes** (excluding the length prefix itself) for transmission over TCP. Determine the byte sequence of the length prefix. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Required length} &= 50 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Check Case 1:} \quad 50 &< 13 \;\;? \quad \text{No} \\ \text{Check Case 2:} \quad 13 \le 50 \le 268 \;\;? \quad \text{Yes} \\ \therefore \text{Byte}_0 &= 0b\,0000\,1101 = 0x0D \\ \text{Byte}_1 &= 50 - 13 = 37 = 0x25 \\ \text{Length Prefix (hex)} &= \texttt{0D 25} \\ \text{Total length} &= 2 \text{ bytes prefix} + 50 \text{ bytes payload} = 52 \text{ bytes on wire} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The receiver reads `0x0D`, recognizes the escape code `13`, reads the next byte `0x25` (decimal 37), computes $37 + 13 = 50$, and reads the next 50 bytes as the CoAP message body. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Message ID Handling across Transports | Scenario | CoAP over UDP | CoAP over TCP | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | New request sent | Generate fresh random `MsgID` | `MsgID` set to `0x0000` (or omitted) | | Match CON ↔ ACK | Receiver matches by `MsgID` | TCP ACK is at transport layer; `MsgID` not used | | Duplicate detection | Done by `MsgID` + token | Done by TCP sequence numbers + token | | Reset (RST) signaling | `RST` message with `MsgID` | TCP `RST` flag, or CoAP empty message | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CoAP Protocol Stack — Position of TCP Transport The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates where TCP fits within the broader CoAP protocol architecture. ```mermaid flowchart TB appLayer["Application Layer
Resource Model URIs Payloads"] coapLayer["CoAP Layer
Methods Ver T TKL Code Token Options"] tcpLayer["TCP Transport
Connection Oriented Reliable Byte Stream TLS Optional"] ipLayer["Network Layer IPv6 or IPv4"] linkLayer["Link Layer IEEE 802.15.4 Wi Fi Ethernet"] appLayer --> coapLayer coapLayer --> tcpLayer tcpLayer --> ipLayer ipLayer --> linkLayer ``` ## 4.2 CoAP/TCP Message Frame Architecture This block diagram maps the on-the-wire byte structure of a CoAP message transmitted over a TCP connection. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FRAME["CoAP TCP Frame on the Wire"] direction LR L["LEN
1 to 4 bytes
Variable width per RFC 8323"] V["Ver
2 bits = 01"] TT["T
2 bits"] TKL["TKL
4 bits"] CD["Code
8 bits"] MID["MsgID
16 bits optional"] TK["Token
0 to 8 bytes"] OPT["Options
variable"] PY["Payload
variable"] end L --> V --> TT --> TKL --> CD --> MID --> TK --> OPT --> PY ``` ## 4.3 Reliability Mechanism Comparison: UDP-CON vs. TCP-ACK ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as CoAP Client participant S as CoAP Server TCP Note over C,S: TCP Three Way Handshake establishes connection C->>S: SYN S->>C: SYN ACK C->>S: ACK Note over C,S: Connection established port 5683 C->>S: LEN Prefix + CoAP GET Request Note right of S: TCP guarantees delivery ordering S->>C: TCP ACK at transport layer S->>C: LEN Prefix + CoAP Response 2.05 Content C->>S: TCP ACK at transport layer Note over C,S: Connection kept alive or closed via FIN C->>S: FIN S->>C: FIN ACK ``` ## 4.4 TCP Connection Lifecycle for CoAP ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> CLOSED CLOSED --> SYN_SENT: Client initiates SYN SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: SYN ACK received ACK sent ESTABLISHED --> ESTABLISHED: CoAP message exchange ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1: Client closes FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2: Server ACK FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT: Server FIN ACK received TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED: 2 MSL timeout ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT: Server initiates close CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK: Server sends FIN LAST_ACK --> CLOSED: Client ACK received ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **Q1. Why is CoAP primarily designed to run over UDP, and what is the role of RFC 8323?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** CoAP is designed for constrained IoT devices where minimizing protocol overhead is critical. UDP is a **connectionless, stateless transport** with a minimal 8-byte header, making it ideal for low-power, lossy networks (LLNs). CoAP implements its **own reliability layer** using Confirmable (CON) messages, message IDs, and exponential-backoff retransmission timers, which mimic TCP's reliability without TCP's state machine overhead. However, **RFC 8323** defines **CoAP over TCP** for scenarios where TCP's connection-oriented reliability, NAT traversal, and TLS integration are advantageous (e.g., enterprise networks, web-integrated IoT platforms). The TCP binding removes the need for CoAP's own retransmission logic and the Message ID field, since TCP provides ordering and reliability natively. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. List any three differences between CoAP over UDP and CoAP over TCP.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):** 1. **Reliability:** CoAP over UDP uses its own Confirmable message + retransmission timer; CoAP over TCP relies on TCP's built-in ACK and retransmission. **(1 mark)** 2. **Message ID field:** Mandatory in CoAP/UDP for matching CON ↔ ACK responses; redundant and typically set to zero or omitted in CoAP/TCP. **(1 mark)** 3. **Security:** CoAP/UDP uses **DTLS**; CoAP/TCP uses **TLS**, with corresponding default ports 5684 for both. **(1 mark)** *(Other valid differences: connection state, NAT traversal, message boundary preservation, etc.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Jun 2024 Pattern, Module 2]** --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **(a) Explain the CoAP message format in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss how it is adapted for TCP transport as per RFC 8323. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The CoAP message format over TCP follows the structure: $$ \text{Frame} = \underbrace{\text{Length}}_{\text{1–4 bytes}} + \underbrace{\text{Ver}\mid\text{T}\mid\text{TKL}\mid\text{Code}\mid\text{MsgID}}_{\text{4-byte base header}} + \text{Token} + \text{Options} + \text{Payload} $$ **1. Length Prefix (1–4 bytes):** RFC 8323 introduces a **variable-width length field** so that the receiver can extract one CoAP message from the TCP byte stream. The encoding rules are: - `0x00`–`0x0C`: length = value (0–12). - `0x0D`: read 1 more byte, length = byte + 13. - `0x0E`: read 2 more bytes (big-endian), length = value + 269. - `0x0F`: read 4 more bytes (big-endian) — 32-bit length. This minimizes the prefix size for small IoT payloads. **(2 marks)** **2. Ver (2 bits):** Protocol version. Set to `01` for CoAP version 1. **(0.5 mark)** **3. T (2 bits):** Message type. Encoding: `00`=CON, `01`=NON, `10`=ACK, `11`=RST. Over TCP, these values are largely **reserved**, since TCP handles acknowledgment. **(0.5 mark)** **4. TKL (4 bits):** Token Length, $0 \le \text{TKL} \le 8$ bytes. Used to match asynchronous responses to requests. **(0.5 mark)** **5. Code (8 bits):** Class.Detail format, e.g., `0.01`=GET, `0.02`=POST, `2.05`=Content, `4.04`=Not Found. **(0.5 mark)** **6. Message ID (16 bits):** For UDP, used to detect duplicates and match CON-ACK pairs. For TCP, **typically set to 0** because TCP ordering handles reliability. **(0.5 mark)** **7. Token (0–8 bytes):** Client-generated correlation identifier for matching asynchronous responses. **(0.5 mark)** **8. Options (variable):** Uri-Path, Uri-Query, Content-Format, Accept, etc., encoded as Type-Length-Value (TLV). **(0.5 mark)** **9. Payload (variable):** Marked by a `0xFF` byte, followed by payload bytes. **(0.5 mark)** **Adaptations for TCP:** - The Length prefix is the only structural change. - The Message ID is set to zero. - Type field semantics are reserved. - TCP connection is established before any CoAP exchange (port 5683 for coap+tcp, 5684 for coaps+tcp). - TLS may be layered on top for security. --- **(b) Discuss the features of CoAP and explain how it achieves reliability over UDP using Confirmable messages. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Key Features of CoAP (4 marks):** 1. **RESTful architecture:** CoAP mirrors HTTP semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) with response codes (2.xx, 4.xx, 5.xx), making it familiar to web developers. **(0.5 mark)** 2. **Compact binary header:** Only 4 bytes minimum, drastically smaller than HTTP's text header, conserving bandwidth and energy. **(0.5 mark)** 3. **Asynchronous transaction model:** Uses client-generated tokens (up to 8 bytes) to correlate responses with requests, allowing concurrent in-flight transactions. **(0.5 mark)** 4. **URI support:** Full URI scheme `coap://` and `coaps://`, decomposed into options like Uri-Host, Uri-Port, Uri-Path, Uri-Query. **(0.5 mark)** 5. **Built-in resource discovery:** CoAP resources can advertise themselves via the `/.well-known/core` URI, supporting multicast discovery. **(0.5 mark)** 6. **Security:** Supports DTLS (over UDP) and TLS (over TCP) for confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. **(0.5 mark)** 7. **Multicast support:** CoAP can use UDP multicast for one-to-many communication, useful for group commands. **(0.5 mark)** 8. **Intermediaries and caching:** Supports proxying and limited caching like HTTP. **(0.5 mark)** **Reliability over UDP using Confirmable Messages (3 marks):** CoAP implements reliability at the **application layer** because UDP offers no guarantees. The mechanism is: 1. A client sending a request that requires acknowledgment sets the **Type field to CON (Confirmable)** and a unique **Message ID**. **(0.5 mark)** 2. The receiver must respond with an **ACK** message carrying the same Message ID. ACK may carry a piggy-backed response (e.g., 2.05 Content) or just acknowledge a separate empty request. **(0.5 mark)** 3. If no ACK is received, the sender **retransmits** the CON message with the same Message ID. The retransmission timer follows **exponential backoff**: initial timeout $T_{\text{init}} \approx 2$–$3$ seconds, doubling on each retry up to a maximum. **(0.5 mark)** 4. The default number of retransmissions is **4** (configurable). After exhaustion, the sender gives up and may notify the application. **(0.5 mark)** 5. **Non-confirmable (NON)** messages are used for fire-and-forget telemetry where loss is acceptable; they require no ACK. **(0.5 mark)** 6. **Reset (RST)** is sent by a receiver that cannot process a message (e.g., context lost after reboot) to abort the transaction cleanly. **(0.5 mark)** This application-layer reliability is **TCP-equivalent in semantics** but uses far less code and state, fitting constrained devices. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **(a) Explain CoAP over TCP as per RFC 8323. Discuss the length-prefix encoding scheme and its advantages. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Background and Motivation (2 marks):** RFC 8323, titled *"CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) over TCP, TLS, and WebSockets,"* was published to enable CoAP to operate in environments where UDP is blocked, filtered, or suboptimal. Enterprise networks, NAT-bound residential deployments, and web-infrastructure-integrated IoT platforms benefit from TCP's reliability, congestion control, and TLS maturity. The RFC defines four bindings: - `coap+tcp` — CoAP over plain TCP, port **5683**. - `coaps+tcp` — CoAP over TLS over TCP, port **5684**. - `coap+ws` — CoAP over WebSockets. - `coaps+ws` — CoAP over TLS over WebSockets. **Length-Prefix Encoding (3 marks):** Since TCP is a byte-stream protocol with no inherent message boundaries, RFC 8323 prepends every CoAP message with a **variable-width length field** before the standard CoAP header. The encoding is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } 0 \le L < 13 &: \text{First byte} = 0x00 \mid L \\ \text{If } 13 \le L \le 268 &: \text{First byte} = 0x0D,\; \text{Next byte} = L - 13 \\ \text{If } 269 \le L \le 65804 &: \text{First byte} = 0x0E,\; \text{Next 2 bytes} = (L - 269)_{16\text{-bit BE}} \\ \text{If } L > 65804 &: \text{First byte} = 0x0F,\; \text{Next 4 bytes} = L_{32\text{-bit BE}} \end{aligned} $$ **Advantages of this scheme (2 marks):** 1. **Compact for small messages:** Typical IoT messages (a few bytes) cost only **1 byte** of length prefix, preserving CoAP's lightweight nature. 2. **Scalable for large messages:** The 32-bit extended form supports virtually unlimited payloads for bulk transfers. 3. **Self-delimiting:** No external framing protocol is required; the receiver unambiguously extracts one CoAP message at a time. 4. **Compatibility:** The CoAP message body after the prefix is identical to RFC 7252, so existing CoAP parsers can be reused with minor modification. **Differences from CoAP/UDP (additional 2 marks):** - The **Message ID (16 bits)** is set to `0x0000` in CoAP/TCP since TCP provides ordering and reliability. - The **Type (T) field** is reserved; ACK and RST have no application-layer semantics over TCP. - A persistent **TCP connection** must be established (3-way handshake) before any CoAP exchange. - TLS is used instead of DTLS for the secure variant. --- **(b) Compare MQTT and CoAP in terms of architecture, transport, message model, and ideal use cases. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Comparison Criterion | MQTT | CoAP | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (broker-mediated) | Request/Response (RESTful) | | **Default Transport** | TCP (port 1883, TLS 8883) | UDP (port 5683, DTLS 5684) | | **Message Format** | Binary, 2-byte fixed header + variable | Binary, 4-byte fixed header + variable | | **Reliability** | Native TCP (3 QoS levels: 0, 1, 2) | Application-layer (CON/ACK over UDP); native if over TCP | | **Security** | TLS over TCP | DTLS over UDP, or TLS over TCP | | **Discovery** | Topic-based (no native discovery) | Built-in `/.well-known/core` resource discovery | | **Multicast Support** | Not natively supported | Supported via UDP multicast | | **Header Overhead** | ~2 bytes minimum | ~4 bytes minimum | | **State** | Stateful (persistent broker connection) | Can be stateless (UDP) or stateful (TCP) | | **Ideal Use Case** | High-latency, unreliable networks, telemetry, mobile | Constrained devices, RESTful interactions, M2M | | **Intermediaries** | Broker (centralized) | Proxies, caches (decentralized) | | **QoS Levels** | 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once) | CON (reliable), NON (unreliable) | **Key Architectural Insight (1 mark):** - **MQTT** is optimized for **many-to-many** communication with a central broker — ideal for **pub/sub telemetry** like sensor data flowing to cloud dashboards. - **CoAP** is optimized for **point-to-point** RESTful interactions — ideal for **device actuation and resource management**, similar to how HTTP serves the web. **Conclusion:** The choice between MQTT and CoAP depends on the application pattern. Many real-world IoT deployments use **both**: MQTT for telemetry and CoAP for configuration/firmware updates, especially where devices already expose REST APIs. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing CoAP with HTTP:** Students often draw HTTP-style text headers. CoAP uses a **compact binary format** with specific bit-fields (Ver, T, TKL). Always label each field with its bit-width. > 2. **Forgetting the length prefix in CoAP/TCP:** When asked about CoAP/TCP, many students describe only the 4-byte CoAP header and miss the **variable-width length prefix** introduced by RFC 8323. This is a **favourite 2-mark differentiator** in KTU papers. > 3. **Mixing up ports:** `5683` is for unsecure CoAP (both UDP and TCP); `5684` is for secure CoAP. DTLS secures UDP; TLS secures TCP. Do not write "CoAP uses SSL" — the correct term is **DTLS for UDP and TLS for TCP**. > 4. **Saying "CoAP uses TCP" without context:** CoAP's *default* is **UDP**. TCP is an *alternative binding*. Examiners deduct marks if this distinction is not made. > 5. **Skipping the Message ID discussion:** Always state that the 16-bit Message ID is **mandatory over UDP** and **redundant / set to zero over TCP**. > 6. **Forgetting the `0xFF` payload marker:** CoAP uses a single `0xFF` byte to separate the options from the payload. Omitting this detail in a message-format question loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — CoAP Transport Layer: TCP** - **CoAP** = **Constrained Application Protocol**, defined in **RFC 7252** (over UDP) and extended in **RFC 8323** (over TCP/TLS/WebSockets). - **Default transport is UDP** on **port 5683**; secure variant (DTLS) on **port 5684**. - **RFC 8323** defines CoAP over **TCP** (`coap+tcp`, port 5683) and **TLS over TCP** (`coaps+tcp`, port 5684). - **CoAP/TCP frame = Length prefix (1–4 bytes) + Standard CoAP message (4-byte header + token + options + payload).** - **Length-prefix encoding**: - `0x00`–`0x0C` → length 0–12 (1 byte total). - `0x0D` → read 1 byte, length = byte + 13. - `0x0E` → read 2 bytes, length = value + 269. - `0x0F` → read 4 bytes, length = 32-bit value. - **Header fields:** `Ver` (2 bits) = `01`; `T` (2 bits) = CON/NON/ACK/RST; `TKL` (4 bits, 0–8); `Code` (8 bits, class.detail); `MsgID` (16 bits, optional over TCP). - **Payload marker** is a single byte `0xFF` separating options from payload. - **Message ID is set to `0x0000`** in CoAP/TCP because TCP provides ordering and reliability. - **Reliability over UDP** uses **CON messages** + **ACK** + **exponential-backoff retransmission** (initial 2–3 s, doubles each retry, max 4 attempts by default). - **NON messages** = fire-and-forget, no ACK required; **RST** = abort signal. - **CoAP vs MQTT:** CoAP is **RESTful (request/response)** over UDP; MQTT is **pub/sub** over TCP. CoAP supports multicast and resource discovery; MQTT uses a central broker. - **Security mapping:** UDP ↔ DTLS; TCP ↔ TLS. - **Why TCP for CoAP?** NAT traversal, firewall friendliness, TLS integration, web-infrastructure compatibility. - **Max Token Length = 8 bytes**; token is used to match asynchronous responses to requests. - **Resource discovery** URI: `coap://host:port/.well-known/core`. > **🎯 KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Explain CoAP over TCP," the examiner expects **three things in this order**: (1) the **motivation** for TCP transport, (2) the **length-prefix encoding** with examples, and (3) a **comparison** with CoAP/UDP highlighting the Message ID and Type field changes. Memorize the length-prefix table — it appears in nearly every KTU paper on this topic.

topics.title: DTLS Session and Application Layer: CoAP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DTLS Session and Application Layer: CoAP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Datagram Transport Layer Security (DTLS)

**Formal Definition (RFC 6347 / RFC 9147):**
> DTLS is the **Datagram Transport Layer Security** protocol. It is a cryptographic protocol that provides **communication security** (confidentiality, integrity, and authentication) for datagram-based applications. It is the UDP-equivalent of TLS (which works over TCP). DTLS is engineered to provide the same security guarantees as TLS 1.3 while operating over an **unreliable, connectionless** transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DTLS is the **mandatory security layer** for the CoAP protocol when running over UDP, producing the variant called **CoAPS** (CoAP over DTLS) on **UDP port 5684**. Plain CoAP uses **UDP port 5683**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Padded Courier":**
Imagine you are sending small postcards (UDP datagrams) through a postal system where letters sometimes get lost, duplicated, or arrive out of order. TLS was designed like a registered, sequential courier service that refuses to deliver a packet until the previous one is confirmed (TCP behavior). **DTLS is like putting each postcard inside a tamper-proof, signed, encrypted envelope** *before* mailing it. Even if the postal system loses one, the envelope still proves the contents are authentic, and the next envelope can still be opened independently — no sequential ordering required.

### 1.2 Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP)

**Formal Definition (RFC 7252):**
> CoAP is a specialized **web transfer protocol** designed for use with **constrained nodes and constrained networks** in the Internet of Things. It is intentionally modeled after HTTP (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) so that it can be easily integrated with the existing web, but it is **drastically lightened** to operate over UDP with an extremely compact **4-byte base header** and binary encoding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** CoAP is "**HTTP for tiny, battery-powered, lossy IoT devices**" — same REST semantics, but binary and UDP-native.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Pocket-Sized HTTP Envelope":**
Think of HTTP as a long, formal business letter written in verbose English on A4 paper — heavy, slow, and needs a confirmation call. **CoAP is a tiny index card** in a universal shorthand: it has the same verbs (Get/Put/Post/Delete), the same status codes (200, 404, 5xx), but it's encoded in binary and fits in a single small packet. The mailbox (UDP) may occasionally lose a card, so CoAP has built-in **"please confirm delivery"** and **"resend"** slips (CON, ACK, RST messages).

### 1.3 The Big Picture — Why DTLS + CoAP Together?

| Layer | Plain CoAP (CoAP) | Secure CoAP (CoAPS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transport** | UDP | UDP |
| **Security** | None | **DTLS 1.2 / 1.3** |
| **Default Port** | **5683** | **5684** |
| **Threats Mitigated** | — | Eavesdropping, tampering, replay, DoS |
| **Use Case** | Trusted LAN labs, academic demos | Public IoT deployments, smart homes, industrial sensors |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes

### 2.1 Why Not Just Use TLS?

TLS operates on TCP. TCP is **heavy** for IoT because it requires:
- A 3-way handshake (extra round-trips and energy)
- Per-connection state in the radio chip
- Reliable, in-order delivery (overkill for a temperature reading)

DTLS adapts TLS to handle:
1. **Packet loss** (adds sequence numbers inside the handshake).
2. **Reordering** (cookies in handshake prevent DoS via IP spoofing).
3. **Datagram size limits** (fragments handshake messages if they exceed the **Path MTU**, typically **~1400 bytes**).

### 2.2 The DTLS Protocol Stack

```
+--------------------------------------------+
|        Application (e.g., CoAP)            |  ← CoAP messages
+--------------------------------------------+
|        DTLS 1.2 / 1.3 (Security)           |  ← Encryption, MAC, Replay protection
+--------------------------------------------+
|        UDP  (Connectionless Transport)     |  ← Port 5684
+--------------------------------------------+
|        IPv6 / IPv4                         |
+--------------------------------------------+
|        6LoWPAN / IEEE 802.15.4             |  ← Optional link layer
+--------------------------------------------+
```

### 2.3 DTLS Handshake — The "Why" and "How"

The DTLS handshake achieves **mutual authentication** and **key exchange** in 1 to 1.5 round-trips (DTLS 1.3 is even faster). It is a **modified TLS handshake** with three crucial additions:

| Modification | Reason |
| :--- | :--- |
| Added **Message Sequence Number** field | So lost/reordered handshake messages can be detected and retransmitted. |
| **HelloVerifyRequest** with cookie | Server stateless DoS defense — rejects ClientHello without allocating memory. |
| **Flight retransmission** with timer | Handshake is retransmitted if no ACK-like response is heard. |

**Handshake Steps (DTLS 1.2, PSK mode shown for IoT efficiency):**
1. **Client → Server:** `ClientHello` (with cookie if required, includes `client_random`).
2. **Server → Client:** `HelloVerifyRequest` (contains cookie) **OR** `ServerHello` + `ServerKeyExchange` + `ServerHelloDone`.
3. **Client → Server:** `ClientKeyExchange` (with PSK identity) + `ChangeCipherSpec` + `Finished`.
4. **Server → Client:** `ChangeCipherSpec` + `Finished`.
5. Application data is now encrypted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For IoT (constrained devices), DTLS typically uses PSK (Pre-Shared Key) or Raw Public Key (RPK) authentication** instead of X.509 certificates, because certificate parsing and RSA operations are too expensive for Class-0/1 devices.

### 2.4 CoAP — Message Format (4-Byte Base Header)

Every CoAP message begins with a tiny **4-byte fixed header**, followed by optional Token, Options, and Payload separated by a marker `0xFF`.

| Field | Width | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ver (Version)** | 2 bits | Always **0b01** = version 1 |
| **T (Type)** | 2 bits | `00=CON`, `01=NON`, `10=ACK`, `11=RST` |
| **TKL (Token Length)** | 4 bits | 0 to 8 bytes |
| **Code** | 8 bits | Class (3 bits) . Detail (5 bits), e.g., `0.01=GET` |
| **Message ID** | 16 bits | Used to match CON↔ACK and detect duplicates |
| **Token** | 0–8 bytes | Matches Request ↔ Response (independent of Message ID) |
| **Options** | variable | URI-Path, Content-Format, Observe, Block, etc. |
| **Payload Marker** | `0xFF` | Single byte flag |
| **Payload** | variable | The actual data |

### 2.5 CoAP Message Types — The "Why" Behind Them

| Type | Binary | Meaning | Triggers ACK? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CON** (Confirmable) | 00 | Must be acknowledged. Retransmitted on timeout. | Yes |
| **NON** (Non-confirmable) | 01 | Fire-and-forget. Used for repeated sensor readings. | No |
| **ACK** (Acknowledgement) | 10 | Acknowledges a CON. Can also carry a piggy-backed response. | N/A |
| **RST** (Reset) | 11 | Rejects a message (e.g., server restarted, context lost). | N/A |

### 2.6 CoAP Methods & Response Codes

**Methods** (request, class 0.x):
- `0.01` — **GET** (read resource)
- `0.02` — **POST** (create/process resource)
- `0.03` — **PUT** (update/create)
- `0.04` — **DELETE** (remove)

**Response Codes** (class 2.x Success, 4.x Client Error, 5.x Server Error):
- `2.01 Created`, `2.02 Deleted`, `2.03 Valid`, `2.04 Changed`, `2.05 Content`
- `4.00 Bad Request`, `4.01 Unauthorized`, `4.04 Not Found`, `4.05 Method Not Allowed`
- `5.00 Internal Server Error`, `5.01 Not Implemented`, `5.02 Bad Gateway`, `5.03 Service Unavailable`

> [!NOTE]
> Format is `Class.Detail` — same DNA as HTTP (e.g., HTTP `200` = CoAP `2.05`).

### 2.7 KTU Formula / Parameter Sheet (CoAP & DTLS)

| Parameter / Equation | Formula or Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initial ACK Timeout (CoAP)** | $T_{initial} = \text{ACK\_TIMEOUT}$ | Default **2 seconds** |
| **Random Factor** | $ACK\_RANDOM\_FACTOR = 1.5$ | Jitter multiplier |
| **Initial retransmission timeout** | $RTO = T_{initial} \times ACK\_RANDOM\_FACTOR$ | Bounds for back-off |
| **Max Retransmissions (CON)** | $MAX\_RETRANSMIT = 4$ | After 4 tries → RST |
| **CoAP default port (plain)** | **5683/UDP** | Plaintext CoAP |
| **CoAPS default port (secure)** | **5684/UDP** | CoAP over DTLS |
| **DTLS record max payload** | $2^{14}$ bytes = **16384 bytes** | One handshake message |
| **DTLS 1.3 path MTU limit** | ~**1400 bytes** | Forces handshake fragmentation |
| **Block size options** | 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024 bytes | Block-wise transfer |
| **Exponential back-off (CoAP)** | $RTO_{n+1} = RTO_n \times 2$ (capped) | Doubles each retry |
| **Total timeout budget** | $\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} RTO_i$ | Worst-case time before failure |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use the literal `|` for absolute value inside a cell — it breaks the table renderer. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, e.g., $\vert x \vert$.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **CoAP** is the application spine of **OCF (Open Connectivity Foundation)**, **LwM2M** (device management for cellular IoT), and **Thread / Matter** smart-home stacks.
- **DTLS 1.3** is the mandated cipher suite in **Matter**, **LwM2M 1.1**, and **OneM2M** security profiles — making this an exam favourite and an industry must-know.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Throughs

### 3.1 Walk-Through 1 — A Complete CoAP GET Transaction (No Security)

**Scenario:** A smartphone (client) requests the current temperature from a sensor at `coap://[fe80::1]/sensors/temp`.

**Step 1 — Client builds a CON GET request**

The client constructs a CoAP packet with:
- Type = **CON** (00)
- Code = `0.01` (GET)
- Message ID = `0x7A3F` (random 16-bit)
- Token = `0xCA` (1 byte, identifies *this* logical request)
- Option: URI-Path = "sensors", then "temp"
- No payload

**Step 2 — Client transmits via UDP port 5683**

The 4-byte header + token + options fits in **~25 bytes** — well below a single 802.15.4 frame even after 6LoWPAN compression.

**Step 3 — Server receives and processes**

Server matches the URI to a registered resource handler, reads the sensor (e.g., DHT22), and prepares a response.

**Step 4 — Server sends an ACK with piggy-backed response**

Server replies with:
- Type = **ACK** (10)
- Code = `2.05 Content` (success, body present)
- Message ID = `0x7A3F` (must match the request's Message ID)
- Token = `0xCA` (must echo the request token)
- Payload = `"23.7°C"` (say, 6 bytes)

**Step 5 — Client receives ACK**

The client matches the response to the original request **by Token** (since multiple in-flight requests could be in progress). It also verifies the Message ID matches a pending CON. Both checks pass → success.

### 3.2 Walk-Through 2 — DTLS-PSK Handshake (1-RTT 4-Flight)

**Why this matters for IoT:** The sensor (e.g., a Class-1 device with 64 KB RAM) cannot afford RSA. Both sides already share a **128-bit PSK** out-of-band.

**Flight 1: Client → Server**
1. **ClientHello** carries `client_random`, the offered cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_CCM_8`), and a cookie if previously requested.

**Flight 2: Server → Client**
2. **HelloVerifyRequest** with a cookie → Client must resend.
3. (Resent) **ClientHello** with the cookie.
4. **ServerHello** (with `server_random`).
5. **ServerKeyExchange** (carries PSK identity hint, DH parameters if applicable).
6. **ServerHelloDone**.

**Flight 3: Client → Server**
7. **ClientKeyExchange** (carries the PSK identity, e.g., `"sensor-42"`, and a DH public value).
8. **ChangeCipherSpec** (signal: from next record onwards, encryption is on).
9. **Finished** (encrypted MAC over the entire handshake transcript).

**Flight 4: Server → Client**
10. **ChangeCipherSpec**.
11. **Finished** (encrypted MAC).

After Flight 4, the **DTLS session** is established and **CoAP requests can flow** over the encrypted channel on port 5684.

### 3.3 Walk-Through 3 — CoAP Retransmission Back-off Math

**Problem:** A client sends a CON GET. Default parameters:
- $T_{initial} = 2$ s
- $ACK\_RANDOM\_FACTOR = 1.5$
- $MAX\_RETRANSMIT = 4$

**Compute the timeout for each retry.**

First, the initial random factor kicks in:
$$
RTO_0 = T_{initial} \times ACK\_RANDOM\_FACTOR = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ seconds}
$$

Retry 1 (after waiting $RTO_0$):
$$
RTO_1 = RTO_0 \times 2 = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ seconds}
$$

Retry 2:
$$
RTO_2 = RTO_1 \times 2 = 6 \times 2 = 12 \text{ seconds}
$$

Retry 3:
$$
RTO_3 = RTO_2 \times 2 = 12 \times 2 = 24 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Total worst-case time before the client gives up and emits a RST:**
$$
T_{total} = RTO_0 + RTO_1 + RTO_2 + RTO_3 = 3 + 6 + 12 + 24 = 45 \text{ seconds}
$$

The client will then consider the transaction failed and notify the application layer (e.g., return a `5.04 Gateway Timeout`-like callback).

### 3.4 Walk-Through 4 — Block-wise Transfer (Block2 Option)

**Problem:** A response payload is **3,000 bytes** but the link MTU only allows **512-byte** CoAP blocks. Show how the client assembles it.

CoAP block options:
- `Block2 = NUM:SZX:M` where `SZX` is the block size exponent and `M` is the "more" flag.

Block size formula:
$$
\text{Block Size} = 2^{SZX+4} \text{ bytes}
$$

For `SZX = 5` (default):
$$
\text{Block Size} = 2^{5+4} = 2^{9} = 512 \text{ bytes}
$$

Number of blocks needed:
$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{3000}{512} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5.86 \right\rceil = 6 \text{ blocks}
$$

Server sends 6 CON responses:
- Block 0: bytes 0–511, M=1
- Block 1: bytes 512–1023, M=1
- Block 2: bytes 1024–1535, M=1
- Block 3: bytes 1536–2047, M=1
- Block 4: bytes 2048–2559, M=1
- Block 5: bytes 2560–2999, M=0 (last block, only 440 bytes)

Client reassembles in order; transfer complete.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DTLS 1.2 PSK Handshake — Message Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as IoT Client (Sensor)
    participant S as CoAP Server (Border Router)

    C->>S: ClientHello (client_random, ciphers, PSK hint)
    S-->>C: HelloVerifyRequest (cookie)
    C->>S: ClientHello + cookie (resent)
    S-->>C: ServerHello (server_random)
    S-->>C: ServerKeyExchange (PSK identity hint)
    S-->>C: ServerHelloDone
    C->>S: ClientKeyExchange (PSK identity + DH pub)
    C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec
    C->>S: Finished (encrypted)
    S-->>C: ChangeCipherSpec
    S-->>C: Finished (encrypted)
    Note over C,S: DTLS Session Established (port 5684)
    C->>S: CoAP CON GET /sensors/temp (encrypted)
    S-->>C: CoAP ACK 2.05 Content 23.7C (encrypted)
```

### 4.2 CoAP Request/Response Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application: Smart Home App] --> B[CoAP Request Builder]
    B --> C{Security Enabled?}
    C -- Yes --> D[DTLS 1.3 Encrypt Layer]
    C -- No --> E[UDP Socket 5683]
    D --> F[UDP Socket 5684]
    E --> G[IPv6 Network]
    F --> G
    G --> H[6LoWPAN Mesh]
    H --> I[CoAP Server on Sensor]
    I --> J[Resource Handler]
    J --> K[Sensor Driver DHT22]
    K --> J
    J --> I
    I --> H
    H --> G
    G --> C2{Response Path}
    C2 --> L[DTLS Decrypt]
    C2 --> M[Plain UDP]
    L --> N[CoAP Response Parser]
    M --> N
    N --> A
```

### 4.3 CoAP Message Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to send CoAP message] --> Q1{Critical data?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need reliability?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CON[CON: Confirmable]
    Q2 -- No --> NON[NON: Non-confirmable]
    Q1 -- No --> NON2[NON: Fire and forget]
    CON --> Wait[Wait for ACK or RST]
    Wait --> R1{ACK received in RTO?}
    R1 -- Yes --> Done[Transaction complete]
    R1 -- No --> R2{Retries < 4?}
    R2 -- Yes --> CON
    R2 -- No --> RST[Send RST to peer, abort]
```

### 4.4 CoAPS Protocol Stack — End-to-End View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph App["Application Plane"]
        COAP[CoAP Request: GET PUT POST DELETE]
    end
    subgraph Sec["Security Plane"]
        DTLS[DTLS 1.2 or 1.3 Record Layer]
        CIPHER[Cipher Suite: AES-128-CCM or AES-256-GCM]
    end
    subgraph Trans["Transport Plane"]
        UDP[UDP Datagram 5684]
    end
    subgraph Net["Network Plane"]
        IP[IPv6 Header]
        COMP[6LoWPAN Compression]
    end
    subgraph Link["Link Plane"]
        RADIO[IEEE 802.15.4 Radio 127-byte frame]
    end
    COAP --> DTLS --> CIPHER --> UDP --> IP --> COMP --> RADIO
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Differentiate between CoAP and HTTP. Why is CoAP preferred for IoT?**

**Model Answer (key points — write any 3 for full marks):**
1. **Transport:** CoAP runs over **UDP** (port 5683/5684); HTTP runs over **TCP** (port 80/443). TCP's handshake, state, and reliability machinery are too heavy for constrained devices.
2. **Encoding:** CoAP uses a **4-byte binary header** with binary options; HTTP uses verbose **ASCII text headers** (often hundreds of bytes).
3. **Message pattern:** CoAP uses **CON/ACK/RST/NON** message types for built-in reliability over UDP; HTTP relies on TCP for reliability.
4. **Built-in features for IoT:** CoAP natively supports **resource observation** (server-push), **block-wise transfer** for large payloads, and **discovery** via `/.well-known/core`.
5. **Security:** CoAP uses **DTLS** (lightweight, datagram-aware); HTTP uses **TLS** (TCP-bound).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` Why is DTLS used in IoT instead of TLS? List two modifications DTLS makes to the TLS handshake.**

**Model Answer:**
TLS is designed for TCP, which is connection-oriented and provides reliable, in-order delivery. UDP does not, so DTLS adapts TLS with the following modifications:

1. **Message Sequence Numbers inside the handshake** — allow retransmission of lost handshake flights because UDP can drop or reorder packets.
2. **HelloVerifyRequest with a stateless cookie** — defends the server against DoS attacks via IP spoofed ClientHello floods (the server doesn't allocate state until the client proves its IP by echoing the cookie).
3. *(Bonus)* **Path-MTU-aware fragmentation** — DTLS fragments large handshake messages to fit within a single datagram, avoiding IP-layer fragmentation on constrained links.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice 1)**
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **DTLS record layer** and the **DTLS handshake protocol** for an IoT client using **PSK (Pre-Shared Key) authentication**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe the **CoAP message format** in detail. Explain the four **CoAP message types** (CON, NON, ACK, RST) and the role of **Message ID** and **Token**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**DTLS Record Layer:**
The DTLS record layer fragments, compresses, **authenticates** (MAC), and **encrypts** outgoing messages. Each record has the structure:

```
+--------------------+
