<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define an LED. Explain the phenomenon of electroluminescence in a p–n junction LED.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **LED (Light Emitting Diode):** A LED is a **semiconductor p–n junction device** that emits **monochromatic light** when forward biased.
>
> **Electroluminescence Phenomenon:**
> When a forward bias voltage exceeding the **knee voltage ($V_f$)** is applied, electrons from the n-region and holes from the p-region are injected into the **depletion (active) region**. They **recombine**, and the excess energy equal to the **band gap $E_g$** is released as a **photon** of frequency $f = E_g / h$. Because the semiconductor is a **direct band-gap material** (e.g., GaAs, GaN), this recombination is *radiative*, producing light efficiently. The wavelength is given by $\lambda = h c / E_g$.

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the phenomenon explanation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**List any three differences between a conventional p–n junction diode and a Light Emitting Diode (LED).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Parameter | PN Junction Diode | LED |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Material** | Silicon or Germanium (indirect band-gap) | GaAs, GaP, GaN (direct band-gap) |
| 2 | **Energy Release** | Recombination energy → heat (phonons) | Recombination energy → light (photons) |
| 3 | **Forward Voltage** | ~0.7 V (Si) / 0.3 V (Ge) | 1.2 V to 3.4 V (varies with colour) |
| 4 | **Application** | Rectifier, signal demodulator | Indicator, display, optical communication |
| 5 | **Reverse Breakdown** | Often used in Zener mode | Must be protected — not used in breakdown |

*(Any three clearly explained differences fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either the full Question A or the full Question B** (internal choice per KTU ESE 2024 regulations).

---

### ✦ Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the construction and working principle of an LED with a neat labelled diagram. [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An LED consists of a **p–n junction** formed by doping a **direct band-gap semiconductor** such as GaAs, GaP, or GaN. The p-region is the **anode**, connected to a longer lead, while the n-region is the **cathode**, identified by a **shorter lead** and a **flat spot on the rim of the epoxy case**. The junction is encapsulated in a transparent **epoxy resin lens** that focuses the emitted light. A **reflective cup** beneath the junction directs photons outward.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
1. When a **forward bias** greater than the **knee voltage $V_f$** is applied, the depletion region narrows.
2. Electrons from the n-side cross into the p-side, and holes from the p-side cross into the n-side.
3. In the **active region**, **recombination** of electrons and holes occurs.
4. The energy difference ($E_g$) is released as a **photon**, with wavelength $\lambda = h c / E_g$.
5. The emitted colour is therefore **determined by the semiconductor material's band gap**.
6. For example, GaAs ($E_g = 1.42$ eV) emits infrared at ~874 nm; GaN ($E_g = 3.4$ eV) emits UV at ~365 nm.

**Labelled Schematic:**

```
        ANODE (+)
            │
            │   ┌──────────┐
            ├──►│ P-TYPE   │
            │   │ GaP/GaN  │◄─── Reflective Cup
            │   ├──────────┤
            │   │ JUNCTION │───► ─ ─ ─ ─► LIGHT OUT
            │   │ (Active  │       (Photons)
            │   │  Region) │
            │   ├──────────┤
            ├──►│ N-TYPE   │
            │   │ GaAs/InGaN│
            │   └──────────┘
            │
        CATHODE (−)
```

#### (b) [7 Marks] A green LED has a forward voltage of 2.0 V and is driven by a 5 V Arduino digital pin through a series resistor. If the required forward current is 12 mA, compute (i) the value of the series resistor, (ii) the power dissipated by the LED, and (iii) the total power drawn from the Arduino pin. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply KVL (1 mark):**
$$V_S = V_R + V_f \implies V_R = V_S - V_f = 5 - 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Series Resistor by Ohm's Law (2 marks):**
$$R_S = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{3.0 \text{ V}}{12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 250 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Power dissipated by the LED (2 marks):**
$$P_{LED} = V_f \times I_F = 2.0 \text{ V} \times 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 24 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 4 — Total power from Arduino pin (2 marks):**
$$P_{total} = V_S \times I_F = 5 \text{ V} \times 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 60 \text{ mW}$$

**Final Answer:**
- $R_S = 250 \, \Omega$
- $P_{LED} = 24 \text{ mW}$
- $P_{total} = 60 \text{ mW}$ (resistor wastes 36 mW as heat)

---

### ✦ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain how an LED can be used as a sensor in IoT applications. Differentiate between forward-bias and reverse-bias modes of operation. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**LED as a Sensor (4 marks):**
When an LED is reverse biased, the depletion region widens. Incident **photons** with energy $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ create **electron–hole pairs** in the depletion region, which are swept apart by the electric field, producing a measurable **photocurrent** $I_{photo} = R_\lambda \cdot \Phi_e$. This allows the LED to act as a **narrow-band photodetector**, most sensitive at its own emission wavelength and below.

**IoT Applications (3 marks):**
1. **Wearable pulse oximeters** — green LED both emits light into skin and detects reflected light.
2. **Optical line-following robots** — IR LED emitter + IR LED (reverse-biased) detector pair.
3. **Ambient light adaptive displays** — phone/tablet screen brightness auto-adjust.
4. **Li-Fi receivers** — high-speed modulated LED-light communication.
5. **Smart agriculture** — chlorophyll fluorescence sensing in plant leaves.

**Mode Comparison (Included within the 4 + 3 marks split):**

| Parameter | Forward-Bias Mode | Reverse-Bias Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Emits light (Actuator) | Detects light (Sensor) |
| **Current** | Exponential rise after $V_f$ | Small photocurrent (μA) |
| **Voltage polarity** | + to Anode, – to Cathode | – to Anode, + to Cathode |
| **Carrier action** | Injection across junction | Photon-induced e–h pair generation |
| **Typical use** | Status indicator, lighting | Optical sensing, communication |

#### (b) [7 Marks] With suitable equations, derive the relationship between the band-gap energy of a semiconductor LED and the wavelength of light emitted by it. A blue LED has a band gap of 2.75 eV. Find its emission wavelength and the colour of emitted light. [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
When an electron recombines with a hole across the band gap, the energy released is:
$$E_g = h f$$
Since $f = c / \lambda$:
$$E_g = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$
Rearranging for wavelength:
$$\lambda = \frac{h c}{E_g}$$
Using the convenient form with $h c = 1240$ eV·nm:
$$\lambda (\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})}$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**
$$\lambda = \frac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{2.75 \text{ eV}} = 450.9 \text{ nm}$$

**Identification (1 mark):**
A wavelength of **~451 nm** falls in the **blue region** of the visible spectrum (450–495 nm). ✅

**Final Answer:** The LED emits **blue light at 450.9 nm**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on LED questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the current-limiting resistor** in circuit diagrams — costs **1–2 marks** in 7-mark construction questions.
> 2. **Mixing up units** between eV and Joules in band-gap calculations — always use $h c = 1240$ eV·nm for the shortcut, or convert carefully to SI.
> 3. **Confusing direct and indirect band-gap materials** — remember: *Si & Ge do NOT emit light*; LEDs need *GaAs, GaP, GaN, InGaN, AlGaInP*.
> 4. **Drawing the LED symbol without the arrow** indicating emitted light — the LED symbol is a diode with **two outward-pointing arrows**.
> 5. **Stating that LEDs operate at 0.7 V** like ordinary Si diodes — wrong. Forward voltage is **1.2 V to 3.4 V** depending on colour.
> 6. **Skipping algebraic steps** in derivations — KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark per missing transition.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **LED = Light Emitting Diode** — a direct band-gap semiconductor p–n junction that emits light via **electroluminescence** under forward bias.
- ✅ **Core emission equation:** $\lambda = h c / E_g$, and the practical form $\lambda(\text{nm}) = 1240 / E_g(\text{eV})$.
- ✅ **Direct band-gap materials** (GaAs, GaP, GaN, InGaN) are mandatory — Si/Ge are *not* used for LEDs.
- ✅ **Colour ↔ Forward Voltage ↔ Wavelength ↔ Material** mapping is a frequently tested KTU point.
- ✅ **Always use a series resistor** $R_S = (V_S - V_f) / I_F$ to limit current; typical $I_F$ is **10–20 mA**.
- ✅ **LED is a dual-role device**: *actuator* (forward-biased emitter) and *sensor* (reverse-biased photodetector).
- ✅ **Shockley diode equation** $I_D = I_S(e^{qV_D/\eta kT} - 1)$ governs the exponential I–V curve above the knee.
- ✅ **Luminous efficacy** $\eta_{lum} = \Phi_v / P_{el}$ is the standard metric for LED lighting efficiency (modern white LEDs exceed 150 lm/W).
- ✅ **Thermal management** via $R_{\theta j-a}$ is critical for high-power LEDs to prevent junction overheating and lumen depreciation.
- ✅ **PWM dimming** is preferred over resistive dimming in IoT firmware to preserve energy efficiency.
- ✅ **Optical reciprocity** principle: an LED's responsivity peaks near its own emission wavelength when used as a photodiode.

> **End of Module 3 — IoT Hardware Platforms: Sensors – LED Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Switch # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Network Switch** is a Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) networking device operating under the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model** that connects multiple end devices—such as IoT sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, gateways, and computers—within a **Local Area Network (LAN)**. It uses **Media Access Control (MAC) address learning** and **frame forwarding/switching techniques** to intelligently direct data frames from a source port to the precise destination port, rather than indiscriminately broadcasting to every node (as a hub does). In the **KTU 2024 OEITT723 – Internet of Things** context, a switch forms the **backbone connectivity layer** of an IoT deployment, linking heterogeneous edge devices, edge gateways, and on-premise servers before traffic is uplinked to the cloud via routers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A switch is **not** the same as a hub or a router. It operates at **Layer 2 (Data Link)** using MAC addresses, while a router operates at **Layer 3 (Network)** using IP addresses. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Post Office** > > Imagine a large apartment complex with **100 flats**. Every day, the post office receives **100 letters** intended for different flats. A *hub* would be a careless postman who **shouts every letter aloud** in the corridor—everyone hears, only the right person picks it up (inefficient, insecure, noisy). > > A **switch**, on the other hand, acts like a **smart receptionist** who: > 1. **Reads the name (MAC address)** written on each letter. > 2. **Remembers (learns)** which flat number belongs to which person. > 3. **Walks directly** to that flat and hands over the letter—no shouting, no disturbance to others. > > This is *exactly* how a network switch behaves with **Ethernet frames** and **MAC addresses** in an IoT network. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **MAC Address Size:** **48 bits** (commonly written as `XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX` in hexadecimal). - **Default Switching Speed:** **100 Mbps** (Fast Ethernet), **1 Gbps** (Gigabit), **10 Gbps** (10G Ethernet). - **Switching Fabric Capacity (Backplane):** Measured in **Gbps**, e.g., a 24-port Gigabit switch needs at least **48 Gbps** backplane for full-duplex non-blocking operation. - **Standard:** **IEEE 802.3** (Ethernet) and **IEEE 802.1Q** (VLAN tagging). - **MAC Address Table Size (CAM Table):** Typically **2,000 to 64,000 entries** depending on switch class. --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown A switch performs its duty through a strict, repeatable sequence called the **"Three F's"** — a famous KTU-favorite mnemonic: ### Step 1: **Forwarding (Frame Reception)** - An incoming Ethernet frame arrives on one of the switch's **physical ports (RJ-45 / SFP)**. - The switch stores the frame in a small internal buffer (memory) for processing. ### Step 2: **Filtering (MAC Address Lookup)** - The switch extracts the **Destination MAC Address (DA)** from the frame header. - It consults its **Content Addressable Memory (CAM) Table**—an internal lookup table mapping MAC addresses to port numbers. - **If the destination MAC is FOUND:** The switch forwards the frame **only** out the matching port (unicast). - **If NOT FOUND:** The switch **floods** the frame out *all* ports except the incoming one (unknown unicast flooding). ### Step 3: **Learning (Address Table Update)** - The switch extracts the **Source MAC Address (SA)** from the frame header. - It records a mapping: `Source MAC → Port Number` into the CAM Table. - Each entry has a **default aging timer of 300 seconds (5 minutes)**—if no frame is seen from that MAC within the time, the entry is purged. ## 2.2 Switching Modes (Internal Forwarding Techniques) | # | Mode | How It Works | Latency | Error Checking | KTU Use Case | |---|------|--------------|---------|----------------|--------------| | 1 | **Store-and-Forward** | Entire frame is buffered; CRC checked; then forwarded | High (variable) | ✅ Yes (CRC) | Most modern managed switches; preferred for IoT reliability | | 2 | **Cut-Through** | Forwards as soon as Destination MAC is read (after first 6 bytes) | Low (fixed) | ❌ No | High-speed financial backbones | | 3 | **Fragment-Free (Modified Cut-Through)** | Reads first 64 bytes, then forwards | Medium | Partial (collision check) | Compromise mode | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Remarks | |---------|----------------|------|---------| | **Minimum Backplane Bandwidth** (Non-blocking) | $B_{min} = N \times S \times 2$ | Gbps | $N$ = ports, $S$ = speed per port, factor 2 = full-duplex | | **Total Throughput (Wire-Speed)** | $T = \frac{N \times S \times F}{\text{Frame\_Size}}$ | Mpps | $F$ = efficiency factor | | **MAC Address Range** | $2^{48}$ unique addresses | — | 281,474,976,710,656 global MACs | | **Frame Forwarding Delay (Store-and-Forward)** | $t_d = \frac{L}{R}$ | seconds | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) | | **Collision Domain Reduction** | Each switch port = **1 collision domain** | — | Unlike a hub (1 collision domain) | | **Broadcast Domain** | All ports = **1 broadcast domain** *(unless VLANs are used)* | — | A Layer 3 switch/router separates broadcast domains | | **VLAN IDs Available (802.1Q)** | $2^{12} - 2 = 4094$ | — | VLAN 0 and 4095 are reserved | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Why is a switch better than a hub?"*, your answer **must** mention *collision domain segmentation* and *MAC-based selective forwarding*—not just "it's faster." ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Factories (Industry 4.0):** Industrial **managed PoE switches** power and connect hundreds of IoT sensors, PLCs, and vision systems on a single Ethernet cable. - **Smart Buildings:** Switches connect **HVAC sensors, lighting controllers, access systems, and IP cameras** with VLAN isolation for security. - **Edge Computing Gateways:** IoT gateways aggregate sensor data and forward to cloud via uplink router; switches fan-out connections to many edge devices. - **Automotive In-Vehicle Networks:** Modern cars use **Automotive Ethernet switches** to link ADAS cameras, infotainment, and telematics. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Process — Switch Learning & Forwarding > This section walks through the **canonical KTU exam problem**: *"Given a set of devices connected to a switch, show how the CAM/MAC table is built and how a frame is forwarded."* Solve this step-by-step; it appears almost every semester. ## 3.1 Problem Setup (Worked Analytical Example) **Given:** A switch `SW1` has 4 ports. Four devices are connected: - **PC-A** → Port 1 → MAC: `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01` - **PC-B** → Port 2 → MAC: `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:02` - **PC-C** → Port 3 → MAC: `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:03` - **PC-D** → Port 4 → MAC: `DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:04` **Task:** Show the CAM table evolution when the following sequence of frames is sent, and state the switch action for each: 1. **Frame 1:** `PC-A → PC-B` (Source: A, Destination: B) 2. **Frame 2:** `PC-C → PC-A` (Source: C, Destination: A) 3. **Frame 3:** `PC-B → PC-C` (Source: B, Destination: C) 4. **Frame 4:** `PC-D → PC-E` (Source: D, Destination: `EE:EE:EE:EE:EE:99`) — *PC-E is NOT connected* --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Deduction ### ▶ Initial State of the CAM Table (Empty) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAM Table at } t_0 &= \{\ \text{empty}\ \} \\ \text{Action Default} &= \text{All incoming frames → FLOOD (out all ports except incoming)} \end{aligned} $$ ### ▶ Frame 1: PC-A → PC-B arrives at Port 1 - **Step 1a — Learning (from Source MAC):** - Source MAC = `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01` arrived on **Port 1**. - Switch updates CAM table: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAM Table at } t_1 &= \{ \\ & \ \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01} \rightarrow \text{Port 1} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ - **Step 1b — Forwarding (from Destination MAC):** - Destination MAC = `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:02` is **NOT** in the CAM table. - **Switch Action = FLOOD** out Ports 2, 3, 4 (all except incoming Port 1). - PC-B receives it and accepts; PC-C and PC-D discard (MAC mismatch). ### ▶ Frame 2: PC-C → PC-A arrives at Port 3 - **Step 2a — Learning:** - Source MAC = `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:03` on **Port 3** → add to CAM. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAM Table at } t_2 &= \{ \\ & \ \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01} \rightarrow \text{Port 1}, \\ & \ \text{CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:03} \rightarrow \text{Port 3} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ - **Step 2b — Forwarding:** - Destination MAC = `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01` **IS FOUND** in CAM (Port 1). - **Switch Action = FORWARD selectively** out **only Port 1**. - ✅ PC-A receives the frame. ### ▶ Frame 3: PC-B → PC-C arrives at Port 2 - **Step 3a — Learning:** Adds `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:02 → Port 2` to CAM. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAM Table at } t_3 &= \{ \\ & \ \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01} \rightarrow \text{Port 1}, \\ & \ \text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:02} \rightarrow \text{Port 2}, \\ & \ \text{CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:03} \rightarrow \text{Port 3} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ - **Step 3b — Forwarding:** Destination = `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:03` found at **Port 3** → **selective FORWARD** to Port 3. ### ▶ Frame 4: PC-D → PC-E (unknown MAC) arrives at Port 4 - **Step 4a — Learning:** Adds `DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:04 → Port 4` to CAM. - **Step 4b — Forwarding:** Destination `EE:EE:EE:EE:EE:99` **NOT FOUND** in CAM. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Switch Action} &= \text{UNKNOWN UNICAST FLOOD} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Out Ports 1, 2, 3 (all except incoming Port 4)} \end{aligned} $$ ### ▶ Final CAM Table (Stable State) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Final CAM Table} &= \{ \\ & \ \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01} \rightarrow \text{Port 1}, \\ & \ \text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:02} \rightarrow \text{Port 2}, \\ & \ \text{CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:03} \rightarrow \text{Port 3}, \\ & \ \text{DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:04} \rightarrow \text{Port 4} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The above process is exactly how a switch achieves **micro-segmentation**—each port becomes its own dedicated **collision domain**, eliminating CSMA/CD contention seen in hub-based networks. ## 3.3 Backplane Bandwidth Numerical Example (Worked) **Problem:** A 24-port Gigabit managed switch is to be deployed in an IoT lab. Calculate the **minimum non-blocking backplane bandwidth** required. **Given:** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= 24 \ \text{ports} \\ S &= 1 \ \text{Gbps} \ \text{per port} \\ \text{Full-duplex factor} &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{min} &= N \times S \times 2 \\ &= 24 \times 1 \ \text{Gbps} \times 2 \\ &= 48 \ \text{Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** A non-blocking switch must have an internal switching fabric of **at least 48 Gbps**. --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IoT Network Topology with Switch as Aggregation Layer ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cloud["CLOUD LAYER"] cloud1[("AWS IoT Core / Azure IoT Hub")] end subgraph Edge["EDGE NETWORK (LAN - Switched)"] sw1["L2/L3 MANAGED SWITCH
IoT Aggregation Backbone"] end subgraph Gateway["EDGE GATEWAY LAYER"] gw1[("Edge Gateway 1
Modbus to MQTT")] gw2[("Edge Gateway 2
BLE to MQTT")] end subgraph Sensors["EDGE DEVICES - SENSORS and ACTUATORS"] s1["Temperature Sensor"] s2["Vibration Sensor"] s3["Smart Camera"] s4["RFID Reader"] s5["Motor Controller"] s6["Smart Lighting"] end s1 --- sw1 s2 --- sw1 s3 --- sw1 s4 --- sw1 gw1 --- sw1 gw2 --- sw1 s5 --- gw1 s6 --- gw2 sw1 -->|"Uplink to Router"| cloud1 classDef cloud fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef switchStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef gateway fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sensor fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class cloud1 cloud class sw1 switchStyle class gw1,gw2 gateway class s1,s2,s3,s4,s5,s6 sensor ``` ## 4.2 Internal Switch Operation — Frame Flow and MAC Learning ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Frame Arrives on Incoming Port] --> B[Extract Source MAC Address SA] A --> C[Extract Destination MAC Address DA] B --> D{SA already in CAM Table?} D -- No --> E[Add SA to CAM Table with Incoming Port Number] D -- Yes --> F[Update Aging Timer for SA Entry] C --> G{DA found in CAM Table?} G -- Yes Found --> H[Selectively FORWARD frame
only out matching DA port] G -- No Not Found --> I[FLOOD frame out all ports
except the incoming port] H --> J[Frame reaches correct destination] I --> K[All hosts receive
only target host accepts frame] E --> L[Learning Phase Complete] F --> L L --> M[Switch Waits for Next Frame] classDef inputNode fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decisionNode fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actionNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef floodNode fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C inputNode class D,G decisionNode class E,F,H,J,K,L,M actionNode class I floodNode ``` ## 4.3 Switch vs Hub — Comparative Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HUB["HUB - LAYER 1 BROADCAST"] h1[Device A] -->|Signal In| hub0[Shared Bus Repeater] hub0 -->|Copy to ALL| h2[Device B] hub0 -->|Copy to ALL| h3[Device C] hub0 -->|Copy to ALL| h4[Device D] end subgraph SWITCH["SWITCH - LAYER 2 INTELLIGENT FORWARDING"] d1[Device A MAC AA] -->|Frame| sw0[CAM Table Lookup Engine] sw0 -->|"DA found at Port 3"| d3[Device C MAC CC] sw0 -.->|"Filtered Out"| d2[Device B] sw0 -.->|"Filtered Out"| d4[Device D] end classDef hubStyle fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef switchStyle fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class h1,h2,h3,h4,hub0 hubStyle class d1,d2,d3,d4,sw0 switchStyle ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a Hub, a Switch, and a Router. Mention the OSI layer at which each device operates.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Hub | Switch | Router | |---|---|---|---| | **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) | | **Addressing Used** | None (electrical signal repeat) | MAC Address (48-bit) | IP Address (32-bit / 128-bit) | | **Forwarding Method** | Broadcast to all ports | Selective / flooded based on CAM | Routing table lookup | | **Collision Domain** | One (shared) | One per port | One per port | | **Broadcast Domain** | One | One (split with VLANs) | One per interface | | **Typical Use** | Legacy small LANs | Modern LAN / IoT edge | Inter-networking, WAN | A switch is preferred in **IoT networks** because it isolates collision domains and provides deterministic, MAC-based forwarding for hundreds of edge devices. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the term "MAC Address Learning" in a network switch. What is the default aging timer?** **Model Answer:** **MAC Address Learning** is the process by which a switch dynamically builds its **Content Addressable Memory (CAM) table** by inspecting the **Source MAC Address (SA)** of every incoming Ethernet frame and recording the corresponding **port number** on which that frame arrived. - For each frame received, the switch performs three actions: 1. Records the `SA → Port` mapping in the CAM table. 2. Looks up the `Destination MAC (DA)` in the table. 3. If DA is found → **selectively forward**; if not → **flood**. - **Default Aging Timer:** **300 seconds (5 minutes)** — after which, an unused MAC entry is automatically removed from the CAM table to keep the table fresh and prevent stale entries. This enables the switch to deliver frames intelligently, reducing unnecessary broadcast traffic in IoT deployments. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 | Internal Choice Set 1]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal architecture of a **Layer 2 network switch**. Describe the role of the **CAM table**, **switching fabric (backplane)**, and **port buffers** in IoT deployments. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **24-port Gigabit Ethernet switch** is to be deployed at the edge of a smart manufacturing IoT network. Calculate the **minimum non-blocking backplane bandwidth** required. If each port must support **2 Gbps full-duplex** traffic simultaneously, what is the new minimum backplane requirement? Justify why a managed switch is preferred over an unmanaged switch in industrial IoT. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A ### Part (a) — Switch Architecture and IoT Role A Layer 2 Ethernet switch consists of the following internal blocks: ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ SWITCH INTERNAL ARCHITECTURE │ │ │ │ [Port 1]──┐ │ │ [Port 2]──┤ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ [Port 3]──┼────►│ INPUT BUF │────►│ MAC ENGINE │ │ │ [Port 4]──┤ └──────────────┘ │ (CAM Table) │ │ │ ... │ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ [Port N]──┘ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ ▼ │ │ │ SWITCHING FABRIC │◄──────────────┐ │ │ │ (CROSSBAR / BACKPLANE) │ │ │ │ └────────┬─────────────┘ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ OUTPUT BUFFERS │──►[Port X] │ │ │ └──────────────────┘ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` - **CAM Table:** Stores `MAC ↔ Port` mappings for selective forwarding; built via MAC learning. - **Switching Fabric (Backplane):** High-speed internal bus that physically connects input ports to output ports; bandwidth must support all ports simultaneously (non-blocking). - **Port Buffers (Input/Output):** Temporarily store frames during congestion or when output port is busy; size in KB affects packet-loss behavior. **IoT Relevance:** - Industrial IoT (IIoT) deployments have **hundreds of sensors** generating continuous streams. - Switch's **micro-segmentation** prevents collision-domain contention among devices. - **VLAN support** in managed switches isolates OT (Operational Technology) traffic from IT traffic, critical for **cybersecurity** in factories. ### Part (b) — Backplane Bandwidth Numerical **Given for Case 1 (1 Gbps per port):** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= 24 \ \text{ports} \\ S &= 1 \ \text{Gbps} \\ \text{Full-duplex factor} &= 2 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{min,1} &= N \times S \times 2 \\ &= 24 \times 1 \times 2 \\ &= 48 \ \text{Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Given for Case 2 (2 Gbps full-duplex per port):** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{min,2} &= 24 \times 2 \times 2 \\ &= 96 \ \text{Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** The new minimum backplane requirement is **96 Gbps**. **Why Managed > Unmanaged in Industrial IoT:** | Feature | Unmanaged Switch | Managed Switch (Preferred for IIoT) | |---|---|---| | VLAN Support | ❌ No | ✅ Yes — traffic segmentation | | QoS (Quality of Service) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes — prioritize critical sensor data | | Port Mirroring | ❌ No | ✅ Yes — for diagnostics | | SNMP Monitoring | ❌ No | ✅ Yes — remote monitoring | | Redundancy Protocols (STP/RSTP) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes — fault tolerance | | Security (Port Security, 802.1X) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes — prevents rogue devices | A managed switch provides **deterministic, secure, and observable** connectivity — essential for **mission-critical IoT** systems. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 | Internal Choice Set 2]` **(a)** Explain the three switching modes — **Store-and-Forward, Cut-Through, and Fragment-Free**. State which mode is best suited for IoT networks and justify with two reasons. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider a 4-port switch with the following connection log (in order): - Frame 1: `Src = MAC_11`, `Dst = MAC_22`, arrived on **Port 1** - Frame 2: `Src = MAC_22`, `Dst = MAC_33`, arrived on **Port 2** - Frame 3: `Src = MAC_33`, `Dst = MAC_11`, arrived on **Port 3** - Frame 4: `Src = MAC_44`, `Dst = MAC_55` (unknown), arrived on **Port 4** Build the **final CAM table** and state the switch action for **each frame**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B ### Part (a) — Three Switching Modes | Mode | Description | CRC Check | Latency | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Store-and-Forward** | Switch buffers the **entire frame**, performs CRC check, then forwards. | ✅ Yes | Highest (variable) | **IoT / Industrial / Reliable networks** | | **Cut-Through** | Switch reads only the **first 6 bytes (Destination MAC)**, then immediately forwards. | ❌ No | Lowest (fixed) | High-frequency trading HPC backbones | | **Fragment-Free** | Reads first **64 bytes** (minimum Ethernet frame size after preamble), then forwards. | Partial (collision check) | Medium | Compromise mode | **Best mode for IoT: Store-and-Forward** ✅ **Justification (Two Reasons):** 1. **Error-free delivery:** IoT sensor data (e.g., temperature, pressure readings) is critical; CRC validation ensures corrupted frames are dropped before reaching controllers—preventing false actuation. 2. **Latency is acceptable:** IoT traffic typically runs at 100 Mbps–1 Gbps, and store-and-forward's variable delay (microseconds range) does not break real-time control loops. Cut-through's micro-optimization is unnecessary. ### Part (b) — CAM Table Construction **Initial State:** CAM Table = `{}` (empty) --- **Frame 1: Src = MAC_11, Dst = MAC_22, Incoming Port = 1** - **Learning:** `MAC_11 → Port 1` added. - **Forwarding:** `MAC_22` not in CAM → **FLOOD** out Ports 2, 3, 4. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAM after Frame 1} &= \{\ \text{MAC\_11} \rightarrow \text{Port 1}\ \} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Frame 2: Src = MAC_22, Dst = MAC_33, Incoming Port = 2** - **Learning:** `MAC_22 → Port 2` added. - **Forwarding:** `MAC_33` not in CAM → **FLOOD** out Ports 1, 3, 4. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAM after Frame 2} &= \{ \\ & \ \text{MAC\_11} \rightarrow \text{Port 1}, \\ & \ \text{MAC\_22} \rightarrow \text{Port 2} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Frame 3: Src = MAC_33, Dst = MAC_11, Incoming Port = 3** - **Learning:** `MAC_33 → Port 3` added. - **Forwarding:** `MAC_11` **FOUND** in CAM (Port 1) → **SELECTIVE FORWARD** to **Port 1** only. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAM after Frame 3} &= \{ \\ & \ \text{MAC\_11} \rightarrow \text{Port 1}, \\ & \ \text{MAC\_22} \rightarrow \text{Port 2}, \\ & \ \text{MAC\_33} \rightarrow \text{Port 3} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Frame 4: Src = MAC_44, Dst = MAC_55 (unknown), Incoming Port = 4** - **Learning:** `MAC_44 → Port 4` added. - **Forwarding:** `MAC_55` not in CAM → **UNKNOWN UNICAST FLOOD** out Ports 1, 2, 3. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Final CAM Table} &= \{ \\ & \ \text{MAC\_11} \rightarrow \text{Port 1}, \\ & \ \text{MAC\_22} \rightarrow \text{Port 2}, \\ & \ \text{MAC\_33} \rightarrow \text{Port 3}, \\ & \ \text{MAC\_44} \rightarrow \text{Port 4} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing Learning vs. Forwarding** — Many students *only* update the CAM table and forget to state the **forwarding action** (FLOOD vs SELECTIVE). Both are mandatory for full marks. > 2. **Wrong direction of MAC check** — Source MAC → used for *learning*; Destination MAC → used for *forwarding*. Swapping these two loses 2–3 marks. > 3. **Flooding to incoming port** — The incoming port must **always be excluded** during flooding. Flooding back to the source is a frequent, penalized mistake. > 4. **Aging timer confusion** — Don't confuse the 300-second aging timer with the CAM update timing. Aging = *removal after inactivity*; Learning = *insertion on frame arrival*. > 5. **Unmanaged vs Managed in IIoT** — Just writing "managed is better" is not enough; mention **VLAN, QoS, SNMP, and security features** explicitly. --- ## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Switch = Layer 2 Device** operating at the **Data Link Layer** of the **OSI model**. - **Core function:** Connect multiple devices in a LAN using **MAC addresses** for **selective frame forwarding**. - **"Three F's" Mnemonic:** **F**orward → **F**ilter (CAM lookup) → **F**ill (MAC learning). - **CAM Table** = `MAC Address ↔ Port Number` mapping; built dynamically via **Source MAC learning**; entries **age out after 300 seconds** by default. - **Unknown Unicast = FLOOD**; **Known Unicast = SELECTIVE FORWARD**; **Broadcast = FLOOD to all ports**; **Multicast = FLOOD unless IGMP snooping is enabled**. - **Each switch port = its own collision domain** (micro-segmentation). - **All switch ports = 1 broadcast domain** (unless **VLANs** are configured — then each VLAN is a separate broadcast domain). - **Three Switching Modes:** Store-and-Forward (reliable, used in IoT) | Cut-Through (fastest, no CRC) | Fragment-Free (64-byte check, balanced). - **Backplane Bandwidth Formula:** $B_{min} = N \times S \times 2$ where factor 2 = full-duplex. - **MAC Address Size:** **48 bits**, written as **6 hex octets** (e.g., `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`); total **$2^{48}$** unique global addresses. - **Switch vs Hub:** Hub = Layer 1, broadcast, single collision domain ❌ old; Switch = Layer 2, selective forwarding, per-port collision domain ✅ modern. - **Switch vs Router:** Switch = Layer 2, MAC-based, intra-network; Router = Layer 3, IP-based, inter-network. - **Switch Types in IoT:** **Unmanaged** (plug-and-play, no config) | **Managed** (VLAN, QoS, SNMP) | **Smart** (light managed, web GUI) | **PoE Switch** (powers devices via Ethernet — ideal for IoT sensors/cameras). - **KTU Favourite Keywords to Use in Answers:** *micro-segmentation, CAM table, backplane, full-duplex, collision domain, broadcast domain, VLAN, store-and-forward, unknown unicast flooding.* - **Real-world IoT use case:** Industrial **PoE managed switches** in smart factories, aggregating hundreds of sensors, IP cameras, and PLCs onto a single VLAN-segmented network with QoS for deterministic control.

topics.title: Temperature Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Temperature Sensor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Temperature Sensor** is an electronic device (or transducer) that detects, measures, and converts the physical thermal energy of a system into a quantifiable, processable electrical signal — typically voltage, current, or a change in resistance — proportional to the applied temperature. In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, temperature sensors function as the primary *physical perception layer* node, enabling real-time ambient or object-level thermal monitoring across distributed sensor networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (IoT Context):**
> "A temperature sensor is a device, typically a thermocouple or RTD, that provides temperature measurement through an electrical signal to an IoT node, which then transmits the data over the network to a processing layer for actuation and analytics."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Thermometer-Spy" Analogy:**
> Imagine a tiny, intelligent spy that lives on a wall of a factory boiler. Every time the boiler gets hotter, the spy sends a coded message (an electrical signal) to a control room hundreds of kilometres away. The control room decodes the message and knows the *exact* temperature. If it's too hot, an alarm rings; if it's normal, the heating continues.
> - The **spy** is the *temperature sensor*.
> - The **coded message** is the *electrical signal* (voltage/resistance).
> - The **control room** is the *cloud/IoT server*.
> - The **alarm** is the *actuator response*.

This is exactly what a smart IoT temperature sensor (e.g., **DS18B20** or **DHT22**) does — it monitors, digitizes, and transmits thermal data without human intervention.

### 1.3 Classification of Temperature Sensors

| Category | Sensing Principle | Output Type | Common IoT Models |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Thermocouple** | Seebeck Effect (junction of dissimilar metals) | Millivoltage (mV) | Type K, Type J, MAX6675 |
| **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)** | Change in metal resistance with temperature | Resistance (Ω) | PT100, PT1000 |
| **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** | Non-linear resistance change with temperature | Resistance (Ω) | NTC 10k, B3950 |
| **Semiconductor (Analog)** | Bandgap reference proportional to absolute temperature | Linear Voltage (V) | LM35, TMP36 |
| **Digital IC Sensor** | On-chip ADC + calibration | Digital (I²C, 1-Wire, SPI) | DS18B20, DHT11, BME280 |
| **Infrared (Non-contact)** | Black-body radiation detection | Voltage / Digital | MLX90614, AMG8833 |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (highlighted for KTU exams):**
> - **Accuracy** — Deviation from true value (in **±°C**).
> - **Range** — Minimum and maximum measurable temperature (**−55 °C to +150 °C** for DS18B20).
> - **Resolution** — Smallest detectable change (**0.0625 °C** for DS18B20).
> - **Response Time (τ)** — Time to reach 63.2 % of the final value.
> - **Boltzmann Constant** (*k* = **1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K**) — governs black-body radiation.
> - **Seebeck Coefficient (S)** — Voltage generated per °C of junction temperature difference (e.g., **~41 μV/°C** for Type K thermocouples).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principles (Structured Logic)

The working of any temperature sensor can be broken down into **three fundamental stages**:

1. **Thermal Energy Absorption (Sensing Stage)**
   - The sensing element (e.g., a junction, a metal film, a semiconductor bandgap) absorbs thermal energy from the measured medium.
   - This energy causes a change in a *physical property* of the material (e.g., resistance, voltage, electron mobility).

2. **Signal Conversion (Transduction Stage)**
   - The physical change is converted into a measurable electrical quantity.
   - Example: In an **LM35**, the bandgap voltage scales linearly with absolute temperature; the output is calibrated in mV/°C.

3. **Signal Conditioning & Transmission (IoT Stage)**
   - The raw signal is amplified, linearized, digitized (via ADC), and packaged into a protocol (I²C, SPI, 1-Wire).
   - The IoT node (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi) transmits the data over Wi-Fi, LoRa, ZigBee, or MQTT to the cloud.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Principle

- **Why does a Thermocouple work?** → Due to the *Seebeck Effect*: when two dissimilar metals are joined, a voltage proportional to the temperature difference between the hot and cold junctions is generated.
- **How does an NTC Thermistor work?** → As temperature rises, charge carriers in the semiconductor material multiply, lowering resistance (Negative Temperature Coefficient).
- **Why is DS18B20 popular in IoT?** → It integrates the sensor, ADC, calibration, and a unique **64-bit serial code** on a single chip, enabling multiple sensors on a single 1-Wire data line.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values in tables to prevent markdown breakage.**

| Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Description | Units |
|-------------|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Thermocouple** | $V = S \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | Seebeck voltage from temperature difference | V (volts) |
| **RTD (PT100)** | $R_T = R_0 \cdot \bigl(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^{2}\bigr)$ | Callendar-Van Dusen equation | Ω (ohms) |
| **NTC Thermistor (Steinhart-Hart)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot \bigl[\ln(R)\bigr]^{3}$ | Non-linear resistance-temperature | Kelvin⁻¹ |
| **LM35 (Analog)** | $V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \cdot T$ | Linear voltage output | V |
| **TMP36 (Analog)** | $V_{out} = 500\,\text{mV} + 10\,\text{mV/°C} \cdot T$ | Offset + linear scaling | V |
| **Stefan-Boltzmann Law (IR Sensor)** | $P = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Radiated power per unit area | W/m² |
| **DS18B20 (Digital)** | $\text{Resolution} = 0.0625\,°C$ (12-bit) | 1-Wire digital output | °C |

Where:
- $S$ = Seebeck coefficient (μV/°C)
- $R_0$ = Resistance at 0 °C (e.g., 100 Ω for PT100)
- $\alpha$ = Temperature coefficient of resistance (**≈ 0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C** for platinum)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.670374419 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²/K⁴**
- $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity (0 to 1)
- $A, B, C$ = Steinhart-Hart coefficients (sensor-specific)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision**: RTDs and digital ICs are used in pharmaceutical and laboratory applications.
- **Speed & Range**: Thermocouples dominate industrial furnaces (up to **2300 °C**).
- **Low-Cost IoT**: Semiconductor sensors (LM35) and digital sensors (DS18B20) are deployed in smart homes, cold-chain logistics, wearables, and weather stations.
- **Non-contact**: Infrared sensors (MLX90614) are essential for fever screening, motor health monitoring, and industrial process control.

### 2.5 Comparison Matrix (Selection Guide)

| Feature | Thermocouple | RTD | Thermistor | Semiconductor | Digital IC |
|---------|--------------|-----|------------|---------------|------------|
| Range | Very Wide | Wide | Narrow | Medium | Medium |
| Accuracy | Low–Medium | **High** | High | Medium | **High** |
| Linearity | Low | High | Low | **High** | **High** |
| Cost | Low | High | Low | **Low** | Medium |
| IoT Friendly | No (needs amp) | No (needs bridge) | No (needs ADC) | No (needs ADC) | **Yes (built-in)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Callendar-Van Dusen Equation for RTD

The **resistance-temperature** relationship of a pure metal (like platinum) is derived from the empirical Callendar-Van Dusen equation.

**Step 1 — Linear Approximation (for T ≥ 0 °C):**
At low temperatures, resistance changes nearly linearly with temperature. We write:
$$R_T = R_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha T)$$

**Step 2 — Quadratic Correction (for T < 0 °C):**
At sub-zero temperatures, non-linearity appears. The full equation becomes:
$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \bigl[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^{2} + \gamma (T - 100) T^{3}\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Simplification for T ≥ 0 °C (most common case):**
The cubic term $\gamma (T - 100) T^{3}$ vanishes since $(T - 100) = 0$ at 100 °C is *not* a constraint; the term drops out for positive temperatures, giving:
$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \bigl(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^{2}\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**
For a **PT100** sensor with $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\,°C^{-1}$, $\beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,°C^{-2}$, find the resistance at $T = 100\,°C$.

*Substitution:*
$$R_{100} = 100 \cdot \bigl[1 + (0.00385)(100) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(100)^{2}\bigr]$$

*Step-by-step evaluation:*
$$R_{100} = 100 \cdot \bigl[1 + 0.385 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(10{,}000)\bigr]$$

$$R_{100} = 100 \cdot \bigl[1 + 0.385 - 0.005775\bigr]$$

$$R_{100} = 100 \cdot (1.379225)$$

$$\boxed{R_{100} = 137.9225\,\Omega}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** This matches the IEC 60751 standard, which defines a PT100 as having a resistance of **138.51 Ω at 100 °C** (allowing for higher-order terms). Our simplified quadratic gives a close approximation, suitable for KTU exam purposes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Temperature from NTC Thermistor Using Steinhart-Hart Equation

**Step 1 — The Core Equation:**
The Steinhart-Hart equation is the empirical gold-standard for thermistor modelling:
$$\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot [\ln(R)]^{3}$$

where $T$ is in **Kelvin**, $R$ is in **Ω**, and $A, B, C$ are constants provided by the manufacturer.

**Step 2 — Solving for Temperature (Inverse Form):**
$$T = \dfrac{1}{A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot [\ln(R)]^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to Celsius:**
$$T_{°C} = T_{K} - 273.15$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**
Given an NTC thermistor with coefficients $A = 1.127 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = 2.342 \times 10^{-4}$, $C = 8.730 \times 10^{-8}$, and a measured resistance $R = 10{,}000\,\Omega$. Find the temperature in °C.

*Compute $\ln(R)$:*
$$\ln(10{,}000) = \ln(10^{4}) = 4 \cdot \ln(10) = 4 \cdot 2.302585 = 9.21034$$

*Compute $[\ln(R)]^{3}$:*
$$[9.21034]^{3} = 9.21034 \times 9.21034 \times 9.21034 \approx 781.2$$

*Substitute into the Steinhart-Hart equation:*
$$\dfrac{1}{T_{K}} = 1.127 \times 10^{-3} + (2.342 \times 10^{-4})(9.21034) + (8.730 \times 10^{-8})(781.2)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{K}} = 0.001127 + 0.002157 + 0.0000682$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{K}} = 0.0033522$$

*Invert to find $T_{K}$:*
$$T_{K} = \dfrac{1}{0.0033522} \approx 298.32\,\text{K}$$

*Convert to Celsius:*
$$\boxed{T_{°C} = 298.32 - 273.15 = 25.17\,°C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Notice how the result (~25 °C) makes physical sense — a 10 kΩ NTC thermistor typically has a 10 kΩ resistance at **25 °C** (hence the name). This validates the calculation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection for Common IoT Scenarios

| IoT Application | Recommended Sensor | Justification |
|-----------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Smart Home (Indoor) | **DHT22 / BME280** | Humidity + Temperature, I²C/SPI, low power |
| Cold-Chain Pharma | **DS18B20 (1-Wire)** | High accuracy (±0.5 °C), waterproof probe |
| Industrial Furnace | **Type K Thermocouple** | Wide range (−200 °C to +1350 °C), rugged |
| Wearable Health | **MAX30205** | Medical-grade (±0.1 °C), skin-contact |
| Fever Screening | **MLX90614 (IR)** | Non-contact, ±0.5 °C, 70 cm range |
| Greenhouse | **SHT31 / BME280** | Combined T+RH, calibrated |
| Weather Station | **PT1000 + ADC** | High accuracy over wide outdoor range |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a temperature sensor. List any **four** common types used in IoT applications.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A temperature sensor is an electronic transducer that detects the thermal energy of a system and converts it into a processable electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance) proportional to temperature. In IoT, it acts as the *physical perception layer* node, enabling real-time monitoring of ambient or object thermal conditions.

Four common types used in IoT are:
1. **Thermocouple** (e.g., Type K, MAX6675) — based on the Seebeck Effect.
2. **RTD** (e.g., PT100, PT1000) — resistance change in pure metal.
3. **NTC Thermistor** (e.g., 10k B3950) — non-linear semiconductor resistance.
4. **Digital IC Sensor** (e.g., DS18B20, DHT22) — on-chip ADC + calibrated digital output.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the **Seebeck Effect** with reference to thermocouple operation. Why is cold-junction compensation necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Seebeck Effect** states that when two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed loop and the two junctions are held at different temperatures, an electromotive force (emf) is generated. The voltage generated is given by:
$$V = S \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$
where $S$ is the Seebeck coefficient (e.g., **~41 μV/°C** for Type K).

**Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** is necessary because a thermocouple *measures temperature difference*, not absolute temperature. The reference (cold) junction must be electronically compensated (typically using a semiconductor IC like the MAX31855) so the microcontroller can compute the true hot-junction temperature.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Recommended Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Question]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an IoT-based temperature monitoring system. Identify the role of the *sensing*, *processing*, and *communication* layers. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **PT100** RTD has a nominal resistance of 100 Ω at 0 °C and a temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha = 0.00385\,°C^{-1}$. Using the Callendar-Van Dusen equation, calculate:
> - (i) The resistance at $T = 50\,°C$.
> - (ii) The temperature when the measured resistance is **120 Ω**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The functional architecture of an IoT-based temperature monitoring system has **four functional layers**:

1. **Sensing / Perception Layer** — The temperature sensor (e.g., DS18B20) detects thermal energy and converts it into an electrical signal. For a digital sensor, the ADC is on-chip; for analog sensors (LM35, PT100), an external ADC is required.
2. **Processing / Network Layer** — A microcontroller (ESP32, Arduino, or Raspberry Pi) reads the digital value, applies calibration coefficients, performs linearization (e.g., Steinhart-Hart inversion), and decides on the data packaging format (JSON, MQTT, CoAP).
3. **Communication Layer** — The data is transmitted over a wireless protocol such as **Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, ZigBee, or BLE** to a gateway or directly to the cloud.
4. **Application Layer** — The cloud server stores data in a time-series database, runs analytics, triggers alerts, and displays values on a dashboard or mobile app.

A feedback loop allows an **actuator** (fan, heater, or alarm) to be triggered automatically based on threshold logic.

**Diagram (textual block description for board exam):**
$$T_{\text{process}} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{Wi-Fi/LoRa} \rightarrow \text{Cloud} \rightarrow \text{Dashboard / Actuator}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\,°C^{-1}$, $\beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,°C^{-2}$ (standard IEC 60751 value).

**Part (i) — Resistance at T = 50 °C:**

$$R_{50} = R_0 \cdot \bigl(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^{2}\bigr)$$

$$R_{50} = 100 \cdot \bigl[1 + (0.00385)(50) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(50)^{2}\bigr]$$

$$R_{50} = 100 \cdot \bigl[1 + 0.1925 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(2500)\bigr]$$

$$R_{50} = 100 \cdot \bigl[1 + 0.1925 - 0.00144375\bigr]$$

$$R_{50} = 100 \cdot (1.19105625)$$

$$\boxed{R_{50} = 119.11\,\Omega}$$

**Part (ii) — Temperature when R = 120 Ω:**

Using the simplified linear approximation (since the quadratic correction is very small, this is acceptable for KTU):
$$R = R_0 (1 + \alpha T) \implies T = \dfrac{R - R_0}{R_0 \cdot \alpha}$$

$$T = \dfrac{120 - 100}{100 \cdot 0.00385} = \dfrac{20}{0.385} = 51.95\,°C$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 51.95\,°C}$$

Using the more accurate quadratic form:
$$120 = 100 \cdot \bigl[1 + 0.00385 T + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) T^{2}\bigr]$$

$$1.2 = 1 + 0.00385 T - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} T^{2}$$

$$0.2 = 0.00385 T - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} T^{2}$$

Solving the quadratic gives $T \approx 51.86\,°C$, which closely matches the linear result, validating the KTU expected answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style Question]**

> **(a)** Compare **Thermocouples, RTDs, and NTC Thermistors** in terms of range, accuracy, linearity, and IoT suitability. State one application each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Steinhart-Hart equation** for an NTC thermistor. Given a thermistor with coefficients $A = 1.40 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = 2.37 \times 10^{-4}$, $C = 7.36 \times 10^{-8}$, find the temperature in °C when the resistance is **5,000 Ω**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks (Comparison Table):**

| Parameter | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | NTC Thermistor |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Range** | −200 to +1350 °C (Type K) | −200 to +850 °C | −55 to +150 °C |
| **Accuracy** | ±1 to ±3 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C |
| **Linearity** | Low (non-linear) | High (near-linear) | Low (highly non-linear) |
| **Response Time** | Fast (~1 s) | Moderate (~3–5 s) | Fast (~1 s) |
| **IoT Suitability** | Needs amp + ADC | Needs bridge circuit + ADC | Needs ADC |
| **Application** | Furnace monitoring | Pharmaceutical lab | Battery pack protection |

**One application each:**
- Thermocouple → Industrial furnace control.
- RTD → Laboratory precision thermal baths.
- NTC Thermistor → In-circuit 3D-printer hot-end monitoring.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation of Steinhart-Hart Equation (Brief Steps):**
The electrical conductivity of a semiconductor is an exponential function of inverse temperature. By expanding the natural logarithm of resistance in a Maclaurin series and truncating beyond the cubic term, we obtain the empirical Steinhart-Hart form:
$$\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot [\ln(R)]^{3}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

*Step 1:* Compute $\ln(5000)$:
$$\ln(5000) = \ln(5) + \ln(1000) = 1.6094 + 6.9078 = 8.5172$$

*Step 2:* Compute $[\ln(R)]^{3}$:
$$[8.5172]^{3} \approx 617.96$$

*Step 3:* Substitute into the Steinhart-Hart equation:
$$\dfrac{1}{T_{K}} = 1.40 \times 10^{-3} + (2.37 \times 10^{-4})(8.5172) + (7.36 \times 10^{-8})(617.96)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{K}} = 0.00140 + 0.002019 + 0.0000455$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{K}} = 0.0034645$$

*Step 4:* Invert and convert:
$$T_{K} = \dfrac{1}{0.0034645} = 288.64\,\text{K}$$

$$T_{°C} = 288.64 - 273.15$$

$$\boxed{T_{°C} \approx 15.49\,°C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Kelvin conversion:** The Steinhart-Hart equation outputs **1/T in Kelvin**. Many students forget to subtract **273.15** to convert to Celsius — leading to a -273.15 °C error (loss of 2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing °C with K in the denominator:** Writing the equation as $1/°C$ instead of $1/K$ will yield a wrong final answer.
> 3. **Skipping the $\beta T^{2}$ term in RTD problems:** At temperatures above 100 °C, neglecting the quadratic term leads to errors of 1–2 °C. Always mention both $\alpha T$ and $\beta T^{2}$ terms for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up sensor types:** Writing $V = S \cdot T$ instead of $V = S \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ for thermocouples will lose the conceptual-mark allocation.
> 5. **Wrong exponent in Steinhart-Hart:** Students often write $[\ln(R)]^{2}$ instead of $[\ln(R)]^{3}$ — this is a fatal mistake worth 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Temperature Sensor Definition:** Transducer converting thermal energy → electrical signal.
- **Five Major Types:** Thermocouple, RTD, NTC/PTC Thermistor, Semiconductor (Analog), Digital IC.
- **Seebeck Effect:** $V = S \cdot \Delta T$; basis of thermocouples; needs CJC.
- **RTD Equation:** $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^{2})$; PT100 = 100 Ω at 0 °C; $\alpha = 0.00385$.
- **Thermistor Equation:** $1/T = A + B\ln(R) + C[\ln(R)]^{3}$ (in **Kelvin**!); B3950 = 10 kΩ at 25 °C.
- **LM35:** $V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \cdot T$ — no offset, no negative voltage needed.
- **TMP36:** $V_{out} = 500\,\text{mV} + 10\,\text{mV/°C} \cdot T$ — can read negative temperatures.
- **DS18B20:** 1-Wire digital, **±0.5 °C** accuracy, 0.0625 °C resolution, 64-bit unique address.
- **DHT22 / BME280:** Combined temperature + humidity; BME280 also gives pressure; I²C.
- **IR Sensors (MLX90614):** Non-contact; measure **black-body radiation** via Stefan-Boltzmann law: $P = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.670374419 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$.
- **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Mandatory for thermocouple-based absolute temperature reading.
- **IoT Node Architecture:** Sensor → ADC → MCU → Wireless → Cloud → Dashboard/Actuator.
- **Resolution Formula:** For a 12-bit ADC with 3.3 V reference: $\text{Resolution} = V_{ref} / 2^{12} \approx 0.806\,\text{mV/LSB}$.
- **Wheatstone Bridge:** Used to read small resistance changes in RTDs.
- **Selection Rule of Thumb:** High accuracy → RTD; Wide range → Thermocouple; Low cost + IoT → Digital IC (DS18B20); Non-contact → IR (MLX90614).
- **Never forget:** Steinhart-Hart outputs **Kelvin**, not Celsius!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optical Fiber (Fiber Optic)** is a dielectric, cylindrical waveguide made of high-purity silica glass (SiO₂) or plastic, designed to transmit information in the form of light pulses (electromagnetic waves in the optical frequency range, typically **850 nm, 1300 nm, and 1550 nm**) from one end to the other with minimal loss. It operates on the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**, where the core has a slightly higher refractive index (n₁) than the surrounding cladding (n₂).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of IoT, fiber optics serves as the **high-bandwidth, low-latency, electromagnetic-immune physical layer backbone** for interconnecting IoT gateways, edge servers, and data centers. The term "Fiber Optic" in the syllabus encompasses the physical construction, the principle of light propagation, the optical fiber link, and the sensors/actuators based on fiber optics for IoT applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a fiber optic cable as a **"plumbing pipe for light."**

Imagine a long, flexible glass tube with a mirror-coated interior. If you shine a flashlight straight into one end, the light bounces off the walls at perfect angles, zigzagging its way down the tube without escaping. This is exactly how optical fiber works! 

- The **glass core** is the central highway where light travels.
- The **cladding** acts like a perfect mirror that traps the light (instead of metal, it uses the physics of refraction).
- The **jacket** is the protective plastic coating that prevents the glass from breaking when you bend the cable.

In IoT terms, think of a **smart factory** where hundreds of sensors (temperature, vibration, pressure) sit near heavy electrical motors generating massive electromagnetic interference (EMI). Wi-Fi fails, copper cables fail, but **fiber optic cables** keep transmitting data perfectly because they carry *light*, not electricity — EMI cannot disturb them!

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants are mandatory for KTU numerical problems:

| Symbol | Constant / Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **c** | Speed of light in vacuum | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| **λ** | Operating wavelength bands | **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm** |
| **n (silica)** | Refractive index of pure silica | **~1.46** |
| **Δn** | Index difference (n₁ − n₂) | ~0.01 to 0.02 |
| **θ_c** | Critical angle (typical) | ~70° to 80° |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Recap for KTU Viva:**
> - **Refractive Index (n):** The ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to the speed of light in a medium: $n = c / v$.
> - **Total Internal Reflection (TIR):** The complete reflection of a light ray at the boundary of two media when the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Light Propagation

The light propagation in an optical fiber follows a strict 5-step physics pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Light Injection at the Source:** An **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** for short-range/MMF or a **Laser Diode (LD)** for long-range/SMF injects a modulated light pulse into the fiber core. The input cone of light must fall within the **Acceptance Cone** to be guided.
- **Step 2 — Refraction at the Air–Core Interface (Snell's Law):** As the light enters the fiber from air ($n_0 = 1.0$), it bends according to **Snell's Law**: $n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$. The light ray now travels through the denser core at a refracted angle.
- **Step 3 — Total Internal Reflection at Core–Cladding Interface:** When the ray hits the core-cladding boundary, it encounters a higher-to-lower index transition ($n_1 > n_2$). If the angle of incidence is **greater than the critical angle ($\theta_c$)**, the light is **100% reflected** back into the core with zero leakage.
- **Step 4 — Guided Propagation via Multiple TIRs:** The light ray undergoes thousands of TIRs per meter, traveling in a zig-zag (step-index) or smooth helical (graded-index) path. This is the "guided mode."
- **Step 5 — Photodetection at the Receiver End:** At the destination, a **PIN photodiode** or **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** converts the light pulses back into electrical signals.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Physics

- **Why is TIR needed?** Because without a perfect mirror boundary, light would leak out of the fiber within centimeters. TIR provides a **lossless** boundary (in theory) because no light energy crosses into the cladding.
- **How is the critical angle defined?** It is the *minimum* angle of incidence (measured from the normal) above which TIR occurs. Below this angle, light refracts and escapes.

**Deriving the critical angle from Snell's Law:**
At the critical angle, the refracted angle in the cladding equals $90°$.

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear every year in KTU exams.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Speed of light in medium | $v = c / n$ | m/s |
| 2 | Snell's Law | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Critical Angle | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$ | degrees or radians |
| 4 | Numerical Aperture (NA) | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sin \theta_a$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Acceptance Angle ($\theta_a$) | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | degrees |
| 6 | Fractional Index Change ($\Delta$) | $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ | dimensionless, typically $\Delta \ll 1$ |
| 7 | NA (approx. for small $\Delta$) | $NA \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Number of Modes (V-number) | $V = (2\pi a / \lambda) \cdot NA$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Modes in Multimode Fiber | $M \approx V^2 / 2$ | integer |
| 10 | Attenuation / Loss | $\alpha = (10/L) \log_{10}(P_{in} / P_{out})$ | dB/km |
| 11 | Pulse Broadening (Dispersion) | $\Delta\tau = L \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot D$ | seconds (D in ps/nm·km) |
| 12 | Bandwidth-Distance Product | $B \cdot L$ | MHz·km or GHz·km |
| 13 | Refractive Index relation | $n_1 = c / v_1$ , $n_2 = c / v_2$ | — |
| 14 | Brewster's Angle (polarization) | $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$ | degrees |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in IoT & Real-World Applications

- **5G Fronthaul & Backhaul:** Fiber connects 5G base stations (gNodeB) to the core network with latencies below **1 ms**.
- **Submarine Data Cables:** The internet backbone — 99% of intercontinental data travels through undersea fiber optic cables.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Fiber sensors monitor **temperature, strain, pressure, and vibration** in oil refineries, smart grids, and high-voltage substations.
- **Medical IoT:** Fiber-optic endoscopes and **FBG (Fiber Bragg Grating) sensors** for minimally invasive surgery.
- **Automotive IoT:** LiDAR systems in autonomous vehicles use fiber-based laser sources.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Numerical Aperture (NA) from Snell's Law

**Given:** A step-index optical fiber with core refractive index $n_1$ and cladding refractive index $n_2$. Light enters from air ($n_0 = 1.0$) at an external acceptance angle $\theta_a$.

**Find:** Expression for Numerical Aperture.

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the Air-Core Interface (Point A):**
The light enters the fiber from air. Let $\theta_a$ be the acceptance angle in air and $\theta_1$ be the refracted angle inside the core.

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Snell's Law at the Core-Cladding Interface (Point B):**
The refracted ray inside the core strikes the core-cladding boundary. The angle of incidence at this boundary is $(90^\circ - \theta_1)$ (geometry of the triangle formed by the ray).

For TIR to occur, this angle of incidence must equal the critical angle $\theta_c$:

$$
n_1 \sin(90^\circ - \theta_1) = n_2 \sin 90^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Equation:**
Using the identity $\sin(90^\circ - \theta_1) = \cos \theta_1$:

$$
n_1 \cos \theta_1 = n_2
$$

**Step 4 — Express $\cos \theta_1$ in terms of $\sin \theta_1$:**

$$
\cos \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into Step 3:**

$$
n_1 \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_1} = n_2
$$

**Step 6 — Square Both Sides:**

$$
n_1^2 (1 - \sin^2 \theta_1) = n_2^2
$$

$$
n_1^2 - n_1^2 \sin^2 \theta_1 = n_2^2
$$

$$
n_1^2 \sin^2 \theta_1 = n_1^2 - n_2^2
$$

$$
\sin \theta_1 = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

**Step 7 — From Step 1, Express $\sin \theta_a$:**

$$
\sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

$$
\boxed{NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: V-Number and Number of Modes

**The V-number (V parameter)** is a normalized frequency that determines how many modes a multimode fiber can support.

$$
V = \frac{2 \pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA
$$

where $a$ is the core radius and $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength.

**For Multimode Fiber (MMF):**

$$
\boxed{M \approx \frac{V^2}{2}}
$$

**For Single-Mode Fiber (SMF):** The condition for single-mode operation is $V < 2.405$ (the first root of the Bessel function $J_0$).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A step-index optical fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. Calculate: (a) Critical angle, (b) Numerical Aperture, (c) Acceptance angle. Take $n_0 = 1.0$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Critical Angle:**

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.45}{1.50}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9667) = 75.16^\circ
$$

**(b) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2}
$$

$$
NA = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475}
$$

$$
\boxed{NA = 0.384}
$$

**(c) Acceptance Angle:**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.384)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_a = 22.59^\circ}
$$

## 3.4 Attenuation and Loss Calculation

**Problem:** An optical signal of power $100 \ \mu W$ is launched into a fiber. After traveling 50 km, the received power is $2 \ \mu W$. Calculate the attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ in dB/km.

**Solution:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{10}{50} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\alpha = 0.2 \times \log_{10}(50) = 0.2 \times 1.699
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha = 0.34 \ \text{dB/km}}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber. What is its significance in fiber optic communication?**

**Model Answer:**

Numerical Aperture (NA) is a dimensionless quantity that defines the light-gathering ability of an optical fiber. It is mathematically defined as the sine of the acceptance angle $\theta_a$:

$$
NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

**Significance:**
- **(1) Light Collection Efficiency:** A higher NA means the fiber can accept light from a wider cone, making coupling from LEDs easier.
- **(2) Mode Capacity:** NA directly determines the V-number, which controls the number of propagating modes: $V = (2\pi a / \lambda) \cdot NA$.
- **(3) Bandwidth Trade-off:** Higher NA allows more modes but increases modal dispersion, reducing bandwidth.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $NA = n_1 - n_2$. This is WRONG. NA involves the **square root of the difference of squares** of indices, not the difference of indices.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Explain the principle of Total Internal Reflection (TIR). State the two conditions required for TIR to occur.**

**Model Answer:**

Total Internal Reflection is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray striking the boundary between two media is completely reflected back into the denser medium, with **zero transmission** into the rarer medium.

**Two Essential Conditions for TIR:**
- **(1) Light must travel from a denser medium to a rarer medium:** $n_1 > n_2$ (core to cladding).
- **(2) Angle of incidence must exceed the critical angle:** $\theta_i > \theta_c$, where $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$.

If either condition fails, the light refracts and escapes the fiber. TIR is the foundational principle that allows light to be guided through kilometers of fiber with negligible loss.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) Derive the expression for Numerical Aperture (NA) and Acceptance Angle of a step-index optical fiber starting from Snell's Law. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a step-index fiber with core index $n_1$ and cladding index $n_2$ ($n_1 > n_2$). Light enters from air ($n_0 = 1.0$) at the axial face. The maximum external angle at which light enters and still undergoes TIR is the **acceptance angle $\theta_a$**.

**Step 2 — Snell's Law at Air-Core Interface (Entry Point):**

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1
$$

where $\theta_1$ is the angle of the refracted ray inside the core (measured from the fiber axis).

**Step 3 — Geometry at Core-Cladding Interface:** The refracted ray travels and strikes the core-cladding boundary. By geometry, the angle of incidence at this boundary is $(90^\circ - \theta_1)$.

**Step 4 — TIR Condition (Acceptance Limit):** For the ray to be just guided, the angle of incidence must equal the critical angle:

$$
n_1 \sin(90^\circ - \theta_1) = n_2 \sin 90^\circ
$$

$$
n_1 \cos \theta_1 = n_2
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\theta_1$:**

$$
\cos \theta_1 = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

$$
\sin \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into Step 2:**

$$
\sin \theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\right)}
$$

**(b) An optical fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. Determine: (i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance Angle, (iii) Critical Angle, (iv) Fractional Index Difference $\Delta$. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
NA = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588}
$$

$$
\boxed{NA = 0.2425}
$$

**(ii) Acceptance Angle:**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_a = 14.03^\circ}
$$

**(iii) Critical Angle:**

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.46}{1.48}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9865)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_c = 80.58^\circ}
$$

**(iv) Fractional Index Difference:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.48 - 1.46}{1.48} = \frac{0.02}{1.48}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta = 0.01351 \text{ or } 1.351\%}
$$

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the different types of optical fibers based on refractive index profile and mode of propagation. Compare their bandwidth and applications. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Classification Based on Refractive Index Profile:**

**(1) Step-Index Fiber:** The core has a **uniform refractive index** $n_1$ throughout, with an **abrupt (step) change** to $n_2$ at the cladding boundary. Light travels in sharp zig-zag paths.

**(2) Graded-Index Fiber:** The core refractive index **varies parabolically** — highest at the center and decreases radially toward the cladding. Light travels in smooth, curved (helical) paths. Higher-index central region causes rays near the axis to travel slower (faster in physics) but shorter distance, while off-axis rays travel faster but longer distance — both arrive at the same time, **reducing modal dispersion**.

**Classification Based on Mode of Propagation:**

**(1) Single-Mode Fiber (SMF):** Core diameter is very small (8–10 µm). Only **one mode** propagates (V < 2.405). Requires laser source. **No modal dispersion**, very high bandwidth (>100 GHz·km). Used for long-haul telecom, submarine cables, 5G backhaul.

**(2) Multimode Fiber (MMF):** Core diameter is large (50 or 62.5 µm). **Hundreds of modes** propagate simultaneously. Can use LED source. Suffers from **modal dispersion**, lower bandwidth (typically 500 MHz·km for OM2). Used for LAN, data centers, short-range IoT.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Step-Index MMF | Graded-Index MMF | Single-Mode Fiber |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Core Diameter | 50–100 µm | 50–62.5 µm | 8–10 µm |
| Index Profile | Step | Graded (parabolic) | Step |
| Source | LED | LED | Laser |
| Bandwidth | Low (~20 MHz·km) | Medium (500 MHz·km) | Very High (100s of GHz·km) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical Application | Short LAN | LAN, Data Center | Long-haul, Submarine, 5G |

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an optical fiber communication link. List any four advantages of optical fiber communication. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Explanation:**

The OFC link consists of three main sections: **Transmitter, Fiber Channel, and Receiver**.

- **Transmitter Section:** The input electrical signal (voice/data) is encoded and fed to a **driver circuit**, which modulates a **light source (LED or Laser Diode)**. The optical signal is launched into the fiber via a coupling lens.
- **Fiber Channel:** The **optical fiber** carries the modulated light via TIR over long distances. **Repeaters/EDFA amplifiers** boost the signal periodically to compensate for attenuation.
- **Receiver Section:** A **photodetector (PIN/APD)** converts the optical signal back to electrical form. A **signal conditioner** (amplifier + filter) restores the original waveform for the output.

**Four Advantages of Optical Fiber Communication:**
- **(1) Enormous Bandwidth:** THz capacity, supporting 100+ Gbps data rates.
- **(2) Very Low Attenuation:** 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm — much lower than copper.
- **(3) EMI Immunity:** Immune to electromagnetic interference, ideal for industrial IoT.
- **(4) Security & Lightweight:** Extremely difficult to tap, no signal radiation; lighter than copper cables.
- **(5)** No short-circuit fire hazard, ideal for explosive/chemical plant IoT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** When drawing the block diagram, students often **omit the modulator/driver** between the input signal and the light source. Without explicit mention of the **modulation step**, you lose **2 marks**. Also, listing only "high speed" or "less loss" without numerical values is considered a vague answer. Always state **quantitative values** like "0.2 dB/km loss at 1550 nm" to score full marks.
