Step 4 — Apply the LM35 scale factor.

$$
T \;(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{V_{in}}{0.010 \; \text{V/}^{\circ}C} = \dfrac{1.4990}{0.010}
$$

Step 5 — Final result.

$$
T = 149.90 \;^{\circ}C
$$

> **Sanity check & correction:** This exceeds the LM35's normal range (−55 to +150 °C). A reading this high is plausible only at the upper extreme, which would be unusual in a lab. Students must verify the ADC pin and reference selection. The procedural marks, however, are valid for the calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — print this before entering the exam hall.**

- **Arduino IDE = open-source, cross-platform, GPL-licensed C/C++ tool** for AVR/ARM/ESP MCUs.
- **Two mandatory functions in every sketch:** `void setup()` (runs once) and `void loop()` (runs forever).
- **IDE pipeline:** Editor → Pre-Processor → Compiler → Linker → Uploader → Bootloader → MCU.
- **Default IDE programmer:** *AVRISP mkII* for AVR; *Esptool* for ESP; *Bossac* for SAM.
- **Default baud rate for Serial Monitor:** **9600 bps** — must match `Serial.begin()`.
- **Uno's ADC is 10-bit → 1024 levels → $V_{step} = V_{ref}/1024$.**
- **ESP32's ADC is 12-bit → 4096 levels → $V_{step} = V_{ref}/4096$** (better resolution, non-linear at extremes).
- **`analogWrite` resolution:** 8-bit on Uno (0–255 maps to 0–100 % duty cycle).
- **Arduino Uno = ATmega328P, 32 KB flash, 2 KB SRAM, 5 V logic, no wireless.**
- **Arduino Mega 2560 = ATmega2560, 256 KB flash, 8 KB SRAM, 54 digital I/O, 5 V logic.**
- **ESP32 = Xtensa LX6 dual-core, 4 MB flash, 520 KB SRAM, 3.3 V, built-in WiFi + BLE — the *IoT favorite*.**
- **Board Manager** installs cores (compilers + uploaders); **Library Manager** installs reusable C++ packages.
- **Sketch file extension:** `.ino` (UTF-8 plain text).
- **PWM frequency on Uno pins 9, 10, 11: 490 Hz; pins 5, 6: 980 Hz.**
- **KTU 2024 expectation:** Be able to pick the *correct board* given constraints (WiFi, pins, voltage, power).
- **KTU 2024 expectation:** Be able to convert an ADC reading to voltage *and* to a physical quantity (temperature, light, current).

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Editor, Compiler, Uploader, Bootloader, Flash, Run."* Trace this chain in any descriptive answer, and the examiner will know you understand the full Arduino ecosystem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ESP8266
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ESP8266

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESP8266** is a low-cost, low-power **System-on-Chip (SoC)** manufactured by **Espressif Systems (Shanghai, China)**. It integrates a **32-bit Tensilica L106 Diamond Standard 106Micro** microcontroller, a **Wi-Fi transceiver** compliant with **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n**, and a full **TCP/IP stack**, enabling any microcontroller to connect to a 2.4 GHz wireless network.

The chip is widely used as a **Wi-Fi enabler** for embedded devices and is often paired with Arduino, Raspberry Pi, or used standalone with the Arduino IDE.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Wi-Fi Walkie-Talkie for Microcontrollers"**
>
> Imagine your Arduino UNO is a person who can think (process data) and speak (output via serial), but is **deaf to Wi-Fi** — it cannot hear the internet. The **ESP8266 acts like a translator/earpiece** that clips onto the Arduino. When the Arduino "speaks" serial commands (`AT` commands), the ESP8266 translates them into wireless packets and broadcasts them to your home router, which in turn passes them to the internet. Conversely, it brings back replies from the cloud.

## 1.3 Key Hardware & Electrical Specifications

| Parameter | Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Voltage** | **3.0 – 3.6 (Typ. 3.3)** | V |
| **Supply Current (Avg TX)** | **~70** | mA |
| **Peak Current (Burst TX)** | **~200 – 300** | mA |
| **Deep Sleep Current** | **~20** | µA |
| **CPU** | Tensilica L106 @ **80 / 160** | MHz |
| **SRAM (Instruction)** | **64** | KB |
| **SRAM (Data)** | **96** | KB |
| **Flash Memory (external)** | **1 – 16** | MB |
| **Wi-Fi Standards** | **802.11 b/g/n** | — |
| **Frequency Band** | **2.412 – 2.484** | GHz |
| **GPIO Pins** | **17** (multiplexed) | — |
| **ADC** | **10-bit, 0 – 1.0** | V (max) |
| **Operating Temp** | **-40 to +125** | °C |

> [!WARNING]
> The ESP8266's GPIO pins are **NOT 5V tolerant**. Supplying 5V will permanently damage the chip. Always use a **3.3V regulator** (e.g., AMS1117-3.3) or a logic level shifter when interfacing with 5V Arduino boards.

## 1.4 Physical Form Factors

The chip is available in multiple modules:
- **ESP-01** — Most popular, 8 pins (2 GPIO exposed), 1 MB flash
- **ESP-12E/F** — 22 pins, 11+ GPIO, FCC-certified antenna
- **NodeMCU (V2/V3)** — Dev board with CP2102/CH340 USB-UART, onboard voltage regulator, micro-USB
- **Wemos D1 Mini** — Compact NodeMCU variant, breadboard friendly

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture (Block-Level)

The ESP8266 is composed of four major functional blocks:

- **CPU Subsystem** — Tensilica L106 core, ROM bootloader, on-chip SRAM
- **Wi-Fi Radio (MAC + Baseband)** — Implements 802.11 PHY, with integrated PA, LNA, antenna switch, and balun
- **Peripherals** — UART (×2), SPI (×2), I²C, I²S, PWM (×4), 10-bit ADC, GPIO
- **Clock & Power Management** — Internal PLL, multiple power domains enabling sleep modes

## 2.2 The Three Operating (Wi-Fi) Modes

The chip can act in three logical wireless roles, set by the `AT+CWMODE` command:

| Mode | Value | Behaviour | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Station (STA)** | `1` | Joins an existing Wi-Fi AP (router) | Most IoT nodes reporting to cloud |
| **Soft-AP** | `2` | Creates its own Wi-Fi hotspot | Direct device configuration / provisioning |
| **STA + AP** | `3` | Both simultaneously | Gateway / bridge device |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The TCP/IP networking functions (sockets, DNS, DHCP) are handled **entirely inside the ESP8266 firmware**. The host microcontroller only needs to send simple ASCII **AT commands** over UART — this is why the chip is called a "Wi-Fi coprocessor."

## 2.3 Power-Save Modes (KTU High-Yield)

| Mode | Typical Current | CPU State | Wi-Fi State | Wake-up |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Active (TX/RX)** | **70 – 300 mA** | ON | ON | — |
| **Modem-Sleep** | **15 mA** | ON | Radio OFF between DTIMs | Automatic (DTIM beacon) |
| **Light-Sleep** | **0.5 mA** | Suspended | OFF | Wake via GPIO / timer |
| **Deep-Sleep** | **20 µA** | OFF (state lost) | OFF | External RST / GPIO16 → RST |

> [!NOTE]
> **Deep Sleep** retains **nothing** in RAM. To wake periodically, the **RTC** must be pre-programmed with a wake-up interval (e.g., `esp_deep_sleep(60e6)` = 60 s). GPIO16 must be wired to the **RST** pin for self-wake.

## 2.4 Pin Reference (Most Common — ESP-12E)

| Pin | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GND** | Ground reference |
| 2 | **GPIO2** | General IO (must be HIGH at boot) |
| 3 | **GPIO0** | Boot mode select (LOW = Flash mode; HIGH = Run mode) |
| 4 | **RX / GPIO3** | UART Receive |
| 5 | **TX / GPIO1** | UART Transmit (prints boot messages @ 74880 baud) |
| 6 | **CH_PD** | Chip enable (must be HIGH to operate) |
| 7 | **RST** | Active-LOW reset |
| 8 | **VCC** | 3.3 V supply |
| 9-11 | GPIO4/5/14, GPIO12/13/15/16 | General purpose, PWM, I²C, SPI |

## 2.5 Key Formulas (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Replace every vertical bar `|` with `\vert` in equations to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{active}\,t_{on} \;+\; I_{sleep}\,t_{off}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Avg current budget over a duty cycle $T$ | Battery lifetime estimation |
| 2 | $t_{life} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{batt} \;\cdot\; \eta}{P_{avg} \;/\; V_{batt}}$ | Battery capacity $\eta$ efficiency | How long an IoT node runs |
| 3 | $P_{r} \;=\; P_{t} \, G_{t} \, G_{r} \,\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Friis free-space equation | RF link-budget for Wi-Fi range |
| 4 | $d_{max} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} P_{r,min}}}$ | Max distance with $P_{r,min}$ sensitivity | Outdoor range calculation |
| 5 | $R_{b} \;=\; N_{dbps} \;\cdot\; \text{coding rate} \;\cdot\; \dfrac{1}{OFDM_{sym}}$ | OFDM bit rate | Wi-Fi throughput (802.11n) |
| 6 | $V_{ADC} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{input}}{V_{ref}}\,(2^{10}-1)$ | 10-bit ADC reading | Sensor interfacing |

### Real-World Utility

The formulas above are used in **battery-powered IoT node design** (e.g., smart agriculture sensors that sleep and wake every 5 minutes) and in **RF planning** for indoor Wi-Fi mesh networks. The deep-sleep current of **20 µA** is what makes the ESP8266 attractive for **months-long deployments** on a 2 × AA battery pack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Battery Lifetime for a Duty-Cycled Sensor Node

> **Problem:** A soil-moisture sensor node uses an ESP8266. The active (transmit) phase draws $I_{active} = 70$ mA for $t_{on} = 3$ s, and the deep-sleep phase draws $I_{sleep} = 20$ µA for $t_{off} = 297$ s. A 2 × AA alkaline pack provides $V_{batt} = 3.0$ V and $C_{batt} = 2500$ mAh. Find (i) the average current, (ii) the average power, and (iii) the expected battery life (assume $\eta = 0.85$).

### Step (i) — Average Current

The cycle period is:

$$T_{cycle} \;=\; t_{on} \;+\; t_{off} \;=\; 3 \;+\; 297 \;=\; 300 \text{ s}$$

The average current is the time-weighted mean:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} \;&=\; \dfrac{I_{active}\,t_{on} \;+\; I_{sleep}\,t_{off}}{T_{cycle}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \dfrac{(70 \times 10^{-3})(3) \;+\; (20 \times 10^{-6})(297)}{300} \\[4pt]
&=\; \dfrac{0.210 \;+\; 0.00594}{300} \\[4pt]
&=\; \dfrac{0.21594}{300} \\[4pt]
&=\; 7.198 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.72 \text{ mA}}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Note:* $I_{sleep} \, t_{off} = 20 \mu A \times 297 s = 5.94$ mA·s, which is $\approx 2.8\%$ of the active contribution — the **active phase dominates** even though it is 100× shorter.

### Step (ii) — Average Power

$$P_{avg} \;=\; I_{avg} \cdot V_{batt} \;=\; 7.198 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.0 \;\approx\; \mathbf{2.16 \text{ mW}}$$

### Step (iii) — Battery Life

The useful charge (after efficiency losses) is:

$$C_{useful} \;=\; C_{batt} \cdot \eta \;=\; 2500 \times 0.85 \;=\; 2125 \text{ mAh}$$

The runtime is:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{life} \;&=\; \dfrac{C_{useful}}{I_{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{2125 \text{ mAh}}{0.72 \text{ mA}} \\[4pt]
&\approx\; 2951 \text{ hours} \;\approx\; \mathbf{123 \text{ days} \;\approx\; 4 \text{ months}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A simple soil-moisture node with 1% duty cycle and deep sleep can run **~4 months** on a 2500 mAh pack — this is the central engineering reason IoT firmware developers love the ESP8266.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Maximum Indoor Range (Friis Equation)

> **Problem:** A Wi-Fi transmitter with $P_{t} = +20$ dBm, $G_{t} = G_{r} = 2$ dBi operates at $f = 2.45$ GHz. The receiver sensitivity is $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm. Estimate the maximum line-of-sight range.

### Step 1 — Wavelength

$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{c}{f} \;=\; \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}} \;\approx\; 0.1224 \text{ m} \;=\; 12.24 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange Friis

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{max} \;&=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{t}\,G_{t}\,G_{r}\,\lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2}\,P_{r,min}}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Linear Power

$$P_{t} \;=\; 10^{20/10} \;=\; 100 \text{ mW}$$
$$P_{r,min} \;=\; 10^{-90/10} \;=\; 10^{-9} \text{ mW} \;=\; 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mW}$$
$$G_{t} G_{r} \;=\; 10^{2/10} \times 10^{2/10} \;=\; 1.585 \times 1.585 \;\approx\; 2.512$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{max} \;&=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{100 \times 2.512 \times (0.1224)^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \times 10^{-9}}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{100 \times 2.512 \times 0.01498}{157.91 \times 10^{-9}}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{3.762}{1.5791 \times 10^{-7}}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \sqrt{2.383 \times 10^{7}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{4881 \text{ m}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{4.9 \text{ km}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Real-World Loss Margin

Indoors, walls attenuate by **15 – 30 dB**. Realistic **indoor range** ≈ **40 – 80 m**; **outdoor LoS** ≈ **200 – 300 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical** Friis range = 4.9 km.
> **Practical** outdoor ≈ 300 m.
> **Indoor** ≈ 50 m.
> The 30 – 40 dB gap is called the **path-loss margin** or **fade margin** in RF link-budget design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AT Command Set (Most Tested in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **high-yield** — the following AT commands appear in nearly every KTU module test on ESP8266.

| # | AT Command | Function | Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `AT` | UART test / handshake | `OK` |
| 2 | `AT+RST` | Software reset | `OK` + boot messages |
| 3 | `AT+GMR` | Firmware version | `AT version, SDK version` |
| 4 | `AT+CWMODE=<n>` | Set Wi-Fi mode (1=STA, 2=AP, 3=Both) | `OK` |
| 5 | `AT+CWJAP="SSID","pwd"` | Join an AP | `WIFI CONNECTED` / `WIFI GOT IP` |
| 6 | `AT+CWLAP` | List visible APs | List of `<ssid>,<rssi>,<mac>` |
| 7 | `AT+CWQAP` | Disconnect from AP | `OK` |
| 8 | `AT+CIFSR` | Get local IP / MAC | `+CIFSR:APIP,"..."` |
| 9 | `AT+CIPMUX=<n>` | Single (0) or multi (1) connection | `OK` |
| 10 | `AT+CIPSTART=<type>,"addr",<port>` | Open TCP / UDP socket | `CONNECT` |
| 11 | `AT+CIPSEND=<len>` | Send data (followed by raw bytes) | `SEND OK` |
| 12 | `AT+CIPCLOSE` | Close connection | `CLOSED` |
| 13 | `AT+CIPSTATUS` | Connection status | `STATUS:<n>` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ESP8266 Internal Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PS["POWER AND CLOCK UNIT"]
        A1["Voltage Regulator 3.3V"]
        A2["PLL Clock Synthesizer"]
        A3["RTC and Deep Sleep Timer"]
    end

    subgraph CPU["CPU SUBSYSTEM"]
        B1["Tensilica L106 32-bit Core"]
        B2["ROM Bootloader"]
        B3["SRAM 64KB Instruction 96KB Data"]
    end

    subgraph WIFI["WI-FI SUBSYSTEM"]
        C1["802.11 b/g/n MAC"]
        C2["Baseband Processor"]
        C3["2.4 GHz RF Transceiver"]
        C4["Antenna PCB Trace or U.FL"]
    end

    subgraph PER["PERIPHERAL SUBSYSTEM"]
        D1["UART0 and UART1"]
        D2["SPI Master and Slave"]
        D3["I2C Controller"]
        D4["PWM Channels x4"]
        D5["10-bit ADC"]
        D6["GPIO Multiplexer x17"]
    end

    subgraph STK["TCP-IP STACK"]
        E1["LwIP Lightweight IP"]
        E2["DHCP and DNS Client"]
        E3["SNTP and mDNS"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> C3
    A1 --> D1
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> C2
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> D1
    B1 --> D2
    B1 --> D3
    B1 --> D4
    B1 --> D5
    B1 --> D6
    B1 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
```

## 4.2 Typical Cloud-Connection Flow (Node → Router → Cloud)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["SENSOR READS DATA e.g. DHT11"]
    S2["HOST MCU FORMS JSON PAYLOAD"]
    S3["HOST SENDS AT COMMANDS TO ESP8266 OVER UART"]
    S4["ESP8266 CONNECTS TO WI-FI ROUTER USING CWJAP"]
    S5["ESP8266 OPENS TCP SOCKET WITH CIPSTART TO THINGSPEAK"]
    S6["ESP8266 SENDS HTTP GET WITH CIPSEND"]
    S7["CLOUD SERVER RESPONDS WITH 200 OK"]
    S8["HOST ENTERS DEEP SLEEP FOR NEXT CYCLE"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    S8 -.after wake.-> S1
```

## 4.3 ESP-12E Pinout Mapping (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP["LEFT SIDE PINS"]
        P1["1 GND"]
        P2["2 GPIO2"]
        P3["3 GPIO0"]
        P4["4 GPIO3 RX"]
        P5["5 GPIO1 TX"]
    end
    subgraph BOT["RIGHT SIDE PINS"]
        P6["6 CH_PD ENABLE"]
        P7["7 GPIO15 RST"]
        P8["8 VCC 3.3V"]
        P9["9 GPIO14"]
        P10["10 GPIO12"]
        P11["11 GPIO13"]
    end
    TOP --- BOT
```

## 4.4 Power-Save Mode State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACTIVE
    ACTIVE --> MODEMSLEEP: idle no tx
    MODEMSLEEP --> ACTIVE: tx request
    ACTIVE --> LIGHTSLEEP: no activity
    LIGHTSLEEP --> ACTIVE: gpio wake
    ACTIVE --> DEEPSLEEP: command
    DEEPSLEEP --> [*]: reset
    DEEPSLEEP --> ACTIVE: gpio16 pulse to rst
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the three operating modes of the ESP8266 and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The ESP8266 supports three Wi-Fi operating modes configured via the `AT+CWMODE` command:

1. **Station Mode (CWMODE=1):** The ESP8266 acts as a **client** that joins an existing Wi-Fi Access Point (router). *Application:* Smart-home sensor node reporting temperature to the cloud via home Wi-Fi.
2. **Soft-AP Mode (CWMODE=2):** The ESP8266 creates its own Wi-Fi hotspot, allowing other devices to connect directly to it. *Application:* Initial Wi-Fi provisioning of an IoT device via a smartphone app.
3. **Station + AP Mode (CWMODE=3):** The chip acts as both a client and an access point simultaneously. *Application:* A Wi-Fi repeater or gateway that bridges IoT devices to the internet.

**(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Explain the significance of the CH_PD and GPIO0 pins in ESP8266 boot configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

- **CH_PD (Chip Enable, pin 6):** This is the **master enable** pin. It must be pulled **HIGH (3.3 V)** for the chip to function. Pulling it LOW powers down the entire radio and CPU (similar to `EN` on a microcontroller). It is **essential for deep-sleep wake-up control**.

- **GPIO0 (pin 3):** This pin **selects the boot mode** at reset:
  - **GPIO0 = LOW** at reset → enters **Flash Upload Mode** (used to program firmware via UART).
  - **GPIO0 = HIGH** (or floating with internal pull-up) at reset → boots from flash into **normal execution mode**.

Correct boot configuration is critical — leaving GPIO0 LOW causes the module to appear "dead" after a reset.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture** of the ESP8266, listing the major subsystems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **soil-moisture sensor node** is built using an ESP8266. The active TX phase lasts **2 s** at **120 mA**, and the deep-sleep phase lasts **598 s** at **20 µA**. The system uses a **3.0 V, 2000 mAh Li-ion cell** with efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Estimate the **average current, average power, and battery lifetime in days**. **(7 marks)**

#### — Model Solution for Q.A(a) —

**Internal Architecture of ESP8266:**

The ESP8266 SoC contains four major subsystems:

1. **CPU Subsystem** — A 32-bit **Tensilica L106 Diamond Standard** processor clocked up to **160 MHz**, with on-chip **ROM** (bootloader), **64 KB instruction SRAM**, and **96 KB data SRAM**.

2. **Wi-Fi Subsystem** — A complete **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n** radio with:
   - **MAC layer** (CSMA/CA, ACK handling)
   - **Baseband processor** (DSSS/CCK for b, OFDM for g/n)
   - **2.4 GHz RF transceiver** with integrated **PA (Power Amplifier)**, **LNA (Low-Noise Amplifier)**, **antenna switch**, and **balun**.

3. **Peripherals** — **2 × UART**, **2 × SPI**, **I²C**, **I²S**, **4 × PWM**, **10-bit ADC**, and **17 multiplexed GPIO** pins.

4. **TCP/IP Stack (LwIP)** — Implements **TCP/UDP, DHCP, DNS, SNTP** directly on-chip, removing the burden from the host MCU.

**Power Management** is provided by an internal voltage regulator and a dedicated **RTC** that supervises deep-sleep timing.

(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the visual representation.)

**(7 marks — awarded 2 for naming subsystems, 3 for describing each, 2 for diagram)**

#### — Model Solution for Q.A(b) —

**Given:**
- $t_{on} = 2$ s, $I_{active} = 120$ mA
- $t_{off} = 598$ s, $I_{sleep} = 20$ µA
- $V_{batt} = 3.0$ V, $C_{batt} = 2000$ mAh, $\eta = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Cycle Period:**

$$T_{cycle} \;=\; 2 \;+\; 598 \;=\; 600 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Average Current:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} \;&=\; \dfrac{I_{active}\,t_{on} \;+\; I_{sleep}\,t_{off}}{T_{cycle}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \dfrac{(120)(2) \;+\; (0.020)(598)}{600} \\[4pt]
&=\; \dfrac{240 \;+\; 11.96}{600} \\[4pt]
&=\; \dfrac{251.96}{600} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.4199 \text{ mA} \;\approx\; 0.42 \text{ mA}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Average Power:**

$$P_{avg} \;=\; I_{avg} \times V_{batt} \;=\; 0.42 \times 3.0 \;\approx\; \mathbf{1.26 \text{ mW}}$$

**Step 4 — Usable Capacity:**

$$C_{useful} \;=\; 2000 \times 0.80 \;=\; 1600 \text{ mAh}$$

**Step 5 — Battery Life:**

$$t_{life} \;=\; \dfrac{1600}{0.42} \;\approx\; 3809 \text{ hours} \;\approx\; \mathbf{158.7 \text{ days} \;\approx\; 5.3 \text{ months}}$$

**(7 marks — 1.5 for current, 1.5 for power, 4 for lifetime)**

---

### **Question B (Alternative to Question A)** [KTU University Exam – June 2022]
**(a)** Explain the **four power-save modes** of the ESP8266 in a tabular form with typical current consumption and CPU/Radio state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **Friis transmission equation**, compute the **maximum line-of-sight range** of a 2.4 GHz ESP8266 link given $P_{t} = +20$ dBm, $G_{t} = G_{r} = 2$ dBi, and receiver sensitivity $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm. Comment on the **practical indoor range** after considering a **fade margin of 25 dB**. **(7 marks)**

#### — Model Solution for Q.B(a) —

| Mode | Current | CPU | Radio | Wake-up |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Active** | 70 – 300 mA | ON | ON | — |
| **Modem-Sleep** | ~15 mA | ON | OFF between DTIMs | Automatic on DTIM beacon |
| **Light-Sleep** | ~0.5 mA | Suspended | OFF | GPIO edge or RTC timer |
| **Deep-Sleep** | ~20 µA | OFF (state lost) | OFF | External RST or GPIO16→RST pulse |

- **Active** is used during normal data transmission/reception.
- **Modem-Sleep** keeps CPU alive but switches off the radio between DTIM intervals (set by the AP) — saving ~50% power.
- **Light-Sleep** suspends the CPU clock, wakes on any GPIO edge.
- **Deep-Sleep** offers the **lowest current**, but RAM is lost; **GPIO16 must be hard-wired to RST** for self-wake via RTC timer.

**(7 marks — 1 each for current, 1 each for state, 1 for wake-up mechanism, 1 for trade-off discussion)**

#### — Model Solution for Q.B(b) —

**Step 1 — Convert dBm to linear:**

$$P_{t} = 10^{20/10} = 100 \text{ mW}, \quad P_{r,min} = 10^{-90/10} = 10^{-9} \text{ mW}$$
$$G_{t}\,G_{r} = 10^{2/10} \times 10^{2/10} \approx 1.585 \times 1.585 \approx 2.512$$

**Step 2 — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}} \approx 0.1224 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Friis Equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{max} \;&=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{t}\,G_{t}\,G_{r}\,\lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2}\,P_{r,min}}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{100 \times 2.512 \times (0.1224)^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \times 10^{-9}}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{3.762}{1.5791 \times 10^{-7}}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{2.383 \times 10^{7}} \\[4pt]
&\approx\; \mathbf{4881 \text{ m}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{4.9 \text{ km}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply 25 dB Fade Margin:**

$$d_{practical} \;=\; d_{max} \times 10^{-25/20} \;=\; 4881 \times 10^{-1.25} \;=\; 4881 \times 0.0562 \;\approx\; 274 \text{ m}$$

> **Indoor range after fade margin ≈ 270 – 300 m line-of-sight, but typically 30 – 60 m through walls** due to wall attenuation (concrete wall ≈ 10 – 15 dB each, plasterboard ≈ 3 – 6 dB).

**(7 marks — 1 for unit conversion, 2 for formula, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for practical interpretation)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in ESP8266 questions:**
>
> 1. **Boot-mode confusion** — Forgetting that GPIO0 = LOW means flash mode, not a normal boot. Always state the boot pin state explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong unit for current** — Mixing up mA and µA in the duty-cycle formula. Convert ALL currents to **amperes** (or all to mA with care) before substituting.
> 3. **dBm vs mW** — Many students write $-90$ dBm as $-90$ mW. The correct linear value is $10^{-9}$ mW = $1$ nW.
> 4. **Deep sleep wake-up** — Students often forget the **GPIO16 → RST** hardware connection required for self-wake. Just calling a software function is **not enough** in pure AT-command mode.
> 5. **GPIO 5V damage** — A common 2-mark question is "What happens if you connect an ESP8266 GPIO to 5 V?" Answer: **Permanent silicon damage; the chip becomes unusable**.
> 6. **AT+CWJAP** requires **BOTH** SSID and password in quotes with comma, e.g., `AT+CWJAP="HomeNet","secret123"`. Missing the comma is the #1 syntax error.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ESP8266** is a **32-bit Wi-Fi SoC** by **Espressif Systems** integrating **Tensilica L106 CPU + 802.11 b/g/n radio + TCP/IP stack**.
- ✅ **Operating voltage is 3.3 V (NOT 5V tolerant)** — 5 V destroys the chip.
- ✅ **Three Wi-Fi modes**: Station, Soft-AP, Station + AP (set via `AT+CWMODE`).
- ✅ **Boot pins** — `CH_PD = HIGH` (enable), `GPIO0 = HIGH` (run) / `LOW` (flash).
- ✅ **Four power-save modes** — Active (70 mA) → Modem-Sleep (15 mA) → Light-Sleep (0.5 mA) → Deep-Sleep (20 µA).
- ✅ **Deep-sleep wake** requires **GPIO16 wired to RST** for self-timed wake-up.
- ✅ **ADC** is **10-bit**, input range **0 – 1.0 V** (do NOT exceed 1 V).
- ✅ **Average current** = $\dfrac{I_{active}t_{on} + I_{sleep}t_{off}}{T_{cycle}}$ — used for battery-life estimation.
- ✅ **Friis equation** gives theoretical range; **fade margin** of 20 – 30 dB reduces practical indoor range to **~50 m**.
- ✅ **AT command** communication uses **UART** at **115200 baud** (default).
- ✅ **Memory** — On-chip: 64 KB IRAM + 96 KB DRAM; **External flash**: 1 – 16 MB for program + user data.
- ✅ **TX power** is typically **+20 dBm (100 mW)**; **Receiver sensitivity** is around **-90 dBm**.
- ✅ Common modules: **ESP-01** (hobby), **ESP-12E** (compact), **NodeMCU** (developer-friendly), **Wemos D1 Mini** (breadboard).
- ✅ **TCP/IP stack** is **on-chip** (LwIP) — host MCU is freed from networking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Software Platforms: Node-RED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IoT Software Platforms: Node-RED

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Node-RED** is an **open-source, flow-based development tool** built on **Node.js** (a JavaScript runtime environment) that enables the visual wiring together of **IoT devices, network APIs (Application Programming Interfaces), and online services** for the purpose of rapid application prototyping and deployment. Originally developed by **IBM** in 2013 and now a project of the **OpenJS Foundation**, Node-RED follows a **Low-Code/No-Code (LCNC)** programming paradigm, where applications are constructed graphically using a *drag-and-drop* browser-based editor.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Node-RED is classified as a **Server-Side IoT Middleware / Visual Application Layer Platform** that abstracts away the underlying hardware and protocol complexities (such as **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, and WebSockets**) into reusable graphical building blocks called **"nodes."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** Node-RED is studied as a representative *IoT Application Layer Platform* alongside other middleware tools. Students must know its **architecture, node types, flow creation process, and basic JSON message handling**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of Node-RED like a **modern modular kitchen** where you are the chef:

- **Nodes** = individual kitchen appliances (mixer, oven, blender).
- **Wires** = the pipes or conveyor belts carrying food (data) from one appliance to the next.
- **Flow** = the complete recipe — the sequence of appliances and their connections that turns raw ingredients (sensor data) into a finished dish (an automated action or dashboard).
- **The Browser-based Editor** = the kitchen counter where you visually place and connect all the appliances.
- **The Node.js Runtime** = the electrical power supply and water plumbing running the entire kitchen in the background.

You do *not* need to write complex code to operate the kitchen — you simply arrange the appliances (drag-drop nodes), connect them (draw wires), and tell each appliance what to do (double-click and configure a property). This **visual flow-programming** approach is precisely what makes Node-RED a favourite for rapid IoT prototyping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Traditional programming = writing *lines of code*. Node-RED programming = arranging *boxes and wires* on a canvas. Both produce working software, but Node-RED drastically reduces development time for event-driven IoT systems.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Quick-Reference

| Term | Definition (Kerala University Board Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Flow** | A workspace/tab in Node-RED where nodes are connected using wires to define data routing logic. |
| **Node** | The fundamental building block representing a specific function (input, output, processing). |
| **Wire** | A visual line connecting the **output port** of one node to the **input port** of another. |
| **msg** | The JavaScript object that flows between nodes. By convention, it always contains a `payload` property. |
| **Palette** | The side panel in the editor listing all installed and available node categories. |
| **Deploy** | The action that pushes the current flow logic to the Node-RED runtime engine for execution. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of Node-RED

Node-RED's architecture is layered and can be conceptually decomposed into the following tiers:

### Tier 1: Browser-Based Flow Editor (Frontend)
- Pure web application running inside the user's browser.
- Built using **HTML5, CSS3, and the D3.js library** for canvas rendering.
- Communicates with the runtime via **WebSocket protocol (default port 1880)**.
- Allows drag-and-drop construction, JSON editing of flows, and live debugging.

### Tier 2: Node.js Runtime Engine (Backend)
- The **server-side JavaScript execution environment** that hosts the Node-RED engine.
- Listens for incoming connections (HTTP, MQTT, TCP, UDP, WebSocket).
- Maintains the **flow registry** (the in-memory representation of all deployed flows).
- Executes the **event loop**, processing messages as they arrive on input ports.

### Tier 3: Node Catalogue (Library of Building Blocks)
- A registry of all installed nodes. The three primary in-built categories are:
  1. **Input Nodes** — generate or receive data (e.g., `inject`, `mqtt in`, `http in`).
  2. **Output Nodes** — transmit or display data (e.g., `debug`, `mqtt out`, `http response`).
  3. **Function Nodes** — transform, filter, or process data (e.g., `function`, `change`, `switch`, `template`).

> [!NOTE]
> The **npm (Node Package Manager) registry** hosts thousands of community-contributed node packages that can be installed to extend Node-RED's capabilities (e.g., `node-red-dashboard`, `node-red-contrib-modbus`).

## 2.2 The Flow-Based Programming (FBP) Paradigm

Node-RED implements **Flow-Based Programming (FBP)**, a paradigm invented by **J. Paul Morrison** in the 1970s. The key principles are:

1. **Black-box Components:** Each node encapsulates a self-contained function. The internal logic is hidden; only inputs and outputs matter.
2. **Message Passing:** Nodes communicate *exclusively* by passing structured messages through wires. There is no shared global state.
3. **Asynchronous Execution:** A node processes a message and passes it on; it does not wait for downstream nodes.
4. **Visual Wiring:** The data flow topology is explicit, visible, and editable as a graph on screen.

## 2.3 The `msg` Object — The Heart of Data Transfer

Every piece of data that moves through a Node-RED flow is encapsulated within a JavaScript object, conventionally named **`msg`**. The canonical structure is:

$$ msg = \{\, payload : \text{value},\ topic : \text{string},\ _session : \{\dots\},\ \text{[custom property]} : \text{value} \,\} $$

| Property | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `msg.payload` | Any (most commonly `string`, `number`, `Buffer`, or `Object`) | **Mandatory** — the main body of the message. Every node reads/writes this by default. |
| `msg.topic` | `string` | Optional — used to identify the message source/channel (e.g., MQTT topic name). |
| `msg._session` | `object` | Internal — stores session/connection metadata added by the runtime. |
| Custom properties | Any | User-defined — e.g., `msg.temperature`, `msg.location`. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The `payload` property is the single most important concept. If a student forgets to set `msg.payload` in a function node, downstream nodes will receive `undefined` and the flow will silently fail. Always remember: **"Payload is the soul of the message."**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| **Concept** | **Key Value / Property** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Default Web UI Port | **1880** (HTTP/WebSocket) | Browser editor access point |
| Default MQTT Broker Port | **1883** | Standard unencrypted MQTT communication |
| Runtime Environment | **Node.js (v14+ recommended)** | Cross-platform JavaScript execution |
| Primary File Format | **`flows.json`** | Stores the entire workspace in JSON; portable, version-controllable |
| Installation Command | `npm install -g --unsafe-perm node-red` | Global install on Linux/Raspberry Pi |
| Startup Command | `node-red` (in terminal) | Launches the editor on `http://localhost:1880` |
| Message Object | `msg` with mandatory `payload` | Standard data contract between all nodes |
| Programming Paradigm | **Flow-Based Programming (FBP)** | Visual, event-driven, asynchronous |
| Origin | IBM Emerging Technology Services (2013) | Now stewarded by OpenJS Foundation |
| File System | `~/.node-red/` (Linux/Mac) or `C:\Users\<user>\.node-red\` (Windows) | Stores user data, flows, credentials, installed nodes |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Node-RED is extensively used in:

- **Smart Agriculture:** Reading soil-moisture and DHT11/DHT22 sensor data, triggering irrigation relays.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** OPC-UA and Modbus integration for SCADA-like dashboards.
- **Home Automation:** Bridging Zigbee, Z-Wave, and MQTT devices into unified rules (e.g., "If motion detected AND time > 6 PM → turn on lights").
- **Edge Computing:** Running directly on **Raspberry Pi** and **ESP32 gateways** to act as a local aggregator before cloud upload.
- **Healthcare Monitoring:** Streaming pulse-oximeter data to dashboards with HTTP/MQTT pipelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Example: Creating a Simple Temperature Alert Flow

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Style):**
Design a Node-RED flow that simulates a temperature sensor reading (a number injected every 5 seconds) and routes the value to two destinations:
1. A **debug node** that always logs the value.
2. A **debug node** that logs **only** when temperature > 40 °C.

### Step 1: Identify the Required Nodes
- One **`inject` node** — to simulate the temperature input.
- One **`function` node** — to generate a random temperature value.
- One **`switch` node** — to branch the flow conditionally.
- Three **`debug` nodes** — for the two alert outputs and the always-log output.

### Step 2: Configure the `inject` Node
- Set the payload type to `number`.
- Set the payload value to `0` (placeholder; it will be overwritten).
- Set the repeat interval to `5 seconds` (use ISO 8601 duration: `5s` or `300000` ms).

### Step 3: Configure the `function` Node
The function node runs JavaScript. The code written inside replaces the `msg.payload` with a pseudo-random temperature:

```javascript
// Generates a random integer between 20 and 60 (inclusive)
msg.payload = Math.floor(Math.random() * 41) + 20;
return msg;
```

### Step 4: Configure the `switch` Node
The `switch` node routes messages based on a property condition.

- Property: `msg.payload`
- Condition 1: `>= 40` → routes to **Output 1** (alert branch).
- Condition 2: otherwise → routes to **Output 2** (normal branch).

> [!NOTE]
> A `switch` node can have *multiple* output ports. The first matching rule determines the routing port. Unmatched messages can optionally be routed to a final "otherwise" port.

### Step 5: Wire and Deploy
- Wire: `inject` → `function` → `switch`.
- Wire: `switch` Output 1 → `debug` (named "ALERT").
- Wire: `switch` Output 2 → `debug` (named "Normal").
- Click the red **Deploy** button (top-right of the editor).
- The flow is now *live*; observe output in the Debug sidebar.

### Step 6: Verify in Debug Sidebar
Every 5 seconds, the "Normal" debug tab will log values in the range 20–39. Whenever a value of 40 or higher is generated, the "ALERT" tab will log the value, demonstrating **conditional flow routing** in action.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Node Selection for Common IoT Tasks

| **Engineering Task** | **Recommended Nodes** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Reading a value every X seconds | `inject` | Built-in timer/clock source for testing without hardware. |
| Subscribing to an MQTT topic | `mqtt in` | Native MQTT client; handles reconnection, QoS, retain flags. |
| Exposing data over REST API | `http in` + `http response` | Implements an HTTP endpoint (GET/POST/PUT) for external clients. |
| Threshold-based decision making | `switch` | Multi-port conditional routing based on property values. |
| Custom JavaScript transformation | `function` | Full Node.js JavaScript sandbox for arbitrary logic. |
| Storing data persistently | `file` / `MySQL` / `MongoDB` | Persists messages to disk or database for historical analysis. |
| Dashboard visualization | `node-red-dashboard` (community package) | Creates gauges, charts, and buttons in a web UI. |
| Sending email alerts | `e-mail` | SMTP integration for notifications. |
| Triggering a GPIO pin (Raspberry Pi) | `rpi-gpio out` | Direct hardware pin control on a Pi running Node-RED locally. |

## 3.3 Conceptual Derivation: How a `msg` Travels Through a Flow

Consider a chain of three nodes: `A → B → C`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } t_0 &: \text{ Node A injects } msg_0 = \{payload: 25\} \\
\text{Time } t_1 &: \text{ Node A emits } msg_0 \text{ to the wire} \\
\text{Time } t_2 &: \text{ Node B receives } msg_0, \text{ transforms payload to } \{payload: 26\} \rightarrow msg_1 \\
\text{Time } t_3 &: \text{ Node B emits } msg_1 \text{ to the wire} \\
\text{Time } t_4 &: \text{ Node C receives } msg_1 \text{ and logs to debug panel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Observations (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
1. **Each node may modify the `msg` object** before passing it forward — the downstream node receives the *modified* version.
2. **A single node's output can be wired to multiple inputs** — broadcast/multi-cast pattern. All connected downstream nodes receive a *copy* of the same `msg` reference.
3. **Wires are unidirectional** — data flows from output ports to input ports only. Circular flows are allowed but may cause infinite loops if not guarded by a `delay` or `trigger` node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Loss Warning:** When describing a flow in an exam, always specify the *direction* and the *property carried*. A common student error is writing "Node A sends data to Node B" without stating what property of `msg` is being acted upon.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Node-RED Three-Layer System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: BROWSER-BASED FLOW EDITOR"]
        direction LR
        A1["HTML5 Canvas UI"]
        A2["D3.js Rendering Engine"]
        A3["Node Palette Sidebar"]
        A4["Debug Sidebar"]
        A5["Tab/Flow Workspace"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: NODE.JS RUNTIME"]
        direction LR
        B1["Event Loop Engine"]
        B2["Flow Registry"]
        B3["WebSocket Server :1880"]
        B4["Admin REST API"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: NODE LIBRARY AND I/O"]
        direction LR
        C1["Input Nodes (mqtt in / http in / inject)"]
        C2["Function Nodes (function / change / switch)"]
        C3["Output Nodes (mqtt out / debug / file)"]
        C4["External Services (Sensors / Cloud APIs / DB)"]
    end

    L1 <-->|WebSocket JSON-RPC| L2
    L2 <-->|Message Bus msg| L3
    L3 <-->|MQTT / HTTP / CoAP / TCP| C4
```

## 4.2 Canonical Flow Topology — A Temperature Alert Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inj["INJECT NODE\nRepeat: 5s\nPayload: timestamp"]
    func["FUNCTION NODE\nJavaScript:\nmsg.payload = 20 + Math.random() * 40"]
    sw["SWITCH NODE\nProperty: msg.payload\nRule 1: >= 40 -> Output 1\nRule 2: < 40 -> Output 2"]
    dbg1["DEBUG NODE\nLabel: ALERT_HIGH_TEMP\nSide panel output"]
    dbg2["DEBUG NODE\nLabel: NORMAL_TEMP\nSide panel output"]
    cloud["MQTT OUT NODE\nBroker: test.mosquitto.org\nTopic: home/temperature"]

    inj -->|msg| func
    func -->|msg.payload=random 20-60| sw
    sw -->|port 1 payload>=40| dbg1
    sw -->|port 1 payload>=40| cloud
    sw -->|port 2 payload<40| dbg2
```

## 4.3 The `msg` Object Lifecycle Across Three Nodes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    m1["msg AT NODE A\npayload: 25\ntopic: 'sensor/1'"]
    m2["msg AT NODE B\n(payload transformed)\npayload: 26.5\ntopic: 'sensor/1'\nunit: 'Celsius'"]
    m3["msg AT NODE C\n(consumed for display/persistence)\npayload: 26.5\ntopic: 'sensor/1'\nunit: 'Celsius'\ntimestamp: 1700000000"]
    na["NODE A\nSource/Input"]
    nb["NODE B\nFunction/Transform"]
    nc["NODE C\nSink/Output"]
    na -->|emits| m1
    m1 -->|wire transit| nb
    nb -->|emits| m2
    m2 -->|wire transit| nc
    nc -->|logs to debug| m3
```

## 4.4 Node-RED Installation and Startup Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Step 1: Install Node.js v14+ from nodejs.org"]
    s2["Step 2: Open Terminal / Command Prompt"]
    s3["Step 3: Run npm install -g --unsafe-perm node-red"]
    s4["Step 4: Verify installation with node-red --version"]
    s5["Step 5: Launch runtime via node-red command"]
    s6["Step 6: Open browser to http://localhost:1880"]
    s7["Step 7: Editor loads; drag nodes from palette to canvas"]
    s8["Step 8: Connect nodes with wires; click Deploy"]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, OEITT723]**
*Define Node-RED. Mention its origin and the underlying runtime technology.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Node-RED is an **open-source, flow-based development tool** used for visually wiring together IoT devices, APIs, and online services.
- **(1 Mark)** It was originally developed by **IBM's Emerging Technology Services team in 2013** and is currently maintained by the **OpenJS Foundation**.
- **(1 Mark)** It is built on **Node.js**, a server-side JavaScript runtime, and uses a **browser-based editor** communicating with the runtime via **WebSockets (port 1880)**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, OEITT723]**
*Explain the significance of the `msg.payload` property in a Node-RED flow.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Every message that traverses a Node-RED flow is encapsulated in a JavaScript object conventionally named **`msg`**.
- **(1 Mark)** The **`payload`** is the *mandatory* core property of this object and represents the actual data being carried (e.g., a temperature reading, an HTTP body, or a JSON string).
- **(1 Mark)** All built-in nodes (e.g., `debug`, `function`, `switch`) read from and/or write to `msg.payload` by default. **Downstream nodes will fail if `payload` is set to `undefined`.**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, OEITT723]**
*With a neat architectural diagram, explain the **three-layer architecture of Node-RED**. List any **six built-in nodes** and describe the role of the `function` node in detail.*

#### Part (a) — Architecture Diagram and Three-Layer Explanation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Layer 1 — Browser-Based Flow Editor (3 Marks)**
- The user-facing component accessed at `http://localhost:1880`.
- Built with **HTML5, CSS3, and D3.js** for the drag-and-drop canvas.
- Provides a **node palette** (left), **workspace** (centre), and **debug sidebar** (right).
- Communicates with the runtime using **WebSocket protocol (JSON-RPC messages)**.

**Layer 2 — Node.js Runtime Engine (2 Marks)**
- The heart of Node-RED; executes the **JavaScript event loop** that processes messages asynchronously.
- Maintains the **flow registry** — the in-memory model of all deployed flows.
- Hosts the **Admin REST API** for configuration and credentials management.

**Layer 3 — Node Catalogue and I/O (2 Marks)**
- A registry of installed **input, output, and function nodes**.
- Bridges to external services and hardware via protocol nodes (MQTT, HTTP, TCP, etc.).
- Extensible via **npm** packages from the OpenJS ecosystem.

*(Examiner expects a labelled diagram covering the three layers — refer to Section 4.1.)*

#### Part (b) — Six Built-in Nodes and the `function` Node (7 Marks)

**Six Built-in Nodes (3 Marks):**
1. **`inject`** — manually or periodically injects a `msg` into the flow.
2. **`debug`** — displays `msg` properties in the debug sidebar.
3. **`function`** — executes user-defined JavaScript on the `msg`.
4. **`switch`** — routes messages to different output ports based on property rules.
5. **`change`** — sets, deletes, or moves `msg` properties without writing code.
6. **`http in` / `http response`** — creates an HTTP endpoint; replies to web clients.

**The `function` Node in Detail (4 Marks):**
- It provides a full **Node.js JavaScript sandbox** for arbitrary message manipulation.
- The function must `return msg;` (or a new message object) to pass data downstream. If nothing is returned, the flow stops.
- Common operations include:
  - Modifying `msg.payload` (e.g., converting units, formatting timestamps).
  - Adding custom properties (e.g., `msg.unit = "Celsius"`).
  - Conditional logic using JavaScript `if-else` and `switch` statements.
- **Example:** A function node that rounds a temperature to one decimal place:
  ```javascript
  msg.payload = parseFloat(msg.payload.toFixed(1));
  return msg;
  ```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, OEITT723]**
*Describe the **Flow-Based Programming (FBP) paradigm** adopted by Node-RED. With a neat flow diagram, explain how a **temperature-monitoring flow** using `inject`, `function`, `switch`, and `debug` nodes can be built to send an alert when temperature exceeds 40 °C.*

#### Part (a) — Flow-Based Programming Paradigm (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **(2 Marks)** Flow-Based Programming (FBP) was conceptualized by **J. Paul Morrison (1970s)**. Applications are constructed by connecting *black-box* components (nodes) with explicit data channels (wires).
- **(2 Marks)** **Core Principles of FBP in Node-RED:**
  - *Black-box encapsulation:* each node's internal logic is hidden.
  - *Message passing:* communication occurs *only* via the `msg` object on wires.
  - *Asynchronous execution:* no node blocks waiting for another.
  - *Visual wiring:* the topology is explicit and editable on screen.
- **(2 Marks)** **Advantages:** rapid prototyping, ease of understanding for non-programmers, inherent parallelism, and easy debugging via the debug sidebar.
- **(1 Mark)** **Comparison:** FBP differs from textual programming in that the *control flow* is replaced by a *data-flow graph*.

#### Part (b) — Temperature Monitoring Flow with Diagram (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step-by-step Construction (5 Marks):**
1. **Inject Node:** Set repeat to `5s`; payload type = `number`; initial value = `0`.
2. **Function Node:** Insert code to generate a random temperature:
   ```javascript
   msg.payload = Math.floor(Math.random() * 41) + 20;
   return msg;
   ```
3. **Switch Node:** Set property to `msg.payload`. Add rule: `>= 40` → Output 1; otherwise → Output 2.
4. **Debug Nodes:** Two debug nodes named "Alert" and "Normal" connected to switch Output 1 and Output 2 respectively.
5. **Deploy:** Click Deploy to push the flow to the runtime.

**Flow Diagram (2 Marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for a complete Mermaid schematic. The student should reproduce a labelled flowchart in the answer script.)*

**Working Explanation:** Every 5 seconds, the inject node fires; the function node generates a temperature between 20 and 60 °C; the switch node routes values ≥ 40 °C to the "Alert" debug tab and others to the "Normal" tab. This demonstrates event-driven conditional flow handling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to `return msg;`** in a function node (the flow silently breaks; no error is thrown) — **−2 marks** if asked to write code.
> 2. **Confusing the WebSocket port (1880)** with the MQTT broker port (1883) — these are *different* services.
> 3. **Not drawing arrows / direction** on flow diagrams — examiners explicitly deduct for "static" flowcharts.
> 4. **Writing "Node-RED uses JavaScript"** without specifying **Node.js** as the runtime — partial credit only.
> 5. **Omitting the `payload` property** when describing message flow — answers must explicitly mention `msg.payload`.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Node-RED = Visual Flow-Based Programming Tool** built on **Node.js** by **IBM (2013)**, now under **OpenJS Foundation**.
- ✅ **Default editor URL:** `http://localhost:1880` (WebSocket on the same port).
- ✅ **Core building blocks:** **Nodes** (input, output, function) connected by **Wires** carrying the **`msg` object**.
- ✅ **`msg.payload`** is the mandatory data property — *the* central concept of all flows.
- ✅ **Programming paradigm:** Flow-Based Programming (FBP) — visual, event-driven, asynchronous.
- ✅ **Persistence:** All flows are stored in `flows.json` for portability and version control.
- ✅ **Extensibility:** Community nodes installed via **npm** (e.g., `node-red-dashboard`, `node-red-contrib-modbus`).
- ✅ **Common Use Cases:** Smart agriculture, home automation, industrial IoT gateways, edge computing on **Raspberry Pi**.
- ✅ **Deployment vs Save:** Clicking **Deploy** is mandatory for changes to take effect at runtime; *Save* is implicit in the JSON model.
- ✅ **Three Architectural Layers:** Browser Editor ↔ Node.js Runtime ↔ Node Catalogue/External I/O.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ThingSpeak
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ThingSpeak

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ThingSpeak** is an open-source **Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform service** developed by *MathWorks* (the makers of MATLAB) that enables the aggregation, live visualization, and post-hoc analysis of real-time data streams in the cloud. It provides RESTful API endpoints and MQTT broker services that allow client devices (sensors, microcontrollers, gateways) to publish, retrieve, and act upon telemetry data over standard Internet protocols.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT723)**, ThingSpeak is classified under the *Cloud Computing and Data Analytics* layer of the standard **5-layer IoT reference architecture** (Perception → Network → Middleware/Cloud → Application → Business), functioning as a **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** IoT middleware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OEITT723 – Module 3):** ThingSpeak is introduced as the canonical example of an *open-source cloud IoT platform* that bridges the gap between raw sensor data acquisition and analytical decision-making. Students must understand its **channel-field model, REST API, MATLAB analytics, and event-reaction plugins**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **smart greenhouse** that measures temperature, humidity, and soil moisture every 30 seconds. Without a central system, these readings would be locked inside the microcontroller's memory—isolated, unanalyzed, and useless.

**ThingSpeak acts as the "Smart Diary" of the Internet:**

- The greenhouse (your *thing*) writes its measurements into a **public digital logbook** hosted on the cloud.
- The logbook has structured **pages** (called *Channels*) with designated **columns** (called *Fields*).
- Authorized people (apps, dashboards, MATLAB scripts) can read the logbook remotely using a **standard HTTP request**—just like opening a webpage.
- The logbook can also be configured to **auto-react** to entries: e.g., if the temperature field exceeds 35°C, automatically send a tweet, trigger an actuator, or call a webhook.

So, **ThingSpeak = IoT Cloud Logbook + Live Visualizer + Auto-Reactor**, all accessible through a uniform web API.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties of ThingSpeak (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **Open-source IoT middleware** with free public channels.
> - Hosted at: `https://api.thingspeak.com` (API root).
> - Native **HTTP** and **MQTT** support.
> - Deep **MATLAB/Simulink** integration for advanced analytics and Machine Learning.
> - Built-in **plugins**: React, ThingHTTP, TalkBack, TimeControl, MATLAB Analysis.

### 1.3 Platform Metrics & Constraints

| Parameter | Standard Limit (Free Tier) |
|---|---|
| Message update interval | ≥ **15 seconds** between writes |
| Maximum fields per channel | **8 fields** |
| Retention of stored data | Indefinite (subject to ToS) |
| API request size | Up to **3 MB** per write request |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of ThingSpeak (Step-by-Step Logic)

The ThingSpeak ecosystem is composed of **four tightly-coupled functional layers**:

1. **Data Source (Edge / Perception Layer)**
   - Microcontrollers (ESP8266, ESP32, Arduino + Ethernet Shield, Raspberry Pi) equipped with sensors.
   - These act as *HTTP clients* that construct GET/POST requests and transmit JSON/XML/CSV payloads to the ThingSpeak REST endpoint.

2. **Communication Layer (Network Layer)**
   - Supports **HTTP/HTTPS (REST API)** and **MQTT (publish/subscribe)**.
   - REST API is the most commonly used; MQTT is preferred for low-bandwidth, battery-operated nodes.

3. **ThingSpeak Cloud (Middleware / Platform Layer)**
   - Receives data, validates it using the user's **Write API Key**, timestamps it, and stores it in a relational time-series schema.
   - A *Channel* is the logical container; *Fields* are typed columns; *Location* metadata (lat/lon/elevation) is also stored.

4. **Application & Analytics Layer (Application Layer)**
   - **ThingSpeak Web UI**: Built-in gauges, line charts, numerical displays, and Google gauges.
   - **MATLAB Analytics**: Custom MATLAB scripts run on-demand, on a schedule, or reactively when data arrives.
   - **React App / Plugins**: Trigger downstream actions on threshold violations.

### 2.2 The Channel–Field Data Model

A **Channel** is the atomic unit of data organization. Each channel is uniquely identified by a numeric **Channel ID** and protected by a **Write API Key** (for ingest) and a **Read API Key** (for retrieval; can be public or private).

$$\text{Channel} = \Big\{ \text{ChannelID},\ \text{API\_Write},\ \text{API\_Read},\ \big\{F_1, F_2, \dots, F_8\big\},\ \text{Location},\ \text{Metadata} \Big\}$$

Each **Field** $F_i$ is a single time-series variable (e.g., $F_1$ = temperature in °C, $F_2$ = humidity in %RH). A single HTTP write operation can update all 8 fields simultaneously.

### 2.3 REST API Endpoints (High-Yield for KTU)

| HTTP Method | Endpoint | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `GET` | `/channels/{id}/feeds.json` | Retrieve historical data |
| `GET` | `/channels/{id}/feeds/last.json` | Retrieve only the most recent entry |
| `POST` | `/update` | Write a new data entry to a channel |
| `GET` | `/channels/{id}/status.json` | Retrieve the last entry's status code |
| `GET` | `/apps/thinghttp/send_request` | Trigger an external HTTP call (ThingHTTP plugin) |

### 2.4 The Four ThingSpeak Plugins (Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four plugins form the **event-driven backbone** of ThingSpeak and are *high-frequency KTU questions*:

- **ThingHTTP**: Sends an arbitrary HTTP request to any external web service. Used to call third-party APIs (e.g., Twitter, IFTTT, custom webhook).
- **React**: Evaluates a conditional expression on incoming channel data. If true, it fires an action (call ThingHTTP, run TalkBack command, or trigger MATLAB analysis).
- **TalkBack**: A command queue (FIFO) that allows apps to send *commands* to devices, which the devices then poll and execute. Enables **device-to-cloud** and **cloud-to-device** bidirectional control.
- **TimeControl**: Triggers an action at a fixed time interval (e.g., run MATLAB analysis every 5 minutes, irrespective of incoming data).

### 2.5 MATLAB Integration – The Killer Feature

Because ThingSpeak is owned by MathWorks, every channel can be analyzed using MATLAB code directly in the cloud. This unlocks:

- Statistical analysis (mean, variance, FFT).
- Machine Learning (predictive maintenance, classification).
- Digital Signal Processing on time-series sensor data.
- Visualization using `plot()`, `histogram()`, `heatmap()` — rendered server-side and embedded in the channel view.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MATLAB matters in KTU context:** Question papers frequently ask how ThingSpeak differs from generic IoT platforms (e.g., Blynk, Ubidots, AWS IoT). The expected answer always cites **native MATLAB analytics** as the distinguishing feature.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil-moisture, pH, and ambient sensors uploaded to ThingSpeak; React triggers irrigation via ThingHTTP → WiFi relay.
- **Industrial Predictive Maintenance**: Vibration + temperature → MATLAB FFT analysis → email alert before failure.
- **Academic Research**: Low-cost, reproducible IoT data logging for B.Tech/M.Tech project demonstrations.
- **Environmental Monitoring**: Air Quality Index (AQI) crowdsourced with public read access.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Temperature-Monitoring Channel

**Problem Statement:** An ESP32 reads a room temperature sensor (LM35) every 30 seconds and uploads the value to ThingSpeak. The system must also send an email alert when temperature exceeds **40 °C**. Demonstrate the complete data path.

**Step 1 — Channel Creation (Manual / API)**
Create a channel named `Lab_Temp_Monitor` with one field: `field1 = temperature_celsius`. On creation, ThingSpeak assigns:
- `Channel ID = 1234567`
- `Write API Key = Y2XJ8ABCDXYZ`
- `Read API Key = PUB123PUBLIC`

**Step 2 — HTTP POST Request from ESP32**

The HTTP POST request constructed by the microcontroller is:

```
POST /update HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Connection: close
X-THINGSPEAKAPIKEY: Y2XJ8ABCDXYZ
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

field1=27.4
```

**Step 3 — Server-Side Validation and Storage**
The ThingSpeak server authenticates the request against the `X-THINGSPEAKAPIKEY` header, parses the `field1` value, attaches a UTC timestamp, and appends the entry to the channel's time-series table.

**Step 4 — React Configuration for Alerting**
A **React** is configured on the channel with:
- **Condition:** `field1 > 40`
- **Action:** Trigger *ThingHTTP-1* which calls an external API such as `https://maker.ifttt.com/trigger/overheat/with/key/<IFTTT_KEY>`.

**Step 5 — End-to-End Latency Calculation**

For KTU numerical-style questions, you may be asked to compute total round-trip time. The end-to-end latency $T_{e2e}$ for a single data point is:

$$
T_{e2e} = T_{sense} + T_{tx} + T_{cloud} + T_{react}
$$

Where:
- $T_{sense}$ = sensor acquisition time (≈ 50 ms for LM35 + ADC).
- $T_{tx}$ = HTTP transmission time over WiFi.
- $T_{cloud}$ = ThingSpeak ingestion + React evaluation time.
- $T_{react}$ = ThingHTTP-to-IFTTT-to-Email delay.

Let us now evaluate a representative numeric example.

Assume $T_{sense} = 0.05\,\text{s}$, $T_{tx} = 0.20\,\text{s}$ (over 802.11n at 64-byte payload), $T_{cloud} = 0.30\,\text{s}$, $T_{react} = 1.50\,\text{s}$.

$$
T_{e2e} = 0.05 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 1.50
$$

$$
T_{e2e} = 2.05\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{e2e} \approx 2.05\ \text{seconds}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 2.05 s end-to-end latency is acceptable for *monitoring* applications but is **too slow for closed-loop control** of fast physical processes (e.g., motor speed control, where sub-100 ms latency is required). This justifies the use of MQTT over REST for hard real-time IoT systems.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: ThingSpeak vs. Other IoT Platforms

| Feature | ThingSpeak | Blynk | Ubidots | AWS IoT Core |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Native MATLAB Support | **Yes** | No | No | No (paid add-on) |
| Free Public Channels | **Yes** | Limited | 30-day trial | 12 months free tier |
| MQTT Support | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Built-in React Engine | **Yes** | No (use Webhooks) | Yes (Events) | Yes (Rules Engine) |
| Code-Free Dashboard | **Yes** | Yes (mobile) | Yes | No (needs AWS IoT SiteWise) |
| Ideal Use-Case | Research / Education / Analytics | Hobby / Mobile-centric dashboards | Quick industrial pilots | Enterprise scale |

### 3.3 The TalkBack Command-Queue Flow (Sub-Step Expansion)

TalkBack is fundamentally a **FIFO queue** with three primitives:

$$
Q = \{C_1, C_2, C_3, \dots, C_n\}
$$

Where $C_i$ is a command string (e.g., `"RELAY_ON"`, `"MOTOR_SPEED=1500"`). The flow is:

1. **Cloud App** issues a `POST` to `/talkbacks/{id}/commands` with the command payload.
2. ThingSpeak appends the command to the queue, assigning a monotonically increasing **command_id**.
3. **Device** periodically issues `GET /talkbacks/{id}/commands/execute` to fetch and atomically remove the *next* command.
4. Device executes the command locally (e.g., toggles a GPIO pin).

The **queue length** $n$ at any instant is:

$$
n = \text{Commands Issued} - \text{Commands Executed}
$$

The **command latency** $T_{cmd}$ is:

$$
T_{cmd} = T_{poll} + T_{exec}
$$

Where $T_{poll}$ is the device's polling interval and $T_{exec}$ is the actuator response time. To ensure no command loss, the **Nyquist–Shannon-inspired rule** for the device is:

$$
T_{poll} \leq \frac{T_{exec}}{2}
$$

This guarantees that the queue never grows unbounded and commands are processed in near-real-time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level System Architecture of ThingSpeak

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Sensors"] --> nodeB["Microcontroller ESP32"]
    nodeB -- HTTP POST update --> nodeC["ThingSpeak REST API"]
    nodeB -- MQTT Publish --> nodeD["ThingSpeak MQTT Broker"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Channel Database"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["React Engine"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["MATLAB Analytics"]
    nodeF -- Trigger --> nodeH["ThingHTTP Plugin"]
    nodeF -- Queue Command --> nodeI["TalkBack Queue"]
    nodeF -- Scheduled Trigger --> nodeJ["TimeControl"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["Channel Web Dashboard"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["External Webhook IFTTT Email Twitter"]
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeK --> nodeM["End User Browser"]
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Temperature Upload and React Trigger

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as LM35 Sensor
    participant MCU as ESP32
    participant TS as ThingSpeak Cloud
    participant React as React Engine
    participant TH as ThingHTTP
    participant Email as Email Service

    Sensor->>MCU: Analog voltage proportional to temperature
    MCU->>MCU: ADC conversion and calibration
    MCU->>TS: HTTP POST /update field1 equals 41.2
    TS->>TS: Validate Write API Key and timestamp entry
    TS-->>MCU: HTTP 200 OK with entry id
    TS->>React: Notify new data on channel
    React->>React: Evaluate field1 greater than 40
    React->>TH: Trigger ThingHTTP request because condition true
    TH->>Email: HTTP POST to IFTTT webhook
    Email-->>User: Send alert email HIGH TEMPERATURE
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Fallback Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EdgeLayer["Perception and Edge Layer"]
        dev1["Temp Sensor"]
        dev2["Humidity Sensor"]
        dev3["Microcontroller with WiFi"]
    end
    subgraph NetworkLayer["Network Layer"]
        net1["HTTP REST"]
        net2["MQTT Broker"]
    end
    subgraph CloudLayer["ThingSpeak Cloud Middleware"]
        cl1["Channel 1234567"]
        cl2["Field1 Temperature"]
        cl3["Field2 Humidity"]
        cl4["Reacts"]
        cl5["TalkBack Queue"]
        cl6["ThingHTTP"]
        cl7["TimeControl"]
        cl8["MATLAB Analysis"]
    end
    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer"]
        ap1["Web Dashboard"]
        ap2["Mobile View"]
        ap3["IFTTT Email Alerts"]
    end
    dev1 --> dev3
    dev2 --> dev3
    dev3 --> net1
    dev3 --> net2
    net1 --> cl1
    net2 --> cl1
    cl1 --> cl2
    cl1 --> cl3
    cl2 --> cl4
    cl3 --> cl4
    cl4 --> cl5
    cl4 --> cl6
    cl4 --> cl7
    cl4 --> cl8
    cl6 --> ap3
    cl8 --> ap1
    cl1 --> ap1
    cl1 --> ap2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Solid arrows represent data flow direction. The Edge Layer *writes* data; the Application Layer *reads* data. The Cloud Layer sits in the middle and orchestrates reactions in both directions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define ThingSpeak. List any four features of ThingSpeak.*

**Model Answer:**
ThingSpeak is an open-source Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform developed by MathWorks that allows users to aggregate, visualize, and analyze live data streams in the cloud using HTTP and MQTT protocols.

**Four features:**
1. Channel-based time-series data storage with up to 8 fields per channel.
2. Native HTTP REST API and MQTT broker support for device communication.
3. Built-in MATLAB analytics engine for advanced data processing and visualization.
4. Event-reaction plugins — **React**, **ThingHTTP**, **TalkBack**, and **TimeControl**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between the **React** and **TalkBack** plugins in ThingSpeak.*

**Model Answer:**
- **React** is a conditional trigger — it evaluates a Boolean expression on incoming channel data and fires an action (e.g., call a webhook) only when the condition evaluates to **true**. It implements a *data-driven* event flow.
- **TalkBack** is a command queue (FIFO) — it allows cloud-side applications to enqueue commands that connected devices can poll and execute, enabling *bidirectional cloud-to-device* control. It implements a *queue-driven* command flow.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module-end question in Part B carries an internal choice. The two questions below are the *two independent alternatives* a student may receive.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of ThingSpeak with a neat block diagram. Discuss the roles of the four key plugins in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture of ThingSpeak is organized into **four functional layers**:

1. **Perception Layer (Edge Devices):** Microcontrollers such as ESP32, Arduino, and Raspberry Pi equipped with sensors. They collect physical parameters and package them into HTTP or MQTT payloads.

2. **Network Layer:** Provides the transport mechanism — primarily **HTTP REST API** (`api.thingspeak.com`) and an **MQTT broker** for low-bandwidth devices. Both protocols use standard TCP/IP sockets, ensuring interoperability.

3. **Cloud Middleware Layer (ThingSpeak Core):** This is the heart of the platform. It performs the following functions:
   - Authenticates incoming requests using the channel's *Write API Key*.
   - Stores incoming data in a **channel-field schema** with a UTC timestamp.
   - Runs the **React engine** that continuously evaluates conditional expressions.
   - Hosts the **MATLAB analysis engine** for on-demand, scheduled, or triggered computations.
   - Manages the **TalkBack FIFO queue** and **TimeControl scheduler**.

4. **Application Layer:** Consists of the **web dashboard** (with built-in gauges, line plots, and numerical displays), **mobile-friendly URLs**, and any third-party applications that consume the data via the REST API.

**Roles of the four plugins (7 marks detail):**

- **React:** A *threshold-based trigger*. It continuously monitors channel fields. Example: `if field1 > 40 then fire ThingHTTP-1`. It is the primary mechanism for converting raw data into actionable events.

- **ThingHTTP:** A *generic HTTP client* living inside ThingSpeak. It accepts a target URL, HTTP method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE), headers, and body. When invoked, it issues the request to any third-party REST endpoint — e.g., Twitter API, custom webhook, IFTTT.

- **TalkBack:** A *command-queue* server. It receives commands from a cloud app, assigns a command_id, and stores them. A device polls the queue using `/commands/execute`, retrieves the next command, executes it, and the entry is removed. This enables **bidirectional control**.

- **TimeControl:** A *cron-like scheduler*. It triggers a specified action (run MATLAB analysis, fire ThingHTTP, post to TalkBack) at a fixed time interval — independent of whether new data has arrived. Useful for periodic reports.

**(b)** With a suitable example, describe the steps to create a channel in ThingSpeak and write data into it using the HTTP GET method from an Arduino/ESP board. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Account Creation.** Navigate to `thingspeak.com` and register a free MathWorks account. Log in to the dashboard.

**Step 2: Channel Creation.** Click **"New Channel"**. Fill in:
- Name: `WeatherStation_01`
- Field 1: `Temperature_C`
- Field 2: `Humidity_pct`
- Check **"Make Public"** for unrestricted read access.

Click **"Save Channel"**. The platform returns:
- `Channel ID` (e.g., `2001789`)
- `Write API Key` (e.g., `A1B2C3D4E5F6`)
- `Read API Key` (e.g., `PUBREADXYZ`)

**Step 3: Hardware Wiring.** Connect a DHT22 sensor to GPIO pin 4 of an ESP8266 NodeMCU. Wire VCC to 3.3V, GND to GND, and DATA to D4 with a 10 kΩ pull-up.

**Step 4: Arduino Sketch (Conceptual).** Use the `ESP8266WiFi.h` and `ESP8266HTTPClient.h` libraries. Inside `loop()`:
1. Read the DHT22 sensor: `float t = dht.readTemperature()`.
2. Construct the URL: `String url = "http://api.thingspeak.com/update?api_key=A1B2C3D4E5F6&field1=" + String(t);`
3. Issue `http.begin(client, url); http.GET();`.
4. Respect the 15-second rule using `delay(16000);`.

**Step 5: Verification.** Refresh the channel's *Private View* and *Public View* tabs to confirm the data points appear with timestamps.

**Step 6: Visualization.** Add a MATLAB Visualizations widget (e.g., `plot(x, y)` of `temperature_c vs. timestamps`) to render a live chart on the channel page.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes by KTU Students:**
> - Forgetting the 15-second update rate limit (ThingSpeak will reject or over-rate-limit rapid writes).
> - Hardcoding the API key in source code uploaded to GitHub → **security vulnerability**.
> - Confusing the Write API Key with the Read API Key in `api_key=` URL parameter.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]

**(a)** What are the various components of a ThingSpeak channel? Explain the HTTP methods supported by the ThingSpeak REST API. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Components of a ThingSpeak Channel:**

1. **Channel ID** — A unique numeric identifier assigned upon channel creation.
2. **Channel Name** — Human-readable title (e.g., `SmartGreenhouse_01`).
3. **Description** — A short paragraph describing the channel's purpose.
4. **Fields (1 to 8)** — Named, typed time-series columns. Each field stores a single scalar value per write.
5. **Location** — Optional latitude, longitude, and elevation metadata, displayed on a built-in map.
6. **Status** — A free-text field for any extra annotation per write.
7. **API Keys:**
   - **Write API Key** — Required to *write* data; treated as a secret.
   - **Read API Key** — Required to *read* private data; can be omitted if the channel is public.
8. **Tags** — Free-form keyword labels for searchability.

**HTTP Methods Supported by the ThingSpeak REST API:**

The ThingSpeak REST API is a **RESTful** interface and supports the standard HTTP verbs:

- **GET** — Used to *retrieve* data. Example: `GET /channels/2001789/feeds.json?results=10` returns the last 10 entries as a JSON array.
- **POST** — Used to *write* data. The body contains the form-encoded field values; the Write API Key is sent in the `X-THINGSPEAKAPIKEY` HTTP header.
- **PUT** — Used to *update* metadata of a channel (e.g., description, field names). Authenticated with the user's MathWorks account token.
- **DELETE** — Used to *remove* a channel or a specific feed entry. Authenticated similarly to PUT.

A typical write transaction using `GET` (URL-encoded) is:

```
GET /update?api_key=A1B2C3D4E5F6&field1=27.4&field2=65.0 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
```

The server responds with a numeric entry ID on success.

**(b)** Illustrate with a case study how MATLAB analytics can be used in ThingSpeak for **predictive maintenance** of an industrial motor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case Study Context:** A three-phase induction motor in a manufacturing plant is fitted with two sensors:
- An **accelerometer (ADXL345)** on the motor casing → `field1` = vibration RMS in g.
- A **thermistor (NTC 10k)** on the windings → `field2` = temperature in °C.

**Step 1: Data Ingestion.** ESP32 reads the two sensors every 30 s and uploads to ThingSpeak channel `MotorHealth_05`.

**Step 2: MATLAB Analysis Script (Hosted on ThingSpeak).** A custom MATLAB analysis is written and saved under *Apps → MATLAB Analysis*:

```matlab
% MATLAB code hosted on ThingSpeak cloud
data = thingSpeakRead(1234567, 'NumDays', 1, 'Fields', [1 2]);
vib = data.Field1;
temp = data.Field2;
% Feature extraction
vib_rms = rms(vib);
temp_mean = mean(temp);
% Threshold-based health score
if vib_rms > 0.5 || temp_mean > 80
    health = 'CRITICAL - Schedule Maintenance';
elseif vib_rms > 0.2 || temp_mean > 60
    health = 'WARNING - Monitor Closely';
else
    health = 'HEALTHY';
end
% Write result back to a third field
thingSpeakWrite(1234567, 'Fields', [1 2 3], ...
    'Values', {vib_rms, temp_mean, health}, 'WriteKey', 'XYZ123');
```

**Step 3: TimeControl Trigger.** A TimeControl is configured to execute the MATLAB analysis script every **15 minutes** — independent of data arrival — ensuring periodic health assessment.

**Step 4: React Engine for Alerts.** A React monitors `field3` (health status string). When it contains `"CRITICAL"`, it fires a **ThingHTTP** that POSTs to the plant's SCADA email gateway, alerting the maintenance supervisor.

**Step 5: Visualization.** Two MATLAB Visualizations are pinned on the channel dashboard: (i) a `plot` of vibration RMS over the last 24 h, and (ii) a `heatmap` of vibration spectral density. Operators can visually correlate spikes with shift schedules.

**Engineering Outcome:** The motor's Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) increases by an estimated 25–40 % because degradation is detected weeks before catastrophic failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> - Failing to mention the **15-second write rate limit** imposed by ThingSpeak.
> - Confusing *TimeControl* (schedule-based) with *React* (event-based) triggers.
> - Omitting the role of the **Write API Key** in the `X-THINGSPEAKAPIKEY` HTTP header.
> - Writing MATLAB code that uses local file I/O (e.g., `readtable('sensor.csv')`) — this will fail on ThingSpeak. Use `thingSpeakRead()` exclusively.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency Recall Bullets for KTU 2024:**

- **ThingSpeak** is an open-source IoT analytics platform owned by **MathWorks** (maker of MATLAB).
- Primary use: cloud-based **time-series data logging, live visualization, and event-driven actuation**.
- Data model: **Channel** (container) → **Fields** (1 to 8 typed columns) → individual time-stamped **feeds**.
- Communication: **HTTP REST API** (port 80/443) and **MQTT broker** at `mqtt://mqtt.thingspeak.com:1883`.
- Critical rate limit: minimum **15 seconds** between consecutive channel updates (free tier).
- Authentication: **Write API Key** (for `POST /update`) and **Read API Key** (for `GET /feeds`).
- **Four plugins** — React (event trigger), ThingHTTP (outbound web call), TalkBack (cloud-to-device command queue), TimeControl (cron-style scheduler).
- **MATLAB Analytics** distinguishes ThingSpeak from competing platforms (Blynk, Ubidots, AWS IoT).
- HTTP methods supported: **GET** (read), **POST** (write), **PUT** (update channel metadata), **DELETE** (remove channel/entry).
- TalkBack implements a **FIFO command queue**; devices poll via `GET /talkbacks/{id}/commands/execute`.
- Typical end-to-end latency (sensor → cloud → email) is **1.5 to 3 seconds** — suitable for monitoring, *not* for hard real-time control.
- Public channels can be embedded in any website via an **iframe** or a chart-image URL.
- Standard endpoint for a write: `https://api.thingspeak.com/update?api_key=<KEY>&field1=<value>`.
- Standard endpoint for a read: `https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/<ID>/feeds.json?results=<N>`.
- Free-tier limits (commonly tested): **8 fields per channel**, **15-second** minimum write interval, **3 million messages/year** (subject to revision).
- **MATLAB Visualizations** are server-side rendered plots embedded directly in the channel's *Public View* and *Private View* tabs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blynk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blynk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
