N_{\text{msg}} = 40{,}000 \times 43{,}200 = 1.728 \times 10^9 \text{ messages}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Payload Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 1.728 \times 10^9 \times 200 = 3.456 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{\text{MB}} = 3.456 \times 10^{5} \text{ MB}
$$

$$
B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{3.456 \times 10^{5}}{2.592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.1333 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Billable Monthly Cost (3 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{billable}} = 1.728 \times 10^9 - 1 \times 10^9 = 7.28 \times 10^8
$$

$$
N_{\text{M}} = \frac{7.28 \times 10^8}{10^6} = 728 \text{ million}
$$

$$
C = 728 \times 1.00 = \$728 \text{ per month}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — AWS IoT (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **AWS IoT Core** = managed cloud platform for IoT; server-less; pay-per-use.
> ✅ **Six core components** = Device Gateway, Message Broker, Rules Engine, Device Shadow, Registry, Identity Service.
> ✅ **Primary protocols** = MQTT (8883), MQTT-over-WebSockets (443), HTTPS (8443), LoRaWAN.
> ✅ **MQTT** = lightweight pub/sub; 2-byte header; QoS 0/1/2.
> ✅ **Topic wildcards** = `+` (one segment), `#` (multi-segment).
> ✅ **Rules Engine** = SQL-based routing (`SELECT … FROM 'topic' WHERE …`); triggers Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, SNS, SQS, Kinesis.
> ✅ **Device Shadow** = JSON document with `desired`, `reported`, `delta`; enables offline-tolerant control.
> ✅ **Registry** = logical catalogue of Things; supports Thing Types and Thing Groups.
> ✅ **Authentication** = X.509 certificates (per device), IAM, Cognito, Custom Authorizer.
> ✅ **Free tier** = first **1 billion** messages per month free.
> ✅ **Throughput formula** = $N_{\text{msg}} = N_{\text{devices}} \times f_{\text{msg}} \times T_{\text{sec}}$.
> ✅ **Cost formula** = billable messages × price/million.
> ✅ **Bandwidth formula** = $B_{\text{eff}} = S_{\text{payload}} \times f_{\text{msg}} / 10^6$.
> ✅ **Edge runtimes** = AWS IoT Greengrass, FreeRTOS.
> ✅ **Always draw the layered block diagram** in exam answers (Devices → Gateway → Broker → Rules → AWS Services).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Google Cloud IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Google Cloud IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Google Cloud IoT (formerly Google Cloud IoT Core)** is a fully managed, serverless service provided under the Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that enables secure, bidirectional communication, management, ingestion, and processing of large volumes of telemetry data from globally distributed IoT devices. It provides a centralized, cloud-native bridge between physical hardware (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers) and Google's suite of backend analytics and storage services.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Google Cloud IoT is studied as a reference architecture for understanding **managed cloud IoT platforms**, illustrating how device identity, telemetry ingestion, command dispatch, and data analytics integrate within a hyperscaler ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Google Cloud IoT Core was officially **deprecated by Google in August 2021** and fully shut down on **August 16, 2023**. The KTU syllabus still references it for its architectural teaching value. In current GCP environments, customers are directed to partner IoT services (e.g., ClearBlade IoT, Memfault, Actility). However, the *architectural concepts* (Device Registry, Protocol Bridge, Pub/Sub, BigQuery integration) remain highly examinable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Control Tower"

Imagine a **busy international airport**:
- The **aircraft** flying in from every corner of the world = IoT devices (sensors, microcontrollers).
- The **radar and control tower** = Google Cloud IoT Core (orchestrates everything).
- The **ground crew and baggage handlers** = Cloud Pub/Sub (moves luggage = data to the right terminals = services).
- The **warehouse** = BigQuery / Cloud Storage (where luggage is sorted and stored).
- The **pilot's license and passport** = Cryptographic keys (JWT-based device authentication).

Just like the control tower does NOT physically carry luggage or fly planes but only **manages the flow of information**, Google Cloud IoT does NOT store the data permanently or run analytics on it. It is purely a **secure ingestion and command-dispatch layer** that hands off to other GCP services for processing.

## 1.3 Key Components — At a Glance

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Cloud IoT Core Service** | Fully managed service for device connection & management |
| **Device Manager** | Maintains identity (registry) of every device |
| **Protocol Bridge** | Connects devices via MQTT or HTTP |
| **Cloud Pub/Sub** | Asynchronous messaging bus for ingestion & commands |
| **Cloud Functions / Dataflow** | Serverless event processing |
| **BigQuery / Cloud Storage** | Long-term analytics & warehousing |
| **Cloud IAM** | Identity & access management for devices & operators |
| **Cryptographic Keys (JWT)** | Device authentication & TLS security |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition Box:** A *device registry* in Google Cloud IoT is a logical container of devices with shared properties (region, MQTT topic, telemetry routing), uniquely identified within a GCP project. It functions as the **single source of truth** for device identity.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & References

- **Telemetry event size limit:** 256 KB per message
- **Maximum QoS supported (MQTT):** QoS 0 and QoS 1
- **Authentication protocols:** TLS 1.2 with asymmetric keys (RS256 / ES256)
- **Device-to-cloud protocols:** MQTT (port 8883), HTTP (POST)
- **Cloud-to-device protocol:** MQTT only
- **Service uptime SLA (historical):** 99.9% monthly availability
- **Default region support:** us-central1, europe-west1, asia-east1 (historical)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Google Cloud IoT

Google Cloud IoT follows a **four-layer architecture**, which is a frequently asked 14-mark question in KTU university exams. Understanding each layer in depth is critical.

### **Layer 1 — Device Layer (Edge / Field)**
- Composed of physical hardware: microcontrollers (ESP32, Raspberry Pi, STM32), sensors (temperature, humidity, GPS, accelerometer), and actuators.
- Each device runs an MQTT or HTTP client.
- Devices must possess a **unique private key (RSA or EC)** for signing **JSON Web Tokens (JWTs)**.
- Devices typically have constrained resources (memory, CPU, bandwidth).

### **Layer 2 — Connectivity / Ingestion Layer (Cloud IoT Core)**
- This is the heart of Google Cloud IoT.
- Has two sub-components:
  - **Device Manager** — handles identity, configuration, and lifecycle of devices.
  - **Protocol Bridge** — terminates device connections over MQTT/HTTP and normalizes them.

### **Layer 3 — Messaging Layer (Cloud Pub/Sub)**
- **Cloud Pub/Sub** is a globally distributed, real-time messaging service.
- Two primary topic types in Google Cloud IoT:
  - **Telemetry topic** — receives device → cloud data.
  - **Configuration & Command topics** — sends cloud → device updates.
- Guarantees **at-least-once delivery** with global ordering options.

### **Layer 4 — Processing & Analytics Layer**
- **Cloud Functions** for lightweight event-driven logic.
- **Dataflow** for stream processing (Apache Beam based).
- **BigQuery** for OLAP-style analytics.
- **Cloud Storage** for archival of raw data.
- **ML Engine / Vertex AI** for predictive modeling.

## 2.2 Operational Walkthrough — Data Flow Steps

1. **Device Boot & JWT Generation:** Device generates a short-lived JWT signed with its private key, embedding claims like `iat`, `exp`, `aud` (project ID).
2. **TLS Handshake:** Device opens a TLS 1.2 connection to the MQTT/HTTP bridge using the JWT.
3. **Registry Lookup:** Device Manager verifies the JWT, checks the device registry, and authenticates the device.
4. **Topic Subscription/Publishing:** 
   - Device publishes telemetry to `/devices/{device-id}/events`.
   - Device subscribes to `/devices/{device-id}/config` and `/devices/{device-id}/commands`.
5. **Pub/Sub Forwarding:** Protocol Bridge forwards the telemetry message to the configured **Pub/Sub topic**.
6. **Downstream Processing:** Subscribers (Cloud Functions, Dataflow, BigQuery streaming inserts) consume messages.
7. **Cloud-to-Device Commands:** Operators publish commands via Pub/Sub, which are routed back to the device.

## 2.3 Why Google Cloud IoT? (Engineering Utility)

- **Horizontal Scalability:** Built on Google's global infrastructure (Spanner, Borg, Colossus).
- **Serverless:** No infrastructure to provision, patch, or scale.
- **Protocol Flexibility:** Supports both lightweight MQTT (IoT-friendly) and HTTP (web-friendly).
- **Tight GCP Integration:** Plug-and-play with BigQuery, Dataflow, AI/ML tools.
- **Security by Design:** Mutual TLS + asymmetric JWT signature for every connection.

## 2.4 Comparison: Google Cloud IoT vs Other Hyperscaler IoT Platforms

| Feature | Google Cloud IoT | AWS IoT Core | Azure IoT Hub |
|---------|------------------|--------------|---------------|
| Ingestion Protocols | MQTT, HTTP | MQTT, HTTP, WebSockets, LoRaWAN | MQTT, HTTP, AMQP, WebSockets |
| Device Identity | JWT (RS256/ES256) | X.509, Cognito, IAM | SAS tokens, X.509 |
| Messaging Bus | Cloud Pub/Sub | Kinesis / SNS | Event Grid / Service Bus |
| Stream Processing | Dataflow | Kinesis Data Analytics | Stream Analytics |
| Analytics Warehouse | BigQuery | Redshift | Synapse |
| Device Shadow | ❌ (manual via config topic) | ✅ (native) | ✅ (native) |
| Deprecation Status | **Deprecated Aug 2023** | Active | Active |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **absence of a native Device Shadow** in Google Cloud IoT is a very common KTU 2-mark question. AWS IoT Core and Azure IoT Hub provide a virtual "shadow" of each device to track desired vs reported state — Google Cloud IoT required developers to implement this using the configuration topic manually.

## 2.5 KTU Formula & Boundary Sheet (High-Yield)

| Parameter / Concept | Value / Rule | Engineering Significance |
|---------------------|--------------|---------------------------|
| MQTT Broker Port | **8883** (TLS) | Standard secure MQTT port |
| HTTP Endpoint | `https://cloudiotdevice.googleapis.com/v1/{path}` | REST API for control plane |
| Telemetry Max Size | **256 KB** per message | Prevents single message DoS |
| Token Validity | Recommended **1 hour** | JWT `exp` claim limit |
| Asymmetric Algorithms | RS256, ES256 | RSA-256 / ECDSA P-256 |
| Pub/Sub Throughput | Up to **millions of msgs/sec** | Auto-scaled, global |
| Registry Limit | Soft limit; project-bound | Logical grouping of devices |
| Topics per Device | Telemetry, Config, Commands | Three independent channels |
| QoS Levels (MQTT) | QoS 0, QoS 1 only | No QoS 2 support |
| Default Retain Flag | Disabled | Devices must persist config locally |

## 2.6 Security Model (Why & How)

**The Why:** IoT devices are inherently vulnerable — physically accessible, often running outdated firmware, deployed in untrusted networks. A robust cloud IoT platform must mitigate:
- Device spoofing
- Man-in-the-middle attacks
- Replay attacks
- Unauthorized command injection

**The How — Three Pillars:**

1. **Device Identity (Asymmetric Cryptography)**
   - Each device has a public key registered in the Device Registry.
   - The private key never leaves the device.
   - During connection, the device signs a JWT using the private key.

2. **Transport Security (TLS 1.2)**
   - All communication over the wire is encrypted.
   - Mutual TLS optional for MQTT.

3. **Authorization (Pub/Sub IAM Bindings)**
   - Operators (human users) authenticate via Google IAM.
   - Pub/Sub topics enforce fine-grained access control (publisher/subscriber roles).

> [!NOTE]
> **JWT Claim Structure** (KTU favorite 7-mark topic):
> - `iss` — Issuer (always `unused` for Google Cloud IoT device JWTs)
> - `sub` — Subject (the device ID path, e.g., `projects/myproj/locations/region/registries/myreg/devices/mydev`)
> - `aud` — Audience (the GCP project ID)
> - `iat` — Issued at (Unix timestamp)
> - `exp` — Expiration (Unix timestamp; must be within 1 hour of `iat`)

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil sensors → Cloud IoT Core → Pub/Sub → BigQuery → Dashboards for irrigation control.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Industrial equipment telemetry → Cloud IoT → Dataflow → ML model → Alerts via Cloud Functions.
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic, pollution sensors globally → Pub/Sub → Real-time visualization dashboards.
- **Connected Vehicles:** Fleet telemetry → Cloud IoT → BigQuery → Route optimization ML models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of JWT Authentication Flow (Step-by-Step)

This is a high-yield 7-mark derivation. We derive the **chronological authentication sequence** a device executes when connecting to Google Cloud IoT.

### **Step 1: Token Construction**
The device constructs a JWT with the following claims:

$$
JWT_{claims} = \{ iss = \text{unused}, \ sub = P_{dev}, \ aud = P_{proj}, \ iat = T_0, \ exp = T_0 + 3600 \}
$$

Where:
- $P_{dev}$ = `/projects/{projectID}/locations/{region}/registries/{regID}/devices/{devID}`
- $P_{proj}$ = GCP project identifier
- $T_0$ = current Unix time in seconds
- $3600$ = recommended token lifetime in seconds (1 hour)

### **Step 2: Token Signing**
The device signs the JWT header and payload using its **private key** with the registered algorithm (RS256 or ES256):

$$
S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(\text{Base64URL}(H) \ \vert\ \text{Base64URL}(P))
$$

Where:
- $H$ = JWT Header (alg, typ)
- $P$ = JWT Payload (claims)
- $K_{priv}$ = device's private key
- $S$ = signature bytes

### **Step 3: MQTT CONNECT Packet Assembly**
The device creates an MQTT CONNECT packet using the signed JWT in the `password` field, with username typically `unused`:

$$
M_{CONNECT} = \{\texttt{username} = \text{unused}, \ \texttt{password} = H.P.S \}
$$

### **Step 4: TLS Handshake Initiation**
The device opens a TLS 1.2 session to `mqtt.googleapis.com` on port **8883**:

$$
T_{handshake} \approx T_{RTT} \cdot 1.5
$$

(Typical TCP + TLS handshake adds ~1.5 round trips before MQTT CONNECT.)

### **Step 5: Server-Side Verification by Device Manager**

1. **Extract JWT** from the MQTT password field.
2. **Decode header** to determine algorithm (RS256/ES256).
3. **Fetch public key** from the Device Registry using `$sub$.
4. **Verify signature**:
$$
\text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(S) = \text{true} \iff \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H.P) = S
$$
5. **Validate `exp`** claim:
$$
T_{now} < T_{exp} \quad \text{(otherwise reject as expired)}
$$
6. **Validate `aud`** claim matches the GCP project.

### **Step 6: Topic Authorization**
The Device Manager checks the registry's IAM bindings to determine whether the device has publish/subscribe rights on the configured topics:

$$
A_{topic} = \text{IAM}_{check}(R_{perms}, D_{id}, T_{name})
$$

Where:
- $R_{perms}$ = required permissions (e.g., `pubsub.publisher`)
- $D_{id}$ = authenticated device ID
- $T_{name}$ = target topic name

### **Step 7: Connection Established (CONNACK)**
A successful MQTT CONNACK packet is sent to the device:

$$
M_{CONNACK} = \{\texttt{returnCode} = 0, \texttt{sessionPresent} = 0\}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Telemetry Throughput Estimation

A frequently asked KTU numerical: **Given `N` devices publishing at `R` Hz with payload size `B` bytes, estimate the Pub/Sub throughput required.**

### **Step 1: Define Variables**
- $N$ = number of devices
- $R$ = telemetry rate (messages per second per device)
- $B$ = payload size (bytes)
- $T$ = total throughput requirement

### **Step 2: Per-Device Throughput**

$$
T_{dev} = R \cdot B
$$

### **Step 3: Aggregate Throughput**

$$
T_{agg} = N \cdot T_{dev} = N \cdot R \cdot B
$$

### **Step 4: Account for Pub/Sub Overhead**

Each Pub/Sub message adds metadata (~100 bytes for message ID, attributes, publish time):

$$
T_{pubsub} = N \cdot R \cdot (B + 100)
$$

### **Step 5: Account for TLS Overhead**

TLS 1.2 with AES-128-GCM adds ~5-7% protocol overhead:

$$
T_{wire} = T_{pubsub} \cdot 1.07
$$

### **Step 6: Conversion to Mbps**

$$
T_{mbps} = \frac{T_{wire} \cdot 8}{10^6}
$$

### **Worked Example (KTU Style)**

**Problem:** 10,000 sensors each send telemetry at 0.5 Hz with 512-byte payloads. Estimate aggregate throughput in MB/hr.

**Step 1: Per-device rate**
$$
T_{dev} = 0.5 \cdot 512 = 256 \text{ bytes/sec}
$$

**Step 2: Aggregate rate**
$$
T_{agg} = 10000 \cdot 256 = 2,560,000 \text{ bytes/sec}
$$

**Step 3: Add Pub/Sub overhead**
$$
T_{pubsub} = 2,560,000 + (10000 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 100) = 2,560,000 + 500,000 = 3,060,000 \text{ bytes/sec}
$$

**Step 4: Per hour**
$$
T_{hr} = 3,060,000 \cdot 3600 = 11,016,000,000 \text{ bytes/hr} \approx 10.5 \text{ GB/hr}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\approx 10.5 \text{ GB/hr}$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Topic Routing Logic

Google Cloud IoT supports two modes for routing telemetry to Pub/Sub topics:

| Mode | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Default** (Registry-level) | Single Pub/Sub topic set for the entire registry | Homogeneous fleets |
| **Per-device override** | Each device carries a `MqttConfig` specifying its own topic | Multi-tenant or filtered routing |

The decision matrix is given by:

$$
\text{Route}(D) = 
\begin{cases}
T_{registry}, & \text{if } D_{config} = \varnothing \\
T_{device}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = a specific device
- $D_{config}$ = per-device `MqttConfig` field
- $T_{registry}$ = registry default topic
- $T_{device}$ = device-specific topic

## 3.4 Configuration Update Latency Model

Cloud → device configuration updates follow this sequence:

$$
t_{total} = t_{publish} + t_{pubsub} + t_{bridge} + t_{mqtt}
$$

Where:
- $t_{publish}$ = operator's publish call latency
- $t_{pubsub}$ = Pub/Sub end-to-end delivery (typically <100 ms)
- $t_{bridge}$ = Protocol Bridge internal processing
- $t_{mqtt}$ = device's MQTT receive time

**Typical end-to-end latency:** $\approx 200-500 \text{ ms}$ globally.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a "Device Registry" in Google Cloud IoT and list its key attributes.**

**Model Answer:**
A **device registry** in Google Cloud IoT is a logical container in a specific GCP region that groups IoT devices sharing common properties such as MQTT topic configurations, telemetry routing destinations, and authentication settings. Key attributes include:

1. **Registry ID** — unique within a GCP project and region.
2. **Region** — physical GCP region (e.g., `us-central1`).
3. **Event notification configs** — Pub/Sub topic for telemetry routing.
4. **MqttConfig** — MQTT-enabled state and access control.
5. **HTTPConfig** — HTTP bridge enablement.
6. **IAM bindings** — access policies for operators.
7. **Credentials** — collection of public keys of all devices in the registry.

It serves as the **single source of truth** for device identity and acts as a policy boundary for the entire device fleet.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2022] Why does Google Cloud IoT use JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for device authentication instead of static API keys?**

**Model Answer:**
Google Cloud IoT uses **asymmetric JWTs** for the following reasons:

1. **No shared secret risk** — only the public key is stored in the cloud; the private key never leaves the device, eliminating server-side secret compromise.
2. **Replay protection** — short-lived JWTs (≤1 hour) prevent attackers from reusing stolen tokens.
3. **Cryptographic proof of identity** — the signed JWT proves the device possesses the private key, enabling non-repudiation.
4. **Scalability** — no centralized key database lookup is needed; verification is local using the registered public key.
5. **Standards compliance** — JWT is RFC 7519 and integrates with industrial IoT security standards.
6. **Algorithm flexibility** — supports RS256 (RSA) and ES256 (ECDSA), letting devices choose hardware-friendly options.

Static API keys would require server-side storage, rotation complexity, and provide weaker non-repudiation guarantees.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **KTU Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer ANY ONE of the following.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]*

#### **(a)** Explain in detail the **four-layer architecture of Google Cloud IoT**, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Cloud Pub/Sub as the messaging backbone. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Discuss the **security mechanisms** in Google Cloud IoT. Explain device authentication using JWTs and the TLS 1.2 handshake sequence. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **(a) Four-Layer Architecture (7 marks)**

The Google Cloud IoT architecture is organized into four well-defined layers:

**Layer 1 — Device/Edge Layer:**
- Comprises physical devices: microcontrollers (e.g., ESP32, Raspberry Pi, STM32), sensors (temperature, humidity, GPS, motion), and actuators.
- Each device runs a thin MQTT or HTTP client.
- Devices are resource-constrained; they typically have limited compute, memory, and power.
- Each device must store a **private key** (RSA 2048/4096 or EC P-256) in secure storage (e.g., ATECC608, TPM, secure element).

**Layer 2 — Cloud IoT Core (Ingestion Layer):**
- The fully managed service that handles device connectivity.
- Two sub-components:
  - **Device Manager** — maintains the device registry, handles identity verification, and provides a control plane API.
  - **Protocol Bridge** — terminates incoming MQTT (port 8883) and HTTP connections from devices; forwards data to Pub/Sub.
- Supports horizontal scaling — handles millions of concurrent device connections.

**Layer 3 — Messaging Layer (Cloud Pub/Sub):**
- Acts as the **asynchronous messaging bus** between Cloud IoT Core and downstream services.
- Provides three key topic types:
  - **Telemetry topic** — receives device → cloud data.
  - **Configuration topic** — sends cloud → device config updates.
  - **Commands topic** — sends cloud → device operational commands.
- Guarantees **at-least-once delivery** with replay capabilities.
- Decouples ingestion from processing, enabling serverless downstream services.

**Layer 4 — Processing & Analytics Layer:**
- **Cloud Functions** for lightweight, event-driven logic (e.g., threshold alerts).
- **Dataflow** (Apache Beam-based) for complex stream processing (windowed aggregations, joins).
- **BigQuery** for OLAP analytics and SQL-based queries on telemetry.
- **Cloud Storage** for long-term archival of raw data.
- **Vertex AI / AutoML** for predictive models (e.g., anomaly detection, predictive maintenance).
- **Looker / Data Studio** for visualization dashboards.

**Role of Cloud Pub/Sub:**
1. **Decoupling** — separates ingestion from processing, allowing independent scaling.
2. **Asynchronous buffering** — absorbs traffic spikes and prevents backpressure on devices.
3. **Fan-out** — multiple subscribers can consume the same telemetry stream (e.g., one to BigQuery, one to alerting).
4. **Replay & dead-letter handling** — messages can be replayed if processing fails.
5. **Global reach** — multi-region replication for high availability.

*(Block diagram already provided in Section 4.5 — students should redraw a clean version with labeled arrows.)*

---

#### **(b) Security Mechanisms (7 marks)**

Google Cloud IoT security is built on **three pillars**:

**Pillar 1 — Device Identity & Authentication via JWT**

A JWT is a compact, URL-safe token format defined by **RFC 7519**. In Google Cloud IoT, the device constructs a JWT with the following claims:

- `iss` = `"unused"` (literal string for Google Cloud IoT)
- `sub` = the full device resource path
- `aud` = the GCP project ID
- `iat` = issue time (Unix seconds)
- `exp` = expiration time (must be ≤ 1 hour after `iat`)

The JWT is signed with the device's private key using **RS256** (RSA-SHA256) or **ES256** (ECDSA with P-256 curve). The signature is appended as the third dot-separated segment:

$$
\text{JWT} = \text{Base64URL}(H) \cdot \text{Base64URL}(P) \cdot \text{Base64URL}(S)
$$

The corresponding public key is pre-registered in the Device Registry.

**Pillar 2 — Transport Security via TLS 1.2**

- All device traffic is encrypted over the wire using **TLS 1.2** (port 8883 for MQTT).
- The Protocol Bridge presents Google's server certificate to the device.
- The client (device) presents no client certificate; instead, the JWT replaces the client certificate in the MQTT CONNECT packet's `password` field.
- TLS prevents eavesdropping, tampering, and forging.

**Pillar 3 — Authorization via IAM**

- Operators (humans) authenticate via **Google IAM**.
- Pub/Sub topics enforce fine-grained access controls (publisher, subscriber, viewer roles).
- Devices do not use IAM directly — they use the JWT model.
- Registries can have IAM bindings to limit who can read/write device configurations.

**TLS 1.2 Handshake Sequence (Summary):**
1. **ClientHello** — device sends supported cipher suites, random nonce.
2. **ServerHello + Certificate** — bridge responds with its public certificate.
3. **Key Exchange** — device generates pre-master secret, encrypts with server's public key.
4. **Finished** — both sides send encrypted `Finished` message.
5. **MQTT CONNECT** with JWT — device sends MQTT CONNECT with the JWT in the password field.
6. **JWT Verification** — Device Manager validates the signature against the registered public key.
7. **CONNACK** — on success, connection is established.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **device authentication (JWT)** with **operator authentication (IAM)**. Make sure to clearly distinguish them in the answer.
> - Do NOT state that devices use X.509 client certificates — Google Cloud IoT uses **JWT in the MQTT password field**, NOT client certificates.
> - Forgetting the `exp` claim validity (≤ 1 hour) is a common 1-mark loss.
> - Do not say Cloud Pub/Sub *stores* data — it *buffers* it briefly. Storage is BigQuery / Cloud Storage's job.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]*

#### **(a)** Describe the **components of Google Cloud IoT Core** in detail. Explain the role of the **Device Manager** and **Protocol Bridge**. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Compare Google Cloud IoT with **AWS IoT Core** and **Azure IoT Hub** based on protocol support, device identity, and analytics integration. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **(a) Components of Cloud IoT Core (7 marks)**

Google Cloud IoT Core has two primary service components:

**1. Device Manager**

The Device Manager is the **control plane** of Google Cloud IoT. It is responsible for:

- **Registry management** — creating, updating, deleting device registries and devices via REST/gcloud APIs.
- **Identity verification** — fetching the device's public key, verifying the JWT signature, and validating `exp` claim.
- **Lifecycle management** — enabling, disabling, blocking, and deleting devices.
- **Configuration management** — pushing config updates to devices via the configuration topic.
- **State tracking** — recording the last-seen telemetry timestamp for each device (Note: this is the LAST EVENT TIME, not a true device shadow).
- **IAM enforcement** — checking whether the operator has permission to perform CRUD operations on the registry.

The Device Manager exposes a **REST API** (HTTP) for control plane operations:
- `projects.locations.registries.create`
- `projects.locations.registries.devices.list`
- `projects.locations.registries.devices.sendCommand`

**2. Protocol Bridge**

The Protocol Bridge is the **data plane** of Google Cloud IoT. It is responsible for:

- **Accepting connections** from devices over MQTT (port 8883) or HTTP (POST).
- **TLS termination** — handles the TLS 1.2 handshake on behalf of the GCP service.
- **JWT extraction and forwarding** — pulls the JWT from the MQTT password and forwards to Device Manager for validation.
- **Topic translation** — converts device telemetry topic paths to Pub/Sub publish calls.
- **Bidirectional routing** — subscribes to the command Pub/Sub topic and forwards commands back to devices via MQTT.
- **Load balancing** — distributes device connections across multiple backend servers using Google's global load balancer.

**3. Auxiliary Components (Brief):**
- **Cloud Pub/Sub** — messaging bus (managed by GCP, not part of IoT Core).
- **GCP IAM** — authentication for operators.
- **Cloud Monitoring/Logging** — observability for IoT Core.

---

#### **(b) Comparison: Google Cloud IoT vs AWS IoT Core vs Azure IoT Hub (7 marks)**

| Comparison Axis | Google Cloud IoT | AWS IoT Core | Azure IoT Hub |
|-----------------|------------------|--------------|---------------|
| **Ingestion Protocols** | MQTT (8883), HTTP | MQTT (8883), HTTP (8443), WebSockets, LoRaWAN | MQTT (8883), HTTP, AMQP, WebSockets |
| **Cloud-to-Device Protocol** | MQTT only | MQTT, WebSockets | MQTT, AMQP |
| **Device Identity Model** | JWT (RS256/ES256) with private keys on device | X.509 certificates, IAM credentials, Cognito | SAS tokens, X.509 certificates |
| **Device Shadow** | ❌ (manual via config topic) | ✅ (native, JSON state doc) | ✅ (native, desired vs reported state) |
| **Message Broker / Bus** | Cloud Pub/Sub | AWS Kinesis, SNS, SQS | Azure Event Grid, Service Bus |
| **Stream Processing** | Dataflow (Apache Beam) | Kinesis Data Analytics | Azure Stream Analytics |
| **Data Warehouse** | BigQuery | Amazon Redshift | Azure Synapse |
| **ML Integration** | Vertex AI | SageMaker | Azure ML |
| **Identity Service** | Cloud IAM | AWS IAM + Cognito | Azure Active Directory |
| **Visualization** | Looker Studio | QuickSight | Power BI |
| **Edge Compute Support** | Limited (Cloud IoT Edge, deprecated) | AWS Greengrass | Azure IoT Edge |
| **Current Status (2024)** | ❌ Deprecated Aug 2023 | ✅ Active | ✅ Active |

**Key Distinctions to Emphasize in Exam:**

1. **Device Shadow:** AWS and Azure offer a **native device shadow** (a virtual JSON document representing desired vs reported device state). Google Cloud IoT required developers to implement shadow-like functionality using the configuration topic manually — this is a frequently tested differentiator.

2. **Protocol Breadth:** Azure is the most protocol-rich (4 protocols), Google is the most restricted (MQTT + HTTP).

3. **Identity Model:** Google uses pure asymmetric JWTs — simpler but requires careful device-side key management. AWS offers the most flexibility (X.509, Cognito, custom auth). Azure uses SAS tokens (symmetric HMAC) or X.509.

4. **Analytics:** Google pairs naturally with **BigQuery** (serverless SQL warehouse), while AWS uses Redshift + Athena, and Azure uses Synapse + Data Lake.

5. **Deprecation Caveat:** Google Cloud IoT is the only one of the three that has been deprecated, making architectural study of it more of a pedagogical exercise than a production recommendation.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Architecture:** 4 layers — Device → Cloud IoT Core → Pub/Sub → Analytics.
- ✅ **Two main components of IoT Core:** Device Manager (control plane) and Protocol Bridge (data plane).
- ✅ **Two ingestion protocols:** MQTT (8883) and HTTP.
- ✅ **Cloud-to-device protocol:** MQTT only.
- ✅ **Authentication mechanism:** Asymmetric JWT (RS256 or ES256), signed with device's private key.
- ✅ **JWT expiry:** Must be ≤ **1 hour** from `iat`.
- ✅ **Transport security:** TLS 1.2 over port 8883.
- ✅ **Telemetry message size limit:** **256 KB**.
- ✅ **Supported MQTT QoS levels:** QoS 0 and QoS 1 only (no QoS 2).
- ✅ **Default Pub/Sub topic format:** `/devices/{device-id}/events` for telemetry; `/devices/{device-id}/config` and `/devices/{device-id}/commands` for downlink.
- ✅ **At-least-once delivery** is the default guarantee of Cloud Pub/Sub.
- ✅ **No native device shadow** — must be implemented via configuration topic.
- ✅ **Deprecation status:** Google Cloud IoT Core was deprecated Aug 2021, fully shut down Aug 16, 2023.
- ✅ **BigQuery + Dataflow** are the standard downstream analytics & stream processing services.
- ✅ **Device Registry** is region-bound and acts as the policy boundary.
- ✅ **JWT claims to remember:** `iss="unused"`, `sub` (device path), `aud` (project ID), `iat`, `exp`.
- ✅ **Comparison differentiators:** Device shadow availability, protocol breadth, identity model.
- ✅ **Default Pub/Sub topic for telemetry:** configured at the registry level, can be overridden per device.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain Google Cloud IoT," always structure your answer around the **4-layer architecture**, mention **JWT authentication**, and tie it together with **Pub/Sub** as the messaging backbone. Always draw a **block diagram** — it earns 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microsoft Azure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Microsoft Azure?

**Microsoft Azure** (formerly known as **Windows Azure**) is a public cloud computing platform operated by Microsoft. It provides a broad and growing set of integrated cloud services — including compute, analytics, storage, and networking — that organizations and developers can use to build, deploy, and manage applications and IoT solutions through Microsoft-managed data centers distributed globally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Microsoft Azure is a comprehensive *Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)* and *Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)* cloud computing platform that offers a rich suite of IoT-specific services (such as **Azure IoT Hub**, **Azure IoT Central**, and **Azure IoT Edge**) for connecting, monitoring, and managing billions of IoT devices at global scale.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Post Office" Metaphor

Imagine you want to send and receive mail from millions of people across the world, but you do not want to build your own post office network, train your own postal workers, or buy your own delivery trucks. Instead, you walk up to a counter at a massive, already-built post office and simply rent a mailbox, hire a sorting clerk, and pay only for the letters you actually handle. That giant post office — with its trucks, staff, sorting machines, security, and tracking system — is **Microsoft Azure**.

In this analogy:
- **You (the developer)** = Customer wanting to build an IoT application
- **Mailboxes** = Storage accounts, databases, IoT Hubs
- **Sorting clerks** = Azure Functions, Stream Analytics, Event Grid
- **Delivery trucks** = Network bandwidth and ExpressRoute
- **Security guards** = Azure Active Directory, role-based access control
- **Tracking system** = Azure Monitor and Application Insights

You pay only for what you use (**consumption-based pricing**), and Azure handles the heavy lifting of infrastructure, scaling, and security.

## 1.3 The Three Service Models Offered by Azure

| Service Model | Full Name | KTU Key Definition | User Responsibility | Azure Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Rent raw virtual machines, storage, and networks | OS, Middleware, Applications, Data | Hardware, Virtualization, Networking |
| **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Deploy applications without managing underlying infrastructure | Applications and Data | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Virtualization, Hardware |
| **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Use ready-made software hosted by Azure | Only user data and access | Entire stack from app to hardware |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3, the focus is specifically on **Azure IoT services** — particularly how Azure acts as the *Cloud Layer* in the standard **3-layer IoT architecture** (Perception → Network → Cloud → Application).

## 1.4 Why Azure is Critical for IoT

IoT deployments generate **massive volumes of telemetry** (often in **TB/day**). On-premise servers cannot scale elastically. Azure solves this through:

1. **Elastic Scalability** — Resources scale up/down automatically with device count
2. **Global Reach** — Data centers in **60+ regions** worldwide
3. **Built-in IoT Protocols** — Native support for **MQTT**, **AMQP**, and **HTTPS**
4. **Enterprise Security** — **X.509 certificate** authentication and **AES-256** encryption
5. **Integration** — Tight integration with **Power BI**, **Azure ML**, and **Dynamics 365**

## 1.5 Core Azure Terminology Every KTU Student Must Know

- **Subscription** — A billing unit; each Azure account has at least one.
- **Resource Group (RG)** — A logical container that holds related Azure resources.
- **Region** — A geographical area containing one or more data centers (e.g., *East US*, *West Europe*).
- **Availability Zone (AZ)** — Physically separate data centers within a region for high availability.
- **Tenant** — A dedicated, isolated instance of Azure Active Directory for an organization.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** — Microsoft's uptime commitment, typically **99.9% (Three 9s)** to **99.99% (Four 9s)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why cloud platforms for IoT?"*, always mention three keywords: **Scalability, Reliability, and Cost-Efficiency (Pay-per-use)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Microsoft Azure IoT

The Azure IoT reference architecture is structured in **four logical layers**, each with specific responsibilities:

### Layer 1 — Device Layer (Perception/Edge)
- Physical sensors and microcontrollers (e.g., **Raspberry Pi**, **ESP32**, **Azure Sphere**)
- Edge devices running **Azure IoT Edge** runtime
- Protocols used: **MQTT (port 8883)**, **AMQP (port 5671)**, **HTTPS (port 443)**

### Layer 2 — Ingestion / Gateway Layer
- **Azure IoT Hub** — bidirectional messaging service
- **Azure IoT Central** — SaaS solution for simplified IoT app management
- Handles device identity, authentication, and command routing

### Layer 3 — Processing / Analytics Layer
- **Azure Stream Analytics** — real-time event processing
- **Azure Functions** — serverless event-driven compute
- **Azure Event Grid** — event routing service
- **Azure Logic Apps** — workflow orchestration

### Layer 4 — Storage, ML, and Presentation Layer
- **Azure Cosmos DB** — globally distributed NoSQL database
- **Azure SQL Database** — relational storage
- **Azure Blob Storage** — for unstructured data (images, videos, logs)
- **Azure Time Series Insights** — for time-series IoT data
- **Power BI** — visualization dashboards

## 2.2 Azure IoT Hub — The Heart of Azure IoT

**Azure IoT Hub** is a managed service hosted in the cloud that acts as a central message hub for bi-directional communication between IoT applications and the devices it manages.

### Key Capabilities
- **Per-device authentication** using X.509 certs or SAS tokens
- **Device twins** — JSON documents storing device state metadata
- **Direct methods** — synchronous device commands
- **Cloud-to-device (C2D) messaging**
- **Device-to-cloud (D2C) telemetry** at massive scale

### IoT Hub Tiers (Pricing Skews)

| Tier | Max Messages/Day | Suitable For | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free (F1)** | 8,000 messages/day | Testing and learning | 1 unit only |
| **Standard S1** | 400,000 messages/day/unit | Moderate IoT workloads | Advanced routing |
| **Standard S2** | 6,000,000 messages/day/unit | Large-scale IoT | High throughput |
| **Standard S3** | 300,000,000 messages/day/unit | Mission-critical | Maximum scale |

## 2.3 Azure IoT Edge — Intelligence at the Edge

**Azure IoT Edge** moves cloud analytics and custom business logic to the edge device itself. This is essential when:
- **Bandwidth is limited** (e.g., remote oil rigs, ships)
- **Latency is critical** (e.g., industrial robots, autonomous vehicles)
- **Connectivity is intermittent** (e.g., agricultural sensors)

> [!NOTE]
> IoT Edge modules are Docker containers running on the edge device, deployed and managed remotely from the cloud via IoT Hub.

## 2.4 Communication Protocols — Deep Dive

| Protocol | Port | Transport | Header Size | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MQTT** | 8883 (TLS) | TCP | 2 bytes | Low-bandwidth, battery devices |
| **AMQP** | 5671 (TLS) | TCP | 8 bytes | Reliable enterprise messaging |
| **HTTPS** | 443 | TCP/TLS | ~8 KB header | Simple web-based telemetry |
| **Custom Protocol Gateway** | Configurable | Any | Variable | Proprietary industrial protocols |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield for KTU ESE numericals**. Memorize units and constants.

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Units / Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uptime Availability** | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Measures reliability |
| **SLA Uptime (Annual)** | $Uptime_{hrs} = 8760 \times \dfrac{SLA\%}{100}$ | Hours/year | Compute allowed downtime |
| **Throughput (Messages/sec)** | $T = \dfrac{N_{msgs}}{T_{window}}$ | msgs/sec | IoT Hub capacity check |
| **Storage Capacity** | $S = N_{devices} \times f_{sample} \times S_{sample} \times 86400$ | Bytes/day | Daily telemetry storage |
| **Bandwidth Required** | $BW = N_{devices} \times M_{avg} \times 8$ | bits/sec | Network sizing |
| **Latency (Queuing)** | $L_q = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$ (M/M/1 model) | seconds | Queueing delay |
| **Cost Estimation** | $C_{monthly} = \sum (U_i \times P_i \times 730)$ | INR/USD | Monthly Azure bill |
| **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Unitless | Data optimization |

> **Standard Constants (Azure):**
> - Hours per month billing = **730**
> - Hours per year = **8760**
> - Days per year = **365**
> - 1 KB = **1024 bytes** ; 1 MB = $1024^2$ bytes
> - Azure SLA minimum (VMs) = **99.9%**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Azure IoT underpins smart city projects (e.g., **Barcelona smart lighting**), predictive maintenance in manufacturing (**Rolls-Royce jet engines**), connected healthcare (remote patient monitoring), and precision agriculture. Understanding Azure's service model is essential for any KTU student aspiring to become a **Cloud Solutions Architect** or **IoT Developer**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Downtime Allowed by an Azure SLA

Suppose a KTU exam question states: *"An Azure VM has an SLA of 99.95%. Calculate the maximum permissible downtime per year and per month."*

### Step 1 — Identify Constants
- Total hours in a year = **8760 hours**
- Total days in a year = **365 days**
- SLA = **99.95%**

### Step 2 — Apply the Uptime Formula
The annual uptime in hours is calculated as:

$$
Uptime_{hrs} = 8760 \times \frac{SLA}{100}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values
$$
Uptime_{hrs} = 8760 \times \frac{99.95}{100}
$$
$$
Uptime_{hrs} = 8760 \times 0.9995
$$
$$
Uptime_{hrs} = 8755.62 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Permitted Downtime
$$
Downtime_{hrs} = 8760 - Uptime_{hrs}
$$
$$
Downtime_{hrs} = 8760 - 8755.62
$$
$$
Downtime_{hrs} = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Minutes for Intuition
$$
Downtime_{mins} = 4.38 \times 60 = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Monthly Average
$$
Downtime_{monthly} = \frac{4.38}{12} = 0.365 \text{ hours/month} \approx 21.9 \text{ minutes/month}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Daily Storage Requirement for a Sensor Network

> **Problem:** A smart agriculture deployment uses **1,000 IoT soil sensors**. Each sensor sends **one telemetry packet every 10 seconds**, where each packet is **256 bytes**. Compute the **total daily storage** required in **MB/day**.

### Step 1 — Compute Number of Packets Per Sensor Per Day
$$
N_{packets} = \frac{86400 \text{ seconds}}{10 \text{ sec/packet}} = 8640 \text{ packets/sensor/day}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total Packets from All Sensors
$$
N_{total} = 1000 \times 8640 = 8,640,000 \text{ packets/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Bytes Per Day
$$
S_{bytes} = 8,640,000 \times 256 = 2,211,840,000 \text{ bytes/day}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to MB
$$
S_{MB} = \frac{2,211,840,000}{1024^2} = \frac{2,211,840,000}{1,048,576}
$$
$$
S_{MB} \approx 2110.43 \text{ MB/day} \approx 2.11 \text{ GB/day}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Insight
At this rate, **1 month produces ~63 GB** of raw data — confirming why **cloud storage (Azure Blob)** is essential over on-premise servers.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth Calculation for a Video-Based IoT Surveillance System

> **Problem:** **200 IP cameras** stream **720p video at 1 Mbps each** to Azure Media Services. Find the **total bandwidth** required and the **monthly data transfer** in TB.

### Step 1 — Total Bandwidth
$$
BW = 200 \times 1 \text{ Mbps} = 200 \text{ Mbps}
$$
Convert to bytes/sec:
$$
BW_{bytes} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{8} = 25,000,000 \text{ bytes/sec} = 25 \text{ MB/s}
$$

### Step 2 — Daily Data Transfer
$$
D_{daily} = 25 \times 86400 = 2,160,000 \text{ MB/day} = 2.16 \text{ TB/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Monthly Transfer (30 days)
$$
D_{monthly} = 2.16 \times 30 = 64.8 \text{ TB/month}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Azure vs. Competitor IoT Platforms

| Feature | **Microsoft Azure IoT** | **AWS IoT Core** | **Google Cloud IoT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core Hub Service | IoT Hub | AWS IoT Core | Cloud IoT Core (deprecated 2023) |
| Edge Runtime | Azure IoT Edge | AWS Greengrass | Google Edge TPU |
| Primary Protocol | MQTT, AMQP, HTTPS | MQTT, HTTPS, LoRaWAN | MQTT, HTTP |
| Stream Processing | Stream Analytics | Kinesis | Dataflow |
| Time Series DB | Time Series Insights | Timestream | Bigtable |
| Visualisation | Power BI | QuickSight | Looker |
| SLA (Standard) | **99.9%** | 99.9% | 99.9% |
| Identity Service | Azure Active Directory | AWS IAM | Google IAM |

## 3.5 Decision Matrix: When to Choose Which Azure IoT Service

| Scenario | Recommended Azure Service | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Connecting **<10,000 devices** with simple dashboard | **Azure IoT Central** | SaaS-based, fastest deployment |
| Connecting **millions of devices** with custom backend | **Azure IoT Hub** | Maximum scale and control |
| Running ML at the **edge** with poor connectivity | **Azure IoT Edge** | Offline operation, low latency |
| Storing **time-series telemetry** | **Azure Time Series Insights** | Optimized for IoT data |
| Triggering actions on **device events** | **Azure Event Grid + Functions** | Serverless, event-driven |
| Creating a **digital replica** of physical systems | **Azure Digital Twins** | Spatial intelligence graph |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Azure IoT Reference Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Sensor1] --> GW[Azure IoT Edge Gateway]
    A2[Sensor2] --> GW
    A3[Sensor3] --> GW
    A4[Actuator Node] --> GW
    GW -->|MQTT TLS 8883| IOTHUB[Azure IoT Hub]
    IOTHUB -->|Routing Rules| SA[Stream Analytics Job]
    IOTHUB -->|Routing Rules| EG[Event Grid Topic]
    IOTHUB -->|Routing Rules| TSI[Time Series Insights]
    SA -->|Processed Events| FUNC[Azure Functions]
    SA -->|Aggregated Data| COSMOS[Azure Cosmos DB]
    FUNC -->|Triggers| BLOB[Azure Blob Storage]
    FUNC -->|Alerts| NOTIF[Notification Hub]
    TSI --> POWERBI[Power BI Dashboard]
    COSMOS --> POWERBI
    EG -->|Logic Apps| WORKFLOW[Business Workflow]
    WORKFLOW --> MOBILE[Mobile and Web Apps]
    NOTIF --> MOBILE
    AAD[Azure Active Directory] --> IOTHUB
    AAD --> COSMOS
    MONITOR[Azure Monitor and Log Analytics] --> IOTHUB
    MONITOR --> SA
```

## 4.2 Data Flow Sequence — Device to Cloud to Action

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DEV as IoT Device
    participant HUB as Azure IoT Hub
    participant SA as Stream Analytics
    participant FN as Azure Function
    participant DB as Cosmos DB
    participant UI as Power BI

    DEV->>HUB: Send telemetry (MQTT, port 8883)
    HUB->>HUB: Authenticate SAS token or X.509
    HUB->>SA: Route message to Stream Analytics input

    SA->>FN: Trigger alarm event on threshold breach
    FN->>HUB: Send C2D message to actuate device

    DB-->>UI: Return real-time data
    UI-->>User: Display live dashboard
```

## 4.3 Azure IoT Hub Message Routing Topology


## 4.5 Azure IoT Service Selection Tree (Decision Flow)
