The **Sense–Process–Actuate loop** is the foundational operational cycle of any IoT system. It comprises three interconnected phases:

1. **Sense Phase (Data Collection):** Physical phenomena (temperature, motion, light) are captured by **sensors** and converted into electrical signals. These are digitized by an **ADC** and formatted using protocols such as **MQTT** or **CoAP**. Example: DHT11 measures ambient temperature.

2. **Process Phase (Data Processing):** The raw digital data undergoes **preprocessing** (filtering, normalization) at the edge, is transmitted over a network, and is then subjected to advanced **analytics** at the fog or cloud tier. Machine learning models may be applied.

3. **Actuate Phase (Action):** Based on the processed decision, control signals are sent to **actuators** (motors, relays, valves), which produce a physical change. Example: A soil-moisture sensor reading below threshold → microcontroller triggers a water-pump relay.

The loop is **closed** because the actuation alters the physical state, which is sensed again in the next cycle — enabling continuous, autonomous IoT operation.

**Four Types of Data Analytics (with IoT examples):**

| Type | Question Answered | IoT Example |
|------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Descriptive** | What happened? | Dashboard showing past 24 hours of room temperature in a smart building |
| **Diagnostic** | Why did it happen? | Root-cause analysis revealing that AC failure caused the temperature spike |
| **Predictive** | What will happen? | ML model forecasting equipment failure 7 days in advance from vibration sensors |
| **Prescriptive** | What should we do? | Optimization engine recommending reduced load + schedule maintenance for the faulty motor |

(Diagrams and tables together — 7 marks: 3 for loop explanation, 2 for diagram, 2 for analytics)

---

**(b) With necessary equations, explain the working of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). A 12-bit ADC has a reference voltage of 3.3 V. Find (i) the step size, and (ii) the digital output when an input of 1.85 V is applied. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of ADC:** An Analog-to-Digital Converter transforms a continuous analog voltage into a discrete binary number. For an $n$-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$, the output is given by:

$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n$$

The smallest voltage change detectable (step size) is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, $V_{in} = 1.85$ V.

**(i) Step Size Calculation:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096}$$

$$\Delta V \approx 8.06 \times 10^{-4}\text{ V} = 0.806\text{ mV/LSB}$$

**(ii) Digital Output Calculation:**

$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n = \frac{1.85}{3.3} \times 4096$$

$$D = 0.5606 \times 4096 = 2296.21$$

Rounding to the nearest integer:

$$D = 2296 \text{ (decimal)} = 100011111000_2 \text{ (binary)}$$

**Conclusion:** A 12-bit ADC with 3.3 V reference can resolve voltages in steps of **0.806 mV**, yielding digital counts from **0 to 4095**.

(7 marks: 2 for theory, 1.5 for each part, 1 for binary conversion, 1 for conclusion)

---

### 📗 Question B: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]`

**(a) Describe the three-tier data processing architecture in IoT (Edge, Fog, Cloud) with a comparison table. State two advantages and two limitations of edge computing. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Modern IoT systems distribute data processing across **three computational tiers** to balance latency, cost, and analytical depth:

1. **Edge Tier:** Computation occurs **on the IoT device itself** (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi Pico). It handles real-time decisions with latency **< 10 ms**. Limited by the device's CPU, memory, and power.

2. **Fog Tier:** Computation occurs on a **local gateway** (Raspberry Pi 4, industrial PC) within the LAN. It performs **data aggregation, local analytics, and ML inference** with medium latency (10–100 ms).

3. **Cloud Tier:** Computation occurs on **remote data centers** (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT). It supports **big data analytics, long-term storage, dashboarding, and global ML model training** with high latency (100 ms+).

**Comparison Table (Summary):**

| Parameter | Edge | Fog | Cloud |
|-----------|------|-----|-------|
| Latency | < 10 ms | 10–100 ms | > 100 ms |
| Location | Device | LAN Gateway | Remote Data Center |
| Best for | Real-time control | Local analytics | Big data & ML training |
| Connectivity required | No | LAN only | Internet (always) |

**Advantages of Edge Computing (2 marks):**
- **Ultra-low latency** — enables safety-critical real-time control.
- **Bandwidth savings** — only summarized/alert data is uploaded, not raw streams.
- **Enhanced privacy** — sensitive data (e.g., medical vitals) never leaves the device.
- **Offline operation** — works even during network outages.

**Limitations of Edge Computing (2 marks):**
- **Limited compute resources** — cannot run heavy ML models.
- **Higher per-device cost** — each node needs sufficient hardware.
- **Difficult to manage at scale** — firmware updates across thousands of edge nodes.
- **Limited storage** — only short-term buffering is feasible.

(7 marks: 3 for tier explanation, 2 for table, 1 advantage, 1 limitation)

---

**(b) Explain data preprocessing in IoT. Apply the Min-Max normalization formula to transform the dataset {12, 18, 5, 27, 9} into the range [0, 1]. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Data Preprocessing in IoT** is the set of operations applied to raw sensor data to make it suitable, efficient, and reliable for downstream analytics. It is critical because sensor data is typically **noisy, incomplete, inconsistent, and redundant**.

**Common Preprocessing Steps:**

1. **Data Cleaning:** Remove or impute NULL/NaN values; eliminate duplicate readings.
2. **Noise Filtering:** Apply moving average, median filter, or Kalman filter to smooth sensor jitter.
3. **Normalization / Scaling:** Transform values to a common range (e.g., [0,1] or [-1,1]) so that features with different units contribute equally to ML models.
4. **Encoding:** Convert categorical data (e.g., "low", "medium", "high") into numeric form.
5. **Data Reduction:** Downsample, aggregate, or compress to reduce transmission/storage load.

**Min-Max Normalization Formula:**

$$x_{norm} = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$

**Given Dataset:** $D = \{12,\ 18,\ 5,\ 27,\ 9\}$

**Step 1: Identify min and max values**

$$x_{min} = 5, \quad x_{max} = 27$$

**Step 2: Compute the range**

$$x_{max} - x_{min} = 27 - 5 = 22$$

**Step 3: Normalize each value**

- For $x = 12$: $\quad x_{norm} = \dfrac{12 - 5}{22} = \dfrac{7}{22} \approx 0.318$

- For $x = 18$: $\quad x_{norm} = \dfrac{18 - 5}{22} = \dfrac{13}{22} \approx 0.591$

- For $x = 5$: $\quad x_{norm} = \dfrac{5 - 5}{22} = 0$

- For $x = 27$: $\quad x_{norm} = \dfrac{27 - 5}{22} = 1$

- For $x = 9$: $\quad x_{norm} = \dfrac{9 - 5}{22} = \dfrac{4}{22} \approx 0.182$

**Final Normalized Dataset:**

$$D_{norm} = \{0.318,\ 0.591,\ 0.000,\ 1.000,\ 0.182\}$$

**Verification:** All values lie in $[0, 1]$ and the relative ordering is preserved — confirming correct application of the formula.

(7 marks: 2.5 for preprocessing theory, 4.5 for the calculation with steps)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the inequality in Nyquist Theorem** — Writing "$f_s = 2 f_{max}$" instead of "$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$" costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Not rounding ADC outputs** — Writing $D = 563.2$ instead of $D = 563$ shows a lack of digital-hardware understanding.
> 3. **Confusing the 4 Analytics types** — Mixing up "Diagnostic" and "Predictive" or forgetting the "What?" question for each.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark questions** — KTU evaluators explicitly deduct 1–2 marks when a block diagram is missing in "Explain with diagram" type questions.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — A formula written without **Hz, V, bits, ms, etc.** looks incomplete in valuation.
> 6. **Writing `|` in LaTeX inside tables** — Breaks the markdown table and creates a formatting mess. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 7. **One-line answers for 7-mark parts** — Examiners expect 1 to 1.5 pages of structured content for a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ The IoT data lifecycle is **Sense → Process → Actuate** (closed feedback loop).
- ✅ **Sensors** collect physical data; **Actuators** produce physical action — they are opposite ends of the loop.
- ✅ **ADC Formula:** $D = (V_{in}/V_{ref}) \times 2^n$ and **Step Size** $= V_{ref}/2^n$.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — must sample at least **twice** the highest frequency.
- ✅ **3 Processing Tiers:** Edge (< 10 ms, real-time) → Fog (10–100 ms, local) → Cloud (> 100 ms, big-data).
- ✅ **4 Analytics Types:** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (the 4 "Whats & Whys").
- ✅ **Min-Max Normalization:** $x_{norm} = (x - x_{min}) / (x_{max} - x_{min})$ — output range $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **EMA Filter:** $EMA_t = \alpha \cdot x_t + (1-\alpha) \cdot EMA_{t-1}$ — used to denoise sensor streams.
- ✅ **MQTT** is the most common lightweight IoT protocol (publish-subscribe, low bandwidth).
- ✅ **Actuators** include: DC motors, servo motors, stepper motors, relays, solenoids, LEDs, buzzers.
- ✅ Always state **units** (Hz, V, ms, bps) in numerical answers — KTU awards marks for this.
- ✅ Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not raw `|`) for absolute-value notation inside LaTeX.
- ✅ Draw **neat block diagrams** for any 7-mark "Explain with diagram" question — missing diagrams cost 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Security ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **IoT Security** is the umbrella discipline that encompasses the strategies, mechanisms, protocols, cryptographic primitives, and architectural controls used to protect the **confidentiality, integrity, availability, and authenticity** of data, devices, networks, and services within an Internet of Things ecosystem. Per the KTU 2024 Scheme, it is treated as a *cross-layer concern* spanning the Perception, Network, and Application layers, and addresses the unique resource-constrained nature of IoT nodes. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (OEITT723 Module 4):** Security in IoT covers *IoT security threats, attack surfaces, security requirements, cryptographic primitives (symmetric, asymmetric, hashing), and lightweight security protocols* designed for constrained devices (e.g., 6LoWPAN, CoAP security, DTLS). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Building" Imagine a large, smart office building with **thousands of tiny sensors** in every room: temperature sensors, motion detectors, smart locks, and cameras. Each sensor is like a *tenant* with limited memory and processing power. Now, picture three classes of troublemakers: 1. **Eavesdroppers** listening in on conversations between sensors and the central server. 2. **Impersonators** pretending to be legitimate sensors and feeding false data (e.g., "the boiler is at 25 °C" when it is actually 150 °C). 3. **Vandals** shutting down the entire network with a single attack. IoT Security is the **security guard, the locked doors, the encrypted phone lines, the visitor logs, and the surveillance cameras** — all working together to keep this building (the IoT ecosystem) safe. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Security Objectives — The CIA Triad (Extended for IoT):** > - **C**onfidentiality — Only authorized parties can read sensor data. > - **I**ntegrity — Data is not tampered with in transit. > - **A**vailability — The system remains operational (resilient against DoS). > - **A**uthenticity — Devices/users are who they claim to be. > - **N**on-Repudiation — Senders cannot deny having sent a message. ### 1.3 Why Traditional Security is Insufficient IoT nodes are fundamentally different from traditional computing endpoints: | Constraint | Traditional Device | Typical IoT Node | |---|---|---| | RAM | 4 – 32 GB | **4 – 256 KB** | | CPU Power | Multi-GHz, 64-bit | **8 – 48 MHz, 8/16-bit** | | Battery | Mains-powered or days | **Months to years on coin cell** | | Connectivity | TCP/IP, HTTP/TCP | **6LoWPAN, CoAP, RPL, IEEE 802.15.4** | | Cryptographic Support | Full AES-256, RSA-2048 | **AES-128, ECC-160, lightweight hash** | This forces the design of **lightweight cryptography** and **elliptic-curve based** schemes (ECC) over classical RSA. ### 1.4 Key Security Terminology - **Threat** — A potential event that could compromise security (e.g., a malware). - **Vulnerability** — A weakness in the system that can be exploited (e.g., default password). - **Attack** — The actual execution of a threat against a vulnerability. - **Attack Surface** — The sum of all possible points of unauthorized entry. - **Risk** — The probability × impact of a successful attack. - **Trust** — The established confidence in an entity's identity and behavior. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three-Layer IoT Security Architecture IoT Security must be analyzed layer-by-layer because each tier has *unique* attack vectors: **Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Edge / Sensor) Security** - Goal: Protect physical devices, sensor data acquisition, and tag-reader communication. - Threats: Node tampering, RFID skimming/cloning, side-channel attacks, physical destruction. - Controls: Tamper-resistant hardware, secure boot, physical unclonable functions (PUFs), lightweight encryption on the device. **Layer 2 — Network Layer (Transport) Security** - Goal: Secure data in transit across constrained networks (6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT). - Threats: Eavesdropping, MITM, routing attacks (sinkhole, wormhole, Sybil), DoS. - Controls: DTLS, IPsec, TLS 1.3, MAC-layer encryption (802.15.4 security modes). **Layer 3 — Application Layer (Cloud / User) Security** - Goal: Secure APIs, user data, analytics, firmware updates (OTA). - Threats: Data breaches, SQL injection, insecure APIs, malicious firmware. - Controls: OAuth 2.0, JWT tokens, secure OTA (signed firmware), API gateways, WAF. ### 2.2 Cryptographic Primitives — The Building Blocks #### A. Symmetric Key Cryptography Same key used for encryption and decryption. **Fast, low memory footprint** — ideal for IoT. - **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** — Block cipher, 128-bit blocks, key sizes 128/192/256 bits. - **DES / 3DES** — Legacy, 56-bit key (broken), replaced by AES. - **Lightweight ciphers for IoT:** PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, ChaCha20. #### B. Asymmetric Key Cryptography Different keys: public (encrypt/verify) and private (decrypt/sign). **Computationally expensive** but solves key distribution. - **RSA** — Based on integer factorization; 1024/2048/4096-bit keys. - **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)** — Provides equivalent security with **much smaller keys** (preferred for IoT). - **Diffie-Hellman (DH/ECDH)** — Key exchange. #### C. Hash Functions One-way functions producing a fixed-length digest. Used for integrity and authentication. - **SHA-1** (160-bit, broken), **SHA-2 family** (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512), **SHA-3 (Keccak)**. - **Lightweight hashes for IoT:** PHOTON, SPONGENT, QUARK. #### D. Message Authentication Codes (MAC) Combine hashing with a secret key to provide integrity + authentication. - **HMAC** (Hash-based MAC), **CMAC**, **GMAC**. ### 2.3 Lightweight Cryptography — Why IoT Needs Special Algorithms A standard 2048-bit RSA signature on an 8-bit microcontroller can take **> 10 seconds** and exceed available RAM. Lightweight cryptography provides equivalent security at a fraction of the resource cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **NIST Standards for Lightweight Cryptography (Finalized 2023):** > - **Ascon** — Selected as the primary standard for lightweight AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) and hashing, ideal for IoT. > - It uses **~2.5 KB of state**, runs on hardware as small as **2,500 gate equivalents**, and offers both AEAD and hash functionality. ### 2.4 Common IoT Security Protocols | Protocol | Layer | Purpose | Key Mechanism | |---|---|---|---| | **DTLS** | Transport (UDP-based) | Secures CoAP | TLS over UDP + cookie-based handshake | | **TLS 1.3** | Transport (TCP) | HTTPS-style security | Modern cipher suites, 1-RTT handshake | | **IPsec** | Network | End-to-end IP security | AH + ESP headers | | **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Security** | Link | Frame encryption/integrity | AES-CCM with keys | | **OAuth 2.0 / JWT** | Application | Authorization | Token-based access | | **X.509 / PKI** | Cross-layer | Identity certificates | Asymmetric signatures | ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are *Board favorites* — expect derivations or numerical substitution questions in the **14-mark** section. | Symbol / Concept | Formula / Relationship | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | Symmetric security (bits) | $S_{\text{sym}} = n$ where $n$ is the AES key length | $128$ or $256$ bits | | Asymmetric key size for equivalent security | $k_{\text{asym}} \approx 6 \cdot k_{\text{sym}}$ to $8 \cdot k_{\text{sym}}$ | Empirical NIST mapping | | ECC key size vs RSA | $k_{\text{ECC}} \approx \frac{1}{6} k_{\text{RSA}}$ (rough equivalent) | $256$-bit ECC $\approx 2048$-bit RSA | | RSA decryption (CRT) | $d_p = d \bmod (p-1),\ d_q = d \bmod (q-1)$ | Faster private-key operation | | Diffie-Hellman shared secret | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | $a,b$ are private; $g^a, g^b$ are public | | Birthday attack probability | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^L)}$ | $L$ = hash output bits, $n$ = attempts | | Hash collisions needed (50%) | $n \approx 1.18 \cdot 2^{L/2}$ | $2^{L/2}$ for $\sim 63\%$ | | Entropy of a password | $H = L \cdot \log_2(C)$ | $L$ = length, $C$ = character set size | | Shannon entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Bits of information per symbol | | Cipher strength (work factor) | $W = 2^k$ | $k$ = effective key bits | | ECDSA signature size | $2n$ bits | $n$ = curve order in bits | | AES block size | $128$ bits fixed | NB, not key size | | Effective attack on DES (brute force) | $2^{56}$ operations | $56$-bit key deprecated | ### 2.6 Equivalency Table — Symmetric vs Asymmetric Strength | Symmetric (AES) | ECC Key Size | RSA Key Size | |---|---|---| | $80$ bits | $163$ bits | $1024$ bits | | $112$ bits | $224$ bits | $2048$ bits | | $128$ bits | $256$ bits | $3072$ bits | | $192$ bits | $384$ bits | $7680$ bits | | $256$ bits | $512$ bits | $15360$ bits | > [!IMPORTANT] > This table is **directly from NIST SP 800-57** and is the *single most-asked* equivalency question in KTU ESE. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Grids** — Tamper-proofing smart meters against electricity theft. - **Healthcare IoT** — HIPAA-compliant transmission of patient vitals from wearables. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Protecting SCADA systems from nation-state attacks (e.g., Stuxnet). - **Autonomous Vehicles** — V2X communication must be authenticated to prevent spoofed traffic signals. - **Smart Home** — Preventing the Mirai-style botnet by enforcing unique, strong device credentials. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 RSA Key Generation — Full Derivation (KTU 14-Mark Favorite) **Goal:** Generate a public-private key pair such that encryption with the public key can only be reversed with the private key. **Step 1 — Choose two large primes.** Select $p$ and $q$, distinct, large primes (e.g., $p = 61$, $q = 53$). $$ p = 61, \quad q = 53 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the modulus $n$.** The modulus is the product of the two primes and is the *public part* of the key. $$ \begin{aligned} n &= p \times q \\ &= 61 \times 53 \\ &= 3233 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$.** For $n = p \cdot q$ with $p, q$ prime, $\phi(n)$ counts integers from $1$ to $n$ coprime to $n$. $$ \begin{aligned} \phi(n) &= (p - 1)(q - 1) \\ &= (61 - 1)(53 - 1) \\ &= 60 \times 52 \\ &= 3120 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Choose public exponent $e$.** Pick $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Common choice: $e = 65537$. $$ e = 65537 \quad \text{(standard, F4 prime)} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the private exponent $d$.** $d$ is the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$: $$ d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} $$ **Method to compute $d$ — Extended Euclidean Algorithm.** $$ \begin{aligned} 3120 &= 0 \times 65537 + 3120 \\ 65537 &= 20 \times 3120 + 3137 \\ 3120 &= 0 \times 3137 + 3120 \\ 3137 &= 1 \times 3120 + 17 \\ 3120 &= 183 \times 17 + 9 \\ 17 &= 1 \times 9 + 8 \\ 9 &= 1 \times 8 + 1 \\ 8 &= 8 \times 1 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Back-substitute to find $d$:** $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 9 - 1 \times 8 \\ &= 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) = 2 \times 9 - 17 \\ &= 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 17 \\ &= 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17 \\ &= 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times (3137 - 1 \times 3120) \\ &= 369 \times 3120 - 367 \times 3137 \\ &= 369 \times \phi(n) - 367 \times e \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ -367 \times 65537 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120} $$ $$ d = -367 \pmod{3120} = 3120 - 367 = 2753 $$ **Verification:** $$ \begin{aligned} d \cdot e \bmod \phi(n) &= 2753 \times 65537 \bmod 3120 \\ &= 180\,418\,361 \bmod 3120 \\ &= 1 \ \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Public Key:** $(e, n) = (65537, 3233)$ **Private Key:** $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$ **Step 6 — Encrypt a plaintext $M$.** $$ C = M^e \bmod n $$ **Step 7 — Decrypt ciphertext $C$.** $$ M = C^d \bmod n $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **security of RSA** rests on the difficulty of factoring $n$ into $p \cdot q$. With $2048$-bit $n$ (i.e., $p, q \approx 1024$-bit), no known classical algorithm can factor in reasonable time. Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer can break RSA in polynomial time — hence the move toward **post-quantum cryptography** (lattice-based, e.g., CRYSTALS-Kyber). --- ### 3.2 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Full Worked Example **Goal:** Allow two parties (Alice and Bob) to derive a shared secret over an insecure channel. **Public parameters (known to all):** a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$. $$ p = 23, \quad g = 5 $$ **Step 1 — Alice picks a private key $a$.** $$ a = 6 $$ **Step 2 — Bob picks a private key $b$.** $$ b = 15 $$ **Step 3 — Alice computes her public key $A$ and sends it.** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= g^a \bmod p \\ &= 5^6 \bmod 23 \\ &= 15625 \bmod 23 \\ &= 8 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Bob computes his public key $B$ and sends it.** $$ \begin{aligned} B &= g^b \bmod p \\ &= 5^{15} \bmod 23 \\ &= 30517578125 \bmod 23 \\ &= 19 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Alice computes the shared secret $K_A$.** $$ \begin{aligned} K_A &= B^a \bmod p \\ &= 19^6 \bmod 23 \\ &= 47045881 \bmod 23 \\ &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Bob computes the shared secret $K_B$.** $$ \begin{aligned} K_B &= A^b \bmod p \\ &= 8^{15} \bmod 23 \\ &= 35184372088832 \bmod 23 \\ &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** $K_A = K_B = 2$. The shared secret is established. $$ (g^a)^b \equiv (g^b)^a \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p} $$ > [!NOTE] > **MITM Vulnerability:** Standard DH is vulnerable to a Man-in-the-Middle. **Authenticated DH (ECDH + digital signatures or STS protocol)** solves this. This is a classic KTU question. --- ### 3.3 AES Round Structure — Block Cipher Internals AES-128 processes a 128-bit block in **10 rounds** of the following structure (excluding the final round): For each round $i$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State}_{i+1} &= \text{MixColumns}\left(\text{ShiftRows}\left(\text{SubBytes}\left(\text{State}_{i}\right)\right)\right) \oplus \text{RoundKey}_{i+1} \end{aligned} $$ **SubBytes** — Nonlinear byte substitution using the S-box (a 256-entry lookup table derived from $\mathbb{F}_{2^8}$ inversion). **ShiftRows** — Cyclic left-shift of the state matrix rows by 0, 1, 2, 3 bytes respectively. **MixColumns** — Linear mixing of each column over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ using a fixed polynomial $c(x) = 03x^3 + 01x^2 + 01x + 02$. **AddRoundKey** — XOR of state with the round key (derived from the original key via the **Key Schedule**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **AES Strength:** A 128-bit key has a $2^{128}$ key space, which is approximately $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ — infeasible to brute-force even at one billion keys/second, it would take $\approx 10^{22}$ years. --- ### 3.4 Birthday Attack on Hash Functions — Probability Derivation **Question:** How many attempts are needed to find a hash collision with probability $p$? **Model:** A hash function maps inputs uniformly at random into a space of $N = 2^L$ outputs. **Probability of NO collision after $n$ draws** (from probability theory): $$ P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{N - k}{N} $$ **Approximating with the exponential bound** ($N \gg n$): $$ P(\text{no collision}) \approx e^{-n^2 / (2N)} $$ **Probability of AT LEAST one collision:** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{collision}) &\approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2N)} \\ \text{where } N &= 2^L \end{aligned} $$ **For 50% probability:** $$ \begin{aligned} 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2N)} &= 0.5 \\ e^{-n^2 / (2N)} &= 0.5 \\ -n^2 / (2N) &= \ln(0.5) = -0.693 \\ n^2 &= 0.693 \times 2N = 1.386 N \\ n &= \sqrt{1.386 \cdot 2^L} = 1.18 \cdot 2^{L/2} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical result:** - For SHA-256 ($L = 256$): $n \approx 1.18 \times 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ trials. - This is the *birthday bound* — the actual work factor is **$2^{L/2}$**, not $2^L$. > [!WARNING] > **Common Exam Pitfall:** Students write $2^{256}$ work factor for SHA-256. The *correct* brute-force collision cost is $2^{128}$, because of the birthday paradox. Examiner deducts full marks if this is missed. --- ### 3.5 Entropy of an IoT Device Passphrase A typical IoT admin passphrase uses uppercase, lowercase, digits, and 10 symbols — total $C = 26 + 26 + 10 + 10 = 72$ characters. For a passphrase of length $L$: $$ H = L \cdot \log_2(C) $$ **Example:** $L = 12$ characters. $$ \begin{aligned} H &= 12 \times \log_2(72) \\ &= 12 \times 6.17 \\ &\approx 74 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ Equivalent to a 74-bit symmetric key — well below AES-128. Recommend $L \geq 22$ for parity with AES-128. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Summary | Property | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (RSA/ECC) | Hash (SHA-256) | |---|---|---|---| | **Key size (128-bit security)** | 128 bits | 3072 (RSA) / 256 (ECC) | N/A (256-bit output) | | **Speed on 8-bit MCU** | Fast (µs) | Slow (s) | Medium (ms) | | **Memory footprint** | ~1 KB code | ~5–10 KB | ~2 KB | | **Confidentiality** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Integrity** | ❌ (need MAC) | ✅ (with signatures) | ✅ Yes | | **Authentication** | ❌ (need MAC) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Key distribution** | ❌ Hard problem | ✅ Easy (public key) | N/A | | **IoT Suitability** | ✅ High | ⚠️ ECC only | ✅ High | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Three-Layer IoT Security Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB appLayer[APPLICATION LAYER]:::app cloud[Cloud Server
Big Data Analytics]:::cloud api[API Gateway]:::api oauth[OAuth 2.0 and JWT Tokens]:::auth firmware[Signed Firmware Updates OTA]:::fw netLayer[NETWORK LAYER]:::net tls[TLS 1.3 over TCP]:::proto dtls[DTLS over UDP and CoAP]:::proto ipsec[IPsec End to End]:::proto macsec[IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Security AES CCM]:::proto perLayer[PERCEPTION LAYER]:::per secureboot[Secure Boot and Trusted Firmware]:::boot puf[Physical Unclonable Functions PUF]:::puf tamper[Tamper Resistant Hardware]:::tamper edgecrypt[Lightweight Encryption on Device]:::edge appLayer --> cloud appLayer --> api appLayer --> oauth appLayer --> firmware netLayer --> tls netLayer --> dtls netLayer --> ipsec netLayer --> macsec perLayer --> secureboot perLayer --> puf perLayer --> tamper perLayer --> edgecrypt classDef app fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef net fill:#2d5a2d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef per fill:#5a2d2d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef cloud fill:#4a4a8f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef api fill:#6a4a8f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef auth fill:#8f4a6a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef fw fill:#8f6a4a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef proto fill:#3a6a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef boot fill:#7a3a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef puf fill:#7a5a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef tamper fill:#5a3a7a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef edge fill:#3a7a5a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 IoT Threat Model — Attack Vector Map ```mermaid flowchart LR attacker[ADVERSARY]:::atk phy[Physical Attacks
Tampering, Side Channel]:::threat rf[RF Jamming
Node Capture]:::threat mitm[MITM in Transit
Eavesdropping]:::threat replay[Replay Attack]:::threat sybil[Sybil Attack
Fake Identities]:::threat sink[Sinkhole and Wormhole]:::threat ddos[Denial of Service]:::threat malware[Malware Injection
Firmware Tampering]:::threat cloud[Cloud Data Breach]:::threat api[A P I Exploitation]:::threat attacker --> phy attacker --> rf attacker --> mitm attacker --> replay attacker --> sybil attacker --> sink attacker --> ddos attacker --> malware attacker --> cloud attacker --> api phy --> dev1[Sensor Node]:::dev rf --> dev1 mitm --> dev2[Gateway]:::dev replay --> dev2 sybil --> dev2 sink --> dev2 ddos --> dev2 malware --> dev3[Edge Server]:::dev cloud --> dev4[Cloud Backend]:::dev api --> dev4 classDef atk fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef threat fill:#b22222,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef dev fill:#2f4f4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 DTLS Handshake for Constrained IoT (Block Flow) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as IoT Client
(Sensor Node) participant S as CoAP Server
(Gateway) participant K as Key Store
(PSK or PKI) Note over C,S: Phase 1 - Hello with Cookie C->>S: ClientHello (random, cipher suites, no cookie) S->>C: HelloVerifyRequest (cookie) C->>S: ClientHello (with cookie) Note over C,S: Phase 2 - Key Exchange S->>C: ServerHello, ServerKeyExchange, ServerHelloDone C->>S: ClientKeyExchange, ChangeCipherSpec, Finished S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec, Finished Note over C,S: Phase 3 - Secure Data Transfer C->>S: Application Data (encrypted, MACed) S->>C: Application Data (encrypted, MACed) Note over C,K: PSK lookup or certificate validation S->>K: Retrieve pre-shared key or verify cert K->>S: Key material returned ``` ### 4.4 Lightweight Cryptography Suitability Map ```mermaid flowchart TB start[Select Cryptographic Primitive]:::root needConf[Need Confidentiality?]:::q needInt[Need Integrity Only?]:::q needBoth[Need Both? AEAD]:::q aesUse[Use AES 128 in CCM Mode]:::rec aeadUse[Use ASCON AEAD NIST 2023 Standard]:::rec hashUse[Use SHA 256 or Lightweight PHOTON]:::rec pubKey[Asymmetric Required?]:::q eccUse[Use ECC secp256r1 or Ed25519]:::rec rsaAvoid[Avoid RSA in Constrained Devices]:::warn pufUse[Use PUFs for Device Identity]:::rec start --> needConf start --> needInt start --> needBoth needConf --> pubKey needConf --> aesUse needInt --> hashUse needBoth --> aeadUse pubKey --> eccUse pubKey --> rsaAvoid start --> pufUse classDef root fill:#003366,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef q fill:#666633,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef rec fill:#2d5a2d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef warn fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List and briefly explain the three main security requirements in IoT (CIA Triad). Why is a fourth requirement, "Authenticity", often added for IoT systems?** **Model Answer:** The **CIA Triad** forms the foundation of information security in any system, including IoT: 1. **Confidentiality:** Ensures that sensor data, control commands, and personal information are accessible only to *authorized* entities. In IoT, this prevents adversaries from learning, for example, a patient's heart rate from a wearable. 2. **Integrity:** Guarantees that the data has not been altered, deleted, or replayed during transmission. Critical in industrial IoT where a tampered "boiler temperature" reading could cause physical damage. 3. **Availability:** Ensures that the IoT system remains operational when needed. Mitigated through redundancy, rate limiting, and DoS-resistant protocols. A **fourth requirement — Authenticity** — is added because IoT systems frequently have *unattended, low-cost devices* (e.g., deployed in a field) that are easy to clone or impersonate. Authenticity ensures that a node claiming to be "Sensor #42" is genuinely Sensor #42, preventing **Sybil attacks** and **spoofing**. Without authenticity, confidentiality and integrity cannot be meaningfully enforced in a multi-node system. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Do NOT write "CIA is enough for IoT" — KTU examiners explicitly test whether students know that **authenticity and non-repudiation** are *added* for IoT. Missing this costs full marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption? Give one example of each, and state which is preferred for resource-constrained IoT devices.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Symmetric | Asymmetric | |---|---|---| | Keys | Same secret key for encrypt and decrypt | Public key + private key (mathematically linked) | | Speed | **Very fast** (suitable for bulk data) | **Slow** (hundreds of times slower) | | Key size for 128-bit security | 128 bits | 3072 (RSA) or 256 (ECC) | | Example | **AES-128** | **RSA-2048, ECC-256** | | Key distribution problem | Hard (must share secret) | Solved (public key is, by definition, public) | For **resource-constrained IoT nodes** (e.g., 8-bit MCUs with < 64 KB RAM), **symmetric encryption (AES-128) is preferred** because it has a small code footprint (~1 KB) and can encrypt at high speed. Asymmetric primitives like RSA are too heavy; if asymmetric functionality is *strictly required* (e.g., for key exchange or digital signatures), **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** is chosen because a 256-bit ECC key provides the same security as a 3072-bit RSA key, drastically reducing computation and bandwidth. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — Cryptography & Key Exchange **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the RSA algorithm. Generate the public and private keys using $p = 17$ and $q = 11$, and encrypt the plaintext $M = 88$. Show all steps. **(b) (7 Marks)** What is the Diffie-Hellman key exchange protocol? Explain why plain DH is vulnerable to a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack, and how it can be mitigated. **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Choose primes.** $$ p = 17, \quad q = 11 $$ **Step 2 — Compute modulus $n$.** $$ n = p \times q = 17 \times 11 = 187 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Euler's totient.** $$ \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 16 \times 10 = 160 $$ **Step 4 — Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, 160) = 1$.** $$ e = 7 \quad (\text{since } \gcd(7, 160) = 1) $$ **Step 5 — Compute private exponent $d$ using $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$.** Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm: $$ \begin{aligned} 160 &= 22 \times 7 + 6 \\ 7 &= 1 \times 6 + 1 \\ 6 &= 6 \times 1 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ Back-substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 7 - 1 \times 6 \\ &= 7 - 1 \times (160 - 22 \times 7) \\ &= 23 \times 7 - 1 \times 160 \end{aligned} $$ So $d = 23$. **Verification:** $$ 7 \times 23 = 161 = 160 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{160} \ \checkmark $$ **Public Key:** $(e, n) = (7, 187)$ **Private Key:** $(d, n) = (23, 187)$ **Step 6 — Encrypt $M = 88$.** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= M^e \bmod n \\ &= 88^7 \bmod 187 \end{aligned} $$ Using modular exponentiation: $$ \begin{aligned} 88^1 \bmod 187 &= 88 \\ 88^2 \bmod 187 &= 7744 \bmod 187 = 7744 - 41 \times 187 = 7744 - 7667 = 77 \\ 88^4 \bmod 187 &= 77^2 \bmod 187 = 5929 \bmod 187 = 5929 - 31 \times 187 = 5929 - 5797 = 132 \\ 88^7 = 88^4 \times 88^2 \times 88^1 \bmod 187 &= 132 \times 77 \times 88 \bmod 187 \end{aligned} $$ Compute step by step: $$ \begin{aligned} 132 \times 77 \bmod 187 &= 10164 \bmod 187 \\ &= 10164 - 54 \times 187 \\ &= 10164 - 10098 = 66 \\ 66 \times 88 \bmod 187 &= 5808 \bmod 187 \\ &= 5808 - 31 \times 187 \\ &= 5808 - 5797 = 11 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{C = 11} $$ **Step 7 — Decrypt $C = 11$ to verify:** $$ \begin{aligned} M &= C^d \bmod n = 11^{23} \bmod 187 \\ &= 11^{23} \bmod 187 \end{aligned} $$ Modular reduction: $$ \begin{aligned} 11^1 &= 11 \\ 11^2 &= 121 \\ 11^4 &= 121^2 \bmod 187 = 14641 \bmod 187 = 14641 - 78 \times 187 = 14641 - 14586 = 55 \\ 11^8 &= 55^2 \bmod 187 = 3025 \bmod 187 = 3025 - 16 \times 187 = 3025 - 2992 = 33 \\ 11^{16} &= 33^2 \bmod 187 = 1089 \bmod 187 = 1089 - 5 \times 187 = 1089 - 935 = 154 \\ 11^{23} = 11^{16} \times 11^4 \times 11^2 \times 11^1 &= 154 \times 55 \times 121 \times 11 \bmod 187 \end{aligned} $$ Compute: $$ \begin{aligned} 154 \times 55 \bmod 187 &= 8470 \bmod 187 = 8470 - 45 \times 187 = 8470 - 8415 = 55 \\ 55 \times 121 \bmod 187 &= 6655 \bmod 187 = 6655 - 35 \times 187 = 6655 - 6545 = 110 \\ 110 \times 11 \bmod 187 &= 1210 \bmod 187 = 1210 - 6 \times 187 = 1210 - 1122 = 88 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{M = 88} \ \checkmark $$ **Model Solution — Part (b):** The **Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange** allows two parties to derive a common secret over an insecure channel. Public parameters: a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$. Each party picks a private random ($a, b$), exchanges public values ($g^a \bmod p$, $g^b \bmod p$), and both compute $g^{ab} \bmod p$ as the shared secret. **Plain DH is vulnerable to a Man-in-the-Middle attack:** an attacker Mallory can intercept Alice's $g^a$ and Bob's $g^b$, substitute her own $g^{m_a}$ with Alice and $g^{m_b}$ with Bob. Alice ends up sharing a key with Mallory (not Bob), and Bob shares a key with Mallory. Mallory can read and modify all traffic. **Mitigation — Authenticated DH variants:** 1. **ECDH + Digital Signatures:** Each party signs its public key with a long-term private key (verified via certificates). Mallory cannot forge signatures. 2. **STS (Station-to-Station) Protocol:** Combines DH with public-key certificates and MACs. 3. **DTLS 1.3 / TLS 1.3:** Both enforce server (and optionally client) certificate-based authentication, eliminating the MITM gap. 4. **Pre-Shared Keys (PSK-DH):** In constrained IoT, a factory-burned PSK authenticates the exchange. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — IoT Security Protocols & Lightweight Crypto **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the unique security challenges in IoT as compared to traditional internet security. List and explain any **four** specific attack types that target IoT systems. **(b) (7 Marks)** Compare AES-128, RSA-2048, and ECC-256 in terms of security strength, key size, and IoT suitability. Why is **lightweight cryptography** essential, and name the **NIST standard** for lightweight AEAD. **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Unique Challenges of IoT Security:** 1. **Resource Constraints:** IoT devices often have < 256 KB RAM, < 1 MB flash, and run on coin-cell batteries. Classical 2048-bit RSA is infeasible. 2. **Heterogeneity:** Diverse hardware, OS, and protocols (Zigbee, LoRa, BLE, Wi-Fi) — no single security policy fits all. 3. **Scale:** Millions of devices in a deployment — manual patching is impossible; OTA updates must be cryptographically signed. 4. **Physical Exposure:** Devices in the field are easy to capture, tamper with, and reverse-engineer. 5. **Unattended Operation:** Devices may be deployed for years without human intervention. 6. **Privacy Sensitivity:** Wearables, smart homes, and healthcare devices collect intimate data. **Four Specific IoT Attacks:** 1. **Node Capture Attack:** Adversary physically captures a sensor node, extracts cryptographic keys from its memory (e.g., via JTAG or side-channel), and impersonates it. Mitigation: PUFs, tamper-resistant packaging, key erasure on tamper. 2. **Sybil Attack:** A single malicious node creates multiple fake identities to subvert reputation systems, vote multiple times in distributed decisions, or disrupt routing. Mitigation: identity certification by a trusted authority, resource testing. 3. **Sinkhole Attack:** A compromised node advertises attractive routing metrics and attracts all traffic through itself — then drops, alters, or eavesdrops on packets. Mitigation: rank verification in RPL, multi-path routing. 4. **Side-Channel Attack:** Attacker observes power consumption, EM emissions, or timing of cryptographic operations to extract the secret key. Mitigation: constant-time algorithms, masking, noise injection. *(Other valid answers: Replay, Wormhole, RFID Skimming, Mirai-style Botnet, Firmware Tampering.)* **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Comparison Table:** | Property | AES-128 | RSA-2048 | ECC-256 | |---|---|---|---| | Cryptographic Type | Symmetric (block cipher) | Asymmetric (integer factorization) | Asymmetric (elliptic curve) | | Key Size | 128 bits | 2048 bits | 256 bits | | Effective Security | 128 bits | ~112 bits | ~128 bits | | Operations/sec on 8-bit MCU | Thousands | < 1 | Tens | | RAM Footprint | ~1 KB | ~10 KB | ~5 KB | | IoT Suitability | ✅ Excellent (most preferred for bulk data) | ❌ Avoid | ✅ Good (preferred when asymmetric is needed) | | Use Case | Encrypting sensor payloads | Legacy certificate systems, key exchange | Modern IoT: ECDH, ECDSA, Ed25519 | **Why Lightweight Cryptography is Essential:** Classical cryptographic primitives were designed for servers and PCs. IoT nodes have: - **Tight memory budgets** (sometimes < 16 KB total flash), - **Severe energy constraints** (must run for years on a coin cell), - **Real-time requirements** (control loops in IIoT cannot tolerate multi-second crypto delays). Lightweight algorithms reduce **gate count**, **code size**, and **energy per bit** while preserving a provable security level (typically 80–128 bits). **NIST Lightweight Standard:** **Ascon** — selected by NIST in **February 2023** as the standard for **lightweight authenticated encryption (AEAD)** and **lightweight hashing**. It uses only ~2.5 KB of state, fits in ~2,500 gate equivalents, and is well-suited to RFID tags, sensor nodes, and medical implants. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(b):** > 1. **Do not confuse lightweight cryptography with "weak" cryptography.** It is rigorously analyzed and standardized — *not* a shortcut. > 2. **Do not state "DES" or "MD5" as IoT-recommended** — both are broken. > 3. **Many students write "Ascon is from 2010" — incorrect.** It was *submitted* in 2014, *finalized* as NIST standard in **2023**. Examiner checks the year. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **CIA + A + N** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, Non-Repudiation) are the six pillars of IoT security. - **AES-128** is the **workhorse** for IoT confidentiality and integrity (in CCM/GCM modes). - **ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072** in security strength — prefer ECC in constrained devices. - **SHA-256 collision resistance** is $2^{128}$, not $2^{256}$ — the **birthday bound** is $1.18 \cdot 2^{L/2}$. - **RSA modulus $n = p \cdot q$; $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$; $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.** - **DH shared secret:** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$, with public values $A = g^a \bmod p$, $B = g^b \bmod p$. - **Three security layers:** Perception (PUFs, secure boot, AES-CCM), Network (DTLS, IPsec, 802.15.4 security), Application (OAuth, JWT, signed firmware). - **Top IoT attacks to memorize:** Node Capture, Sybil, Sinkhole, Wormhole, Replay, Side-Channel, MITM, DoS/DDoS, RFID Cloning, Firmware Tampering. - **Lightweight crypto standards:** Ascon (NIST 2023) for AEAD and hashing; PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, ChaCha20 for stream/block operations. - **DTLS** is TLS adapted for UDP and is the canonical security wrapper for **CoAP** in 6LoWPAN networks. - **Entropy formula:** $H = L \cdot \log_2(C)$ — for IoT passphrases, target $\geq 128$ bits of entropy. - **Public-key cryptography solves key distribution**, while symmetric cryptography is **fast** — IoT systems use **both**: ECC for handshake, AES for data. - **Post-quantum risk:** Both RSA and ECC are vulnerable to Shor's algorithm — **lattice-based crypto (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium)** is the migration path. - **Mirai botnet (2016):** Hijacked > 600,000 IoT devices using default credentials — established hard-coded-password prohibition as a *battery-included* security requirement. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for IoT Attacks: "P-S-S-R-D"** → **P**hysical tampering, **S**ybil, **S**inkhole, **R**eplay, **D**oS. Covers ~80% of KTU questions.

topics.title: IoT Security Challenges: Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT Security Challenges: Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Security Threats** refer to any potential malicious action, circumstance, or event that can compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of an IoT system, the data it processes, or the underlying communication infrastructure. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, an IoT threat is formally defined as *"any condition that has the potential to cause harm to an IoT asset, resource, or the information it carries, originating from physical, network, application, or data-layer vulnerabilities."*

An **IoT threat** is distinct from an *IoT vulnerability* (the weakness itself) and an *IoT attack* (the actual exploitation). The threat represents the *possibility* of harm, the capability of an adversary, and the *intent* to exploit a weak point in the IoT architecture.

### 1.2 Key Threat Vocabulary (KTU Terminology)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Threat** | A potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system that can be exploited by a threat. |
| **Risk** | The probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by its impact. |
| **Attack** | The actual attempt to compromise a system by exploiting a vulnerability. |
| **Attack Surface** | The sum of all possible points (hardware, software, network) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data. |
| **Threat Vector** | The path or means by which an attacker gains unauthorized access. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Smart Home as a Bank Vault**
>
> Imagine your IoT-enabled smart home is a **high-security bank vault**.
> - The **smart lock** on your front door is the *perimeter firewall*.
> - The **CCTV cameras** are your *network intrusion detection sensors*.
> - The **vault room** (where your valuables sit) is your *IoT cloud database*.
> - The **guard at the gate** is your *authentication system*.
>
> Now, an **IoT threat** is essentially any *plausible method* a thief could use to break in — picking the lock, bribing the guard, tunneling through the walls, or even stealing the CCTV footage to disable it. The threat is the *strategy*, not the actual break-in. The **vulnerability** is a *flaw* in the lock design, and the **attack** is the thief *actually picking the lock*.
>
> Because a smart home has **dozens of entry points** (smart bulb firmware, voice assistant microphone, Wi-Fi router, smart fridge, etc.), the *attack surface* is enormous compared to a traditional house with just one door. This is precisely why IoT threats are uniquely dangerous.

### 1.4 Why IoT is a High-Value Threat Target

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **four structural reasons** why IoT ecosystems are uniquely vulnerable:

1. **Resource Constraints**: Most IoT devices (sensors, RFID tags) run on low-power microcontrollers (e.g., **ESP8266**, **Arduino Uno** with only **2 KB RAM** and **32 KB Flash**), making strong encryption like **AES-256** or **TLS 1.3** computationally infeasible.

2. **Heterogeneity**: An IoT system integrates devices running different protocols (**MQTT, CoAP, ZigBee, BLE, LoRaWAN**), creating inter-protocol trust gaps that attackers can exploit.

3. **Physical Accessibility**: Unlike servers in a locked datacenter, IoT devices are often deployed in public or unprotected environments (traffic cameras, smart meters, industrial sensors), making **physical tampering** a real threat.

4. **Massive Scale**: A single **Mirai botnet** attack in 2016 compromised over **600,000 IoT devices** to launch a **1.2 Tbps DDoS attack** on Dyn DNS — demonstrating how threats scale exponentially in IoT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The *Mirai Botnet* is a compulsory real-world case study. Remember the magic numbers: **~600,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps attack volume, October 2016, target = Dyn DNS provider, source code = public on GitHub**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CIA Triad (Foundational Threat Model)

Every IoT threat fundamentally violates **one or more pillars** of the CIA Triad. KTU 2024 expects students to map each threat to the CIA pillar it attacks.

$$ \text{Security Threat} = f(\text{Confidentiality}, \text{Integrity}, \text{Availability}) $$

- **Confidentiality (C)**: *Disclosure* threats — eavesdropping, sniffing, data leakage.
- **Integrity (I)**: *Modification* threats — data tampering, man-in-the-middle injection, firmware modification.
- **Availability (A)**: *Disruption* threats — DDoS, jamming, physical destruction.

### 2.2 Classification of IoT Threats (4-Layer Architecture)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recommends the **4-Layer IoT Architecture** for threat classification. Each layer has a unique threat profile.

| Layer | Component | Primary Threats | CIA Pillar Violated |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Perception Layer** | Sensors, Actuators, RFID | Physical tampering, node capture, signal jamming, side-channel attacks | A, C |
| **Network Layer** | Gateways, Routers, Cloud links | Eavesdropping, MITM, DoS, routing attacks, replay attacks | C, I, A |
| **Middleware Layer** | Cloud platform, Data storage | SQL injection, data breach, account hijacking, insider attack | C, I |
| **Application Layer** | User apps, Web dashboards | Phishing, cross-site scripting (XSS), authentication bypass, malicious firmware updates | C, I, A |

### 2.3 Top 10 IoT Threats (KTU High-Priority List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **10 threats** are mandatory for KTU 2024 Module 4. At least **one Part A question** (3 marks) and one **Part B sub-question** (7 marks) will be drawn from this list.

#### 2.3.1 Physical Threats
- **Node Capture / Physical Tampering**: Attacker physically accesses a sensor node, extracts cryptographic keys via JTAG/UART debug ports, or replaces the node with a malicious clone.
- **Side-Channel Attacks**: Power consumption, electromagnetic emanations, or timing analysis to extract secret keys from smart cards or RFID tags.

#### 2.3.2 Network & Communication Threats
- **Eavesdropping (Passive Sniffing)**: Using tools like **Wireshark** to capture unencrypted MQTT or ZigBee traffic and extract sensor data.
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)**: Attacker secretly relays and possibly alters communication between two IoT devices (e.g., a smart thermostat and a server).
- **Replay Attack**: Captures a valid authentication message and replays it later to gain unauthorized access.
- **Routing Attack (Sinkhole / Wormhole)**: Malicious node advertises itself as the best route, capturing all traffic passing through it.

#### 2.3.3 Software & Application Threats
- **Firmware Hijacking**: Injecting malicious code into the OTA (Over-The-Air) update process.
- **Malware & Botnets**: Infecting thousands of devices to form a botnet for coordinated attacks (e.g., **Mirai, BrickerBot, Hajime**).
- **Injection Attacks**: SQL/NoSQL injection in IoT cloud dashboards; command injection in firmware APIs.

#### 2.3.4 Data & Cryptographic Threats
- **Cryptanalysis**: Breaking weak encryption (e.g., brute-forcing **WEP**, exploiting hardcoded passwords).
- **Privacy Leakage**: Inferring user behavior from sensor data (e.g., smart meter data reveals when you shower or sleep).
- **Insecure Default Credentials**: Many devices ship with `admin:admin` — a huge attack surface.

#### 2.3.5 Availability Threats
- **DoS / DDoS Attack**: Flooding the IoT gateway or cloud server with requests, exhausting bandwidth, CPU, or memory.
- **Jamming Attack**: Transmitting strong radio noise on the wireless channel (**2.4 GHz, 868 MHz**) to disrupt ZigBee/LoRa communication.
- **Battery Exhaustion Attack**: Forcing a node to repeatedly transmit, draining its battery in hours instead of years (sleep deprivation attack).

### 2.4 Threat Modeling Framework: STRIDE

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIDE** is the **gold-standard** threat modeling framework developed by **Microsoft** and is part of the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each letter represents a category of threat that an IoT system must defend against.

| Letter | Threat Category | Definition | Example in IoT |
|--------|----------------|------------|----------------|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Impersonating another device or user | Fake sensor node sends false temperature reading |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Modifying data or code in transit/storage | Altering firmware OTA update payload |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Denying an action was performed | Sensor claims it never sent faulty data |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Exposing confidential data | Smart camera feed leaked to internet |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Making the system unavailable | Flooding gateway with MQTT CONNECT packets |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Gaining higher access than authorized | User with "read" privilege gets "admin" via API exploit |

### 2.5 Risk Quantification Formula (High-Yield)

KTU 2024 expects students to be able to compute a **Risk Score** for IoT threats using the standard formula:

$$ \text{Risk} = \text{Probability (Likelihood)} \times \text{Impact} $$

In quantitative form, this is often expressed as:

$$ R = P \times I \times V $$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk Score (unitless, range 1–25 or 1–100 depending on scale)
- $P$ = Probability of threat occurrence (1 = rare, 5 = almost certain)
- $I$ = Impact severity (1 = negligible, 5 = catastrophic)
- $V$ = Vulnerability exposure factor (1 = well-patched, 5 = completely exposed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Matrix Interpretation (for KTU theory answers):**
> - **Low Risk**: $1 \le R \le 4$ — Accept, monitor
> - **Medium Risk**: $5 \le R \le 12$ — Mitigate within 6 months
> - **High Risk**: $13 \le R \le 19$ — Mitigate immediately
> - **Critical Risk**: $20 \le R \le 25$ — Cease operation until fixed

### 2.6 Attack Tree Model (KTU Theory Favorite)

An **attack tree** is a hierarchical diagram where the **root node** = attacker's goal, and **branches** = ways to achieve it. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to construct a 2-level attack tree for any given IoT threat scenario.

For example, the goal **"Compromise Smart Home"** branches into:
- **Sub-goal 1**: Compromise Wi-Fi router
- **Sub-goal 2**: Compromise IoT device directly
- **Sub-goal 3**: Compromise cloud account

Each sub-goal further branches into leaf-level *attack techniques* (brute force, default password, firmware exploit, etc.).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

IoT threat analysis is not academic — it is **mission-critical** in:
- **Healthcare IoT**: Pacemaker hacking (FDA recalled **465,000 pacemakers** in 2017 due to firmware vulnerability).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Stuxnet destroyed **1,000 Iranian centrifuges** in 2010 by targeting PLC firmware.
- **Smart Cities**: Traffic light manipulation by adversaries.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Sensor spoofing (e.g., fooling a Tesla's lane detection with painted road lines).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Threat-to-Layer-to-Defense Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important analytical matrix** for KTU 2024 Module 4. Memorize the *mapping between threat, attack surface, and mitigation*. Questions worth 7–14 marks are often structured around this matrix.

| # | Threat | Attack Surface | Violated CIA | Impact Level | Primary Mitigation |
|---|--------|----------------|--------------|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Eavesdropping | Wireless channel (ZigBee, BLE) | Confidentiality | High | AES-128 link-layer encryption |
| 2 | MITM | TLS/SSL handshake | Confidentiality, Integrity | Critical | Mutual TLS, certificate pinning |
| 3 | DDoS | Gateway / Cloud API | Availability | Critical | Rate limiting, anycast, scrubbing |
| 4 | Node Tampering | Physical access to sensor | Integrity, Confidentiality | High | Tamper-proof enclosures, PUFs |
| 5 | Firmware Hijack | OTA update channel | Integrity, Availability | Critical | Signed firmware, secure boot |
| 6 | Replay Attack | Authentication tokens | Authentication, Integrity | Medium | Nonces, timestamps, session expiry |
| 7 | Botnet Infection | Default credentials | All three | Critical | Unique passwords, auto-update |
| 8 | Jamming | Radio frequency band | Availability | High | Frequency hopping, spread spectrum |
| 9 | Battery Exhaustion | Wireless transmission | Availability | Medium | Sleep scheduling, energy-aware routing |
| 10 | Routing (Sinkhole) | RPL/6LoWPAN network | Confidentiality, Integrity | High | Trust-based routing, RPL security |
| 11 | SQL Injection | Cloud database | Confidentiality, Integrity | High | Parameterized queries, WAF |
| 12 | Privacy Leakage | Sensor data streams | Confidentiality | Medium | Data anonymization, differential privacy |

### 3.2 Derivation: Computing the Risk Score for an IoT Threat

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):**
*"A smart home deployment has 200 devices. Historical data shows that a DDoS attack on the cloud gateway occurs with probability P = 3/100 per month. Each successful attack causes a downtime of 6 hours, affecting 200 users, with an estimated business loss of ₹5 lakh per hour. Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)."*

**Step 1: Identify the Standard Risk Quantification Equation**

$$ \text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} $$

Where:
- **ALE** = Annualized Loss Expectancy (₹/year)
- **SLE** = Single Loss Expectancy (₹ per incident)
- **ARO** = Annualized Rate of Occurrence (incidents/year)

**Step 2: Calculate the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**

$$ \text{SLE} = \text{Downtime (hours)} \times \text{Loss per hour} $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Downtime} &= 6 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Loss per hour} &= \text{₹ } 5,00,000 \\
\text{SLE} &= 6 \times 5,00,000 \\
\text{SLE} &= \text{₹ } 30,00,000 \text{ (Thirty Lakhs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each successful DDoS incident costs the company exactly thirty lakh rupees in direct user-facing losses. This figure represents the *single event impact*.

**Step 3: Convert Monthly Probability to Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)**

The threat has a probability of 3/100 = 0.03 incidents **per month**.
To annualize, multiply by 12 months:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARO} &= P_{\text{monthly}} \times 12 \\
\text{ARO} &= 0.03 \times 12 \\
\text{ARO} &= 0.36 \text{ incidents per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** On average, the company should expect 0.36 DDoS incidents annually — i.e., one incident roughly every 33 months.

**Step 4: Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
\text{ALE} &= 30,00,000 \times 0.36 \\
\text{ALE} &= \text{₹ } 10,80,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The company should budget at least **₹ 10.8 lakh per year** for DDoS mitigation (WAF, scrubbing service, redundancy) to make the *cost of defense* less than the *expected annual loss*.

**Step 5: Decision Threshold**

If a mitigation costs **₹ 6 lakh/year**, the mitigation is **justified** because:
$$ \text{Cost of Defense (₹ 6L)} < \text{ALE (₹ 10.8L)} $$

The **net savings** per year = ALE − Defense Cost:
$$ \text{Savings} = 10,80,000 - 6,00,000 = \text{₹ } 4,80,000 \text{ per year} $$

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Estimating Botnet Scale

**Problem Statement:**
*"Each compromised IoT device can generate X attack packets per second. The target server has a bandwidth of B Mbps. If the attacker needs to saturate the link, find the minimum number of bots N required."*

**Step 1: State the Saturation Condition**

$$ N \times X \geq B \times 10^6 \text{ packets/second} $$

**Step 2: Solve for N**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &\geq \frac{B \times 10^6}{X} \\
N_{\min} &= \left\lceil \frac{B \times 10^6}{X} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example**

If $B = 100$ Mbps and $X = 1,000$ packets/second per bot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\min} &= \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1000} \\
N_{\min} &= 100,000 \text{ bots}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** With each compromised IoT camera sending 1,000 packets/sec, the attacker needs **at least 100,000 devices** to saturate a 100 Mbps link. The Mirai botnet, with 600,000 devices, could saturate links **6× larger** — explaining its devastating 1.2 Tbps scale.

### 3.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---------|---------|--------------|
| $R = P \times I$ | Basic Risk Score | Theoretical risk analysis |
| $R = P \times I \times V$ | Weighted Risk Score | When vulnerability exposure is known |
| $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Annualized Loss Expectancy | Cost-benefit analysis of mitigation |
| $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Single Loss Expectancy | Impact of a single incident |
| $N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{B \times 10^6}{X} \right\rceil$ | Minimum botnet size | DDoS attack feasibility analysis |
| $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Payload bits}}{\text{Total bits incl. overhead}}$ | Protocol efficiency | MQTT vs CoAP comparison |
| $\text{Battery Life} = \frac{\text{Capacity (mAh)}}{\text{Avg Current Draw (mA)} \times 24}$ | Energy budget | Battery exhaustion attack analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $R = P \times I$, students often write the *product* but forget the **unit**. Risk is **dimensionless**. If you are asked for monetary risk, multiply by the **Asset Value (AV)** to get **₹**:
> $$ \text{Risk (₹)} = P \times I \times AV $$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: IoT Threat Landscape (4-Layer Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER - User Apps and Dashboards"]
        A1["Phishing Attack"]
        A2["Cross Site Scripting XSS"]
        A3["Malicious Firmware Update"]
        A4["Authentication Bypass"]
    end

    subgraph MidLayer["MIDDLEWARE LAYER - Cloud and Storage"]
        M1["SQL Injection"]
        M2["Data Breach"]
        M3["Account Hijacking"]
        M4["Insider Threat"]
    end

    subgraph NetLayer["NETWORK LAYER - Gateways and Routers"]
        N1["Eavesdropping"]
        N2["Man in the Middle MITM"]
        N3["DDoS Attack"]
        N4["Replay Attack"]
        N5["Sinkhole Routing"]
    end

    subgraph PerLayer["PERCEPTION LAYER - Sensors and Actuators"]
        P1["Physical Tampering"]
        P2["Node Capture"]
        P3["Side Channel Attack"]
        P4["Jamming Attack"]
        P5["Battery Exhaustion"]
    end

    Attacker["MALICIOUS ACTOR"] --> AppLayer
    Attacker --> MidLayer
    Attacker --> NetLayer
    Attacker --> PerLayer

    PerLayer --> NetLayer
    NetLayer --> MidLayer
    MidLayer --> AppLayer
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: STRIDE Threat Model Applied to Smart Home IoT

```mermaid
graph LR
    Goal["ATTACKER GOAL: Control Smart Home"]

    Goal --> S["S - SPOOFING"]
    Goal --> T["T - TAMPERING"]
    Goal --> R["R - REPUDIATION"]
    Goal --> I["I - INFORMATION DISCLOSURE"]
    Goal --> D["D - DENIAL OF SERVICE"]
    Goal --> E["E - ELEVATION OF PRIVILEGE"]

    S --> S1["Fake thermostat node"]
    S --> S2["Stolen smartphone credential"]

    T --> T1["Modify OTA firmware"]
    T --> T2["Alter sensor readings"]

    R --> R1["Cloud logs deleted"]
    R --> R2["Fake audit trail"]

    I --> I1["Camera feed leaked"]
    I --> I2["Smart meter data sold"]

    D --> D1["DDoS on cloud API"]
    D --> D2["ZigBee jamming"]

    E --> E1["User becomes admin via API exploit"]
    E --> E2["Bypass RBAC policy"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Attack Tree for IoT Botnet Recruitment

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root["GOAL: Build IoT Botnet of 100K Devices"]

    Root --> S1["Sub-goal 1: Discover Vulnerable Devices"]
    Root --> S2["Sub-goal 2: Compromise Devices"]
    Root --> S3["Sub-goal 3: Command and Control"]

    S1 --> L1a["Shodan search by default credentials"]
    S1 --> L1b["Scan Telnet port 23"]
    S1 --> L1c["CVE database lookup for firmware"]

    S2 --> L2a["Brute force admin:admin"]
    S2 --> L2b["Exploit unpatched CVE"]
    S2 --> L2c["Phishing firmware update link"]

    S3 --> L3a["Register C2 server on Tor"]
    S3 --> L3b["Encrypt bot-to-C2 channel"]
    S3 --> L3c["DGA for backup domains"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Risk Assessment Workflow (KTU Examiner Favorite)

```mermaid
graph TB
    Start["Step 1: Identify IoT Assets"] --> Step2["Step 2: List Potential Threats"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Map Threats to Vulnerabilities"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Step 4: Compute P x I x V Risk Score"]
    Step4 --> Step5{"Risk Score Range?"}
    Step5 -->|R less than 4| Out1["Low - Accept and Monitor"]
    Step5 -->|R between 5 and 12| Out2["Medium - Mitigate in 6 months"]
    Step5 -->|R between 13 and 19| Out3["High - Mitigate Immediately"]
    Step5 -->|R greater than 20| Out4["Critical - Halt Operation"]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Mirai Botnet Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TB
    Ph1["PHASE 1: Reconnaissance"] --> Ph2["PHASE 2: Infection via Telnet"]
    Ph2 --> Ph3["PHASE 3: Load Malware Binary"]
    Ph3 --> Ph4["PHASE 4: Report to C2 Server"]
    Ph4 --> Ph5["PHASE 5: Synchronized Attack Launch"]
    Ph5 --> Ph6["Target Down - 1.2 Tbps DDoS"]

    Ph1 --> D1["Scan IP ranges for open port 23"]
    Ph2 --> D2["Try 60 default username-password pairs"]
    Ph3 --> D3["Wget malicious binary from loader server"]
    Ph4 --> D4["Bot waits for attack command"]
    Ph5 --> D5["All bots send UDP flood to target"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Fallback (if Mermaid fails to render):**
> The above diagrams collectively represent the **Threat-to-Attack-Defense Chain** of an IoT system. Imagine four vertically stacked layers (Perception → Network → Middleware → Application), each layer being attacked by a different threat class, all converging on a single malicious actor at the top. The defender must apply **STRIDE categorization** to each threat, then compute the **Risk Score** using the $R = P \times I \times V$ equation, and finally decide on a mitigation strategy based on the computed risk range. This block architecture is the standard *defense-in-depth* model endorsed by NIST for IoT security.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is to be answered in **30–50 words**. KTU 2024 valuation gives 1 mark for keyword, 1 mark for brief explanation, 1 mark for example/illustration.

---

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]

**Define an IoT security threat. Differentiate between a threat, a vulnerability, and an attack with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **threat** is any potential event that can cause harm to an IoT system, such as a DDoS attack. A **vulnerability** is the weakness that enables the threat, e.g., default credentials in a camera. An **attack** is the actual exploitation, e.g., a hacker logging into that camera using `admin:admin`. Thus, the *threat* is the idea, the *vulnerability* is the flaw, and the *attack* is the action.

**[Keyword Coverage:** threat + vulnerability + attack + example = 3 marks**]**

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]

**What is the STRIDE threat model? List its six categories with one IoT example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> STRIDE is a Microsoft-developed threat modeling framework with six categories. **S** – Spoofing (fake sensor node), **T** – Tampering (modified firmware), **R** – Repudiation (deleted logs), **I** – Information Disclosure (leaked camera feed), **D** – Denial of Service (cloud DDoS), **E** – Elevation of Privilege (user gains admin). It is used during IoT design to systematically identify all possible threat classes.

**[Keyword Coverage:** STRIDE + 6 letters + IoT example = 3 marks**]**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This First**
> - **Part (a) is 7 marks, Part (b) is 7 marks.** Do NOT spend 14 marks on (a) and zero on (b).
> - **Diagrams are mandatory for full marks.** A 14-mark STRIDE question with *no diagram* loses a guaranteed **2 marks** for "lack of illustration."
> - **Numerical questions demand units.** Writing "Risk = 15" without a label (low/medium/high) costs 1 mark.
> - **Avoid vague phrases** like "security is important." Use *technical* terms: *CIA triad, STRIDE, defense-in-depth, mutual TLS, hardware root-of-trust.*

---

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) Classify the major IoT security threats across the 4-layer IoT architecture (Perception, Network, Middleware, Application). For each layer, list at least 2 specific threats and one corresponding mitigation technique.** [7 marks]

**(b) Explain the STRIDE threat model in detail. Apply it to a Smart Healthcare IoT system (wearable ECG monitor sending data to a hospital cloud) and identify at least 4 potential threats with their mitigations.** [7 marks]

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 4-Layer IoT Threat Classification [7 marks]**

**Perception Layer (Sensors/RFID):**
- **Threat 1: Physical Tampering** — Attacker opens a deployed sensor, extracts crypto keys via JTAG pins, and replaces the firmware. **Mitigation:** Use *tamper-evident enclosures* and *hardware security modules (HSM)* with *Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs)*.
- **Threat 2: Node Capture** — A weather sensor is physically removed and reverse-engineered. **Mitigation:** Enable *secure boot* so the device only boots signed firmware, rendering cloned nodes useless.

**Network Layer (Gateways/Routers):**
- **Threat 1: Eavesdropping** — Attacker uses Wireshark to sniff unencrypted ZigBee traffic between a smart bulb and hub. **Mitigation:** Enable **AES-128 CCM** link-layer encryption.
- **Threat 2: MITM Attack** — Attacker proxies communication between a smart meter and utility server. **Mitigation:** Use **mutual TLS** with certificate pinning.

**Middleware Layer (Cloud/Storage):**
- **Threat 1: SQL Injection** — Malicious payload `' OR '1'='1` entered into a search field extracts the entire user database. **Mitigation:** Use *parameterized queries* and *Web Application Firewall (WAF)*.
- **Threat 2: Account Hijacking** — Stolen credentials used to access industrial control dashboards. **Mitigation:** Enforce *Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)* and *role-based access control (RBAC)*.

**Application Layer (User Apps):**
- **Threat 1: Phishing** — Fake firmware update email tricks user into installing malware. **Mitigation:** *Code signing* of updates and *user awareness training*.
- **Threat 2: XSS** — Malicious script in IoT forum steals session cookie. **Mitigation:** *Content Security Policy (CSP)* headers and *input sanitization*.

> **Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
> - 4 layers identified: 1 mark
> - 8 threats listed (2 per layer): 4 marks
> - 4 mitigations (1 per layer): 2 marks

#### **Part (b) — STRIDE on Smart Healthcare IoT [7 marks]**

**Introduction to STRIDE (2 marks):**
STRIDE is a 6-category threat modeling methodology. Each letter represents a class of threat, ensuring systematic coverage during the design phase of any IoT system.

**Application to Wearable ECG Monitor System (5 marks):**

| STRIDE | Threat Description | Mitigation |
|--------|-------------------|------------|
| **Spoofing** | Attacker forges an ECG device MAC address to send fake vitals | Mutual TLS with device certificates |
| **Tampering** | Doctor's prescription altered in transit | HMAC-SHA256 integrity check |
| **Repudiation** | Patient denies receiving a critical alert | Immutable cloud audit logs with timestamps |
| **Information Disclosure** | ECG data leaked during transmission | AES-256 encryption of all patient data |
| **Denial of Service** | Hospital cloud flooded, alerts delayed | Rate limiting + redundant cloud regions |
| **Elevation of Privilege** | Nurse accesses admin-only prescription panel | RBAC with principle of least privilege |

**Diagram (compulsory for full marks):**

```
Wearable ECG --> [TLS Tunnel] --> Gateway --> [Encrypted MQTT] --> Hospital Cloud --> Doctor App
   |                |                |                |                    |
[HW Crypto]    [Mutual Auth]    [Firewall]     [AES-256 Storage]     [MFA Login]
```

> **Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
> - STRIDE definition: 1 mark
> - All 6 categories listed: 2 marks
> - 4+ specific threats with mitigations: 3 marks
> - Diagram: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a) With reference to the Mirai botnet attack of October 2016, explain how IoT devices were compromised, the scale of the attack, and the specific vulnerabilities exploited. What lessons did the industry learn?** [7 marks]

**(b) An industrial IoT (IIoT) factory has 500 temperature sensors. Historical data shows a sensor failure (causing a process shutdown) occurs with probability 0.04 per month. Each shutdown costs the factory ₹8 lakh in lost production. Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) and decide whether a ₹5 lakh/year predictive maintenance system is justified.** [7 marks]

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Mirai Botnet Case Study [7 marks]**

**Background (2 marks):**
The Mirai botnet, whose source code was released publicly on **GitHub on 30 September 2016** by its author "Anna-senpai," infected approximately **600,000 IoT devices** — primarily IP cameras, DVRs, and home routers — by exploiting **default factory credentials** (e.g., `root:xc3511`, `admin:1234`).

**Infection Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. **Reconnaissance:** Mirai bot scans random IP addresses on **TCP port 23 (Telnet)** and **port 2323**.
2. **Brute Force:** It tries a hardcoded list of **60 username-password pairs** based on default manufacturer credentials.
3. **Compromise:** On success, it reports the victim's IP to a **Command & Control (C2) server** and downloads the Mirai malware binary.
4. **Persistence:** The bot kills competing malware (like Qbot) and blocks port 23 to prevent re-infection by rivals.

**Scale of Attack (1 mark):**
On **21 October 2016**, Mirai launched a **1.2 Tbps DDoS attack** on **Dyn**, a major DNS provider. This disrupted access to Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, GitHub, and Airbnb for most of the US East Coast.

**Industry Lessons (2 marks):**
- Manufacturers must enforce **unique per-device passwords** at the factory.
- Default Telnet/SSH access should be **disabled** in firmware.
- Auto-update mechanisms are critical for patching known CVEs.
- Network-level anomaly detection (e.g., spike in outbound traffic) can identify infected devices early.

> **Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
> - Mirai background: 2 marks
> - Infection steps: 2 marks
> - Scale and target: 1 mark
> - Industry lessons: 2 marks

#### **Part (b) — ALE Computation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Identify Given Quantities (1 mark)**

$$ P_{\text{monthly}} = 0.04, \quad \text{Downtime cost} = \text{₹ } 8,00,000 $$

**Step 2: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) (2 marks)**

Each failure causes a shutdown costing ₹ 8,00,000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} \\
\text{SLE} &= 8,00,000 \times 1.0 \quad (\text{100% loss per incident}) \\
\text{SLE} &= \text{₹ } 8,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARO} &= P_{\text{monthly}} \times 12 \\
\text{ARO} &= 0.04 \times 12 \\
\text{ARO} &= 0.48 \text{ incidents per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
\text{ALE} &= 8,00,000 \times 0.48 \\
\text{ALE} &= \text{₹ } 3,84,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Cost-Benefit Decision (2 marks)**

Compare the ALE (expected loss) with the defense cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defense Cost} &= \text{₹ } 5,00,000 \text{ per year} \\
\text{Expected Loss (ALE)} &= \text{₹ } 3,84,000 \text{ per year} \\
\text{Net Position} &= \text{ALE} - \text{Defense Cost} = 3,84,000 - 5,00,000 \\
\text{Net Position} &= -\text{₹ } 1,16,000 \text{ (LOSS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** The predictive maintenance system is **NOT justified** purely on direct-cost grounds — it would cost ₹1.16 lakh **more** per year than the expected loss.

**However**, the company should consider:
- **Indirect costs** (brand reputation, customer churn, regulatory fines) not captured in ALE.
- **Worst-case scenarios** (e.g., a single catastrophic failure causing a fire = ₹50 lakh+).
- **Risk aversion** — most factories still invest in maintenance to avoid tail-risk.

> **Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
> - Identifying given values: 1 mark
> - SLE calculation: 2 marks
> - ARO and ALE calculation: 2 marks
> - Decision with justification: 2 marks

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Rapid Revision Checklist — IoT Security Challenges: Threats**

- **Core Concept:** A *threat* is the *potential* for harm, a *vulnerability* is the *weakness*, an *attack* is the *actual exploitation* — do NOT confuse these terms.
- **CIA Triad:** Every threat violates Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability. Always map a threat to its CIA pillar in exam answers.
- **4-Layer Architecture:** Perception (sensors), Network (gateways), Middleware (cloud), Application (apps). Each layer has unique threats.
- **Top 10 Threats (memorize):** Eavesdropping, MITM, DDoS, Node Tampering, Firmware Hijack, Replay, Botnet, Jamming, Battery Exhaustion, Sinkhole Routing.
- **STRIDE Acronym:** **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.
- **Mirai Magic Numbers:** **600,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps, October 2016, Dyn DNS, port 23, 60 default credentials**.
- **Risk Formula:** $R = P \times I \times V$ (dimensionless score from 1–25). Risk ranges — Low (1–4), Medium (5–12), High (13–19), Critical (20–25).
- **ALE Formula:** $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ — used for cost-benefit analysis of mitigation investments.
- **Botnet Saturation:** $N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{B \times 10^6}{X} \right\rceil$ — minimum bot count to saturate a link of bandwidth B at X packets/sec per bot.
