> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node represents a processing stage; arrows denote data or model-parameter flow. In **Diagram 2**, the **dashed arrows** explicitly visualize the *privacy guarantee* of federated learning — raw data remains at each client.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT). List any two key differences between traditional IoT and AIoT systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
AIoT is the integration of Artificial Intelligence techniques — such as machine learning, deep learning, and federated learning — with IoT infrastructure to enable connected devices to autonomously analyze data, learn patterns, and make decisions at the edge or in the cloud, with minimal human intervention.

**Difference 1 (1 mark):**
Traditional IoT follows a *sense-transmit-display* pipeline that is rule-based and human-driven, whereas AIoT adds a *learn-predict-act* cognitive layer that is model-driven and autonomous.

**Difference 2 (1 mark):**
Traditional IoT transmits raw sensor streams to the cloud for processing, whereas AIoT performs local inference at the edge, transmitting only compressed events or anomalies, thereby reducing bandwidth consumption and latency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is TinyML? Mention two of its typical IoT applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
TinyML refers to the deployment of optimized machine learning models — typically quantized to INT8 or lower precision — on ultra-low-power microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M class) with memory budgets below **256 KB** and power consumption in the **milliwatt** range.

**Application 1 (1 mark):** Always-on keyword spotting in smart speakers and wearables, where the device must detect a wake-word without draining the battery.

**Application 2 (1 mark):** Vibration-based anomaly detection on industrial rotating machinery (motors, pumps, bearings) for predictive maintenance, where inference must run on-device at the sensor node.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain the three-tier architecture of an AIoT system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Edge AI in reducing latency and bandwidth. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* With a suitable example, explain Federated Learning in the context of IoT. Derive the FedAvg aggregation formula and state two privacy benefits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Three-Tier AIoT Architecture (7 marks):**

The AIoT architecture is organized into **three hierarchical tiers** (1 mark for naming, 4 marks for explanation, 2 marks for diagram + edge role):

1. **Perception Tier (Tier 1):** Comprises the IoT sensors and actuators — MEMS accelerometers, cameras, microphones, temperature and humidity probes. These generate the raw multimodal data stream. **(1 mark)**

2. **Edge / Network Tier (Tier 2):** Hosts the edge AI inference engine on microcontrollers (Cortex-M, RISC-V). Performs quantized CNN/LSTM inference locally. Connectivity layer uses 5G URLLC, LoRa, or Wi-Fi 6E for uplink of compressed inference events. **(2 marks)**

3. **Cognition Tier (Tier 3):** Cloud or on-premise server hosting heavy training jobs, digital twin simulations, and federated aggregation logic. **(1 mark)**

**Role of Edge AI in reducing latency and bandwidth:** By performing inference locally on the edge node, raw sensor data (which may stream at kHz) is compressed into a single inference event (e.g., "bearing fault, 87% confidence"). This reduces latency from cloud round-trip (~50–200 ms) to local inference (~5–50 ms) and reduces uplink bandwidth by a factor of $DRR$ (Data Reduction Ratio), typically **100× to 1000×**. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Federated Learning with Example + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Example (2 marks):** Consider 5 hospitals across Kerala collaboratively training a chest X-ray pneumonia detector. Each hospital holds sensitive patient data that **cannot legally** leave its premises. Federated learning allows each hospital to train the CNN locally on its own data, then transmit *only the model weights* (a few MB) to a central aggregator.

**FedAvg Derivation (3 marks):** Let $K$ clients participate, with client $k$ holding $n_k$ data samples and producing local weights $w_k$ after local SGD. The total sample count is:

$$n = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k$$

The aggregated global model is the data-weighted average of local models:

$$w_{global} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} w_k$$

The coefficient $\frac{n_k}{n}$ ensures that hospitals with larger datasets exert proportionally greater influence on the global model, while the sum of coefficients is unity (conservation).

**Privacy Benefits (2 marks):**
1. **Raw data never leaves the client** — only model gradients/weights are shared, which are mathematically harder to reverse-engineer into original patient images.
2. **Regulatory compliance** — satisfies HIPAA, GDPR, and India's DPDP Act because no personal data is transferred across organizational boundaries.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Define Digital Twin in the context of AIoT. Explain how AI integration enables predictive maintenance in a smart factory. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of an Edge-AI based wearable health monitoring system. Compute the model size for a 1D-CNN with layers [128→64→32→2] using INT8 quantization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Digital Twin + Predictive Maintenance (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A *Digital Twin* is a continuously synchronized virtual replica of a physical asset (motor, turbine, building) that mirrors its real-time state through bidirectional IoT data flow. The synchronization error is:

$$\varepsilon_{sync} = \vert x_{real}(t) - x_{twin}(t) \vert$$

**AI-enabled Predictive Maintenance Workflow (5 marks):**
1. **Sensors** (vibration, current, temperature) mounted on the motor stream data to the edge node. **(1 mark)**
2. **Edge AI (TinyML CNN)** runs quantized inference locally and detects vibration signatures indicative of bearing wear. **(1 mark)**
3. The detected event is relayed to the **cloud digital twin**, which updates the virtual motor's degradation state. **(1 mark)**
4. A **physics-informed LSTM** predicts the *Remaining Useful Life (RUL)* by extrapolating the wear curve. **(1 mark)**
5. When RUL drops below a threshold (e.g., 7 days), the system **autonomously schedules maintenance**, orders parts, and notifies the operator — *before* failure occurs. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Wearable Edge-AI Architecture + Model Size Computation (7 marks):**

**Architecture Diagram (3 marks):** Block-level flow: PPG/ECG electrodes → Analog front-end (AFE) → MCU with 1D-CNN inference → Bluetooth LE uplink → Companion smartphone/cloud for visualization and federated updates. *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1, treating it as the wearable equivalent.)*

**Model Size Computation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify dimensions:**
- Layer 1: $n_{in} = 128$, $n_{out} = 64$
- Layer 2: $n_{in} = 64$, $n_{out} = 32$
- Layer 3: $n_{in} = 32$, $n_{out} = 2$

**Step 2 — Compute parameters per layer (weights + biases):**

$$P_1 = (128 \times 64) + 64 = 8{,}256$$

$$P_2 = (64 \times 32) + 32 = 2{,}080$$

$$P_3 = (32 \times 2) + 2 = 66$$

**Step 3 — Sum total parameters:**

$$P_{total} = 8{,}256 + 2{,}080 + 66 = 10{,}402$$

**Step 4 — Apply INT8 quantization (8 bits/weight):**

$$M_{model} = 10{,}402 \times 1 \text{ byte} = 10{,}402 \text{ bytes} \approx 10.16 \text{ KB}$$

**Conclusion:** The model consumes only **~10 KB**, easily fitting within a Cortex-M0+ flash budget of 256 KB — confirming the wearable design is feasible for sub-mW operation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AI with ML:** AI is the *broader field*; ML is a *subset*. TinyML is a *subset of ML*. Marks deducted if hierarchy is muddled.
> 2. **Forgetting the Privacy Argument:** When discussing Federated Learning, merely stating *"weights are shared"* is **incomplete**. Examiners expect the explicit privacy-preservation claim.
> 3. **Unit Errors:** Energy in Joules, power in Watts, time in seconds — mixing units is the **#1 mark-loss cause** in derivations.
> 4. **Skipping the Verification Step:** Always close derivations with a "$\checkmark$" or "Verified" line — shows the examiner you sanity-checked.
> 5. **Treating "Future Trends" as Vague:** Examiners expect *concrete named technologies* (TinyML, Federated Learning, Neuromorphic, GenAI) — not generic phrases like "AI will improve things."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — AI Integration in IoT (Module 4):**

- ⭐ **AIoT = AI + IoT** — the defining trend is moving from *connected sensing* to *autonomous cognition*.
- ⭐ **Three-tier architecture:** Perception → Edge/Network → Cognition.
- ⭐ **Edge AI reduces latency** from ~100 ms (cloud) to **< 50 ms** (local) and bandwidth by **100×–1000×**.
- ⭐ **TinyML** = ML on **< 256 KB** flash MCUs at **mW** power; quantization to INT8 is the key enabler.
- ⭐ **Model size formula:** $M = 4 \times P_{params} \times Q$ (bytes), with $Q$ in bits/weight.
- ⭐ **FedAvg aggregation:** $w_{global} = \sum_k \frac{n_k}{n} w_k$ — weighted by data volume; raw data never leaves client.
- ⭐ **Federated learning** is the **privacy-preserving** AI technique for cross-organizational IoT (healthcare, finance, smart cities).
- ⭐ **Digital Twin** is a virtual replica with sync error $\varepsilon_{sync} = \vert x_{real} - x_{twin} \vert$.
- ⭐ **Neuromorphic computing** targets **< 1 mJ/inference** and **< 1 ms** latency for always-on sensing.
- ⭐ **Generative AI (LLMs)** for IoT enables conversational interfaces but requires **cloud-scale** compute — not edge-deployable today.
- ⭐ **5G URLLC** provides **< 1 ms** air-interface latency, enabling closed-loop AIoT control.
- ⭐ **Standard KPIs to memorize:** Inference latency, energy per inference, data reduction ratio, model accuracy, F1-score.
- ⭐ **Six AI techniques in IoT:** Supervised, Deep Learning, Reinforcement, Federated, TinyML, Generative.
- ⭐ **Always state 2+ constraints** when justifying technology choice in exams (privacy + bandwidth, latency + power, etc.).
- ⭐ **Formula to remember cold:** $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{tx} + T_{cloud} + T_{act}$.
- ⭐ **Industrial impact metric:** Predictive maintenance reduces unplanned downtime by **30–50%**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT for Sustainability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT for Sustainability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Internet of Things for Sustainability (IoT-S)** refers to the systematic application of interconnected sensing, actuation, communication, and data-analytics technologies to monitor, measure, manage, and optimize the consumption of natural resources (energy, water, air, soil) and to reduce the ecological footprint of human activities across industrial, agricultural, urban, and domestic domains.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme OEITT723 Module 4 framework, IoT for Sustainability is positioned as a cross-disciplinary engineering paradigm that converges **Green Computing**, **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** of the United Nations, and **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** to deliver measurable environmental, economic, and social impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> IoT for Sustainability = Use of IoT architectures (perception–network–edge–cloud–application layers) for achieving UN SDG targets such as SDG 6 (Clean Water), SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities), SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption), and SDG 13 (Climate Action).

## 1.2 Core Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Green IoT** | Energy-efficient design of IoT devices, networks, and data centers to minimize power consumption and e-waste. |
| **Sustainable IoT** | IoT solutions whose entire lifecycle (manufacture, deployment, operation, disposal) has a net-zero or net-positive environmental impact. |
| **Precision Agriculture** | Site-specific crop management using IoT sensors to optimize water, fertilizer, and pesticide use. |
| **Smart Microgrid** | A localized energy grid with IoT-based DER (Distributed Energy Resources) that balances load and renewable generation. |
| **Carbon-Intelligent IoT** | IoT systems that shift workloads to times/locations with cleaner grid energy based on real-time carbon intensity. |
| **Digital Twin (DT)** | A virtual replica of a physical asset used for predictive sustainability simulations. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Patient Monitor"**
> Imagine a critically ill patient in an ICU. Doctors attach sensors that continuously measure heart rate, oxygen level, blood pressure, and temperature. If any parameter crosses a safe threshold, alarms ring and corrective medicine is administered immediately. The patient survives *because* of continuous, automated, data-driven monitoring.
> **IoT for Sustainability works exactly the same way for Planet Earth.** Sensors continuously measure the "vitals" of our environment — air quality, soil moisture, river pollution, grid load. The moment any vital crosses an unsafe threshold, automatic corrective actions (irrigation, ventilation, load-shedding) are triggered. Without IoT, we are like a doctor who checks the patient only once a year — by then it is too late.

## 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics

The following standard sustainability metrics are most commonly evaluated in KTU examination questions:

- **Carbon Footprint (CF):** Measured in **kg CO₂-equivalent (kgCO₂e)**.
- **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER):** Useful output energy ÷ input energy.
- **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE):** Total facility power ÷ IT equipment power (ideal data center = **1.0**).
- **Water Usage Effectiveness (WUE):** Litres of water per kWh of IT load.
- **Soil Moisture (%VWC):** Volumetric Water Content percentage.
- **PM2.5 / PM10:** Particulate matter in μg/m³ (WHO safe limit: **15 μg/m³** annual mean for PM2.5).
- **NDVI:** Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (range **−1 to +1**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of Sustainable IoT Systems

A sustainable IoT system follows the standard 5-layer IoT reference model, *augmented* with a **Sustainability Layer** that quantifies environmental impact at every stage.

### Layer-wise Breakdown

**1. Perception / Sensing Layer (Hardware Sustainability)**
- Low-power MCUs (ARM Cortex-M0+, ESP32, RISC-V).
- Energy harvesting modules: solar (PV), piezoelectric, thermoelectric, RF.
- Biodegradable / recyclable PCB substrates to reduce **e-waste**.
- Battery chemistry choices: Li-SOCl₂ (long life), NiMH (recyclable).

**2. Network / Communication Layer (Green Communication)**
- Low-PAN protocols: **LoRaWAN, Zigbee, NB-IoT, BLE 5.0**.
- Selection criterion: minimize **bits-per-joule** transmitted.
- Sleep scheduling & duty cycling to cut idle radio energy.
- Edge computing to reduce backbone data transmission.

**3. Edge / Fog Layer (Local Optimization)**
- Pre-processing, anomaly detection, and actuation at the edge.
- Reduces cloud bandwidth — directly cutting **data-centre PUE overhead**.

**4. Cloud / Analytics Layer (Carbon-Aware Computing)**
- Renewable-powered data centres (Google, Microsoft 24/7 carbon-free goal).
- Workload shifting based on real-time **grid carbon intensity (gCO₂/kWh)**.

**5. Application Layer (SDG Mapping)**
- Dashboards for farmers, city operators, utility engineers.
- Decision-support systems for policy and compliance.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Sustainable IoT — Driver Analysis

- **Climate Emergency:** Global temperature rise mandates 45% emissions cut by 2030 (IPCC).
- **Resource Scarcity:** UN predicts 40% freshwater shortfall by 2030.
- **Operational Cost:** IoT-driven smart buildings cut HVAC energy by **20–30%**.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** ESG reporting, EU CBAM, India PAT scheme.
- **UN SDG Alignment:** Provides measurable KPIs for 17 SDGs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carbon Footprint (CF) | $CF = E_{total} \times CI$ | **kgCO₂e** | Quantify total emissions |
| 2 | Energy per Bit (Energy Efficiency) | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{radio} \cdot t_{tx}}{N_{bits}}$ | **J/bit** | Compare protocol efficiency |
| 3 | Power Usage Effectiveness | $PUE = \dfrac{P_{facility}}{P_{IT}}$ | dimensionless | Data-centre efficiency (ideal = 1) |
| 4 | NDVI (Vegetation Index) | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | dimensionless | Crop health (range −1 to +1) |
| 5 | Soil Moisture (VWC) | $\theta_v = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100$ | **%VWC** | Smart irrigation trigger |
| 6 | Battery Lifetime | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{battery}\,(mAh)}{I_{avg}\,(mA) \times 24 \times 365}$ | **years** | Sensor node longevity |
| 7 | Solar Harvested Energy | $E_{harv} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G \cdot t$ | **Wh** | Energy-neutral sensor design |
| 8 | Sensor Sampling Energy | $E_{sample} = V_{cc} \cdot I_{active} \cdot t_{sample}$ | **Joules** | Duty-cycle optimization |
| 9 | Network Carbon Intensity | $CI_{grid} = \dfrac{\sum_i (P_i \times EF_i)}{P_{total}}$ | **gCO₂/kWh** | Carbon-aware workload shifting |
| 10 | Irrigation Water Saved | $W_{saved} = A \times (ET_c - ET_o) \times K_c$ | **m³** | Smart-agriculture ROI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute values and dividers use the LaTeX `\dfrac` (or `\vert` for `|x|` if needed) to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture (India):** IoT drip-irrigation cuts water use by **40–60%** and fertilizer by **30%**.
- **Smart Grids:** IoT-based demand-response prevents peak-time blackouts and integrates rooftop solar seamlessly.
- **Air Quality Monitoring:** Low-cost PM2.5 sensor networks inform citizens in real time (e.g., Delhi CPCB + IIT-Kanpur PurpleAir deployment).
- **Cold-Chain Logistics:** IoT temperature monitoring cuts food spoilage by **25–50%**, directly supporting SDG 2 (Zero Hunger).
- **Wildfire Detection:** LoRaWAN sensor meshes in forests reduce detection time from hours to **<10 minutes**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating Carbon Footprint of an IoT Deployment

**Problem Statement:**
A smart-building IoT deployment consists of **500 sensor nodes**. Each node consumes an average of **120 mW**, is powered by a grid mix with carbon intensity **CI = 0.72 kgCO₂/kWh**, and operates **24 × 7 × 365** hours per year. Compute the annual operational carbon footprint.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total Power Consumption**

$$
P_{total} = N \times P_{node}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 500 \times 0.120 \text{ W} = 60 \text{ W} = 0.060 \text{ kW}
$$

*Explanation:* We simply multiply the number of nodes by the per-node power to obtain the aggregate power draw of the entire sensor network.

**Step 2 — Total Annual Energy**

$$
E_{total} = P_{total} \times t_{year}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 0.060 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 525.6 \text{ kWh}
$$

*Explanation:* The total operational hours per year equals $24 \times 365 = 8760$ hours. Multiplying power (kW) by time (h) gives energy in kWh.

**Step 3 — Annual Carbon Footprint**

$$
CF = E_{total} \times CI
$$

$$
CF = 525.6 \times 0.72 = 378.43 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{CF \approx 378.43 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e per year}}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** This kind of 3-step plug-and-evaluate problem is a guaranteed 7-marker in KTU ESE. Practice substituting units cleanly — kW, kWh, kgCO₂e.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Lifetime of a LoRaWAN Soil Sensor

**Problem Statement:**
A LoRaWAN soil-moisture sensor uses a **2400 mAh Li-SOCl₂** battery. The active current during a transmission burst is **90 mA for 1.2 s**, and the node wakes up **48 times per day**. Idle (sleep) current is **8 μA**. Compute the theoretical battery lifetime in years.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Active Charge per Day**

$$
Q_{active} = I_{active} \times t_{burst} \times N_{tx}
$$

$$
Q_{active} = 90 \text{ mA} \times 1.2 \text{ s} \times 48 = 90 \times 0.000333 \text{ h} \times 48
$$

**Convert seconds to hours:** $1.2 \text{ s} = \dfrac{1.2}{3600} \text{ h} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ h}$

$$
Q_{active} = 90 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 48 = 1.44 \text{ mAh/day}
$$

*Explanation:* The current is multiplied by the time duration in hours to get charge (mAh), then by the number of transmissions per day.

**Step 2 — Sleep Charge per Day**

$$
Q_{sleep} = I_{sleep} \times 24 \text{ h} = 0.008 \text{ mA} \times 24 = 0.192 \text{ mAh/day}
$$

*Explanation:* The sleep current (8 μA = 0.008 mA) flows for the full 24 hours, giving a small but continuous drain.

**Step 3 — Total Daily Charge Consumption**

$$
Q_{day} = Q_{active} + Q_{sleep} = 1.44 + 0.192 = 1.632 \text{ mAh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Battery Lifetime**

$$
T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{Q_{day} \times 365}
$$

$$
T_{life} = \dfrac{2400}{1.632 \times 365} = \dfrac{2400}{595.68} \approx 4.03 \text{ years}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{T_{life} \approx 4.03 \text{ years}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A 4-year battery life matches the **LoRaWAN Class-A device lifetime target** for unattended outdoor deployments — proving that ultra-low sleep current is the *primary* design lever.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NDVI Computation from Multispectral Sensor

**Problem Statement:**
A satellite / drone multispectral sensor records **NIR = 0.62** and **Red = 0.18** reflectance values for a crop field. Compute NDVI and interpret crop health.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the NDVI Formula**

$$
NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}
$$

$$
NDVI = \dfrac{0.62 - 0.18}{0.62 + 0.18} = \dfrac{0.44}{0.80} = 0.55
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation Table**

| NDVI Range | Vegetation State |
|---|---|
| $< 0$ | Water / Snow / Non-vegetated |
| $0.0 - 0.2$ | Bare soil / Sparse |
| $0.2 - 0.5$ | Moderate vegetation |
| $0.5 - 0.7$ | Dense, healthy vegetation |
| $> 0.7$ | Very dense (peak canopy) |

**Result:** $\boxed{NDVI = 0.55 \Rightarrow \text{Healthy, dense crop growth — no irrigation alert required}}$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
Define **Green IoT**. List any four design strategies that make an IoT system energy-efficient and sustainable.

**Model Answer:**

**Green IoT** is the practice of designing, manufacturing, deploying, and disposing of IoT systems in a manner that minimizes energy consumption, carbon emissions, and electronic waste across the entire lifecycle of the devices and networks.

**Four Design Strategies:**

1. **Energy Harvesting:** Powering sensor nodes from solar, piezoelectric, or RF ambient sources to extend battery life.
2. **Low-Power Communication Protocols:** Using LoRaWAN, Zigbee, NB-IoT, or BLE 5.0 instead of always-on Wi-Fi/4G.
3. **Duty Cycling and Sleep Scheduling:** Switching radios and MCUs into deep-sleep modes when no event is detected.
4. **Edge Computing:** Performing analytics close to the data source to avoid the high energy cost of long-haul cloud transmission.
5. *(Bonus)* Eco-design of hardware using biodegradable PCBs and recyclable battery chemistries.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the role of IoT in achieving **UN Sustainable Development Goal 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation)** with an example.

**Model Answer:**

UN **SDG 6** aims to ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all by 2030. IoT plays a transformative role by enabling **continuous, real-time monitoring** of water resources that was historically impossible.

**Role of IoT:**

- **Smart Water Metering:** Ultrasonic flow sensors and smart meters detect leaks and unauthorized consumption, reducing **Non-Revenue Water (NRW)** by up to **25%**.
- **Quality Monitoring:** pH, turbidity, dissolved-oxygen, and ORP sensors continuously monitor river and reservoir water quality, triggering alerts if contamination is detected.
- **Smart Irrigation:** Soil-moisture sensors coupled with weather forecasts automate precision irrigation, saving **40–60%** of agricultural water.
- **Flood Early Warning:** Water-level sensors (ultrasonic / pressure) in rivers and storm drains provide real-time flood warnings to city authorities.

**Example:** In the city of **Bhubaneswar (India)**, an IoT-based water-quality monitoring system was deployed on the Daya and Bhargavi rivers. LoRaWAN sensor buoys measure pH, turbidity, and dissolved oxygen every 15 minutes, sending data to a cloud dashboard. Authorities receive early warnings, enabling rapid response to industrial pollution events.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — IoT for Smart Agriculture and Water Conservation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the architecture of an IoT-based **precision agriculture system** with a neat block diagram. Explain how soil-moisture, NPK, and weather sensors collectively enable precision irrigation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A smart-irrigation system covers a **2-hectare** field. The conventional flood irrigation uses **12,000 m³ of water per hectare per season**, while the IoT precision system uses only **5,000 m³ per hectare per season**. Calculate the **water saved in m³** and the **percentage reduction**. Also estimate the annual carbon emission avoided if pumping 1 m³ of water emits **0.45 kgCO₂e**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

A precision agriculture system uses the standard IoT reference architecture tailored to farmland:

**1. Perception Layer (Field Sensors):**
- **Soil-moisture sensors** (capacitive / TDR) measure VWC at multiple depths (e.g., 15 cm, 30 cm).
- **NPK sensors** (Ion-Selective Field-Effect Transistor, ISFET) measure Nitrogen, Phosphorus, and Potassium levels.
- **Weather station** (DHT22, anemometer, pyranometer) provides temperature, humidity, wind, and solar radiation.
- **Leaf-wetness and pH sensors** add canopy-level data.

**2. Network Layer:**
- **LoRaWAN** is preferred due to long range (up to 10 km rural), low power, and tolerance of vegetation obstruction.
- **NB-IoT** is used where cellular coverage exists and higher data rates are needed.

**3. Edge Layer:**
- An edge gateway (Raspberry Pi / ESP32 + LoRa concentrator) runs **ET₀ reference evapotranspiration** models using the **Penman-Monteith equation** to compute daily crop water demand.

**4. Cloud & Application Layer:**
- ML models predict 7-day irrigation schedules using sensor data + weather forecast APIs.
- A farmer mobile app delivers alerts and allows remote valve control.

**Collective Working of Precision Irrigation:**

- The soil-moisture sensor's VWC reading is compared with the **field capacity (FC)** and **permanent wilting point (PWP)** thresholds.
- The NPK sensor indicates if fertilizer deficiency is causing crop stress that mimics water stress — preventing false irrigation triggers.
- The weather station's forecasted rain and ET₀ computation prevent unnecessary irrigation if rain is imminent.
- The decision is sent to a **solenoid valve** via the edge gateway, activating drip lines only in the specific zone where soil-moisture is low.

**Result:** This multi-sensor fusion ensures that the *right amount* of water is delivered to the *right zone* at the *right time* — the very definition of **precision irrigation**.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:**
- Field area = 2 hectares
- Conventional water use = 12,000 m³/hectare/season
- IoT water use = 5,000 m³/hectare/season
- Emission factor = 0.45 kgCO₂e per m³ of pumped water

**Step 1 — Water Used by Each Method (Total for 2 ha)**

$$
W_{conv} = 12{,}000 \times 2 = 24{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
W_{IoT} = 5{,}000 \times 2 = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Water Saved**

$$
W_{saved} = W_{conv} - W_{IoT}
$$

$$
W_{saved} = 24{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 14{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Reduction**

$$
\% \text{Reduction} = \dfrac{W_{saved}}{W_{conv}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Reduction} = \dfrac{14{,}000}{24{,}000} \times 100 = 58.33\%
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Carbon Emissions Avoided**

$$
CF_{avoided} = W_{saved} \times 0.45
$$

$$
CF_{avoided} = 14{,}000 \times 0.45 = 6{,}300 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
CF_{avoided} = 6.3 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Final Results:**
- Water saved: $\boxed{14{,}000 \text{ m}^3}$
- Percentage reduction: $\boxed{58.33\%}$
- Carbon avoided: $\boxed{6{,}300 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e} = 6.3 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}}$

---

### Question B — IoT for Smart Energy and Carbon-Aware Computing

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the concept of a **Smart Microgrid** with IoT integration. Explain how IoT enables the integration of renewable energy sources, demand response, and grid stability. List any three communication protocols used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A data centre has **PUE = 1.8** and an IT load of **2 MW**. The grid carbon intensity is **0.65 kgCO₂e/kWh**. Compute the **annual energy consumption of the entire facility**, the **annual IT energy**, and the **annual carbon footprint**. If renewable energy contracts reduce the effective CI to **0.30 kgCO₂e/kWh**, calculate the **annual carbon saving in tCO₂e**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Smart Microgrid Definition:**
A **Smart Microgrid** is a localized, self-contained electricity network — comprising distributed energy resources (DERs), storage, and loads — that can operate **grid-connected or islanded** using IoT-based real-time control.

**IoT Integration in Smart Microgrid:**

1. **Renewable Integration:**
- IoT inverters and smart meters continuously monitor PV output, wind generation, and battery SoC.
- ML-based forecasting predicts renewable generation for the next 24 hours.
- The Energy Management System (EMS) optimizes dispatch: solar + battery + grid in the most carbon-efficient mix.

2. **Demand Response (DR):**
- Smart thermostats, IoT relays, and EV chargers receive **dynamic pricing / load-shed signals** from the utility.
- During peak demand, non-critical loads (HVAC, water heaters) automatically curtail, reducing peak grid stress.
- Customers earn incentives for participating in DR events.

3. **Grid Stability:**
- **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** at IoT nodes measure voltage, frequency, and phase angle at millisecond resolution.
- **FACTS (Flexible AC Transmission Systems)** and **STATCOMs** receive automated setpoints from the EMS to maintain voltage stability when intermittent renewables cause fluctuations.
- **Frequency-Watt and Volt-Var** curves implemented in smart inverters autonomously support grid frequency.

**Three Communication Protocols:**

1. **IEEE 2030.5 (SEP 2.0):** Standard for smart energy management application layer.
2. **Modbus / DNP3:** Industrial protocols for SCADA-talking to RTUs in substations.
3. **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT:** For last-mile connectivity of distributed smart meters and sensors.
4. *(Bonus)* **MQTT:** Lightweight pub/sub messaging for EMS-to-inverter communication.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:**
- PUE = 1.8
- IT Load = 2 MW
- Grid CI = 0.65 kgCO₂e/kWh
- Renewable CI = 0.30 kgCO₂e/kWh
- Time = 8760 hours/year

**Step 1 — Annual IT Energy**

$$
E_{IT} = P_{IT} \times t = 2{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 17{,}520{,}000 \text{ kWh} = 17{,}520 \text{ MWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Facility Energy (Total)**

$$
E_{facility} = PUE \times E_{IT}
$$

$$
E_{facility} = 1.8 \times 17{,}520 \text{ MWh} = 31{,}536 \text{ MWh}
$$

*Explanation:* PUE = Total facility power / IT power. Therefore, total facility energy = PUE × IT energy.

**Step 3 — Annual Carbon Footprint (Original Grid)**

$$
CF_{grid} = E_{facility} \times CI_{grid}
$$

$$
CF_{grid} = 31{,}536 \text{ MWh} \times 0.65 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e/kWh}
$$

Convert MWh → kWh: $31{,}536 \times 1{,}000 = 31{,}536{,}000 \text{ kWh}$

$$
CF_{grid} = 31{,}536{,}000 \times 0.65 = 20{,}498{,}400 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e} = 20{,}498.4 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Carbon Footprint (Renewable Contract)**

$$
CF_{renew} = 31{,}536{,}000 \times 0.30 = 9{,}460{,}800 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e} = 9{,}460.8 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Carbon Saving**

$$
\Delta CF = CF_{grid} - CF_{renew}
$$

$$
\Delta CF = 20{,}498.4 - 9{,}460.8 = 11{,}037.6 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Final Results:**
- Annual IT Energy: $\boxed{17{,}520 \text{ MWh}}$
- Annual Facility Energy: $\boxed{31{,}536 \text{ MWh}}$
- Original CF: $\boxed{20{,}498.4 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}}$
- Renewable CF: $\boxed{9{,}460.8 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}}$
- Annual Carbon Saving: $\boxed{11{,}037.6 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Marks are often lost when students mix kW with MW or forget to convert MWh to kWh. Always write units explicitly and convert at the start.
> 2. **PUE Misapplication:** PUE is **Total/IT**, not **IT/Total**. Multiplying IT energy by PUE gives facility energy, NOT dividing.
> 3. **NDVI Range:** NDVI lies in **−1 to +1**. Forgetting the range loses the "interpretation" mark.
> 4. **Carbon Intensity:** The grid CI differs by country (India ≈ 0.72, EU ≈ 0.25, Global avg ≈ 0.50). Always re-state the value in your calculation step.
> 5. **SDG Mapping:** Examiners award marks for **explicit SDG numbering** (e.g., "SDG 6 — Clean Water"), not vague "sustainability" statements.
> 6. **Diagrams in Part B:** A block diagram is *expected* — students skipping it lose 1–2 marks easily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Recall Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Definition:** IoT for Sustainability = IoT applied to measure, monitor, and reduce environmental footprint across the perception–network–edge–cloud–application stack.
- **Core Concept:** "Sense → Analyze → Act → Report" loop, closed continuously.
- **Green IoT Levers:** (i) Energy harvesting, (ii) Sleep/ duty cycling, (iii) LPWAN protocols, (iv) Edge computing, (v) Renewable-powered data centres.
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $CF = E \times CI$
  - $PUE = P_{facility} / P_{IT}$ (ideal = 1.0)
  - $NDVI = (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red)$ (range −1 to +1)
  - $T_{life} = C_{battery} / (Q_{day} \times 365)$
- **UN SDGs Most Cited:** SDG 2 (Zero Hunger), SDG 6 (Clean Water), SDG 7 (Affordable Energy), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities), SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption), SDG 13 (Climate Action).
- **Standard Sustainable Targets to Quote:**
  - Water savings in smart irrigation: **40–60%**
  - HVAC savings in smart buildings: **20–30%**
  - Food spoilage reduction via cold-chain IoT: **25–50%**
  - PM2.5 WHO annual safe limit: **15 μg/m³**
  - Smart grid peak reduction: **15–25%**
- **Sensors to Domain Mapping:** Soil moisture → Agriculture, PM2.5/CO₂ → Air, Flow/Level → Water, Fill-level/Weight → Waste, Temperature/Humidity → Cold-chain, CT/Voltage → Grid.
- **Protocols to Context:** LoRaWAN → Long-range, low-power outdoor. NB-IoT → Cellular-backed, urban. Zigbee/BLE → Short-range indoor. 5G URLLC → Industrial.
- **Carbon-Aware Computing Concept:** Shift cloud workloads to regions/times with lowest grid carbon intensity — measure is **gCO₂/kWh**.
- **Digital Twin:** Virtual replica used for "what-if" sustainability simulations before physical changes.
- **E-Waste Mitigation:** Use biodegradable substrates, modular hardware, and battery chemistries with long life (Li-SOCl₂) to cut end-of-life waste.
- **Three Pillars of Sustainability:** Environmental + Economic + Social — IoT must address all three for a "sustainable" outcome, not just one.
- **Common Examiner Buzzwords to Use:** *Precision, Edge Intelligence, Carbon-Intelligent, Energy-Neutral Sensing, Closed-Loop Optimization, SDG Mapping, ESG Reporting*.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies: Smart Homes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: Smart Homes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Smart Home** is an IoT-enabled residential environment where physical devices, household appliances, sensors, actuators, and communication networks are interconnected to enable **context-aware automation, remote monitoring, and intelligent decision-making** for enhanced comfort, security, energy efficiency, and convenience. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, a smart home case study demonstrates the practical application of the **perception–network–application** IoT layered architecture in a controlled, real-world consumer setting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> A Smart Home is a system that integrates **sensing (perception layer)**, **connectivity (network layer)**, and **intelligent control (application layer)** to autonomously or semi-autonomously manage domestic functions such as lighting, climate, surveillance, and appliance control via IoT protocols like **MQTT, CoAP, ZigBee, and Wi-Fi**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Butler Who Never Sleeps"**
>
> Imagine hiring a butler who lives in your house **24×7, never gets tired, never forgets**, and can control every light, lock, fan, geyser, and curtain — all by himself, based on your habits. He also calls you on your phone if someone is at the door, and adjusts the AC automatically when temperature rises.
>
> A **Smart Home** is exactly that — but instead of a human butler, you have:
> - **Sensors** = the butler's eyes, ears, and skin (motion, temperature, door, smoke).
> - **Microcontrollers/Cloud** = the butler's brain (Raspberry Pi, ESP32, AWS IoT).
> - **Actuators** = the butler's hands (smart bulbs, smart locks, relays).
> - **Mobile App / Voice Assistant** = your direct line of communication with the butler.
>
> The "**Internet**" in IoT is what allows this butler to talk to you even when you are **out of the country**.

---

## 1.3 Core Entities in a Smart Home (KTU High-Yield Bullets)

| Entity | Role | Example Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Sense environment | PIR motion, DHT22, MQ-2 smoke, reed door switch |
| **Actuators** | Perform action | Smart bulb (Tuya), smart relay, motorized curtain, solenoid lock |
| **Edge Controller** | Local brain | ESP32, Raspberry Pi, Arduino + Shield |
| **Gateway** | Bridges local network to Internet | ZigBee-to-WiFi hub (Philips Hue Bridge) |
| **Cloud Platform** | Remote brain & storage | AWS IoT Core, Google Firebase, Blynk, ThingsBoard |
| **User Interface** | Human control | Mobile App, Alexa Echo Dot, Google Nest Hub |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Smart Home Design

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **bold-marked** because KTU questions frequently test their numerical ranges.
> - **Latency (local control):** < **100 ms**
> - **Latency (cloud control):** **200 ms – 2 s**
> - **Typical packet size (sensor telemetry):** **40 – 250 bytes**
> - **Smart Home device count (average household):** **25 – 50 devices**
> - **Annual data generated per smart home:** **~ 2 GB/day**
> - **Operating frequency bands:** **2.4 GHz (Wi-Fi/ZigBee/BLE)**, **433 MHz / 915 MHz (sub-GHz)**, **5 GHz (Wi-Fi 6)**

---

## 1.5 Why Smart Homes are a Critical KTU Case Study

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Smart Homes are the *default* case study in KTU IoT papers because they touch **every layer** of the IoT reference model. A single smart home question can test:
> 1. Architecture (sensors → gateway → cloud → app)
> 2. Protocol selection (MQTT vs CoAP)
> 3. Security (AES-128, TLS, OAuth 2.0)
> 4. Energy models (battery life of wireless sensor)
> 5. Real-world deployment challenges
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Smart Home (4-Layer Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes the **4-layer IoT reference model** applied to smart homes:

### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer
- **Function:** Converts physical phenomena into electrical signals.
- **Components:** Temperature, humidity, gas, motion, light, door/window contact, water-leak, vibration, and image sensors.
- **Data rate:** Typically **< 10 kbps** per node.
- **Power:** Battery-operated (**3 V coin cell / 18650 Li-ion**), so energy harvesting (solar, piezoelectric) is often discussed.

### Layer 2 — Network / Connectivity Layer
- **Function:** Forwards sensor data to the gateway/cloud.
- **Protocols used:**

| Protocol | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Topology | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11n)** | **2.4 / 5 GHz** | ~50 m indoor | Up to 600 Mbps | Star | High |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | **2.4 GHz** | ~30 m indoor | 250 kbps | Mesh | Low |
| **Z-Wave** | **908.42 MHz** | ~30 m indoor | 100 kbps | Mesh | Low |
| **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** | **2.4 GHz** | ~10 m | 2 Mbps | Star/P2P | Very Low |
| **LoRaWAN** | **868/915 MHz** | 2–5 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Star-of-stars | Ultra-low |
| **MQTT (Application)** | TCP/IP | — | — | Pub/Sub | — |
| **CoAP (Application)** | UDP | — | — | Req/Resp | — |

### Layer 3 — Middleware / Edge Processing Layer
- **Function:** Local aggregation, rule engines, anomaly detection, time-series buffering.
- **Software:** Node-RED, ThingsBoard Edge, AWS Greengrass, Home Assistant.
- **Why edge?** Reduces **bandwidth cost**, **latency**, and **cloud round-trip** — important for safety functions like gas-leak shutoff.

### Layer 4 — Application / Cloud Layer
- **Function:** Long-term storage, ML analytics, user dashboards, third-party integrations.
- **Platforms:** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, Blynk, ThingSpeak.
- **User Interaction:** Android/iOS app, voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant), web dashboard.

---

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works (Intuitive Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Why Sensors?** Without sensors, the system is *blind* — no data means no automation.
> **Step 2 — Why Local Network?** Smart homes have **~25–50 devices** in a small area; mesh protocols (ZigBee/Z-Wave) scale better than Wi-Fi star.
> **Step 3 — Why a Gateway?** Different protocols (ZigBee + Wi-Fi) don't speak to each other; the **gateway translates** them to IP-based MQTT.
> **Step 4 — Why Cloud?** Storage of historical data enables **ML-based pattern recognition** (e.g., "User wakes at 7 AM → pre-heat geyser at 6:50 AM").
> **Step 5 — Why App/Voice?** Human-machine interface for **manual override** and **feedback**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Battery life estimate** | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{battery}\,(mAh) \times V_{battery}}{I_{avg}\,(mA) \times V_{nom}}$ | hours → convert to **years** |
| **Wireless path loss (Log-distance)** | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\,n\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}$ | $n$ = path-loss exponent (**2–4 indoor**) |
| **Received Signal Strength** | $RSSI = P_{tx} + G_{tx} - PL(d) + G_{rx}$ | in **dBm** |
| **MQTT QoS 0** | At-most-once, no ack | Lowest overhead |
| **MQTT QoS 1** | At-least-once, PUBACK | Default for telemetry |
| **MQTT QoS 2** | Exactly-once, 4-way handshake | Used for billing/locks |
| **Latency (cloud round-trip)** | $L_{total} = 2 \times L_{prop} + 4 \times L_{proc} + L_{queue}$ | typical **200–500 ms** Wi-Fi |
| **Sensor sampling theorem** | $f_{s} \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Hz |
| **AES-128 block size** | 128 bits | Default for smart home |
| **CoAP message overhead** | **4 bytes** header | vs MQTT ~**2 byte** fixed + variable |
| **Typical home Wi-Fi bandwidth per device** | $\approx 5$–$10$ Mbps for HD camera | Concurrent video streams cap at **~8 cameras on 100 Mbps link** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule:** All absolute values in formulas above use `\vert ... \vert` and `\mid` to prevent markdown-table breaking.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Auditing:** Smart thermostats (Nest) save **10–15 %** of HVAC energy.
- **Elderly Care:** Wearable + motion sensors detect falls → SMS to caregiver (avg response < 30 s).
- **Insurance:** Smart water-leak sensors reduce **home insurance premiums by 5–20 %**.
- **Industrial-grade deployments:** Used as testbeds for **digital twin** research.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Battery Lifetime of a Wireless Sensor Node

**Problem Statement:** A ZigBee door sensor uses **two AA alkaline cells (2500 mAh each, 1.5 V in series = 3 V)**. Average current draw is **30 µA in sleep, 25 mA for 200 ms every 5 minutes** when transmitting. Calculate the **battery life in years**.

**Given Data:**
- $C_{battery} = 2 \times 2500 = 5000$ mAh
- $V = 3$ V (series)
- Sleep current $I_{sleep} = 30$ µA = $0.030$ mA
- Active current $I_{active} = 25$ mA
- Active duration per cycle $t_{active} = 200$ ms
- Cycle period $T = 5$ min = $300$ s = $300{,}000$ ms

**Step 1 — Compute duty cycle of active mode**

$$
D = \frac{t_{active}}{T} = \frac{200}{300{,}000} = 6.67 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average current consumption**

$$
I_{avg} = I_{sleep} + D \times (I_{active} - I_{sleep})
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.030 + (6.67 \times 10^{-4}) \times (25 - 0.030)
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.030 + 6.67 \times 10^{-4} \times 24.97 = 0.030 + 0.01665
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.04665 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute battery life in hours**

$$
T_{life,h} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{5000}{0.04665} = 1.072 \times 10^{5} \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years**

$$
T_{life,y} = \frac{1.072 \times 10^{5}}{24 \times 365} = \frac{1.072 \times 10^{5}}{8760} \approx 12.24 \text{ years}
$$

**Conclusion:** The ZigBee door sensor can operate for **~12.2 years** on two AA cells — a key selling point in KTU exam scenarios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** If the question states "transmit current **15 mA**" instead of 25 mA, recompute — many students lose marks by using $I_{active}$ directly without subtracting sleep current.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MQTT vs CoAP Overhead Comparison

**Problem:** A temperature sensor reports **"T=24.5C"** (7 bytes payload) every 10 s. Compare the **per-message byte overhead** of MQTT vs CoAP over IPv6.

**MQTT CONNECT + PUBLISH overhead (TLS over TCP/IPv6):**
- Fixed CONNECT/CONNACK: ~50 bytes (one-time per session — amortized)
- Fixed PUBLISH header: **2 bytes**
- Topic name: assume "home/lr/temp" = **13 bytes**
- TLS overhead per record: ~20–30 bytes

$$
\text{Per-message MQTT} = 2 + 13 + 7 + 25 = 47 \text{ bytes (steady-state, approx.)}
$$

**CoAP POST overhead (DTLS over UDP/IPv6):**
- CoAP header: **4 bytes**
- UDP header: **8 bytes**
- IPv6 header: **40 bytes**
- DTLS overhead: ~13–25 bytes

$$
\text{Per-message CoAP} = 4 + 8 + 40 + 7 + 13 = 72 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Conclusion:** For this short payload, **MQTT is more compact on the wire**, but CoAP avoids TCP handshakes and is preferred for **sleepy, battery-constrained** nodes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Smart Home Sub-Systems

| Sub-system | Primary Sensors | Primary Actuators | Cloud Logic | Latency Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lighting Control** | LDR, PIR | Smart bulb (Tuya, Philips Hue) | Daylight-based dimming, geo-fencing | < 500 ms |
| **HVAC / Climate** | DHT22, thermistor | Smart thermostat (relay) | ML schedule, occupancy prediction | < 2 s |
| **Security / Surveillance** | PIR, door reed, IP camera | Siren, smart lock | Intrusion detection, face recognition | < 200 ms (alarm) |
| **Fire / Gas Safety** | MQ-2, MQ-5, smoke, temp | Solenoid valve, exhaust fan | Auto-shutoff, SMS alert | < 100 ms |
| **Energy Management** | CT clamp, smart plug | Relay, dimmer | Time-of-use optimization, solar sync | < 5 s |
| **Elderly Care** | Wrist fall sensor, PIR | Auto-light, voice prompt | Activity-pattern anomaly | < 1 s |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Indoor Path Loss for a Smart Home

**Problem:** A Wi-Fi AP transmits at **$P_{tx} = 20$ dBm**. Reference path loss at $d_0 = 1$ m is $PL(d_0) = 40$ dB. Path-loss exponent $n = 3$ (typical indoor with 2 walls). Find the RSSI at $d = 8$ m.

**Log-distance model:**

$$
PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\,n\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)
$$

$$
PL(8) = 40 + 10 \times 3 \times \log_{10}(8/1)
$$

$$
PL(8) = 40 + 30 \times \log_{10}(8) = 40 + 30 \times 0.903 = 40 + 27.09
$$

$$
PL(8) = 67.09 \text{ dB}
$$

**RSSI assuming unity antenna gains:**

$$
RSSI = P_{tx} - PL(d) = 20 - 67.09 = -47.09 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The signal is well above the typical Wi-Fi sensitivity of **-80 dBm** → robust link.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Smart Home IoT Architecture

## 4.2 Smart Home Communication Protocol Stack

```mermaid

    L1[Application Layer: MQTT / CoAP / HTTP]
    L2[Transport Layer: TCP for MQTT, UDP for CoAP]
    L3[Network Layer: IPv4 / IPv6]

    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4

```

## 4.3 Fire-Safety Sub-System (Detailed Case Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[MQ-2 Gas Sensor] -->|Analog mV| ESP1[ESP32 ADC]
    S2[Smoke Photoelectric] -->|Digital| ESP1
    S3[Temp DS18B20] -->|1-Wire| ESP1

    ESP1 -->|Threshold 800 ppm| DEC1{Decision Logic}
    DEC1 -->|YES| ACT1[Activate Siren]
    DEC1 -->|YES| ACT2[Open Solenoid Valve]
    DEC1 -->|YES| ACT3[Cut Gas via Relay]
    DEC1 -->|YES| MQTT1[MQTT Publish emergency/ home]
    DEC1 -->|NO| WAIT[Wait 5 seconds]

    MQTT1 --> CLOUD1[AWS IoT Core]
    CLOUD1 --> SMS[SMS via Twilio to Owner]
    CLOUD1 --> CALL[Auto-call Fire Dept]
    CLOUD1 --> APP[Push Notification to App]

    WAIT --> S1

    style S1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style S2 fill:#ffe1e1
    style S3 fill:#ffe1e1
```
