```mermaid
    style ACT1 fill:#ff9999
    style ACT2 fill:#ff9999
    style ACT3 fill:#ff9999
    style CLOUD1 fill:#e1ffe1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Smart Home. List any four IoT protocols commonly used in a smart home deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Smart Home** is an IoT-enabled residential environment in which sensors, actuators, communication networks, and intelligent controllers are integrated to enable **context-aware automation, remote monitoring, and energy-efficient management** of household functions such as lighting, climate, security, and appliance control.

Four IoT protocols commonly used in smart homes (1/2 mark each):

1. **MQTT** — Lightweight publish-subscribe messaging over TCP, used between sensors and cloud.
2. **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** — Low-power mesh protocol for short-range device communication.
3. **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** — High-throughput protocol for IP cameras and voice assistants.
4. **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.0)** — Ultra-low-power protocol for wearables and proximity locks.

*(Bonus point: Z-Wave is also acceptable.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Compare MQTT and CoAP for smart home application in terms of transport protocol, message overhead, and QoS model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) | UDP (lightweight, connectionless) |
| **Architecture** | Publish / Subscribe via broker | Request / Response (REST-like) |
| **Header overhead** | ~2 bytes fixed + topic | 4 bytes fixed |
| **QoS levels** | **3 levels** (0, 1, 2) | **2 levels** (CON, NON) + token matching |
| **Best for** | Stable mains-powered nodes | Sleepy battery-powered sensors |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module-Internal Choice Note:** KTU ESE requires students to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-layer architecture of an IoT-based Smart Home system**. Explain the function of each layer with suitable examples of devices and protocols.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ZigBee-based smart home has **15 battery-operated temperature sensors** reporting every 30 seconds. Each sensor uses **2 × AA cells (2500 mAh, 1.5 V series = 3 V)**. Sleep current is **20 µA**, transmit current is **20 mA** for **100 ms** per transmission. Estimate the **battery life in years**.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (a)

**Block Diagram (Already shown in Section 4.1)** — Award 2 marks for diagram.

**Layered Explanation (5 marks):**

**1. Perception Layer (Sensing):** Contains physical sensors that capture environmental data.
- *Examples:* DHT22 (temperature+humidity), PIR (motion), MQ-2 (gas), reed switch (door).
- *Function:* Converts physical phenomena → electrical/digital signals.

**2. Network Layer (Connectivity):** Transports sensor data to the gateway or cloud.
- *Protocols:* ZigBee, Wi-Fi, BLE, Z-Wave.
- *Function:* Provides reliable, low-power data transfer.

**3. Middleware / Edge Layer:** Performs local data aggregation, rule-based decisions, and protocol translation.
- *Tools:* Node-RED, Home Assistant, AWS Greengrass.
- *Function:* Reduces latency and cloud dependency.

**4. Application Layer (Cloud + UI):** Provides storage, analytics, dashboards, and user control.
- *Platforms:* AWS IoT Core, Blynk, Google Firebase.
- *Function:* Long-term learning, remote access via mobile/voice.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (b)

**Given:**
- $C = 5000$ mAh, $V = 3$ V
- $I_{sleep} = 20$ µA = $0.020$ mA
- $I_{active} = 20$ mA
- $t_{active} = 100$ ms
- $T = 30$ s = $30{,}000$ ms

**Step 1: Duty cycle**

$$
D = \frac{100}{30{,}000} = 3.33 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2: Average current**

$$
I_{avg} = 0.020 + (3.33 \times 10^{-3}) \times (20 - 0.020) = 0.020 + 0.0665 = 0.0865 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3: Battery life in hours**

$$
T_h = \frac{5000}{0.0865} = 5.78 \times 10^{4} \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: In years**

$$
T_y = \frac{5.78 \times 10^{4}}{8760} \approx 6.6 \text{ years}
$$

**Answer:** Battery life ≈ **6.6 years** per sensor.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **MQTT protocol** in a smart home system. Explain its **publish-subscribe model**, **QoS levels**, and the use of a **broker** with a suitable diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain any **three security challenges** in smart home IoT deployments and list the **standard countermeasures** for each.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (a)

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport):** Lightweight pub/sub protocol over TCP, ideal for low-bandwidth, unreliable home Wi-Fi links. **(1 mark)**

**Publish-Subscribe Model:** Decouples publishers (sensors) from subscribers (mobile app, cloud). All communication passes through a central **MQTT broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ). **(2 marks)**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Temp Sensor Publisher] -->|publish topic home/bedroom/temp| B[MQTT Broker Mosquitto]
    P2[Motion Sensor Publisher] -->|publish topic home/entry/motion| B
    B -->|subscribe| S1[Mobile App]
    B -->|subscribe| S2[Cloud Logger]
    B -->|subscribe| S3[Alexa Routine]
```

**QoS Levels (3 marks):**
- **QoS 0 — At most once:** Fire-and-forget. Used for non-critical periodic telemetry.
- **QoS 1 — At least once:** PUBACK handshake; duplicates possible. Default for sensor data.
- **QoS 2 — Exactly once:** 4-step handshake (PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP). Used for billing, door-lock commands.

**Broker Role:** The broker is the central router — it filters messages by **topic**, manages **client sessions**, and enforces **access control**.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (b)

**Three Security Challenges & Countermeasures:**

**1. Weak / Default Passwords (1 mark + 1 mark)**
- *Challenge:* Many smart bulbs, IP cameras ship with default credentials (`admin/admin`).
- *Countermeasure:* Enforce **unique strong passwords**, **2FA**, and **forced password reset** on first login.

**2. Eavesdropping on Wireless Traffic (1 mark + 1 mark)**
- *Challenge:* ZigBee/BLE packets can be sniffed, leaking occupancy patterns.
- *Countermeasure:* Use **AES-128 link-layer encryption** (ZigBee) and **TLS/DTLS** at the transport layer.

**3. Insecure Firmware / Lack of Updates (1 mark + 1 mark)**
- *Challenge:* Outdated firmware has known CVEs (e.g., Mirai botnet exploited IP cameras).
- *Countermeasure:* **Signed OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** with **secure boot** and **code signing**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Total ≈ 4–6 marks lost per paper):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4-layer model** — Many students write only "sensors + cloud". Always name **Perception → Network → Middleware → Application** explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing MQTT with CoAP transport** — MQTT uses **TCP**, CoAP uses **UDP**. Mixing these loses 1 mark instantly.
> 3. **Duty cycle miscalculation** — In battery-life problems, students often use $I_{active}$ as the average current. **Always subtract** sleep current and multiply by duty cycle.
> 4. **No security discussion** — A smart-home answer without **AES-128 / TLS / OTA updates** is considered incomplete for full marks.
> 5. **QoS levels confusion** — Quality of Service in MQTT is **0, 1, 2** (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once). Do NOT write "high, medium, low".
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — A 7-mark sub-part without a diagram typically receives **5/7 maximum**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Smart Home** = 4-layer IoT architecture applied to residential automation.
- ✅ **Key Layers:** Perception (sensors) → Network (ZigBee/Wi-Fi/BLE) → Middleware (Edge) → Application (Cloud + UI).
- ✅ **MQTT** = TCP-based, pub/sub, **3 QoS levels (0,1,2)**, low overhead (~2 byte header).
- ✅ **CoAP** = UDP-based, req/resp, **4 byte header**, ideal for sleepy battery nodes.
- ✅ **ZigBee** = 2.4 GHz, mesh, **250 kbps**, used for lights/locks/switches.
- ✅ **Z-Wave** = 908.42 MHz, mesh, **100 kbps**, less interference than 2.4 GHz.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi** = high throughput, mains-powered devices, IP cameras.
- ✅ **BLE 5.0** = ultra-low-power, used in wearables, beacons, smart locks.
- ✅ **Cloud Platforms:** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, Blynk, ThingsBoard.
- ✅ **Battery-life formula:** $T_{life} = \dfrac{C}{I_{sleep} + D \times (I_{active} - I_{sleep})}$.
- ✅ **Log-distance path loss:** $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$.
- ✅ **Security triad:** Confidentiality (AES-128) + Integrity (TLS/DTLS) + Authenticity (OAuth 2.0, certificates).
- ✅ **OTA updates** with **secure boot** are mandatory for production deployments.
- ✅ **Standard latency budgets:** Local < 100 ms, Cloud 200 ms – 2 s, Voice assistants 1–3 s.
- ✅ **Energy savings** of smart thermostats: typically **10–15 %**.
- ✅ **Common exam keywords:** Context-aware, pub/sub, mesh, gateway, edge, OTA, AES-128, QoS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Parking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Smart Parking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology)**
> **Smart Parking** is an IoT-based intelligent transportation system that uses a network of sensors, wireless communication technologies, cloud computing, and data analytics to detect, monitor, and manage the availability of parking spaces in real-time. It is a critical application domain of **Smart City** infrastructure under the broader umbrella of the **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** and **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**.

### 1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving into a massive shopping mall on a busy Saturday afternoon. In a **traditional parking system**, you have no idea which floor or section has empty slots — you waste **15–20 minutes** circling around, burning fuel, and getting frustrated. The display boards are static and often outdated.

Now, picture a **Smart Parking system**: Before you even arrive, a mobile app tells you *"Level B2, Slot 47 is free — book it for ₹20"*. As you approach, a digital sign with a **green arrow** guides you directly to that exact spot. Above your slot, a tiny **red LED** turns **green** the moment you park, and the system automatically deducts the fee, sends an e-receipt, and even reminds you where you parked when you return.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Smart Parking transforms a passive, blind, and frustrating infrastructure into an **active, data-driven, user-centric service** by closing the loop between *physical parking assets* and *digital information systems* using IoT.

### 1.2 Core Components of a Smart Parking Ecosystem

A Smart Parking system is composed of **four tightly integrated layers**:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors):** Ultrasonic, Infrared (IR), Inductive Loop, Camera-based, or LiDAR sensors that detect vehicle presence.
2. **Network Layer (Connectivity):** Wireless protocols such as **Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, BLE**, and cellular (4G/5G) for transmitting sensor data.
3. **Processing Layer (Edge/Cloud):** Microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi) and cloud platforms (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT) for data aggregation, analytics, and storage.
4. **Application Layer (User Interface):** Mobile apps, web dashboards, digital signboards, and APIs that deliver real-time parking information to end-users.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Smart Parking (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Metrics to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **Parking Occupancy Rate (POR):** Percentage of occupied slots at a given time.
> - **Average Search Time (AST):** Time taken by a driver to find a vacant slot (target: **< 3 minutes**).
> - **Parking Turnover Rate (PTR):** Number of vehicles served per slot per day.
> - **Detection Accuracy (DA):** Percentage of correctly identified occupied/vacant states (target: **≥ 98%**).
> - **System Latency (SL):** Time between physical event and digital update (target: **< 2 seconds**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

**Step 1: Sensing & Detection (Perception Layer)**
- Each parking slot is fitted with a **wireless sensor node**.
- When a vehicle parks, the sensor detects the mass/metal/vision signature and toggles its state from `0` (vacant) to `1` (occupied).
- The microcontroller (e.g., **ESP32**) reads this state and prepares a data packet.

**Step 2: Data Encoding & Transmission (Network Layer)**
- The data packet typically contains: *Node ID, Slot ID, Timestamp, Status Byte, Battery Voltage*.
- This packet is transmitted over a **lightweight IoT protocol** like **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** over **TCP/IP**, or **CoAP** for constrained networks.

**Step 3: Edge Processing & Cloud Aggregation (Processing Layer)**
- At the edge, a **gateway** (Raspberry Pi or industrial gateway) collects data from hundreds of sensor nodes using protocols like ZigBee or LoRa.
- The gateway forwards aggregated data to the **cloud**, where analytics engines compute availability, predict demand, and update the database.

**Step 4: User Notification & Action (Application Layer)**
- Users receive updates via **mobile apps, SMS, or in-car navigation**.
- The system may also integrate with **payment gateways** for automated billing and with **smart traffic lights** for dynamic routing.

### 2.2 Sensor Technology Comparison Table

| Sensor Type | Detection Principle | Accuracy | Cost (Relative) | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ultrasonic** | Sound wave reflection (Time-of-Flight) | $\pm 2$ cm | Low | Indoor slot-by-slot detection |
| **Infrared (IR)** | Beam interruption | Moderate | Very Low | Entry/Exit gates |
| **Inductive Loop** | Electromagnetic induction in ground coil | Very High | High | Outdoor, high-traffic |
| **Camera + CV** | Image processing (OpenCV, YOLO) | High (with good lighting) | High | Large area monitoring |
| **Magnetometer** | Earth's magnetic field disturbance | Moderate | Low | Underground, multi-floor |
| **LiDAR** | Laser pulse reflection | Very High | Very High | Premium smart lots |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables Explained | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parking Occupancy Rate (POR)** | $\text{POR} = \frac{N_{occ}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{occ}$ = occupied slots, $N_{total}$ = total slots | Percentage (%) |
| **Vacancy Rate (VR)** | $\text{VR} = 1 - \text{POR} = \frac{N_{vac}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{vac}$ = vacant slots | Decimal (0 to 1) |
| **System Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \frac{T_{target}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | $T_{target}$ = ideal time, $T_{actual}$ = observed time | Percentage (%) |
| **Parking Turnover Rate** | $\text{PTR} = \frac{N_{vehicles\_served}}{N_{slots} \times T_{period}}$ | $N_{vehicles}$ = total served, $T_{period}$ = time in days | vehicles/slot/day |
| **Average Search Time** | $\text{AST} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i$ | $t_i$ = search time of $i^{th}$ driver, $N$ = sample size | minutes |
| **Revenue per Slot** | $\text{RPS} = \text{PTR} \times \text{Rate} \times T_{period}$ | Rate = hourly parking rate | Currency |
| **Time-of-Flight (Ultrasonic)** | $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | $v$ = speed of sound ($\approx 343$ m/s), $t$ = round-trip time | meters (m) |
| **Detection Confidence** | $C = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | TP=True Positive, TN=True Negative, FP, FN | Decimal (0 to 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are not just academic — they are the **KPIs (Key Performance Indicators)** that smart city administrators and parking operators use to **optimize revenue, reduce congestion, and justify ROI** on IoT infrastructure investments. For example, a 1% reduction in average search time across a city can save **tonnes of CO₂ emissions** annually.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Distance Calculation

This is the foundational physics behind ultrasonic parking sensors.

**Given:**
- Speed of sound in air at room temperature: $v \approx 343$ m/s
- Measured round-trip time of ultrasonic pulse: $t$ seconds

**Derivation Steps:**

The sensor emits an ultrasonic pulse that travels to the obstacle (car) and reflects back. The total distance covered is $2d$ (down and back).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total distance covered} &= v \cdot t \\
2d &= v \cdot t \\
d &= \frac{v \cdot t}{2} \\
d &= \frac{343 \cdot t}{2} \\
d &= 171.5 \cdot t \quad \text{(in meters)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The factor of 2 appears because the pulse travels to the car **and** back. The speed of sound in air varies with temperature, so for high-precision systems, a correction formula is applied: $v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ (where $T$ is in °C).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Parking Occupancy Rate with Multi-Zone Aggregation

For a smart parking lot with $Z$ zones, each containing $n_i$ slots:

**Step 1:** Define occupancy of each zone.
$$
\text{POR}_i = \frac{N_{occ,i}}{n_i}
$$

**Step 2:** The global occupancy is the weighted average of all zones.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POR}_{global} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{Z} N_{occ,i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{Z} n_i} \\
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{Z} (n_i \cdot \text{POR}_i)}{N_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Variance of occupancy across zones (used to detect anomalies).
$$
\sigma^2_{POR} = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{i=1}^{Z} (\text{POR}_i - \overline{\text{POR}})^2
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A smart parking facility has **3 zones** with the following data:

| Zone | Total Slots | Occupied Slots | Hourly Rate (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 50 | 40 | 30 |
| B | 30 | 15 | 50 |
| C | 70 | 60 | 25 |

The facility operates for **12 hours/day** with a parking turnover of **2.5 vehicles/slot/day**. Calculate the **(a)** global occupancy rate, **(b)** daily revenue, and **(c)** system efficiency if the target time is 3 minutes and actual search time is 4.5 minutes.

**Solution:**

**(a) Global Occupancy Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 50 + 30 + 70 = 150 \\
N_{occ} &= 40 + 15 + 60 = 115 \\
\text{POR}_{global} &= \frac{115}{150} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.7667 \times 100\% \\
&= 76.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Daily Revenue Calculation:**

First, compute the revenue per slot for each zone:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPS}_A &= 2.5 \times 30 \times 12 = 900 \text{ ₹/slot/day} \\
\text{RPS}_B &= 2.5 \times 50 \times 12 = 1500 \text{ ₹/slot/day} \\
\text{RPS}_C &= 2.5 \times 25 \times 12 = 750 \text{ ₹/slot/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total daily revenue (assuming all slots have the given turnover):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue} &= (50 \times 900) + (30 \times 1500) + (70 \times 750) \\
&= 45000 + 45000 + 52500 \\
&= 142500 \text{ ₹/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) System Efficiency:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{T_{target}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{3}{4.5} \times 100\% \\
&= 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A system efficiency of **66.67%** indicates that the current Smart Parking system is **underperforming** by 33.33%. The operator should consider deploying more sensors, improving the mobile app's route guidance, or using **predictive analytics** to pre-allocate slots.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Smart Parking vs. Traditional Parking

| Parameter | Traditional Parking | Smart Parking (IoT) | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Average Search Time** | 10–15 min | 1–3 min | **5× to 7×** |
| **Fuel Wasted per Search** | ~0.5 L | ~0.05 L | **10×** |
| **CO₂ Emissions** | High | Reduced by 60–70% | **3×** |
| **Occupancy Data** | Manual, delayed | Real-time, automatic | Real-time |
| **Payment** | Cash, manual | Digital, automated | Seamless |
| **Revenue Leakage** | 20–30% | < 5% | **4× to 6×** |
| **User Experience** | Poor | Personalized | Qualitative |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Parking Slot 1\nUltrasonic Sensor] -->|ZigBee/MQTT| node4[Edge Gateway\nRaspberry Pi]
    node2[Parking Slot 2\nIR Sensor] -->|ZigBee/MQTT| node4
    node3[Parking Slot N\nCamera Module] -->|Wi-Fi/Ethernet| node4
    node4 -->|HTTPS/MQTT| node5[Cloud Platform\nAWS IoT Core]
    node5 --> node6[Database\nTimeSeries DB]
    node5 --> node7[Analytics Engine\nML Prediction]
    node6 --> node8[Mobile App\nAndroid/iOS]
    node7 --> node8
    node6 --> node9[Web Dashboard\nAdmin Panel]
    node8 --> node10[User Mobile Device]
    node9 --> node11[Parking Operator]
    node5 --> node12[Payment Gateway\nRazorpay/Stripe]
    node8 --> node12
    node5 --> node13[Digital Signboard\nAt Entry/Exit]
    node5 --> node14[City Traffic\nManagement API]
```

> **Diagram Explanation:** This architecture shows the complete data lifecycle — from physical sensors at individual parking slots, through edge aggregation, to cloud processing, and finally to multiple consumer endpoints (users, operators, city systems).

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — End-to-End Smart Parking Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User (Mobile App)
    participant A as App Server
    participant C as Cloud Database
    participant G as Edge Gateway
    participant S as Slot Sensor
    participant P as Payment Gateway

    U->>A: Request nearest available slot
    A->>C: Query real-time vacancy
    C-->>A: Return slot info (e.g., B2-47)
    A-->>U: Display slot on map with ETA
    U->>A: Reserve slot B2-47
    A->>C: Lock slot for 10 minutes
    U->>S: Arrives and parks at B2-47
    S->>G: Detect vehicle (Status = Occupied)
    G->>C: Update database (B2-47 = 1)
    C->>A: Trigger payment session
    A->>P: Initiate payment (₹20)
    P-->>A: Payment success
    A-->>U: Send e-receipt
    S->>G: Vehicle leaves (Status = Vacant)
    G->>C: Update database (B2-47 = 0)
    C->>A: Free slot for next user
```

### 4.3 Sensor Decision Tree (Detection Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Vehicle Approaches Slot] --> nodeB{Sensor Type?}
    nodeB -->|Ultrasonic| nodeC[Measure Distance d]
    nodeC --> nodeD{d < Threshold?}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Status = OCCUPIED]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeF[Status = VACANT]
    nodeB -->|IR Beam| nodeG[Beam Interrupted?]
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeE
    nodeG -->|No| nodeF
    nodeB -->|Camera + CV| nodeH[Run YOLO Detection]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Vehicle Confidence > 0.8?}
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeE
    nodeI -->|No| nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Update Database and Notify Users]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Mark Slot as Available]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define Smart Parking. List any FOUR key components of an IoT-based Smart Parking system.**

**Model Answer:**
Smart Parking is an IoT-enabled intelligent system that uses sensors, wireless communication, and cloud computing to detect, monitor, and manage the real-time availability of parking spaces in urban environments.

**Four Key Components:**
1. **Sensor Layer** — Ultrasonic, IR, or camera-based sensors to detect vehicle presence.
2. **Communication Layer** — Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, or NB-IoT for data transmission.
3. **Processing Layer** — Edge gateways and cloud platforms for data aggregation and analytics.
4. **Application Layer** — Mobile apps, web dashboards, and digital signboards for user interaction.

*(Word limit: ~80 words | Marks: 3 — 1 for definition + 2 for components)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the concept of Parking Occupancy Rate (POR). Why is it considered a critical KPI in smart city deployments?**

**Model Answer:**
**Parking Occupancy Rate (POR)** is defined as the ratio of currently occupied parking slots to the total available slots, expressed as a percentage.

**Mathematical Expression:**
$$
\text{POR} = \frac{N_{occ}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

**Why it is a Critical KPI:**
1. **Demand Forecasting:** Historical POR data helps predict peak hours and dynamically adjust pricing.
2. **Resource Optimization:** City planners use POR to decide where to build new parking infrastructure.
3. **Revenue Management:** Operators can implement **dynamic pricing** based on real-time POR.
4. **Environmental Impact:** High POR with poor guidance causes fuel wastage; tracking POR helps reduce emissions.

*(Word limit: ~100 words | Marks: 3 — 1 for formula + 2 for explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

> **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-layer architecture of an IoT-based Smart Parking system**. Explain the function of each layer in detail. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A smart parking lot has **4 zones** with capacities of **40, 60, 50, and 30 slots** and current occupancies of **32, 45, 25, and 28 slots** respectively. Calculate: **(i)** global occupancy rate, **(ii)** zone-wise vacancy rate, and **(iii)** the standard deviation of occupancy across zones. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Four-Layer Architecture (7 Marks):**

The IoT Smart Parking system follows a standard four-layer architecture:

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Sensors):**
This is the physical layer where sensors are deployed at each parking slot. Common sensors include ultrasonic sensors (using time-of-flight), infrared sensors (beam interruption), inductive loops (electromagnetic), and cameras (computer vision). These sensors convert the physical presence of a vehicle into an electrical signal.

**Layer 2 — Network Layer (Communication):**
The sensor data is transmitted wirelessly using protocols like **ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, BLE, or Wi-Fi**. The communication must be low-power, reliable, and capable of supporting hundreds of nodes. MQTT is the most common application-layer protocol for message exchange.

**Layer 3 — Processing Layer (Edge + Cloud Computing):**
At the edge, gateways (e.g., Raspberry Pi) aggregate data from local sensor clusters. In the cloud (AWS IoT, Azure), data is stored in time-series databases, and analytics engines (including ML models) predict future occupancy, detect anomalies, and compute KPIs.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (User Interface):**
End-users interact via mobile apps, SMS alerts, or in-car navigation. Operators use web dashboards. The system also interfaces with **payment gateways, traffic management systems, and digital signboards** for a complete service experience.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for diagram, 6 for explanation — 1.5 marks per layer)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**

| Zone | Total Slots ($n_i$) | Occupied ($N_{occ,i}$) | Vacant ($N_{vac,i}$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40 | 32 | 8 |
| 2 | 60 | 45 | 15 |
| 3 | 50 | 25 | 25 |
| 4 | 30 | 28 | 2 |

**(i) Global Occupancy Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 40 + 60 + 50 + 30 = 180 \\
N_{occ} &= 32 + 45 + 25 + 28 = 130 \\
\text{POR}_{global} &= \frac{130}{180} \times 100\% = 72.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Zone-wise Vacancy Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VR}_1 &= \frac{8}{40} = 0.20 = 20\% \\
\text{VR}_2 &= \frac{15}{60} = 0.25 = 25\% \\
\text{VR}_3 &= \frac{25}{50} = 0.50 = 50\% \\
\text{VR}_4 &= \frac{2}{30} = 0.0667 = 6.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Standard Deviation of Occupancy:**

First, compute zone-wise occupancy rates:
$$
\text{POR}_1 = 80\%, \quad \text{POR}_2 = 75\%, \quad \text{POR}_3 = 50\%, \quad \text{POR}_4 = 93.33\%
$$

Mean occupancy:
$$
\overline{\text{POR}} = \frac{80 + 75 + 50 + 93.33}{4} = \frac{298.33}{4} = 74.58\%
$$

Variance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{4} \left[(80-74.58)^2 + (75-74.58)^2 + (50-74.58)^2 + (93.33-74.58)^2\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \left[29.38 + 0.18 + 605.62 + 351.56\right] \\
&= \frac{986.74}{4} = 246.69
\end{aligned}
$$

Standard deviation:
$$
\sigma = \sqrt{246.69} \approx 15.71\%
$$

> **Insight:** A high standard deviation (**15.71%**) indicates **uneven load distribution** — Zone 4 is overcrowded while Zone 3 is underutilized. The system should implement **dynamic routing** to redirect users to vacant zones.

---

> **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Compare and contrast the **sensor technologies** used in Smart Parking systems. Discuss the advantages and limitations of **ultrasonic, camera-based, and inductive loop** sensors. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **MQTT protocol** in detail. How is it used in Smart Parking for communication between sensors, gateways, and the cloud? Draw a publish-subscribe diagram. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Sensor Technology Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Ultrasonic | Camera + CV | Inductive Loop |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Sound wave ToF | Image processing | EM induction |
| **Cost per Slot** | Low (~₹200) | High (~₹2000) | High (~₹1500) |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 2$ cm | 95–99% | 99%+ |
| **Weather Impact** | Low | High (rain/fog) | None |
| **Installation** | Easy, above-ground | Moderate | Requires road cutting |
| **Power** | Low | High | None (passive) |
| **Best For** | Indoor lots | Open outdoor lots | High-traffic roads |

**Ultrasonic** is cost-effective and easy to deploy but suffers from soft-surface reflections. **Camera-based** systems provide rich data (license plate, vehicle type) but require high processing power and are sensitive to lighting. **Inductive loops** are extremely reliable and weather-proof but require **destructive road installation**, making retrofitting expensive.

**(b) MQTT Protocol (7 Marks):**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging protocol designed for constrained IoT devices and low-bandwidth networks. It runs over TCP/IP and uses a **broker-based architecture**.

**Key Components:**
- **Publisher:** The sensor node that sends data.
- **Broker:** A central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) that receives and distributes messages.
- **Subscriber:** The cloud application or dashboard that receives data.
- **Topic:** A hierarchical string like `parking/zoneA/slot47/status`.

**MQTT in Smart Parking:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Slot Sensor\nPublisher] -->|Publishes to\nparking/zoneA/slot47| node3[MQTT Broker\nMosquitto]
    node2[Gateway\nPublisher] -->|Publishes aggregated data| node3
    node3 -->|Subscribed topic| node4[Cloud Analytics\nSubscriber]
    node3 -->|Subscribed topic| node5[Mobile App Backend\nSubscriber]
    node3 -->|Subscribed topic| node6[Digital Signboard\nSubscriber]
```

**Quality of Service (QoS) Levels in MQTT:**
- **QoS 0:** At most once (fire-and-forget) — used for non-critical data.
- **QoS 1:** At least once — used for parking status (default choice).
- **QoS 2:** Exactly once — used for payment confirmations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write "Smart Parking uses GPS" — **GPS does NOT work indoors** in underground parking. Always specify **short-range IoT protocols** like ZigBee/BLE for indoor and **NB-IoT/LoRa** for outdoor.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **POR** (Occupancy Rate) with **PTR** (Turnover Rate). POR is a *snapshot*, while PTR is a *flow metric over time*.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In numerical problems, students forget to multiply the **factor of 2** in the ultrasonic distance formula. The pulse travels to the car AND back.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "MQTT uses HTTP" — **MQTT runs over TCP, not HTTP**. They are completely different protocols.
> 5. **Mark Loss Alert:** In 14-mark questions, students skip the **diagram** and lose 1–2 marks. Always include a **neat block diagram** even if not explicitly asked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Smart Parking**

- **Definition:** Smart Parking is an IoT system for real-time detection, monitoring, and management of parking slot availability.
- **Architecture:** 4 layers — **Perception → Network → Processing → Application**.
- **Key Sensors:** Ultrasonic (ToF), IR, Inductive Loop, Camera + CV, Magnetometer, LiDAR.
- **Communication Protocols:** ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, BLE, Wi-Fi, MQTT (app layer).
- **Core Formula 1 (POR):** $\text{POR} = \frac{N_{occ}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$
- **Core Formula 2 (Ultrasonic):** $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$ with $v \approx 343$ m/s.
- **Core Formula 3 (Efficiency):** $\eta = \frac{T_{target}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\%$
- **Core Formula 4 (Revenue):** $\text{RPS} = \text{PTR} \times \text{Rate} \times T_{hours}$
- **MQTT:** Lightweight **publish-subscribe** protocol using a **broker** (Mosquitto is the most popular open-source broker).
- **KPIs to Memorize:** POR, AST, PTR, DA (Detection Accuracy $\geq 98\%$), SL (System Latency $< 2$s).
- **Smart Parking Benefits:** 5–7× faster search, 60–70% less CO₂, 4–6× less revenue leakage.
- **Common Use Cases:** Shopping malls, airports, hospitals, smart cities, university campuses.
- **Edge vs. Cloud:** Use **edge processing** for real-time decisions (e.g., turning a LED green) and **cloud** for analytics and prediction.
- **Security:** Always use **TLS/SSL encryption**, **device authentication**, and **token-based APIs** (e.g., JWT) to prevent unauthorized slot manipulation.
- **Integration:** Smart Parking integrates with **GPS navigation, traffic lights, e-payment systems, and city dashboards** for holistic smart city functioning.

> **End of Module 4 — Smart Parking Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OEITT723 — Internet of Things*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT723 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT in Transportation** refers to the integration of physical vehicles, road infrastructure, and backend computing platforms through a network of sensors, actuators, and communication protocols to enable real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, autonomous decision-making, and intelligent control across the entire transportation ecosystem. It is formally recognized under the broader umbrella of **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** as defined by the U.S. Department of Transportation and ISO 14813 series.

The KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT723, Module 4) categorizes transportation as a **high-impact IoT vertical** that transforms traditional vehicular systems into *Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)*. The core architectural pattern follows a four-layer paradigm: **Sensing → Connectivity → Processing → Application**.

### 1.2 Key Sub-Domains Within IoT-Transportation

| Sub-Domain | KTU 2024 Weight | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** | High | Macro-level traffic optimization |
| **Connected Vehicles (CV)** | High | Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) data exchange |
| **Autonomous Driving (AD)** | Medium | Self-navigating vehicles using sensor fusion |
| **Fleet & Asset Management** | Medium | Real-time logistics visibility |
| **Smart Parking** | Low | Sensor-driven space detection |
| **Electronic Toll Collection (ETC)** | Low | RFID/ANPR-based automated billing |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Orchestra Conductor":** Imagine a city as a giant orchestra. Without a conductor, each musician (vehicle) plays independently, leading to noise, collisions, and chaos. IoT in Transportation acts as an *omnipresent conductor* — every instrument (car, traffic light, road sensor) is fitted with a microphone (sensor) that tells the conductor (cloud/edge platform) exactly what is being played. The conductor instantly signals back (*via 5G/DSRC*) which section should slow down, speed up, or yield. The result: harmony, safety, and efficiency. This "conductor" is the **IoT control plane**.

### 1.4 Core Metrics and Industry Standards

- **Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC):** Operates in the **5.9 GHz** band with a typical range of **300 m** and latency **< 10 ms** (IEEE 802.11p).
- **Cellular-V2X (C-V2X):** Operates over **4G LTE / 5G NR** with latency **< 20 ms** and range up to **1 km**.
- **5G URLLC Target Latency:** **1 ms** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication) — critical for autonomous vehicle safety.
- **GPS Positional Accuracy (民用):** **< 2.5 m** CEP (Circular Error Probable) under clear sky.
- **IEEE 802.11p Standard:** Approved in 2010, governs WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments).
- **SAE J2735:** Standard for **Basic Safety Messages (BSM)** transmitted in V2V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Examiners expect you to know that DSRC ≈ 5.9 GHz, V2X latency targets are in **single-digit milliseconds**, and GPS accuracy is **~2.5 m CEP**. Recalling these boundary values typically earns full 3-mark Part A answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of IoT-Transportation (Operational Logic)

The transportation vertical is decomposed into five interacting pillars. Each pillar maps directly onto a layer of the standard IoT reference architecture (typically the **oneM2M** or **IEEE P2413** model).

**Pillar 1 — Sensing & Perception Layer**
- On-board sensors: **LiDAR, RADAR, Ultrasonic, Cameras, IMU, GPS, OBD-II port readers**.
- Roadside sensors: **Inductive loops, piezoelectric sensors, video image detectors, acoustic sensors**.
- Function: Convert physical phenomena (vehicle presence, speed, weather) into digital signals.

**Pillar 2 — Communication & Networking Layer**
- Short-range: **DSRC (802.11p)**, Bluetooth, BLE.
- Long-range: **C-V2X (LTE-V / 5G NR-V2X)**, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT (for fleet).
- Backhaul: Fiber, LTE/5G, Satellite (for remote logistics).

**Pillar 3 — Edge / Fog Processing**
- Reduces round-trip latency by processing data *near the vehicle* (RSU, MEC server).
- Critical for collision-avoidance decisions where cloud round-trip is too slow.

**Pillar 4 — Cloud & Big Data Analytics**
- Aggregates petabytes of telemetry for traffic prediction, route optimization, anomaly detection.
- Uses streaming frameworks (Kafka, Spark) and ML models (LSTM for traffic flow forecasting).

**Pillar 5 — Application Services**
- Navigation (Google Maps, Waze), predictive maintenance, ride-sharing, insurance telematics, emergency response.

### 2.2 V2X Communication — The Heart of Connected Transportation

**V2X** (Vehicle-to-Everything) is a superset that branches into four sub-paradigms. KTU examiners frequently test the difference between these.

| Sub-Paradigm | Communication Partner | Use-Case Example |
|---|---|---|
| **V2V** (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) | Other cars | Forward collision warning, blind spot alert |
| **V2I** (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure) | Roadside Units (RSU), traffic signals | Signal phase timing, curve speed warning |
| **V2P** (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian) | Smartphone, wearable | Pedestrian crossing alerts |
| **V2N** (Vehicle-to-Network) | Cellular base station / cloud | Real-time HD map updates, weather data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why V2X matters:** A human driver's reaction time is approximately **1.5 seconds**. At 100 km/h, the vehicle travels **~42 m** before braking. V2X reduces reaction time to **< 10 ms**, cutting stopping distance by **~40 m** — often the difference between a crash and a safe stop.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` in place of the pipe symbol `|` inside any table row to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = k \times u$ | **Fundamental traffic flow relation** (Flow = Density × Speed) | vehicles/hour |
| 2 | $u = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$ | **Greenshields speed-density model** | km/h, veh/km |
| 3 | $q_{\max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$ | **Maximum flow (capacity)** — occurs at $k = k_j / 2$ | vehicles/hour |
| 4 | $u_m = \dfrac{u_f}{2}$ | **Speed at maximum flow** | km/h |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{3600}{q}$ | **Average time headway** between vehicles | seconds |
| 6 | $d_{\text{stop}} = d_{\text{react}} + d_{\text{brake}} = u \cdot t_r + \dfrac{u^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$ | **Total stopping distance** | metres |
| 7 | $t_r \approx 1.5 \text{ s}$ | **Standard human perception-reaction time** | seconds |
| 8 | $\mu \in [0.7, 0.9]$ for dry asphalt | **Coefficient of friction** (tire–road) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Capacity} = c \cdot s \cdot \dfrac{3600}{h}$ | **Lane capacity** (c = lanes, s = saturation flow) | vehicles/hour |
| 10 | $\text{PCU} = \dfrac{\text{Vehicle length (m)} + \text{Spacing (m)}}{6 \text{ m}}$ | **Passenger Car Unit** conversion | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{BEAM} = 6 \text{ MHz}$ or $10 \text{ MHz}$ | **DSRC channel bandwidth** | MHz |
| 12 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_p$ | **Friis transmission equation** (simplified) | dBm |
| 13 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | **Free-space path loss** | dB |
| 14 | $E[\text{latency}]_{\text{V2X}} < 100 \text{ ms}$ | **Safety-message deadline** (5GAA spec) | milliseconds |
| 15 | $\text{LoS} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_{ant}}{d_{horizon}}\right)$ | **Line-of-sight geometry** for V2I | radians / degrees |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs (Tesla, Toyota, BMW):** Use V2X to coordinate lane-merging and platooning, reducing fuel consumption by **up to 15%** in truck convoys.
- **Smart Cities (Singapore, Barcelona):** Deploy adaptive traffic signals driven by IoT sensors — Singapore has reduced average commute time by **~25%** since 2018.
- **Logistics (Maersk, DHL):** IoT-enabled cold chain monitoring reduces spoilage losses by **30–40%** in pharma logistics.
- **Insurance (Progressive, Pay-As-You-Drive):** Telematics-based premiums lower accident rates by **~10%** among enrolled drivers (per Deloitte 2022 report).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — "From Sensor to Steering Decision"

| Stage | Mathematical / Logical Transformation | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Sensing | $s(t) = f(\text{physical stimulus}) + n(t)$ | Sensor output $s(t)$ corrupted by noise $n(t)$; requires filtering |
| 2. Sampling | $s[n] = s(t)\big\vert_{t=nT_s}$ | Sampling rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ per Nyquist |
| 3. Preprocessing | $s_{filt}[n] = s[n] * h[n]$ | Convolution with filter impulse response |
| 4. Feature extraction | $\mathbf{x} = \Phi(s_{filt}[n])$ | Maps signal to feature vector (e.g., position, velocity) |
| 5. Transmission | $P_r = P_t - L_p + G_t + G_r$ | Friis path-loss model governs link budget |
| 6. Decision | $a = \arg\min_a \text{Cost}(\mathbf{x}, a)$ | Control law (e.g., brake, accelerate, steer) |
| 7. Actuation | $\tau_{brake} = f(F_{brake}, r_{wheel})$ | Brake torque = f(force, wheel radius) |

### 3.2 Derivation: Greenshields Speed-Density-Flow Model (Full Step-by-Step)

The **Greenshields model (1935)** is the classical macroscopic traffic-flow model and is *guaranteed* in KTU examination Part B questions. The derivation is as follows.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation.** Greenshields observed that speed $u$ decreases linearly with density $k$.

$$u = a + b \cdot k$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions.**
- At free-flow density $k \to 0$, speed equals free-flow speed $u_f$.
- At jam density $k = k_j$, speed drops to $0$ (bumper-to-bumper).

Substituting:

$$u_f = a + b(0) \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = u_f$$

$$0 = u_f + b(k_j) \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}$$

**Step 3 — Final linear speed-density relation.**

$$u = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply fundamental relation $q = k \cdot u$ to get flow-density curve.**

$$q = k \cdot u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right) = u_f k - \dfrac{u_f k^2}{k_j}$$

**Step 5 — Maximize flow $q$ to find capacity.** Differentiate $q$ with respect to $k$ and set to zero.

$$\dfrac{dq}{dk} = u_f - \dfrac{2 u_f k}{k_j} = 0$$

$$u_f = \dfrac{2 u_f k_{opt}}{k_j} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_{opt} = \dfrac{k_j}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $k_{opt}$ back into the speed and flow equations.**

$$u_{opt} = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k_j/2}{k_j}\right) = \dfrac{u_f}{2}$$

$$q_{\max} = \dfrac{k_j}{2} \cdot \dfrac{u_f}{2} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$$

This completes the derivation. For typical KTU numericals: if $u_f = 80$ km/h and $k_j = 120$ veh/km, then $q_{\max} = 2400$ veh/hour and $u_{opt} = 40$ km/h.

### 3.3 Derivation: Stopping Distance with Reaction Time

**Given:** A vehicle travelling at initial speed $u$ must stop upon sensing an obstacle.

**Step 1 — Reaction phase (uniform velocity).**
The driver takes time $t_r$ to perceive, decide, and actuate the brake.

$$d_{\text{react}} = u \cdot t_r$$

**Step 2 — Braking phase (uniform deceleration).**
Using kinematic equation $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a d$ with final $v = 0$ and deceleration $a = -\mu g$:

$$0 = u^2 - 2 \mu g \cdot d_{\text{brake}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{u^2}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 3 — Total stopping distance.**

$$d_{\text{stop}} = u \cdot t_r + \dfrac{u^2}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style).**
A vehicle travels at $u = 108$ km/h $= 30$ m/s. Reaction time $t_r = 1.5$ s. Friction $\mu = 0.7$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$d_{\text{react}} = 30 \times 1.5 = 45 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{30^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{900}{13.734} \approx 65.5 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{\text{stop}} = 45 + 65.5 = 110.5 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Connection:** A V2V system with **10 ms latency** would replace the human reaction component $u \cdot t_r$ with a near-zero machine reaction. At 30 m/s, this saves **30 × 1.49 = 44.7 m** — a lifesaving margin.

### 3.4 Analytical Latency Calculation (V2X)

**Step 1 — Define the total latency budget.**

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop} + \tau_{rx} + \tau_{act}}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in representative values for a 5G NR-V2X system.**

$$\tau_{total} = 5 + 8 + 4 + 0.003 + 2 + 3 = 22.003 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Validate against the safety threshold.**

$$\tau_{total} = 22.003 \text{ ms} \ll 100 \text{ ms (SAE J3061 safety deadline)}$$

The system meets the *reactive safety* requirement. For *preventive* safety (platooning, lane merge), the stricter **20 ms** threshold requires 5G URLLC at the physical layer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: IoT Transportation — Layered Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL["PERCEPTION AND SENSING LAYER"]
        nodeA1["Onboard Sensors - GPS IMU LiDAR RADAR Camera OBD II"]
        nodeA2["Roadside Sensors - Inductive Loop Piezo Sensor Video Detector Acoustic Sensor"]

    end

    subgraph NL["COMMUNICATION AND NETWORK LAYER"]
        nodeB1["Short Range - DSRC 802.11p BLE WiFi"]
        nodeB2["Cellular V2X - LTE V 5G NR V2X C V2X"]
        nodeB3["Long Range and Backhaul - LoRaWAN Satellite Fiber"]
    end

    subgraph EL["EDGE AND FOG PROCESSING LAYER"]
        nodeC1["MEC Server at RSU"]

        nodeE4["Logistics - Fleet Tracking Cold Chain ETA Prediction"]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA1 --> nodeB2

    nodeE1 -. Feedback Control .- nodeA1
    nodeE2 -. Feedback Control .- nodeA2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent *forward data flow* (sensing → application). Dashed arrows represent *feedback control* (application commands re-enter the sensing layer to influence actuator behaviour — the defining property of a true Cyber-Physical System).

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: V2X Sub-Paradigm Communication Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["VEHICLE OBU"] -->|V2V| V2["OTHER VEHICLE OBU"]
    V -->|V2I| I["ROADSIDE UNIT RSU"]
    V -->|V2P| P["PEDESTRIAN SMARTPHONE"]
    V -->|V2N| N["CELLULAR BASE STATION AND CLOUD"]
    I -->|I2V back to vehicle| V
    N -->|OTA updates and HD map| V
    P -->|Crossing intent| V
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Smart Traffic Signal (IoT-enabled)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        ind["Inductive Loop Detector"]
        cam["ANPR Camera"]
        emg["Emergency Vehicle Transceiver"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL BLOCK"]
```
